options.text: heavy straining or Valsalva-style lifting unless cleared by the provider

rationale_en: AHA guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (such as brisk walking) per week. Heavy straining or lifting against extreme resistance (especially involving the Valsalva maneuver, which causes breath-holding/straining) triggers sudden, dangerous spikes in blood pressure and should be avoided unless cleared by a provider. Isotonic aerobic walking and stretching are safe and encouraged.

stem_en: Which findings from the well-child note represent risk factors for lead exposure in this toddler? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Well-Child Progress Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Father reports the toddler is active, eating well, and meeting milestones. They recently moved into a rental house built in 1950. The father notices some peeling paint on the windowsills in the playroom. The toddler frequently puts her toys and hands in her mouth.

Objective:
- Age: 18 months
- Weight: 11.2 kg (50th percentile)
- Development: Walks well, speaks 6-10 words, stacks 3 blocks. Development is age-appropriate.
- Screening: Lead screening risk questionnaire is positive due to home age and peeling paint.

options_or_rows_en.text: Normal stack of 3 blocks
Home built in 1950
Weight at the 50th percentile
Peeling paint visible on playroom windowsills
Toddler's frequent hand-to-mouth behavior

rationale_en: Houses built before 1978 commonly contain lead-based paint. Peeling or chipping paint on windowsills is a major hazard as lead dust accumulates there. Toddlers are at high risk due to normal developmental hand-to-mouth behaviors, leading to ingestion of lead dust or paint chips. Normal physical growth (50th percentile) and milestone achievement (stacking 3 blocks) are expected findings and not risk factors.

options_or_rows_en.content: The nurse is developing an educational plan for the parents to reduce lead exposure and absorption. The focus is on environmental control in the home and dietary changes that reduce lead uptake in the body.

stem_en: The nurse interprets the toddler's blood lead level of 18 mcg/dL. Which action is most appropriate for the nurse to implement?

options_or_rows_en.title: Well-Child Progress Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Father reports the toddler is active, eating well, and meeting milestones. They recently moved into a rental house built in 1950. The father notices some peeling paint on the windowsills in the playroom. The toddler frequently puts her toys and hands in her mouth.

Objective:
- Age: 18 months
- Weight: 11.2 kg (50th percentile)
- Development: Walks well, speaks 6-10 words, stacks 3 blocks. Development is age-appropriate.
- Screening: Lead screening risk questionnaire is positive due to home age and peeling paint.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prepare the client for immediate inpatient intravenous chelation therapy
Provide education on environmental control and schedule a repeat venous blood lead test in 1 to 3 months
Instruct the parents to administer oral activated charcoal immediately
Reassure the family that this level is normal and requires no follow-up

rationale_en: A blood lead level (BLL) of 18 mcg/dL is elevated (elevated is defined by CDC as ≥3.5 mcg/dL). It requires parent education on environmental lead sources, nutritional adjustments to lower absorption, and follow-up venous testing (typically within 1-3 months). Inpatient chelation therapy is indicated only for severe lead poisoning (BLL ≥45 mcg/dL). It is not a normal level. Activated charcoal is ineffective because lead is not absorbed by charcoal.

options_or_rows_en.content:

- Development: Walks well, speaks 6-10 words, stacks 3 blocks. Development is age-appropriate.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ensure adequate daily intake of dietary iron and calcium
Encourage the child to eat frequent small meals rather than empty stomach
Provide a high-fat diet containing abundant saturated oils
Indicated
Contraindicated

rationale_en: Dietary iron, calcium, and vitamin C are indicated because they compete with lead for absorption sites in the GI tract; adequate intake reduces lead absorption. Lead is absorbed more rapidly on an empty stomach, so frequent small meals are indicated. High-fat diets are contraindicated because fat increases lead absorption.

options_or_rows_en.content:

options.text: hot tap water straight from the faucet
cold tap water run for 1 to 2 minutes

rationale_en: Dry sweeping or dusting spreads lead-laden dust into the air, causing inhalation risks; wet cleaning methods (wet mop, damp cloths) trap dust and are indicated. Hot tap water dissolves lead from plumbing pipes more readily than cold water, so only cold tap water (run for 1-2 minutes to flush pipes) should be used for drinking, cooking, or formula reconstitution (and then heated if needed). Unfiltered well water is not recommended.

stem_en: Which medication should the nurse expect to administer as the priority pharmacologic intervention to relieve the client's dyspnea?

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospice Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Client reports severe breathlessness at rest. Feels like he 'cannot catch his breath' and expresses anxiety about suffocating. Denies pain. Comfort is the primary goal of care.

Objective:
- Respiratory rate: 28 breaths/min, shallow.
- Heart rate: 96 bpm.
- Oxygen saturation: 92% on room air.
- Lungs: Crackles audible in bilateral bases.
- General: Anxious facial expression, using intercostal accessory muscles for breathing. Alert and able to swallow.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prescribed low-dose oral or sublingual liquid morphine
High-dose intravenous furosemide
Intravenous lorazepam bolus
Prescribed albuterol nebulizer therapy

rationale_en: In palliative and hospice care, low-dose opioids (especially oral/sublingual liquid morphine) are the gold standard and priority drug for managing severe dyspnea ('air hunger'). Morphine reduces respiratory rate, decreases the perception of breathlessness, dilates pulmonary vessels, and reduces heart failure preload, without causing significant respiratory depression in these carefully titrated doses. While furosemide treats fluid overload, morphine directly targets the symptom of air hunger. Albuterol is for bronchospasm, not cardiac congestion. Lorazepam reduces anxiety but is secondary to morphine for dyspnea.

stem_en: Which medication should the nurse expect to administer as the priority pharmacologic intervention to relieve the client's dyspnea?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Reassessment Note
options_or_rows_en.content: The client has received two doses of prescribed oral morphine liquid (2.5 mg every 2 hours). Currently resting in semi-Fowler's position with a personal cooling fan blowing gently across his face. The client states his breathing feels much easier. His face is relaxed, and he is sleeping occasionally.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prescribed low-dose oral or sublingual liquid morphine

stem_en: The nurse monitors the client's response to morphine. Select the appropriate options from the dropdown menus to complete the explanation of the drug's action in palliative care.

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospice Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Client reports severe breathlessness at rest. Feels like he 'cannot catch his breath' and expresses anxiety about suffocating. Denies pain. Comfort is the primary goal of care.

Objective:
- Respiratory rate: 28 breaths/min, shallow.
- Heart rate: 96 bpm.
- Oxygen saturation: 92% on room air.
- Lungs: Crackles audible in bilateral bases.
- General: Anxious facial expression, using intercostal accessory muscles for breathing. Alert and able to swallow.

options.text: systemic vasodilation and pooling of blood

rationale_en: In dyspnea, morphine blunts the respiratory center's response to hypercapnia (elevated carbon dioxide) and hypoxia, reducing the frantic drive to breathe. Hemodynamically, morphine causes systemic vasodilation (venodilation), which pools blood in the periphery, reducing venous return (preload) to the heart and easing pulmonary congestion. It does not cause vasoconstriction or bronchoconstriction.

stem_en: The nurse implements non-pharmacological comfort measures for the client. For each nursing action, click to specify if it is indicated or contraindicated for this client.

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospice Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Client reports severe breathlessness at rest. Feels like he 'cannot catch his breath' and expresses anxiety about suffocating. Denies pain. Comfort is the primary goal of care.

Objective:
- Respiratory rate: 28 breaths/min, shallow.
- Heart rate: 96 bpm.
- Oxygen saturation: 92% on room air.
- Lungs: Crackles audible in bilateral bases.
- General: Anxious facial expression, using intercostal accessory muscles for breathing. Alert and able to swallow.

options_or_rows_en.text: Position a fan to blow cool air directly across the client's face
Maintain the client in a flat supine position
Apply low-flow oxygen via nasal cannula only if symptomatic and hypoxic
Contraindicated
Indicated

rationale_en: Blowing cool air across the face stimulates the trigeminal nerve (V2/V3 branches), which centrally decreases the sensation of dyspnea; this is highly indicated. Flat supine positioning is contraindicated as it increases venous return, worsening pulmonary congestion and dyspnea. Oxygen therapy in palliative care is indicated for documented hypoxemia with symptomatic distress; if oxygen saturation is normal, air/fan therapy is just as effective and causes less mucosal drying.

stem_en: The nurse reassesses the client 4 hours later. Which findings indicate a successful therapeutic response to the palliative dyspnea management? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospice Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Client reports severe breathlessness at rest. Feels like he 'cannot catch his breath' and expresses anxiety about suffocating. Denies pain. Comfort is the primary goal of care.

Objective:
- Respiratory rate: 28 breaths/min, shallow.
- Heart rate: 96 bpm.
- Oxygen saturation: 92% on room air.
- Lungs: Crackles audible in bilateral bases.
- General: Anxious facial expression, using intercostal accessory muscles for breathing. Alert and able to swallow.

options_or_rows_en.text: Oxygen saturation increased to 100% on high-flow face mask
Absence of intercostal accessory muscle use
Client statement that breathing feels much easier
Respiratory rate of 18 breaths/min, deep and regular
Relaxed facial expression and sleeping occasionally

rationale_en: Signs of successful relief of dyspnea include normalization of respiratory rate (18/min), relaxed facial features (no grimacing or nasal flaring), occasional restful sleep, subjective reports of comfort, and resolved accessory muscle use. High-flow oxygen to achieve 100% saturation is not a goal in palliative end-stage heart failure; it is unnecessary, drying, invasive, and can cause discomfort.

stem_en: Which clinical finding in the triage note is the priority indicator of upper airway compromise?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Parents state the child had mild cold symptoms for two days. Tonight, he woke up with a loud barking cough and sounds like he is struggling to breathe. 

Objective:
- Temperature: 101.1°F (38.4°C)
- Heart rate: 138 bpm
- Respiratory rate: 32 breaths/min
- Oxygen saturation: 94% on room air
- Auscultation: Mild inspiratory stridor audible at rest, clear chest bilaterally.
- Inspection: Agitated and crying; mild subcostal and intercostal retractions visible.

options_or_rows_en.text: Inspiratory stridor at rest
Temperature of 101.1°F (38.4°C)
Oxygen saturation of 94% on room air
Heart rate of 138 bpm

rationale_en: Inspiratory stridor at rest indicates significant swelling and narrowing of the larynx and subglottic airway (croup). While stridor only during agitation is moderate, stridor at rest signifies impending airway obstruction and is the priority finding. Tachycardia and mild desaturation (94%) are consequences of the respiratory distress, but airway obstruction is the primary pathology. Fever is expected with viral croup.

stem_en: Which clinical finding in the triage note is the priority indicator of upper airway compromise?

options_or_rows_en.content: The child was administered prescribed nebulized racemic epinephrine and oral dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg). Currently, the child is resting quietly in his mother's arms. Stridor is no longer audible at rest. Oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Retractions are resolved. Heart rate has decreased to 110 bpm.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Parents state the child had mild cold symptoms for two days. Tonight, he woke up with a loud barking cough and sounds like he is struggling to breathe. 

Objective:
- Temperature: 101.1°F (38.4°C)
- Heart rate: 138 bpm
- Respiratory rate: 32 breaths/min
- Oxygen saturation: 94% on room air
- Auscultation: Mild inspiratory stridor audible at rest, clear chest bilaterally.
- Inspection: Agitated and crying; mild subcostal and intercostal retractions visible.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine as prescribed
Administer oral or intramuscular dexamethasone as prescribed
Perform a throat culture using a tongue depressor to rule out strep throat
Contraindicated
Indicated

rationale_en: Nebulized racemic epinephrine is indicated to quickly reduce subglottic mucosal edema via mucosal vasoconstriction. Dexamethasone is a corticosteroid indicated to decrease airway inflammation (with a longer-lasting effect). Performing a throat culture or using a tongue depressor is strictly contraindicated in kids with croup or suspected epiglottitis, as inserting an object into the posterior pharynx can trigger severe laryngospasm and complete airway obstruction.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Parents state the child had mild cold symptoms for two days. Tonight, he woke up with a loud barking cough and sounds like he is struggling to breathe. 

Objective:
- Temperature: 101.1°F (38.4°C)
- Heart rate: 138 bpm
- Respiratory rate: 32 breaths/min
- Oxygen saturation: 94% on room air
- Auscultation: Mild inspiratory stridor audible at rest, clear chest bilaterally.
- Inspection: Agitated and crying; mild subcostal and intercostal retractions visible.

options.text: decreased level of consciousness and pupillary changes

rationale_en: Racemic epinephrine has a rapid onset (within minutes) but a short duration of action (about 1 to 2 hours). As the medication wears off, the subglottic edema can recur, causing rebound stridor and severe respiratory distress. The nurse must monitor the child in the ED or hospital for at least 2 to 3 hours after administration to ensure symptoms do not rebound.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: Parents state the child had mild cold symptoms for two days. Tonight, he woke up with a loud barking cough and sounds like he is struggling to breathe. 

Objective:
- Temperature: 101.1°F (38.4°C)
- Heart rate: 138 bpm
- Respiratory rate: 32 breaths/min
- Oxygen saturation: 94% on room air
- Auscultation: Mild inspiratory stridor audible at rest, clear chest bilaterally.
- Inspection: Agitated and crying; mild subcostal and intercostal retractions visible.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer prescribed oral dexamethasone only when the child has a barking cough.
Keep the child upright or in a comfortable position when coughing.
Take the child outside into the cool night air if a coughing spasm occurs.
Use a cool-mist humidifier in the child's bedroom.
Monitor for retractions, noisy breathing, or blue discoloration around the lips.

rationale_en: Cool mist humidifier and breathing in cool night air or steam from a hot shower help reduce mucosal swelling and relieve spasm. Staying upright prevents airway compression. Parents must monitor for severe distress signs (retractions, stridor, cyanosis) and seek immediate care. Dexamethasone is a scheduled, one-time or short-course medication, not given PRN for coughing spells.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Treatment Orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
- Blood glucose monitoring before meals and at bedtime.
- Regular insulin subcutaneously before meals based on carbohydrate counting (1 unit per 15g carbs).
- Glargine insulin (long-acting) subcutaneously once daily at bedtime.
- Normal saline lock with saline flushes twice daily.

options_or_rows_en.text: Irritability and confusion
Warm, dry, flushed skin
Shakiness and trembling
Increased urination (polyuria)
Diaphoresis (sweating)

rationale_en: Shakiness, diaphoresis, irritability, confusion, tachycardia, and headache are classic autonomic and neuroglycopenic signs of hypoglycemia. Warm, dry, flushed skin and polyuria (along with polydipsia and polyphagia) are classic indicators of hyperglycemia, caused by osmotic diuresis and cell dehydration.

stem_en: During a class, the child complains of feeling shaky and lightheaded. His blood glucose is checked and is 62 mg/dL. Which action should the school nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Treatment Orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
- Blood glucose monitoring before meals and at bedtime.
- Regular insulin subcutaneously before meals based on carbohydrate counting (1 unit per 15g carbs).
- Glargine insulin (long-acting) subcutaneously once daily at bedtime.
- Normal saline lock with saline flushes twice daily.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the prescribed dose of subcutaneous glucagon
Administer his scheduled bedtime dose of glargine insulin early
Give the child 4 ounces (120 mL) of fruit juice or regular soda
Instruct the child to lie down and recheck in 30 minutes

rationale_en: The client is experiencing mild-to-moderate hypoglycemia (defined as blood glucose <70 mg/dL). If the client is conscious and able to swallow, the 'Rule of 15' should be implemented: administer 15 grams of fast-acting simple carbohydrates (such as 4 oz of juice or soda, 3-4 glucose tablets). Subcutaneous glucagon is indicated only for unconscious or non-swallowing clients. Insulin is contraindicated as it would lower blood glucose further. Rechecking should occur in 15 minutes, not 30 minutes, and simple carbs must not be delayed.

stem_en: The nurse is instructing the parents on subcutaneous insulin injection techniques. For each technique, click to specify if it represents correct or incorrect technique.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Treatment Orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
- Blood glucose monitoring before meals and at bedtime.
- Regular insulin subcutaneously before meals based on carbohydrate counting (1 unit per 15g carbs).
- Glargine insulin (long-acting) subcutaneously once daily at bedtime.
- Normal saline lock with saline flushes twice daily.

options_or_rows_en.text: Rotate insulin injection sites within one anatomical region (e.g., abdomen)
Aspirate for blood return before injecting the insulin
Pinch the skin and inject at a 45-degree or 90-degree angle based on subcutaneous tissue thickness
Incorrect technique
Correct technique

rationale_en: Rotating sites within one anatomical region (such as using different spots on the abdomen) maintains consistent insulin absorption rates while preventing lipodystrophy. Subcutaneous injections do not require aspiration for blood, as major vessels are not targeted and aspiration increases tissue bruising. Pinching the skin and inserting the needle at 45 to 90 degrees ensures the medication is delivered into the subcutaneous space.

stem_en: The nurse is reviewing the exercise and school plan with the parents. Select the appropriate options from the dropdown menus to complete the educational guidelines.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Treatment Orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
- Blood glucose monitoring before meals and at bedtime.
- Regular insulin subcutaneously before meals based on carbohydrate counting (1 unit per 15g carbs).
- Glargine insulin (long-acting) subcutaneously once daily at bedtime.
- Normal saline lock with saline flushes twice daily.

options.text: cancel his soccer practice for the day
administer an extra unit of regular insulin

rationale_en: Exercise increases insulin sensitivity and muscle glucose uptake, causing risk of hypoglycemia. If blood glucose is <100 mg/dL before exercise, a 15g carbohydrate snack must be consumed. Injecting insulin into the subcutaneous tissue of an active limb (the thigh, in a soccer player) increases systemic absorption speed due to increased localized blood flow, triggering rapid-onset hypoglycemia. Abdomen is the preferred site.

stem_en: Which findings in the clinic record support the assessment that the client is experiencing postpartum depression? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Outpatient Clinic Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: The client reports feeling constantly overwhelmed, tearful, and exhausted. She states she has difficulty sleeping even when the baby is sleeping. She expresses feelings of inadequacy as a mother, stating, 'I feel like I can't do anything right for my baby.' Her husband reports she cries frequently and has withdrawn from social interactions.

Screening Tool:
- Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score: 15 (Reference: Score ≥10-13 indicates possible depression and warrants further evaluation).

options_or_rows_en.text: EPDS score of 15
Mild fatigue resolving with a single night of sleep
Feelings of inadequacy and guilt about mothering abilities
Frequent tearfulness and social withdrawal
Difficulty sleeping even when the newborn is sleeping

rationale_en: An EPDS score of 15 (exceeding the threshold of 10-13), sleep disturbance unrelated to the infant's schedule, feelings of worthlessness/guilt, persistent tearfulness, and social withdrawal are classic manifestations of postpartum depression. Mild, transient fatigue that resolves with rest is characteristic of normal postpartum fatigue, whereas the client's exhaustion is persistent and coupled with cognitive-affective depressive symptoms.

stem_en: Which findings in the clinic record support the assessment that the client is experiencing postpartum depression? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Safety Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: The nurse escorts the client to a private room for a safety interview. On questioning, the client denies active suicidal ideation or plans to harm herself. However, she states, 'Sometimes I think my husband and baby would be better off without me.' When asked about thoughts of harming her baby, she appears startled, denies it, and starts crying.

stem_en: Based on the nursing safety interview, which action is the priority for the nurse to implement first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Outpatient Clinic Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: The client reports feeling constantly overwhelmed, tearful, and exhausted. She states she has difficulty sleeping even when the baby is sleeping. She expresses feelings of inadequacy as a mother, stating, 'I feel like I can't do anything right for my baby.' Her husband reports she cries frequently and has withdrawn from social interactions.

Screening Tool:
- Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score: 15 (Reference: Score ≥10-13 indicates possible depression and warrants further evaluation).

options_or_rows_en.text: Initiate an emergency involuntary psychiatric admission
Instruct the husband to take the baby home to prevent harm to the infant
Reassure the client that these feelings are normal and will pass in a few days
Perform a detailed safety assessment, directly asking the client about suicidal plans and intent

rationale_en: The client's statement that her husband and baby 'would be better off without me' is a passive expression of suicidal ideation and hopelessness. The immediate priority is a thorough safety assessment, directly asking about suicidal thoughts, plans, and intent. Separating the baby is premature as she denied intent to harm the infant and did not show postpartum psychosis. Dismissing her feelings as normal is false reassurance and dangerous. Involuntary admission requires documented danger to self or others that cannot be managed voluntarily; assessment must precede this.

stem_en: The nurse compares transient postpartum emotional changes with clinical postpartum depression. Select the appropriate options from the dropdown menus to complete the comparison.

options_or_rows_en.title: Outpatient Clinic Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: The client reports feeling constantly overwhelmed, tearful, and exhausted. She states she has difficulty sleeping even when the baby is sleeping. She expresses feelings of inadequacy as a mother, stating, 'I feel like I can't do anything right for my baby.' Her husband reports she cries frequently and has withdrawn from social interactions.

Screening Tool:
- Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score: 15 (Reference: Score ≥10-13 indicates possible depression and warrants further evaluation).

options.text: supportive home monitoring and rest alone

rationale_en: Postpartum 'baby blues' affect up to 80% of new mothers, peak around day 5, and resolve spontaneously within 10-14 days. Postpartum depression (PPD) is a clinical depressive disorder that affects 10-15% of mothers, lasts beyond 2 weeks, and requires medical assessment, counseling/psychotherapy, and often antidepressants. Rest alone is insufficient for PPD. ECT is reserved for refractory, psychotic cases, not first-line therapy.

options_or_rows_en.title: Outpatient Clinic Note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Subjective: The client reports feeling constantly overwhelmed, tearful, and exhausted. She states she has difficulty sleeping even when the baby is sleeping. She expresses feelings of inadequacy as a mother, stating, 'I feel like I can't do anything right for my baby.' Her husband reports she cries frequently and has withdrawn from social interactions.

Screening Tool:
- Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) score: 15 (Reference: Score ≥10-13 indicates possible depression and warrants further evaluation).

options_or_rows_en.text: Validate the client's feelings and reassure her that postpartum depression is a treatable medical condition, not a personal failure
Coordinate a support network of family and friends to assist with infant care and household tasks
Advise the client to avoid talking about her negative thoughts to prevent worsening her mood
Indicated
Contraindicated

rationale_en: Validating the mother's feelings (r1) reduces guilt and promotes treatment adherence. Coordinating support (r2) ensures safety, reduces exhaustion, and provides rest. Advising the client to avoid talking about negative thoughts (r3) is contraindicated; expressing feelings is therapeutic, while suppression increases isolation and prevents safety monitoring.

stem_en: Based on the triage assessment, which clinical findings support a diagnosis of pyloric stenosis and dehydration? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Maternal Report: The infant vomits after almost every feed. The vomiting has become increasingly forceful, projectile, and non-bilious. He is constantly hungry and eagerly sucks on his pacifier or bottle immediately after vomiting.

Physical Examination:
- Weight: 3.8 kg (Birth weight: 4.1 kg)
- Fontanelle: Depressed
- Mucous membranes: Dry
- Skin turgor: Decreased
- Abdomen: Mildly distended in the epigastric region. A small, firm, olive-shaped mass is palpable to the right of the umbilicus.

options_or_rows_en.text: Constant hunger immediately after vomiting
Palpable olive-shaped epigastric mass
Weight gain from birth weight
Projectile, non-bilious vomiting after feeding
Depressed fontanelle and dry mucous membranes

rationale_en: Projectile non-bilious vomiting, a palpable olive-shaped mass in the epigastric region, and hunger immediately after vomiting are classic signs of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Depressed fontanelles and dry mucous membranes are signs of moderate-to-severe dehydration resulting from excessive vomiting. Weight loss (not weight gain) is expected due to the inability to retain nutrients.

stem_en: Based on the triage assessment, which clinical findings support a diagnosis of pyloric stenosis and dehydration? Select all that apply.

The infant is alert and crying for feeds. The provider has written orders to initiate postoperative feedings.

stem_en: The nurse considers the acid-base and electrolyte changes that occur with severe, persistent vomiting. For each laboratory value below, click to specify whether it would be an expected finding or an unexpected finding in an infant with pyloric stenosis.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Maternal Report: The infant vomits after almost every feed. The vomiting has become increasingly forceful, projectile, and non-bilious. He is constantly hungry and eagerly sucks on his pacifier or bottle immediately after vomiting.

Physical Examination:
- Weight: 3.8 kg (Birth weight: 4.1 kg)
- Fontanelle: Depressed
- Mucous membranes: Dry
- Skin turgor: Decreased
- Abdomen: Mildly distended in the epigastric region. A small, firm, olive-shaped mass is palpable to the right of the umbilicus.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 3.1 mEq/L
Chloride 88 mEq/L
Expected finding for severe vomiting
Unexpected finding

rationale_en: A pH of 7.25 indicates acidosis, which is an unexpected finding in pyloric stenosis (infants with severe, persistent vomiting typically present with hypokalemic, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, with a pH >7.45). Potassium of 3.1 mEq/L (hypokalemia) and Chloride of 88 mEq/L (hypochloremia) are expected findings due to the loss of potassium, hydrogen, and chloride ions in gastric secretions.

stem_en: The nurse is preparing the client for a surgical pyloromyotomy. Select the appropriate options from the dropdown menus to complete the clinical plan.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Maternal Report: The infant vomits after almost every feed. The vomiting has become increasingly forceful, projectile, and non-bilious. He is constantly hungry and eagerly sucks on his pacifier or bottle immediately after vomiting.

Physical Examination:
- Weight: 3.8 kg (Birth weight: 4.1 kg)
- Fontanelle: Depressed
- Mucous membranes: Dry
- Skin turgor: Decreased
- Abdomen: Mildly distended in the epigastric region. A small, firm, olive-shaped mass is palpable to the right of the umbilicus.

options.text: bolus dose of potassium chloride IV push
frequent, small volume breast milk feed

rationale_en: Preoperative management of pyloric stenosis focuses on correcting dehydration and electrolyte imbalances (specifically hypokalemia and hypochloremia) and maintaining NPO status to prevent further vomiting and aspiration risk. IV fluids with potassium chloride are key, but potassium must never be given IV push as it is lethal. Oral intake is contraindicated once the diagnosis is made.

stem_en: The nurse is reviewing orders for postoperative feedings on Postoperative Day 1. Which action should the nurse implement first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Maternal Report: The infant vomits after almost every feed. The vomiting has become increasingly forceful, projectile, and non-bilious. He is constantly hungry and eagerly sucks on his pacifier or bottle immediately after vomiting.

Physical Examination:
- Weight: 3.8 kg (Birth weight: 4.1 kg)
- Fontanelle: Depressed

options_or_rows_en.text: Initiate feedings with small amounts of clear liquids or oral rehydration solution
Administer prescribed antiemetics and delay feeding
Administer a full bottle of standard infant formula
Keep the infant strictly NPO for another 24 hours

rationale_en: Postoperative feedings following a pyloromyotomy typically begin 12 to 24 hours after surgery. Feeding is introduced gradually, starting with small amounts of clear liquids (such as sterile water or electrolyte solution) or diluted formula/breast milk. If tolerated, the volume and concentration are gradually increased. Spitting up is common initially but feeding is not discontinued. Full feeds immediately are contraindicated. Strict NPO is unnecessary unless complications occur.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the client that new medications prevent all deformities now.
Provide a referral to an occupational therapist.
Encourage the client to express her fears about her career.
Teach joint protection techniques.
Inappropriate
Appropriate

rationale_en: Validating fears, providing practical tools (joint protection), and involving specialists (occupational therapy) empower the client. False reassurance about medications preventing *all* deformities is inappropriate.

stem_en: Which statement by the nurse best addresses the client's use of denial?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client states, 'I am terrified my hands are going to become deformed. I work as a graphic designer, and I need my hands. Sometimes I just pretend I don't have this disease, but then my joints ache so badly in the morning.'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'Denial is a normal first step, but you need to move past it now.'
'If you keep pretending you don't have RA, your joints will get worse.'
'It is common to want to ignore a new diagnosis. Let's talk about what makes it hardest to accept.'
'You have a mild case, so there is no need to worry right now.'

rationale_en: This response normalizes the feeling (validates) while gently inviting the client to explore the underlying fears preventing acceptance.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports abdominal fullness and no bowel movement for 4 days. Bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants. She is taking oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen for pain. She is on strict bed rest except for physical therapy. Fluid intake is 800 mL/day.

options_or_rows_en.text: Encourage fluid intake of 2000-3000 mL/day (if not contraindicated).
Administer a prescribed daily stimulant laxative long-term.
Provide high-fiber snacks like prunes or bran.
Administer docusate sodium daily as prescribed.
Question
Implement

rationale_en: Hydration, fiber, and stool softeners (docusate) are first-line treatments. Long-term use of stimulant laxatives causes bowel dependence and should be questioned.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports abdominal fullness and no bowel movement for 4 days. Bowel sounds are hypoactive in all quadrants. She is taking oral hydrocodone-acetaminophen for pain. She is on strict bed rest except for physical therapy. Fluid intake is 800 mL/day.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform a digital rectal examination to check for impaction.
Tell the client to strain harder.
Administer an enema immediately.
Provide privacy and assist the client to the bedside commode.

rationale_en: Positioning and privacy are critical. A commode allows for a natural sitting position which uses gravity to aid defecation, unlike a bedpan.

stem_en: Which cues indicate the wife is experiencing caregiver role strain? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: The wife states, 'I am just so tired all the time. He wanders at night so I can't sleep. I haven't been to my own doctor in a year, and I sometimes forget to take my own blood pressure medicine.' The wife begins to cry during the interview.

options_or_rows_en.text: Sleep deprivation
Neglect of personal health needs
Living in the same home as the client
Constant fatigue
Emotional lability (crying)

rationale_en: Caregiver strain manifests as physical (fatigue, sleep loss), emotional (crying), and behavioral (neglecting own meds/appointments) changes.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: The wife states, 'I am just so tired all the time. He wanders at night so I can't sleep. I haven't been to my own doctor in a year, and I sometimes forget to take my own blood pressure medicine.' The wife begins to cry during the interview.

options_or_rows_en.text: Suggesting the wife ask adult children to sit with the husband one afternoon a week.
Advising the wife to focus harder on the husband's needs to prevent his wandering.
Recommending an adult day care program for the husband.
Providing information on Alzheimer's caregiver support groups.
Inappropriate
Appropriate

rationale_en: Interventions must focus on respite (relief) for the caregiver and peer support. Telling her to try harder exacerbates the guilt and burnout.

stem_en: How should the nurse interpret the client's presentation?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports a 5 lb (2.3 kg) weight loss since his wife died. He states, 'I miss her terribly, and the house feels so empty. I haven't felt like going to my weekly card game.' He also states, 'I know she is in a better place and no longer suffering.'

options_or_rows_en.text: He is experiencing disenfranchised grief.
He is in the denial stage of grief.
He is experiencing normal, uncomplicated grief.
He is experiencing complicated grief requiring psychiatric intervention.

rationale_en: At 2 months post-loss, sadness, mild weight loss, and temporary social withdrawal are normal grieving behaviors. His ability to find meaning ('she is in a better place') indicates healthy processing.

stem_en: What is the nurse's most therapeutic response to the client's statement, 'The house feels so empty'?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Interview
options_or_rows_en.content: Client reports a 5 lb (2.3 kg) weight loss since his wife died. He states, 'I miss her terribly, and the house feels so empty. I haven't felt like going to my weekly card game.' He also states, 'I know she is in a better place and no longer suffering.'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'Perhaps you should consider downsizing to a smaller apartment.'
'You should force yourself to go to your card game to take your mind off it.'
'It sounds like you are feeling very lonely without her here.'
'It will get easier as time passes; you just need to stay busy.'

rationale_en: Reflecting feelings (validating) encourages the client to explore their emotions. Giving advice or offering false reassurance shuts down communication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Has not seen a healthcare provider in 4 years. He lives independently. Reports a mild chronic cough. Asks, 'What shots do I need at my age?'

options_or_rows_en.text: Tdap or Td booster (if due or immunization history unknown)
Pneumococcal vaccine
HPV vaccine
Recombinant zoster vaccine
Influenza vaccine (high-dose)

rationale_en: Adults ≥65 need high-dose influenza, pneumococcal, zoster, and Tdap (if due) vaccines. HPV is not indicated for this age group.

stem_en: The client states, 'I don't want the flu shot because it gave me the flu last time.' What is the nurse's best response?

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Medical history includes COPD and hypertension. Has not seen a healthcare provider in 4 years. He lives independently. Reports a mild chronic cough. Asks, 'What shots do I need at my age?'

options_or_rows_en.text: 'We can skip the flu shot this year since you had a bad reaction.'
'The injected flu vaccine is inactivated and cannot cause the flu. You may have experienced a mild immune response or a cold.'
'You should take acetaminophen before the injection to prevent getting sick.'
'The flu shot contains a live virus, so mild symptoms are normal.'

rationale_en: The injected influenza vaccine contains inactivated virus and cannot cause influenza. Mild systemic reactions (muscle aches, low fever) are a normal immune response.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prolonged bed rest
Alert and cooperative cognitive status
First time out of bed after illness

Self-reported leg weakness

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to transfer the client from the bed to the chair. Order the steps of the transfer.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply a gait belt around the client's waist.
Position the chair parallel to the bed and lock the wheels.
Assist the client to dangle legs on the side of the bed for 1-2 minutes.
Instruct the client to pivot and grasp the chair armrests.
Ensure non-slip footwear is on the client.

rationale_en: Safety requires preparing the environment first (lock chair), preparing the client (footwear, dangling to prevent orthostatic hypotension), applying safety equipment (gait belt), and executing the transfer safely.

stem_en: The nurse is teaching the client how to use the standard walker. Click to specify whether each instruction is correct or incorrect.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nursing Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Adjust the walker height so the handgrips are at the level of the wrist crease.
Move the walker forward 6-8 inches, then step forward into it.
Look down at your feet while walking to ensure placement.
When rising from a chair, pull yourself up using the walker handles.
Correct
Incorrect

rationale_en: Walker height should allow a slight elbow bend (wrist crease level). The walker moves first, then the client steps in. Looking down alters balance. Pulling up on a walker can tip it over.

When rising from a chair, pull yourself up using the walker handles.
Correct
Incorrect

rationale_en: Walker height should allow a slight elbow bend (wrist crease level). The walker moves first, then the client steps in. Looking down alters balance. Pulling up on a walker can tip it over.

options_or_rows_en.content: Client has been on bed rest for 5 days. Provider orders: 'Out of bed to chair twice daily.' Client is alert and cooperative but states, 'My legs feel like jelly.' Physical therapy has recommended the use of a standard walker.

rationale_en: The standard walker sequence is: advance the walker 6–8 inches, step the weaker leg into it, then bring the stronger leg forward. This allows the arms to bear weight through the device during the weaker leg's step. An upright posture maintains the center of gravity over the base of support.

stem_en: The client expresses concern that if she quits smoking, she will gain weight. What is the nurse's most appropriate intervention?

options_or_rows_en.title: Health History
options_or_rows_en.content: Smokes 1 pack per day for 25 years. Has tried quitting twice before using nicotine patches, but relapsed when feeling stressed at work. Currently taking an oral contraceptive.

options_or_rows_en.text: Acknowledge that mild weight gain is common and focus the plan on keeping her hands and mouth busy with healthy snacks.
Reassure her that weight gain is a myth and will not happen.
Advise her to start a strict low-calorie diet on the same day she quits smoking.
Tell her to delay quitting until she has lost 10 pounds.

rationale_en: Weight gain is a common reality due to metabolic changes and substituting food for cigarettes. Acknowledging this and providing healthy alternatives is the best approach. Too many strict changes at once (like a strict diet) can lead to failure.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after infectious stool studies and viral PCRs return negative and the tacrolimus trough is found to be subtherapeutic, the nurse must analyze the cluster of findings (new maculopapular rash on acral surfaces, high-volume watery diarrhea, mildly elevated bilirubin with rising liver enzymes, and onset at day 26 post-transplant). Which conclusion is best supported by these clinical data?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Presentation & Baseline Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
The patient presents to the transplant unit stating that she has developed progressively worsening watery diarrhea over the past two days, now occurring five to six times daily, with crampy abdominal pain that is diffuse and rated five out of ten. She also notes a new rash on her palms, the soles of her feet, and her ears that appeared yesterday and has been spreading to her upper chest and back. She reports decreased appetite and mild nausea but no vomiting. She denies fever, cough, or dysuria. She appears fatigued and mildly uncomfortable but is alert, oriented, and conversational.

Baseline vital signs on admission to the unit:
Temperature: 37.3°C (99.1°F)
Heart rate: 96 beats/min
Blood pressure: 108/68 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 97% on room air
Weight: 64.2 kg (down 2.1 kg from clinic visit 5 days ago)

Skin assessment reveals a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on the palms bilaterally, the soles of the feet, the pinnae of both ears, and scattered across the upper chest and posterior shoulders, estimated to cover approximately 30% of body surface area. The rash blanches with pressure and is non-tender. No bullae, desquamation, or mucosal involvement is noted. Oral mucosa is moist but slightly erythematous. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with diffuse tenderness to palpation, hyperactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No guarding or rebound. Stool is greenish-watery without visible blood. Central line site is clean, dry, and without erythema or drainage. Peripheral edema is absent.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient has developed a delayed Clostridioides difficile infection that requires empiric antibiotic therapy until repeat testing.
The presentation represents a severe allergic drug reaction to prophylactic medications requiring their immediate discontinuation.
The symptoms indicate engraftment syndrome, expected during the first month following allogeneic transplantation.
The patient is experiencing acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that the constellation of rash predominantly on palms, soles, and ears, combined with voluminous watery diarrhea and cholestatic liver enzyme elevation, arising in the third to fourth week after allogeneic transplant with a subtherapeutic immunosuppressant level, is the classic triad of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver. The negative infectious workup further supports this conclusion.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after infectious stool studies and viral PCRs return negative and the tacrolimus trough is found to be subtherapeutic, the nurse must analyze the cluster of findings (new maculopapular rash on acral surfaces, high-volume watery diarrhea, mildly elevated bilirubin with rising liver enzymes, and onset at day 26 post-transplant). Which conclusion is best supported by these clinical data?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Update
options_or_rows_en.content:
84 hours into corticosteroid therapy and 48 hours after the addition of ruxolitinib: The patient has shown early but measurable improvement. Stool output over the past 24 hours has decreased to 900 mL with no further visible blood. The rash has stabilized and begun to fade on the upper chest and ears, now covering approximately 40% of body surface area, with no bullae or desquamation. Abdominal cramping has decreased to 3/10. 

Vital signs:
Temperature: 37.2°C (99.0°F)
Heart rate: 92 beats/min
Blood pressure: 110/70 mmHg
Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
Oxygen saturation: 98% on room air

Urine output over the past 8 hours is 320 mL. She is more alert and reports improved comfort.

Repeat labs:
Potassium: 3.8 mEq/L
Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL
Total bilirubin: 2.4 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin: 1.6 mg/dL
ALT: 72 U/L
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL
Blood urea nitrogen: 26 mg/dL
Albumin: 2.4 g/dL
Tacrolimus trough: 12.1 ng/mL
Glucose: 162 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 8.8 g/dL (post-transfusion)

Blood cultures drawn at the time of fever in Stage 2 have returned with no growth at 48 hours. The team discusses a slow oral diet advancement beginning with clear liquids and ongoing close monitoring.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient has developed a delayed Clostridioides difficile infection that requires empiric antibiotic therapy until repeat testing.
The presentation represents a severe allergic drug reaction to prophylactic medications requiring their immediate discontinuation.
The symptoms indicate engraftment syndrome, expected during the first month following allogeneic transplantation.
The patient is experiencing acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that the constellation of rash predominantly on palms, soles, and ears, combined with voluminous watery diarrhea and cholestatic liver enzyme elevation, arising in the third to fourth week after allogeneic transplant with a subtherapeutic immunosuppressant level, is the classic triad of acute graft-versus-host disease (aGVHD) involving skin, gastrointestinal tract, and liver. The negative infectious workup further supports this conclusion.

options_or_rows_en.text: Document the findings and plan to address the mildly low potassium with dietary supplementation.
Administer the methylprednisolone as ordered and monitor the next scheduled metabolic panel.
Hold the ordered methylprednisolone until the electrolyte imbalances spontaneously correct.
Promptly communicate the findings to the provider to obtain orders for intravenous electrolyte replacement.

rationale_en: The nurse recognizes that hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia in the setting of ongoing diarrheal losses require prompt intravenous or protocol-based replacement, especially because impending high-dose corticosteroids will further exacerbate renal potassium wasting. Magnesium repletion is also necessary to correct the potassium deficit effectively.

options_or_rows_en.text: Decreased tissue perfusion related to dropping hemoglobin.

options_or_rows_en.content:
The patient presents to the transplant unit stating that she has developed progressively worsening watery diarrhea over the past two days, now occurring five to six times daily, with crampy abdominal pain that is diffuse and rated five out of ten. She also notes a new rash on her palms, the soles of her feet, and her ears that appeared yesterday and has been spreading to her upper chest and back. Skin assessment reveals a diffuse erythematous maculopapular rash on the palms bilaterally, the soles of the feet, the pinnae of both ears, and scattered across the upper chest and posterior shoulders, estimated to cover approximately 30% of body surface area. The rash blanches with pressure and is non-tender. No bullae, desquamation, or mucosal involvement is noted. Oral mucosa is moist but slightly erythematous. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with diffuse tenderness to palpation, hyperactive bowel sounds in all four quadrants. No guarding or rebound. Stool is greenish-watery without visible blood. Central line site is clean, dry, and without erythema or drainage. Peripheral edema is absent.

options_or_rows_en.text: Independently discontinue the tacrolimus infusion to prevent permanent renal damage.
Maintain the current tacrolimus infusion rate, as the rising creatinine is solely due to hypovolemia.
Reduce the tacrolimus infusion rate per the provider order and closely monitor urine output.
Administer a loop diuretic to counteract the tacrolimus-induced nephrotoxicity.

rationale_en: The nurse should reduce the tacrolimus infusion rate per the provider order, monitor urine output and fluid balance, and anticipate repeat trough levels. Tacrolimus is a narrow-therapeutic-index calcineurin inhibitor; its supratherapeutic level contributes to acute kidney injury through renal vasoconstriction. Risk factors here are multifactorial, including hypovolemia from diarrhea, the recent rate increase, hepatic dysfunction impairing metabolism, and concomitant fluconazole prophylaxis. The medication must not be independently discontinued due to the high risk of triggering an aGVHD flare.

Stabilization and fading of rash on upper chest and ears

stem_en: Stage 1: For each cue, identify whether it most strongly supports acute delirium from an infection/dehydration process or the client's known baseline dementia.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Abrupt change over 36 hr from usual cognition and behavior
CAM positive with inattention, disorganized thinking, and fluctuating level of consciousness
Baseline word-finding pauses with orientation to person and place
Fever, leukocytosis with bands, suprapubic tenderness, and nitrite-positive pyuric urine
Mild Alzheimer-type dementia documented 6 months ago
Supports baseline dementia
Supports acute delirium

rationale_en: The acute 36-hour change, fluctuating attention and consciousness, positive CAM, fever, inflammatory markers, suprapubic tenderness, and abnormal urinalysis support delirium triggered by symptomatic infection and dehydration. Her documented mild Alzheimer-type dementia explains chronic baseline memory and word-finding limitations but does not explain the abrupt febrile fluctuating presentation.

stem_en: Stage 1: For each cue, identify whether it most strongly supports acute delirium from an infection/dehydration process or the client's known baseline dementia.

options_or_rows_en.title: Improving infection, hydration, and cognition
options_or_rows_en.content: Overnight the provider issued an IM route order, and the client received one IM dose of haloperidol after repeated non-pharmacologic measures failed and she climbed over the side rail despite the bed alarm. ECG/QTc and electrolyte review met facility parameters before administration. She calmed within 30 min and slept for 3 hr. Morning assessment: T 37.1 °C, HR 82/min, BP 130/72 mm Hg, RR 14/min, SpO₂ 98%. Drowsy but arousable; follows one-step commands; recognizes daughter by name; still believes she is at home and gives the year as "the nineties." Ate half of a soft breakfast with cueing. Urine output past 8 hr: 320 mL, pale yellow. Repeat labs: WBC 10,400/µL; Na 140 mEq/L; BUN 24 mg/dL; creatinine 1.0 mg/dL; glucose 130 mg/dL. Final urine culture: E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone. Repeat CAM remains positive but improved: inattention persists but is less severe; answers are relevant but slow; mildly drowsy without prior hyperalert-somnolent fluctuation. Provider plans transition to oral cephalexin when full-meal tolerance is demonstrated; target discharge back to assisted living in 48-72 hr if delirium continues to clear. Daughter asks, "She's better but still not herself — when will she be back to normal?"

Baseline word-finding pauses with orientation to person and place
Fever, leukocytosis with bands, suprapubic tenderness, and nitrite-positive pyuric urine
Mild Alzheimer-type dementia documented 6 months ago
Supports baseline dementia
Supports acute delirium

rationale_en: The acute 36-hour change, fluctuating attention and consciousness, positive CAM, fever, inflammatory markers, suprapubic tenderness, and abnormal urinalysis support delirium triggered by symptomatic infection and dehydration. Her documented mild Alzheimer-type dementia explains chronic baseline memory and word-finding limitations but does not explain the abrupt febrile fluctuating presentation.

stem_en: Stage 1: The client attempts to stand on the stretcher and swings at staff during transport. Which nursing action has the highest priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Request a benzodiazepine before attempting environmental or sensory interventions.
Stay with the client, lower the bed, use a calm voice, and redirect her while protecting her from falling.
Tell the daughter that the behavior is expected progression of dementia and ask her to leave so the client can rest.
Apply bilateral wrist restraints immediately because the client is combative.

rationale_en: Immediate physical safety is the priority. Hyperactive delirium creates high fall and injury risk, so the nurse should remain with the client, keep the bed low, use calm de-escalation, and redirect or reorient while preserving least-restrictive care.

stem_en: Stage 2: The PRN haloperidol order may be used only for severe agitation that creates an imminent safety threat after non-pharmacologic measures fail. Which nursing actions are appropriate before or after giving this medication? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Review ECG/QTc status, potassium/magnesium status, and other QT-prolonging medications per facility protocol.
Document the specific safety threat and the non-pharmacologic measures attempted before giving the dose.
After administration, reassess agitation, level of sedation, blood pressure, and extrapyramidal symptoms.
Use physical restraints automatically after the medication takes effect to keep the client in bed.
Screen for Parkinson disease or dementia with Lewy bodies before using haloperidol.
Give the medication routinely at bedtime to prevent all nighttime confusion.

rationale_en: For delirium-related agitation in a frail older adult, antipsychotic medication is reserved for an imminent safety threat after least-restrictive measures fail. The nurse should document the indication and failed alternatives, complete ordered or protocol-based cardiac/electrolyte and contraindication checks, and monitor effectiveness and adverse effects after administration.

ECG/QTc and electrolyte review met facility parameters before administration. She calmed within 30 min and slept for 3 hr. Morning assessment: T 37.1 °C, HR 82/min, BP 130/72 mm Hg, RR 14/min, SpO₂ 98%. Drowsy but arousable; follows one-step commands; recognizes daughter by name; still believes she is at home and gives the year as "the nineties." Ate half of a soft breakfast with cueing. Urine output past 8 hr: 320 mL, pale yellow. Repeat labs: WBC 10,400/µL; Na 140 mEq/L; BUN 24 mg/dL; creatinine 1.0 mg/dL; glucose 130 mg/dL. Final urine culture: E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone. Repeat CAM remains positive but improved: inattention persists but is less severe; answers are relevant but slow; mildly drowsy without prior hyperalert-somnolent fluctuation. Provider plans transition to oral cephalexin when full-meal tolerance is demonstrated; target discharge back to assisted living in 48-72 hr if delirium continues to clear. Daughter asks, "She's better but still not herself — when will she be back to normal?"

options_or_rows_en.text: Review ECG/QTc status, potassium/magnesium status, and other QT-prolonging medications per facility protocol.
Document the specific safety threat and the non-pharmacologic measures attempted before giving the dose.
After administration, reassess agitation, level of sedation, blood pressure, and extrapyramidal symptoms.
Use physical restraints automatically after the medication takes effect to keep the client in bed.
Screen for Parkinson disease or dementia with Lewy bodies before using haloperidol.
Give the medication routinely at bedtime to prevent all nighttime confusion.

rationale_en: For delirium-related agitation in a frail older adult, antipsychotic medication is reserved for an imminent safety threat after least-restrictive measures fail. The nurse should document the indication and failed alternatives, complete ordered or protocol-based cardiac/electrolyte and contraindication checks, and monitor effectiveness and adverse effects after administration.

stem_en: Stage 2: The PRN haloperidol order may be used only for severe agitation that creates an imminent safety threat after non-pharmacologic measures fail. Which nursing actions are appropriate before or after giving this medication? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency department assessment and initial data
options_or_rows_en.content: Daughter reports: over 36 hr the client became "a completely different person," talked to people who were not there, tried to climb over the bed rail, slept only in brief agitated bursts, refused breakfast, pulled out her hearing aid, and was incontinent of urine twice overnight. Vital signs: T 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) oral, HR 104/min, BP 148/82 mm Hg, RR 20/min, SpO₂ 96% room air. Exam: restless, pulling at gown and blood-pressure cuff, inconsistent command following, oriented to person only, dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, mild suprapubic tenderness, no costovertebral-angle tenderness, no focal motor deficit. Straight catheter specimen: 60 mL cloudy malodorous urine. Labs: WBC 14,200/µL with 82% neutrophils and 6% bands; Hgb 11.8 g/dL; platelets 210,000/µL; Na 146 mEq/L; K 4.0 mEq/L; Cl 108 mEq/L; HCO₃ 24 mEq/L; BUN 32 mg/dL; creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.9); glucose 158 mg/dL; lactate 1.6 mmol/L. Urinalysis: amber, cloudy, specific gravity 1.028, positive leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, large bacteria, 50-100 WBC/hpf, trace protein. Blood cultures and urine culture sent. Head CT: no acute intracranial pathology.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial orders and CAM screen
options_or_rows_en.content: Orders: ceftriaxone 1 g IV every 24 hr; 0.9% sodium chloride 500 mL IV over 2 hr; maintenance IV fluids at 75 mL/hr. The sulfonamide allergy is documented so sulfonamide antibiotics are avoided. During transport the client attempts to stand on the stretcher, swings at the transporter, and shouts, "Get away from me!" Nursing actions: lights dimmed, calm low voice used, gentle reorientation provided, bed placed in lowest position, call light within reach, daughter invited to remain as a familiar presence, hearing aid cleaned and replaced. CAM screen: acute onset yes; inattention yes; disorganized thinking yes; altered level of consciousness yes with fluctuation between hyperalert agitation and brief drowsiness. CAM positive; provider notified. Hour 3: T 38.1 °C, HR 98/min, BP 140/78 mm Hg, RR 18/min, SpO₂ 97%. Urine output: 40 mL in 2 hr, dark amber. Takes only a few sips of water.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response to fluids and ongoing delirium risk
options_or_rows_en.content: IV bolus complete. Hour 10 vitals: T 37.6 °C, HR 92/min, BP 134/76 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO₂ 98%. Less combative but still confused; intermittently recognizes daughter, misidentifies room and time, repeatedly picks at IV site, has dislodged saline lock once, sleeps in 20-minute stretches, then wakes and calls out. Daughter has been at bedside for 10 hr and is exhausted and tearful. Hour 12 labs: WBC 12,800/µL; Na 143 mEq/L; BUN 28 mg/dL; creatinine 1.1 mg/dL; glucose 142 mg/dL. Urine output over past 4 hr: 160 mL, light yellow. Preliminary urine culture: >100,000 CFU/mL Escherichia coli; susceptibilities pending. Braden Scale score 14; high fall-risk score; no meal intake since admission. Bed alarm active.

options_or_rows_en.title: PRN severe-agitation order and facility safety checks
options_or_rows_en.content: Provider order: haloperidol 0.5 mg PO every 6 hr PRN severe agitation that creates imminent risk of self-harm or harm to others; if the client cannot safely take oral medication, contact the provider for an IM route order. Use only if non-pharmacologic measures fail. Facility protocol before first dose when feasible: verify recent ECG/QTc or obtain ECG per protocol; review potassium and magnesium status and other QT-prolonging medications; screen for Parkinson disease or dementia with Lewy bodies; document the safety threat and failed non-pharmacologic measures. After dose: reassess agitation and level of sedation within 30-60 min; monitor for hypotension, extrapyramidal symptoms, and oversedation; continue least-restrictive delirium care.

Morning assessment: T 37.1 °C, HR 82/min, BP 130/72 mm Hg, RR 14/min, SpO₂ 98%. Drowsy but arousable; follows one-step commands; recognizes daughter by name; still believes she is at home and gives the year as "the nineties." Ate half of a soft breakfast with cueing. Urine output past 8 hr: 320 mL, pale yellow. Repeat labs: WBC 10,400/µL; Na 140 mEq/L; BUN 24 mg/dL; creatinine 1.0 mg/dL; glucose 130 mg/dL. Final urine culture: E. coli sensitive to ceftriaxone. Repeat CAM remains positive but improved: inattention persists but is less severe; answers are relevant but slow; mildly drowsy without prior hyperalert-somnolent fluctuation. Provider plans transition to oral cephalexin when full-meal tolerance is demonstrated; target discharge back to assisted living in 48-72 hr if delirium continues to clear. Daughter asks, "She's better but still not herself — when will she be back to normal?"

stem_en: Stage 3: The daughter asks, "She's better but still not herself — when will she be back to normal?" Which response by the nurse is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.text: "The improvement is encouraging. Delirium often clears gradually over days to weeks, sometimes longer in someone with dementia. Our goal is return to her usual baseline, and the facility will need to know what supervision she still needs."
"This behavior means her dementia has permanently advanced, so you should arrange memory-care placement now."
"Since the antibiotic is working, she should be completely back to normal by tomorrow."
"Try not to ask about the timeline; talking about it can make her confusion worse."

rationale_en: This response validates the daughter's concern, recognizes that improvement is real, sets realistic expectations that delirium may resolve more slowly than infection markers, and frames the goal as return to the client's baseline rather than immediate full orientation. It also connects persistent delirium to safe discharge planning.

stem_en: Stage 2: The client has a Braden Scale score of 14, high fall risk, poor intake, urinary incontinence, disrupted sleep, and ongoing confusion. Which nursing interventions reduce hospital-acquired complication risk? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and baseline function
options_or_rows_en.content: Lives in assisted living. Baseline: oriented to person and place, manages meals and grooming with cueing, converses coherently with occasional word-finding pauses, sleeps through the night. History: mild Alzheimer-type dementia, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, remote hip fracture repair. Allergy: sulfonamides (rash). Home medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg every 6 hr PRN joint pain, metformin 500 mg twice daily, multivitamin daily. No indwelling urinary catheter, no recent hospitalization, no reported fall or head trauma, no new medications, no alcohol use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Cluster nighttime care, reduce noise and light at night, and maintain daytime orientation cues.
Reposition at least every 2 hr, use pressure redistribution as indicated, keep skin clean and dry, and apply moisture barrier to the perineum.
Turn off the bed alarm because alarms can be annoying for confused clients.
Maintain fall precautions such as bed in lowest position, bed alarm, non-skid footwear, clear pathways, and purposeful rounding.
Offer small frequent meals, fluids, and cueing; escalate nutrition support if intake does not improve.
Keep the client in bed with restraints so she does not remove the IV or fall.

rationale_en: Delirium, immobility, incontinence, poor intake, sleep disruption, and Braden score 14 create overlapping risks for falls, pressure injury, dehydration, malnutrition, and prolonged delirium. Prevention requires a bundle: fall precautions, skin and moisture care, nutrition/hydration support, and sleep-preserving delirium care.

stem_en: During the postpartum triage call, highlight the findings that form the urgent preeclampsia-with-severe-features cluster requiring immediate emergency evaluation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and discharge status
options_or_rows_en.content: G1P1, delivered a healthy 3,400-g infant vaginally at 39 weeks 2 days. Prenatal BP values were below 130/80, urine protein screens were negative, and there is no history of chronic hypertension, renal disease, autoimmune disease, or gestational hypertension. Discharged postpartum day 1 with BP 118/74, expected lochia rubra, firm fundus, no edema, and instructions to call for headache, vision changes, or BP above 140/90 on the home cuff. Breastfeeding. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours as needed, docusate sodium.

options_or_rows_en.text: Severe frontal headache rated 8/10 unrelieved by ibuprofen.
Intermittent scotomata described as seeing spots.
New facial and periorbital edema that is markedly different from yesterday.
Home BP is 162/108 on two readings five minutes apart.
Lochia has transitioned to light serosa.
Voiding without difficulty with estimated adequate urine output.
Denies chest pain and shortness of breath.
Reports poor sleep related to newborn care.

rationale_en: The severe headache unrelieved by analgesic, visual scotomata, new facial/periorbital edema, and repeated BP of 162/108 on postpartum day 4 are an urgent severe-feature cluster. Expected lochia, adequate voiding, no chest pain, and poor sleep do not make the cluster less urgent.

stem_en: During the postpartum triage call, highlight the findings that form the urgent preeclampsia-with-severe-features cluster requiring immediate emergency evaluation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Teaching needs
options_or_rows_en.content: The patient asks when she can go home and whether this can happen again. Her mother feels guilty for telling her the symptoms were normal and asks what warning signs she should watch for after discharge.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and discharge status
options_or_rows_en.content: G1P1, delivered a healthy 3,400-g infant vaginally at 39 weeks 2 days. Prenatal BP values were below 130/80, urine protein screens were negative, and there is no history of chronic hypertension, renal disease, autoimmune disease, or gestational hypertension. Discharged postpartum day 1 with BP 118/74, expected lochia rubra, firm fundus, no edema, and instructions to call for headache, vision changes, or BP above 140/90 on the home cuff. Breastfeeding. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours as needed, docusate sodium.

Should not delay treatment

rationale_en: The repeated severe-range BP, CNS symptoms, and hyperreflexia with clonus are enough to classify the presentation as preeclampsia with severe features and begin urgent treatment. Proteinuria supports the diagnosis, but severe BP and CNS findings drive immediate action. Pending labs are drawn concurrently and should not delay treatment.

stem_en: The patient asks, 'Will the magnesium bring my blood pressure down?' Which response by the nurse is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and discharge status
options_or_rows_en.content: G1P1, delivered a healthy 3,400-g infant vaginally at 39 weeks 2 days. Prenatal BP values were below 130/80, urine protein screens were negative, and there is no history of chronic hypertension, renal disease, autoimmune disease, or gestational hypertension. Discharged postpartum day 1 with BP 118/74, expected lochia rubra, firm fundus, no edema, and instructions to call for headache, vision changes, or BP above 140/90 on the home cuff. Breastfeeding. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours as needed, docusate sodium.

options_or_rows_en.text: Magnesium sulfate helps prevent seizures; the immediate-release nifedipine is the medication ordered to lower the blood pressure quickly.
Because you are breastfeeding, both magnesium sulfate and nifedipine should be withheld until lactation consults.
The extended-release nifedipine capsule would work just as well for this acute severe BP reading.
Magnesium sulfate is the main antihypertensive, so nifedipine can wait until the loading dose is complete.

rationale_en: Magnesium sulfate is used for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia with severe features; it is not the acute antihypertensive. Immediate-release nifedipine is ordered for rapid BP reduction, while extended-release nifedipine is for maintenance control later.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and discharge status
options_or_rows_en.content: G1P1, delivered a healthy 3,400-g infant vaginally at 39 weeks 2 days. Prenatal BP values were below 130/80, urine protein screens were negative, and there is no history of chronic hypertension, renal disease, autoimmune disease, or gestational hypertension. Discharged postpartum day 1 with BP 118/74, expected lochia rubra, firm fundus, no edema, and instructions to call for headache, vision changes, or BP above 140/90 on the home cuff. Breastfeeding. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours as needed, docusate sodium.

options_or_rows_en.text: Implement seizure precautions, including padded side rails, suction at bedside, dim lighting, and minimized stimulation.
Monitor BP every 5 minutes for the first 20 minutes, then as ordered.
Run the magnesium by gravity tubing to save time if the pump is not immediately nearby.
Start the magnesium sulfate loading dose by infusion pump as ordered.
Wait until the magnesium loading dose is complete before giving nifedipine.
Administer the immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg PO as ordered.

rationale_en: These are concurrent priorities: start magnesium safely by pump for seizure prophylaxis, give immediate-release nifedipine for acute BP reduction, create a low-stimulation seizure-safe environment, and perform close serial BP monitoring. The nurse should not force a sequence that delays either medication or safety precautions.
