options_or_rows_en.title: Background and discharge status
options_or_rows_en.content: G1P1, delivered a healthy 3,400-g infant vaginally at 39 weeks 2 days. Prenatal BP values were below 130/80, urine protein screens were negative, and there is no history of chronic hypertension, renal disease, autoimmune disease, or gestational hypertension. Discharged postpartum day 1 with BP 118/74, expected lochia rubra, firm fundus, no edema, and instructions to call for headache, vision changes, or BP above 140/90 on the home cuff. Breastfeeding. Medications: prenatal vitamins, ibuprofen 600 mg every 6 hours as needed, docusate sodium.

options_or_rows_en.title: Delivery admission baseline labs
options_or_rows_en.content: Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL; hematocrit 35%; platelets 238,000/µL; AST 22 U/L; ALT 18 U/L; creatinine 0.7 mg/dL; uric acid 4.1 mg/dL; LDH 162 U/L; urinalysis negative for protein.

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage call findings
options_or_rows_en.content: At 10:15 AM, the patient reports severe frontal headache 8/10 unrelieved by ibuprofen, intermittent visual spots, new facial/periorbital puffiness, and home BP 162/108 twice, five minutes apart. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, epigastric/RUQ pain, nausea, vomiting, seizure activity, focal weakness, and confusion. Lochia is light serosa and she is voiding without difficulty.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED assessment at 11:10 AM
options_or_rows_en.content: Vitals: T 37.1 C, HR 96, BP 168/112, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air. Repeat BP five minutes later: 164/110. Headache 9/10 with photophobia. Alert and oriented x4. Pupils equal and reactive; cranial nerves grossly intact; grip strength equal. DTRs 3+ at biceps and patella bilaterally with two beats of ankle clonus. Lungs clear; heart regular. Fundus firm just above symphysis pubis. Lower extremities 1+ pitting edema. Clean-catch urine dipstick: 3+ protein. 18-gauge IV placed and Foley inserted with 150 mL clear yellow urine initially. Stat CBC, CMP, LDH, uric acid, coagulation studies, and urine protein-to-creatinine ratio sent.

options_or_rows_en.title: Provider orders and early response
options_or_rows_en.content: Orders: magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading dose over 20 minutes, then 2 g/hr continuous infusion for seizure prophylaxis; immediate-release nifedipine 10 mg PO now for acute BP reduction; repeat BP every 5 minutes for 20 minutes, then every 15 minutes; seizure precautions with padded side rails, suction at bedside, dimmed lighting, and minimized stimulation. Magnesium starts by pump at 11:40 AM; nifedipine 10 mg PO is given at 11:45 AM. BP after nifedipine: 158/104 at 11:50, 152/98 at 11:55, 148/96 at noon, 146/94 at 12:15. Urine output first hour: 60 mL. Headache improves to 6/10; occasional spots persist; DTRs remain 3+ with clonus.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stat lab results at 12:15 PM
options_or_rows_en.content: Hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL; hematocrit 36%; platelets 195,000/µL; AST 34 U/L; ALT 28 U/L; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; uric acid 6.8 mg/dL; LDH 198 U/L; INR 1.0; fibrinogen 380 mg/dL; urine protein-to-creatinine ratio 0.42.

options_or_rows_en.title: Breastfeeding question
options_or_rows_en.content: The patient asks whether she can continue breastfeeding while receiving nifedipine and magnesium. She can usually continue breastfeeding while receiving these medications, but lactation support may be needed because illness, hospitalization, and magnesium therapy can delay lactation or make feeding more difficult.

options_or_rows_en.title: Reassessment findings
options_or_rows_en.content: At 1:00 PM BP is 142/90. At 1:30 PM BP is 138/88; headache is 4/10; visual spots have resolved; facial edema is unchanged; DTRs are 2+ with no clonus; RR 16/min; patient is alert. Urine output second hour is 45 mL. Magnesium continues at 2 g/hr. Later BP: 140/86 at 2:00 PM and 136/84 at 2:30 PM. Provider plan: continue magnesium for 24 hours from the loading dose, start nifedipine ER 30 mg every 12 hours for maintenance BP control, and repeat HELLP panel in 6 hours.

options_or_rows_en.content: The patient asks when she can go home and whether this can happen again. Her mother feels guilty for telling her the symptoms were normal and asks what warning signs she should watch for after discharge.

rationale_en: BP below the severe range, improved headache/visual symptoms, normalized reflexes without clonus, and stable respirations/mentation indicate treatment response and no current magnesium toxicity. Urine output of 45 mL/hr is above the usual minimum for magnesium clearance but still requires close hourly monitoring. Facial edema often resolves over days and is not the acute marker of response.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are clinically significant cues requiring further evaluation for immune-related pneumonitis.

options_or_rows_en.title: Oncology history and current treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old woman with stage IV non-small cell lung cancer is receiving pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks as second-line therapy. She completed cycle 4 nine days ago. Prior carboplatin/pemetrexed was stopped for disease progression. History: remote 30-pack-year smoking history, well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, mild osteoarthritis treated with acetaminophen. No autoimmune disease, no chest radiation, and no known drug allergies. Baseline SpO2 before therapy was 96% on room air with no oxygen requirement.

options_or_rows_en.text: New dry cough began 4 days ago, 9 days after pembrolizumab cycle 4.
Temperature is 37.1 C.
SpO2 is 93% on room air, down from baseline 96%.
Faint bilateral inspiratory crackles are heard at the lung bases.
She pauses briefly to breathe after longer phrases.
No peripheral edema or JVD is present.
CRP is 18 mg/L with a reference value below 5 mg/L.
Creatinine, ALT, AST, and TSH are within the listed reference context.
Capillary refill is less than 2 seconds and oral mucosa is moist.

rationale_en: The new dry cough after recent pembrolizumab, falling SpO2 from baseline, bilateral crackles, exertional dyspnea, and elevated CRP form a lower-respiratory inflammatory cue cluster. Afebrile status and normal perfusion or unrelated labs do not make the respiratory change safe to dismiss.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are clinically significant cues requiring further evaluation for immune-related pneumonitis.

options_or_rows_en.title: Consult recommendations and teaching needs
options_or_rows_en.content: Pulmonology recommends a slow corticosteroid taper over several weeks per facility protocol and says pembrolizumab should remain discontinued unless oncology documents a risk-benefit decision. The oncologist confirms the client will not receive the next pembrolizumab cycle and will be re-evaluated for alternative cancer-treatment planning after recovery. The nurse is asked to teach the client to report worsening dyspnea, new or increasing cough, fever, chest pain, or recurrence of the symptoms that prompted admission during the taper. The husband asks whether she should also stop her blood pressure medication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Oncology history and current treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old woman with stage IV non-small cell lung cancer is receiving pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks as second-line therapy. She completed cycle 4 nine days ago. Prior carboplatin/pemetrexed was stopped for disease progression. History: remote 30-pack-year smoking history, well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, mild osteoarthritis treated with acetaminophen. No autoimmune disease, no chest radiation, and no known drug allergies. Baseline SpO2 before therapy was 96% on room air with no oxygen requirement.

rationale_en: The recent pembrolizumab exposure plus new cough, crackles, desaturation, dyspnea, and CRP elevation fit an immune-mediated lower-respiratory process. Seasonal allergies would more commonly produce sneezing, rhinorrhea, itchy or watery eyes, and upper-airway symptoms rather than crackles and falling oxygen saturation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply supplemental oxygen and titrate to the ordered SpO2 target.
Begin discharge teaching about future cancer-treatment options.
Prepare the next pembrolizumab infusion so treatment is not delayed.
Call pulmonology before starting oxygen or corticosteroids.

rationale_en: The active oxygenation deficit is the immediate physiologic threat. Applying oxygen directly addresses breathing while the nurse then proceeds with corticosteroids, monitoring, and consultation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Oncology history and current treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old woman with stage IV non-small cell lung cancer is receiving pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks as second-line therapy. She completed cycle 4 nine days ago. Prior carboplatin/pemetrexed was stopped for disease progression. History: remote 30-pack-year smoking history, well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, mild osteoarthritis treated with acetaminophen. No autoimmune disease, no chest radiation, and no known drug allergies. Baseline SpO2 before therapy was 96% on room air with no oxygen requirement.

options_or_rows_en.text: Promise that pembrolizumab will restart after the cough improves.
Tell her the oncology team will discuss the long-term treatment plan after she is stabilized.
Initiate methylprednisolone as ordered per the facility protocol.
Verify oxygen delivery is maintaining the ordered SpO2 target and arrange every-2-hour pulse oximetry.
Explain that pembrolizumab is being held now because of serious lung inflammation.
Independently tell her the drug is permanently discontinued and that all cancer treatment is over.

rationale_en: The nurse can give truthful, scope-appropriate information: the drug is held now, oncology will decide and explain the long-term plan, and urgent ordered treatment must continue. Promising restart or independently declaring all treatment over is outside nursing scope and inaccurate.

options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old woman with stage IV non-small cell lung cancer is receiving pembrolizumab 200 mg IV every 3 weeks as second-line therapy. options_or_rows_en.text: Establish every-2-hour pulse oximetry monitoring and document the baseline on-oxygen reading.
Notify or coordinate with pulmonology for co-management after bedside stabilization is underway.
Initiate methylprednisolone as ordered, verifying dose, route, and rate against the protocol order.
Apply supplemental oxygen and confirm SpO2 is moving toward the >=94% target.

rationale_en: This is a clinically real sequence: first correct the immediate oxygenation deficit, then begin the anti-inflammatory corticosteroid treatment, then set up surveillance to detect improvement or deterioration, and then coordinate the consult once urgent bedside actions are underway.

options_or_rows_en.text: SpO2 is 96% on 3 L nasal cannula after being 91% on room air.
PaO2 improved from 68 mmHg on room air to 82 mmHg on 3 L nasal cannula.
CRP decreased from 18 mg/L to 11 mg/L.
Heart rate and respiratory rate decreased to HR 86 and RR 18.
The client says she is not as short of breath.
Bilateral crackles are still present but similar to slightly less prominent.
Does not by itself indicate treatment failure
Supports improving trend

rationale_en: The client is improving because multiple trends are moving in the right direction: oxygenation, vital signs, inflammatory marker, and subjective dyspnea. Persistent crackles at 36 hours do not outweigh the broader improvement because inflammatory lung findings can resolve slowly.

stem_en: At Stage 3, classify each finding when evaluating the response to treatment.

options_or_rows_en.title: Consult recommendations and teaching needs
options_or_rows_en.content: Pulmonology recommends a slow corticosteroid taper over several weeks per facility protocol and says pembrolizumab should remain discontinued unless oncology documents a risk-benefit decision. The oncologist confirms the client will not receive the next pembrolizumab cycle and will be re-evaluated for alternative cancer-treatment planning after recovery. The nurse is asked to teach the client to report worsening dyspnea, new or increasing cough, fever, chest pain, or recurrence of the symptoms that prompted admission during the taper. The husband asks whether she should also stop her blood pressure medication.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 update, highlight the findings that are significant cues requiring immediate nursing action.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record before transfusion
options_or_rows_en.content:
Reason for admission: symptomatic anemia with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and pallor for 2 weeks.
Ordered therapy: two units packed red blood cells; initial transfusion rate 2 mL/min for the first 15 minutes per facility protocol.
Baseline vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 92/min, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on room air.
Assessment: alert and oriented, lungs clear, skin pale/warm/dry, abdomen soft and non-tender, no edema, IV site without redness/swelling/tenderness.
Urine output: about 40 mL/hr, straw-colored urine.
Admission labs: Hgb 6.4 g/dL, Hct 19.2%, WBC 7,800/uL, platelets 218,000/uL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, K 4.6 mEq/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, LDH 190 U/L, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, fibrinogen 310 mg/dL, haptoglobin 95 mg/dL. Urinalysis yellow/clear, no blood.

options_or_rows_en.text: Sudden deep bilateral flank and low back pain during the transfusion.
The client states, "something is wrong — I feel like something terrible is about to happen."
Visible rigors with temperature rising from 36.8 °C to 38.6 °C.
Heart rate increases from 92/min to 118/min.
Blood pressure decreases from 108/68 mmHg to 96/58 mmHg.
The most recent urine appears dark reddish-brown.
The peripheral IV site has no redness, swelling, or streaking.

rationale_en: The cue cluster is abrupt flank/back pain, a sense of impending doom, fever with rigors, tachycardia, falling blood pressure, and dark reddish-brown urine within minutes of starting blood. This pattern requires the nurse to treat the event as an acute transfusion reaction and stop the transfusion immediately. The normal IV site is useful because it makes infiltration or phlebitis less likely, but it is not itself a danger cue.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 update, highlight the findings that are significant cues requiring immediate nursing action.

options_or_rows_en.title: ICU course and confirmed ABO incompatibility
options_or_rows_en.content: The client has been transferred to the ICU after receiving 1,500 mL normal saline. A central venous catheter has been placed, and norepinephrine is infusing at 0.08 mcg/kg/min to maintain MAP above 65 mmHg per ICU protocol. The blood bank confirms the client is A-positive and the transfused unit was B-positive. Current vital signs: T 38.4 °C, HR 120/min, BP 88/56 mmHg on norepinephrine, RR 24/min on 6 L nasal cannula, SpO2 94%. Urine output over the past hour is 8 mL, dark amber and hemoglobin-positive by dipstick. Repeat labs: Hgb 5.2 g/dL, platelets 78,000/uL, K 5.9 mEq/L, creatinine 3.4 mg/dL, total bilirubin 6.1 mg/dL, LDH 2,600 U/L, INR 2.2, aPTT 68 sec, fibrinogen 88 mg/dL. The client is drowsy but arousable, oriented to person only. Peripheral pulses are thready; mottling extends to knees and elbows; oozing continues from catheter sites. ICU team is evaluating for emergent dialysis and blood component therapy.

options_or_rows_en.text: Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction
Mild allergic transfusion reaction
Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction
Transfusion-associated circulatory overload
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction

rationale_en: The most consistent diagnosis is an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. The decisive features are very rapid onset during transfusion, flank/back pain, hypotension, hemoglobinuria, fever with rigors, and a sense of impending doom. These findings fit acute intravascular hemolysis from incompatible red cells.

options_or_rows_en.text: The dark reddish-brown urine
The normal-appearing IV site
The fever and rigors
The client's nausea
The acute hypotension with tachycardia

rationale_en: The immediate threat is cardiovascular collapse: the blood pressure is falling while the heart rate is rising. Fever and hemoglobinuria are important, but inadequate perfusion can become fatal within minutes and must drive the response to stop the antigen exposure and support circulation.

stem_en: At Stage 1, which actions should the nurse take immediately? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record before transfusion
options_or_rows_en.content:
Reason for admission: symptomatic anemia with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and pallor for 2 weeks.

Assessment: alert and oriented, lungs clear, skin pale/warm/dry, abdomen soft and non-tender, no edema, IV site without redness/swelling/tenderness.
Urine output: about 40 mL/hr, straw-colored urine.
Admission labs: Hgb 6.4 g/dL, Hct 19.2%, WBC 7,800/uL, platelets 218,000/uL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, K 4.6 mEq/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, LDH 190 U/L, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, fibrinogen 310 mg/dL, haptoglobin 95 mg/dL. Urinalysis yellow/clear, no blood.

options_or_rows_en.text: Remove the peripheral IV catheter because the line has been exposed to incompatible blood.
Disconnect the blood tubing and keep the IV open with 0.9% normal saline through new tubing per protocol.
Clamp the blood tubing and stop the transfusion.
Save the blood unit with attached tubing and labels for the blood bank investigation.
Flush the blood remaining in the original tubing before switching to saline.
Stay with the client and have another staff member notify the provider and blood bank.

rationale_en: The nurse should stop the transfusion, maintain venous access with 0.9% normal saline using new tubing according to protocol, notify the provider and blood bank while staying with the client, and preserve the unit/tubing/labels. The nurse should not flush the original tubing because that would infuse more incompatible blood, and should not remove the IV because the client is hypotensive and needs access.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record before transfusion
options_or_rows_en.content:

Admission labs: Hgb 6.4 g/dL, Hct 19.2%, WBC 7,800/uL, platelets 218,000/uL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, K 4.6 mEq/L, total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, LDH 190 U/L, INR 1.0, aPTT 28 sec, fibrinogen 310 mg/dL, haptoglobin 95 mg/dL. Urinalysis yellow/clear, no blood.

options_or_rows_en.text: Hold further interventions until the blood bank confirms the exact incompatibility.
Escalate immediately for rapid response or ICU-level care while continuing perfusion support and strict output monitoring.
Administer acetaminophen and reassess the temperature in 30 minutes.
Slow the saline infusion because chronic kidney disease makes fluid overload the main priority.
Complete the transfusion reaction documentation before further escalation.

rationale_en: The client is deteriorating with shock, oliguric acute kidney injury, and DIC despite the initial correct response. The nurse should escalate immediately to rapid response or ICU-level care, continue ordered perfusion support, measure urine output closely, and ensure the blood bank workup and compatible components are moving. Documentation and antipyretics must not delay escalation.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 home-visit data, highlight the findings that are significant cues requiring nursing attention.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial home-health visit record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: Margaret has a 3-year history of Alzheimer's dementia and is followed by a memory-care clinic. Medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily, osteoarthritis of both knees, and remote left hip fracture repair 5 years ago. She has not been seen by the memory-care clinic in 9 months because Diane could not get time off work.

Initial presentation at 10:00 AM Tuesday: Diane appears fatigued with dark circles, took a half-day off work, and must leave by noon. A neighbor will stay with Margaret from noon until Diane returns from work, but this is an informal favor. Diane reports Margaret was up 4 times last night and tried to go outside at 2 AM. Diane has been placing a chair against Margaret's bedroom door at night because she cannot stay awake. Diane has slept less than 4 consecutive hours for weeks, lost 12 lb in 2 months, called in sick to work 3 times last month, and says she promised her father she would never place Margaret in a home.

Margaret is seated in a recliner wearing a urine-stained housecoat with a faint urine odor. A half-eaten bowl of oatmeal is on the side table. Diane reports Margaret has refused more meals and lost about 8 lb over 3 months. Pill bottles are on the kitchen counter with tops off two bottles. A Wednesday-slot pill organizer is open, but today is Tuesday.

options_or_rows_en.text: Margaret is oriented only to her first name.
Her housecoat is urine-stained and has a faint urine odor.
Perineal skin is mildly erythematous from urine contact.
Weight has decreased from 61.6 kg nine months ago to 58 kg today.
Albumin is 3.1 g/dL and prealbumin is 14 mg/dL.
Oral mucosa is slightly dry.
Blood pressure is 148/88 while medication administration is unreliable.
Gait is slow and wide-based with a slight forward lean.
Skin is intact with no bruising, lacerations, or pressure areas.
Grip strength is symmetric and adequate.
Temperature is 36.8 °C and SpO₂ is 97% on room air.
Diane reports sleeping less than four consecutive hours for weeks.
Diane has lost twelve pounds in two months because she forgets to eat.
Diane's hands tremble when she opens the pill bottles.
Diane called in sick to work three times last month.
Diane becomes tearful and says she promised not to place Margaret in a home.
A chair is propped under the bedroom doorknob at night.
Pill bottles are open and the wrong-day pill organizer is in use.
The front door has a deadbolt Margaret can unlock.
Stove knobs are uncovered and enabled.
Throw rugs are present in the kitchen and hallway.
Cleaning supplies are unlocked under the kitchen sink.
Grab bars and night-lights are already present.

rationale_en: The significant cue cluster includes Margaret's cognitive impairment, nutritional decline, possible dehydration, incontinence-related skin risk, elevated BP with unreliable medication administration, gait/fall risk, and multiple environmental hazards. Diane's sleep deprivation, weight loss, tremor, missed work, guilt, unsafe door barricade, and medication disorganization show caregiver role strain that directly affects Margaret's safety. Normal temperature, oxygenation, symmetric grip, intact skin, grab bars, and night-lights do not remove the need for follow-up.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 home-visit data, highlight the findings that are significant cues requiring nursing attention.

options_or_rows_en.title: Follow-up outcomes
options_or_rows_en.content:
Since the first visit, the nurse coordinated a social-work referral, gave Diane Area Agency on Aging and caregiver support group contact information, and contacted the primary care provider about diphenhydramine use, the unreported fall, medication difficulties, weight loss, and BP readings. The provider discontinued diphenhydramine, ordered a pharmacy consultation for alternative formulations, referred Margaret to physical therapy for fall-risk assessment, and referred her to a dietitian.

Diane called the Area Agency on Aging and learned about an adult day program 3 miles away that operates Monday-Friday, 8 AM-4 PM. She is thinking about enrollment. She attended one online caregiver support group and felt less alone. She removed the chair from the bedroom door and installed a front-door alarm that chimes when the door opens. She has not addressed the stove, throw rugs, or unlocked cleaning supplies. Margaret's medications have been given daily with a phone alarm. The pharmacy delivered amlodipine oral liquid, which Margaret accepts in juice. Lisinopril is still crushed in applesauce after pharmacist confirmation that the tablet is crushable.

Margaret appears slightly more alert. Diane reports Margaret slept through the night twice in 5 days since diphenhydramine stopped, with less morning confusion on those days. Urinary incontinence continues 2-3 times daily. Meal intake over the past 2 days is about 50%, improved from the prior 25%-30% estimate. Weight is 57.6 kg, down 0.4 kg from 5 days ago; this trend is too short to interpret definitively. Vitals: T 36.9 °C, HR 74, BP 138/82, RR 16, SpO₂ 98%. Perineal erythema is unchanged. No new falls. Margaret tried to open the front door once at night; the alarm woke Diane, who redirected her without incident. Diane reports some sleep improvement, fewer headaches, and a primary-care appointment next week, but still has no formal respite in place.

Her housecoat is urine-stained and has a faint urine odor.
Perineal skin is mildly erythematous from urine contact.
Weight has decreased from 61.6 kg nine months ago to 58 kg today.
Albumin is 3.1 g/dL and prealbumin is 14 mg/dL.
Oral mucosa is slightly dry.
Blood pressure is 148/88 while medication administration is unreliable.
Gait is slow and wide-based with a slight forward lean.
Skin is intact with no bruising, lacerations, or pressure areas.
Grip strength is symmetric and adequate.
Temperature is 36.8 °C and SpO₂ is 97% on room air.
Diane reports sleeping less than four consecutive hours for weeks.
Diane has lost twelve pounds in two months because she forgets to eat.
Diane's hands tremble when she opens the pill bottles.
Diane called in sick to work three times last month.
Diane becomes tearful and says she promised not to place Margaret in a home.
A chair is propped under the bedroom doorknob at night.
Pill bottles are open and the wrong-day pill organizer is in use.
The front door has a deadbolt Margaret can unlock.
Stove knobs are uncovered and enabled.
Throw rugs are present in the kitchen and hallway.
Cleaning supplies are unlocked under the kitchen sink.
Grab bars and night-lights are already present.

rationale_en: The significant cue cluster includes Margaret's cognitive impairment, nutritional decline, possible dehydration, incontinence-related skin risk, elevated BP with unreliable medication administration, gait/fall risk, and multiple environmental hazards. Diane's sleep deprivation, weight loss, tremor, missed work, guilt, unsafe door barricade, and medication disorganization show caregiver role strain that directly affects Margaret's safety. Normal temperature, oxygenation, symmetric grip, intact skin, grab bars, and night-lights do not remove the need for follow-up.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which interpretation of the findings and nursing response is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial home-health visit record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: Margaret has a 3-year history of Alzheimer's dementia and is followed by a memory-care clinic. Medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily, osteoarthritis of both knees, and remote left hip fracture repair 5 years ago. She has not been seen by the memory-care clinic in 9 months because Diane could not get time off work.

Initial presentation at 10:00 AM Tuesday: Diane appears fatigued with dark circles, took a half-day off work, and must leave by noon. A neighbor will stay with Margaret from noon until Diane returns from work, but this is an informal favor. Diane reports Margaret was up 4 times last night and tried to go outside at 2 AM. Diane has been placing a chair against Margaret's bedroom door at night because she cannot stay awake. Diane has slept less than 4 consecutive hours for weeks, lost 12 lb in 2 months, called in sick to work 3 times last month, and says she promised her father she would never place Margaret in a home.

Margaret is seated in a recliner wearing a urine-stained housecoat with a faint urine odor. A half-eaten bowl of oatmeal is on the side table. Diane reports Margaret has refused more meals and lost about 8 lb over 3 months. Pill bottles are on the kitchen counter with tops off two bottles. A Wednesday-slot pill organizer is open, but today is Tuesday.

options_or_rows_en.text: The home situation is unsalvageable because Diane missed a clinic appointment, so the nurse should recommend immediate removal from the home as the first response.
The problem is only medication nonadherence; the nurse should focus on the pill organizer and avoid discussing caregiver stress or home hazards.
Because Margaret has no bruises or lacerations, the nurse should reassure Diane that no safety documentation or follow-up is needed.
The chair barricade and raised voice prove intentional physical abuse, so the nurse should accuse Diane and demand immediate facility placement before completing the assessment.

The pattern best supports caregiver role strain with unsafe compensatory strategies; respond nonjudgmentally, correct immediate hazards, document, notify the provider/supervisor or social work, and follow agency/state APS policy if neglect is suspected or safety cannot be maintained.

rationale_en: Diane's behavior is unsafe but the overall pattern is most consistent with caregiver role strain: she is exhausted, guilty, tearful, receptive to help, and voluntarily requested support. The nurse should not ignore the hazards or excuse unsafe care; the correct response is nonjudgmental support plus documentation, team notification, safety planning, and policy-guided APS reporting assessment if safety cannot be maintained or neglect is suspected.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which interpretation of the findings and nursing response is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Follow-up outcomes
options_or_rows_en.content:
Since the first visit, the nurse coordinated a social-work referral, gave Diane Area Agency on Aging and caregiver support group contact information, and contacted the primary care provider about diphenhydramine use, the unreported fall, medication difficulties, weight loss, and BP readings. The provider discontinued diphenhydramine, ordered a pharmacy consultation for alternative formulations, referred Margaret to physical therapy for fall-risk assessment, and referred her to a dietitian.

Diane called the Area Agency on Aging and learned about an adult day program 3 miles away that operates Monday-Friday, 8 AM-4 PM. She is thinking about enrollment. She attended one online caregiver support group and felt less alone. She removed the chair from the bedroom door and installed a front-door alarm that chimes when the door opens. She has not addressed the stove, throw rugs, or unlocked cleaning supplies. Margaret's medications have been given daily with a phone alarm. The pharmacy delivered amlodipine oral liquid, which Margaret accepts in juice. Lisinopril is still crushed in applesauce after pharmacist confirmation that the tablet is crushable.

Margaret appears slightly more alert. Diane reports Margaret slept through the night twice in 5 days since diphenhydramine stopped, with less morning confusion on those days. Urinary incontinence continues 2-3 times daily. Meal intake over the past 2 days is about 50%, improved from the prior 25%-30% estimate. Weight is 57.6 kg, down 0.4 kg from 5 days ago; this trend is too short to interpret definitively. Vitals: T 36.9 °C, HR 74, BP 138/82, RR 16, SpO₂ 98%. Perineal erythema is unchanged. No new falls. Margaret tried to open the front door once at night; the alarm woke Diane, who redirected her without incident. Diane reports some sleep improvement, fewer headaches, and a primary-care appointment next week, but still has no formal respite in place.

options_or_rows_en.text: Defer environmental changes until the follow-up visit because Margaret is currently calm.
Focus first on rechecking the blood pressure and teaching Diane to restart the Wednesday pill compartment tomorrow.

Before Diane leaves today, ensure Margaret is not left in an unsafe environment: remove the bedroom-door barricade, use a nonrestrictive door-alert strategy, prevent unsupervised stove access, orient the neighbor to safety monitoring and emergency contacts, and notify the provider about diphenhydramine use.
Schedule a caregiver support group before addressing the stove, door, or neighbor supervision plan.
Prioritize a dietary consult and weekly weights because the albumin and prealbumin are low.

Nutrition, BP, and caregiver support are important but not the most immediate life-safety threats before Diane leaves.

stem_en: Which interventions should the nurse include in the Stage 2 home-safety and caregiver-support plan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial home-health visit record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: Margaret has a 3-year history of Alzheimer's dementia and is followed by a memory-care clinic. Medical history includes hypertension treated with amlodipine 5 mg daily and lisinopril 10 mg daily, osteoarthritis of both knees, and remote left hip fracture repair 5 years ago. She has not been seen by the memory-care clinic in 9 months because Diane could not get time off work.

Initial presentation at 10:00 AM Tuesday: Diane appears fatigued with dark circles, took a half-day off work, and must leave by noon. A neighbor will stay with Margaret from noon until Diane returns from work, but this is an informal favor. Diane reports Margaret was up 4 times last night and tried to go outside at 2 AM. Diane has been placing a chair against Margaret's bedroom door at night because she cannot stay awake. Diane has slept less than 4 consecutive hours for weeks, lost 12 lb in 2 months, called in sick to work 3 times last month, and says she promised her father she would never place Margaret in a home.

Margaret is seated in a recliner wearing a urine-stained housecoat with a faint urine odor. A half-eaten bowl of oatmeal is on the side table. Diane reports Margaret has refused more meals and lost about 8 lb over 3 months. Pill bottles are on the kitchen counter with tops off two bottles. A Wednesday-slot pill organizer is open, but today is Tuesday.

options_or_rows_en.text: Contact the provider and pharmacist about medication formulations and diphenhydramine use; set up alarms, a written schedule, and a clarified pill-organizer routine.
Remove the bedroom-door barricade and use door alarms or contact sensors on exterior doors; consider medical identification and a local wandering-response program.
Continue diphenhydramine at a lower dose and keep the chair barricade until Margaret stops wandering.
Remove throw rugs, lock cleaning supplies, maintain lighting, and coordinate physical therapy for gait and fall-risk assessment.
Prevent unsupervised stove use by using knob covers, removing knobs, or disconnecting the stove when not supervised.

Address nutrition, incontinence, and caregiver support with dietitian referral, preferred small frequent meals, prompted toileting, moisture barrier, social work, Area Agency on Aging, adult day program, caregiver support group, and Diane's own primary-care visit.

rationale_en: The plan must address both sides of the dyad: Margaret's safety risks and Diane's ability to continue caregiving. Appropriate interventions include nonrestrictive wandering alerts, stove control, fall and hazardous-material prevention, medication-formulation and reminder support, provider notification about diphenhydramine, nutrition and incontinence support, respite/adult day resources, social work, caregiver support, and Diane's own health care.

stem_en: During Stage 1, after Margaret is safely away from the stove and Diane says, 'I can't do this anymore. I'm failing her,' which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: Margaret has a 3-year history of Alzheimer's dementia and is followed by a memory-care clinic. Initial presentation at 10:00 AM Tuesday: Diane appears fatigued with dark circles, took a half-day off work, and must leave by noon. Diane has been placing a chair against Margaret's bedroom door at night because she cannot stay awake. Diane has slept less than 4 consecutive hours for weeks, lost 12 lb in 2 months, called in sick to work 3 times last month, and says she promised her father she would never place Margaret in a home.

Margaret is seated in a recliner wearing a urine-stained housecoat with a faint urine odor. A half-eaten bowl of oatmeal is on the side table. Diane reports Margaret has refused more meals and lost about 8 lb over 3 months. Pill bottles are on the kitchen counter with tops off two bottles. A Wednesday-slot pill organizer is open, but today is Tuesday.

options_or_rows_en.text: Immediately list every community resource and safety modification without first responding to Diane's statement that she is failing.

After confirming Margaret is away from the stove, say: 'This is incredibly hard, and I can see how exhausted you are. Asking for help does not mean you are failing her. Let's take this one step at a time together.'
Tell Diane to leave the room so the nurse can correct Margaret's behavior without family interference.
Say: 'You are doing a great job, and everything will get easier once she settles down.'
Say: 'You cannot raise your voice at a person with dementia; if you keep doing that, I will have to report you.'

rationale_en: The nurse first ensures immediate safety, then validates Diane's exhaustion and guilt without judgment. This builds trust and opens the door to later education and safety planning. Lecturing, threatening, false reassurance, premature resource-listing, or excluding Diane bypasses the emotional cue and can reduce engagement.

stem_en: Stage 1: Highlight the findings that establish that Mrs. Ellison's refusal is the governing decision.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Patient: Margaret Ellison, 82 years old.
Admitted from home with NYHA class IV heart-failure exacerbation, worsening dyspnea, edema, and poor oral intake for 5 days.
History: ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 15%, CKD stage 4 with baseline creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, progressive dysphagia followed by speech-language pathology for 6 months. Three hospitalizations in the past 4 months for fluid overload. Outpatient palliative-care referral was recently canceled by family.
Advance directive: son David is named durable power of attorney for health care, to be activated only if Mrs. Ellison loses decisional capacity.
Current orders: IV furosemide 40 mg every 12 hr, fluid restriction 1,200 mL/day, low-sodium cardiac diet with pureed foods and nectar-thick liquids per speech-language pathology, daily weights, telemetry.
Initial statement to admitting nurse: "I don't want any more tubes or machines. I've had a good life. I want to be comfortable."
Initial assessment: alert, conversational, oriented to person/place/time/situation. Mini-Cog 4/5. Mood calm and reflective; denies depression or hopelessness and states she feels "at peace." Vitals: T 36.8 C, HR 92 irregular, BP 104/62, RR 22, SpO2 93% on 2 L nasal cannula. Weight 68.4 kg; documented dry weight 64.2 kg. Bilateral lower-lung crackles, JVD at 45 degrees, 3+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-shin, temporal wasting. Oral mucosa moist. She can manage small sips of nectar-thick water but needs prolonged time to initiate swallowing.
Admission labs: BNP 2,840 pg/mL, Na 131 mEq/L, K 5.1 mEq/L, BUN 48 mg/dL, creatinine 3.2 mg/dL, eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73 m2, glucose 142 mg/dL, albumin 2.6 g/dL, Hgb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 6,200/uL, platelets 188,000/uL, INR 1.1, prealbumin 11 mg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Chart review findings:
The physician documents that Mrs. Ellison understands the risks and benefits of PEG placement, including continued weight loss without it.
She asks clarifying questions about whether the tube will cure her heart failure and whether she may still eat by mouth.
She states, "I understand what you're telling me. I don't want a feeding tube. I want to eat what I can and be comfortable."
The advance directive states that the son's DPOAHC authority activates only if Mrs. Ellison loses decisional capacity.
Mini-Cog score is 4/5 with a normal clock draw.
David says he is the power of attorney and wants the tube placed.
Albumin is 2.6 g/dL and prealbumin is 11 mg/dL.
Speech-language pathology has followed progressive dysphagia for 6 months.

rationale_en: The governing cues are decision-specific capacity documentation, Mrs. Ellison's demonstrated understanding and clarifying questions, her clear values-based refusal, and the advance directive clause showing that the son's surrogate authority is dormant while she has capacity.

stem_en: Stage 1: Highlight the findings that establish that Mrs. Ellison's refusal is the governing decision.

options_or_rows_en.title: Advocacy interventions and palliative-care assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
The nurse has documented Mrs. Ellison's repeated, consistent refusal with direct quotes and capacity observations; informed the charge nurse of the ethical concern; requested palliative care; and arranged a family meeting for the next morning with the attending physician, palliative care team, social worker, and both adult children. David states he has contacted a lawyer and "will get a court order" to have the PEG placed.
Mrs. Ellison is resting and tells the nurse she is glad someone is listening to her. She asks about hospice options at home. Vitals: HR 86, BP 102/58, RR 18, SpO2 95% on 2 L. Weight 66.8 kg. She tolerated small amounts of pureed lunch. Telemetry unchanged.
Palliative-care nurse practitioner note: "Mrs. Ellison demonstrates intact decisional capacity, consistent values-based reasoning, and an informed, voluntary refusal of PEG tube placement. Recommend goals-of-care meeting with family, comfort-focused plan of care, and hospice referral per patient's expressed wishes."

stem_en: Stage 1: David insists that his DPOAHC status allows him to override his mother's refusal and request PEG placement. Whose decision governs at this time?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Patient: Margaret Ellison, 82 years old.
Admitted from home with NYHA class IV heart-failure exacerbation, worsening dyspnea, edema, and poor oral intake for 5 days.
History: ischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 15%, CKD stage 4 with baseline creatinine 2.8 mg/dL, type 2 diabetes on basal insulin, progressive dysphagia followed by speech-language pathology for 6 months. Three hospitalizations in the past 4 months for fluid overload. Outpatient palliative-care referral was recently canceled by family.
Advance directive: son David is named durable power of attorney for health care, to be activated only if Mrs. Ellison loses decisional capacity.
Current orders: IV furosemide 40 mg every 12 hr, fluid restriction 1,200 mL/day, low-sodium cardiac diet with pureed foods and nectar-thick liquids per speech-language pathology, daily weights, telemetry.
Initial statement to admitting nurse: "I don't want any more tubes or machines. I've had a good life. I want to be comfortable."
Initial assessment: alert, conversational, oriented to person/place/time/situation. Mini-Cog 4/5. Mood calm and reflective; denies depression or hopelessness and states she feels "at peace." Vitals: T 36.8 C, HR 92 irregular, BP 104/62, RR 22, SpO2 93% on 2 L nasal cannula. Weight 68.4 kg; documented dry weight 64.2 kg. Bilateral lower-lung crackles, JVD at 45 degrees, 3+ bilateral pitting edema to mid-shin, temporal wasting. Oral mucosa moist. She can manage small sips of nectar-thick water but needs prolonged time to initiate swallowing.
Admission labs: BNP 2,840 pg/mL, Na 131 mEq/L, K 5.1 mEq/L, BUN 48 mg/dL, creatinine 3.2 mg/dL, eGFR 14 mL/min/1.73 m2, glucose 142 mg/dL, albumin 2.6 g/dL, Hgb 9.8 g/dL, WBC 6,200/uL, platelets 188,000/uL, INR 1.1, prealbumin 11 mg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: The decision should be delayed until Mrs. Ellison's daughter can be physically present.
Mrs. Ellison's refusal governs because her decision-making capacity is intact and documented.
David's decision governs because a DPOAHC always has priority over the patient's current choice.
The physician may proceed because PEG placement is medically recommended for poor intake and dysphagia.
The children should decide together because both adult children agree with PEG placement.

rationale_en: A capacitated adult holds the authority to accept or refuse treatment. The DPOAHC is meaningful but dormant because the activation condition—loss of decisional capacity—has not occurred.

options_or_rows_en.text: Advocate for Mrs. Ellison's informed, voluntary refusal so it is not overridden by family pressure or provider ambivalence.
Prioritize correcting malnutrition because albumin and prealbumin are low.
Schedule the consult because allowing GI to speak with her is a neutral way to keep the family calm.
Tell the family the nurse cannot discuss the situation and avoid further contact with them.
Stay neutral and let the physician and family resolve the disagreement without nursing involvement.

rationale_en: The immediate ethical risk is that the patient's autonomous refusal may be undermined. Nursing advocacy means protecting the patient's voice, while still addressing family distress through appropriate interdisciplinary support.

options_or_rows_en.text: Communicate directly with the physician that scheduling a PEG consult over the documented refusal raises an ethical concern.
Confirm with Mrs. Ellison that her decision remains informed and voluntary and assess whether pressure has affected her choice.
Encourage Mrs. Ellison to meet with GI and reconsider PEG placement for her family's sake.
Document her repeated refusal with direct quotes and nursing observations of alertness, orientation, calm affect, and values-based reasoning.

rationale_en: Appropriate advocacy is coordinated: reassess voluntariness, document clearly, address the physician's plan directly, and bring in palliative care, social work, and a family meeting to support goals-of-care clarification without pressuring the patient.

options_or_rows_en.text: Finish documenting the refusal first and wait to speak with the physician after the family meeting is arranged.
Ask David to bring the DPOAHC paperwork to the unit before taking further action.
Respectfully tell the physician without delay that Mrs. Ellison again declined PEG placement, capacity is documented, and scheduling the consult over refusal raises an ethical concern.
Call the ethics committee first before addressing the concern with the physician.
Ask Mrs. Ellison whether she will agree to a one-time GI consult so the family can feel reassured.

rationale_en: The physician's proposed consult is the proximate threat to autonomy. The nurse should use clear structured communication to stop the consult from being placed over the patient's refusal and recommend palliative care and a family meeting instead.

stem_en: Stage 2 data are now available. For each finding, classify whether it is an urgent new cue, a previously identified expected clozapine effect, or a nonemergent metabolic monitoring issue.

options_or_rows_en.title: Day 1 baseline record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: schizophrenia for 7 years; persistent auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions, and functional decline despite adequate trials of risperidone and olanzapine. No seizure disorder, cardiac disease, or hematologic abnormality. Medical history includes obesity (BMI 34), prediabetes (HbA1c 6.1%), and chronic constipation managed with polyethylene glycol. Current smoker, 1/2 pack per day.

Medication start: clozapine 12.5 mg PO once daily on Day 1, with slow titration. Facility policy: hold clozapine and notify the provider for fever ≥38.5 °C, ANC in the moderate/severe neutropenia range, or suspected myocarditis.

Baseline assessment: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 132/84 sitting and 126/80 standing, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 104 kg. Cardiac rhythm regular; lungs clear; abdomen soft and mildly distended with hypoactive bowel sounds. No chest pain, shortness of breath, sore throat, or fever. AIMS score 0.

Baseline labs: WBC 7,200/µL, ANC 4,100/µL, Hgb 14.8 g/dL, platelets 238,000/µL. Fasting glucose 112 mg/dL, total cholesterol 218 mg/dL, LDL 142 mg/dL, triglycerides 190 mg/dL. Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL, CRP 2.1 mg/L. ECG: normal sinus rhythm, QTc 412 ms.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature 38.7 °C with sore throat
Resting HR 118 with new chest pressure
New S3 gallop, bibasilar crackles, and ankle edema
Nighttime drooling noted at Day 10
Orthostatic lightheadedness documented at Day 10
Fasting glucose 124 mg/dL during titration
Expected/previously managed effect
Metabolic monitoring issue
Urgent new cue

rationale_en: Fever with sore throat is a sentinel cue for clozapine-related neutropenia and infection risk. New chest pressure, marked resting tachycardia, dyspnea, S3, crackles, and edema are urgent cardiac cues for myocarditis/heart failure. Drooling and orthostatic symptoms were already identified on Day 10 as expected clozapine adverse effects. Mild hyperglycemia is important for metabolic monitoring but is not the acute emergency.

stem_en: Stage 2 data are now available. For each finding, classify whether it is an urgent new cue, a previously identified expected clozapine effect, or a nonemergent metabolic monitoring issue.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response to initial management
options_or_rows_en.content:
Clozapine has been held, and the provider has been notified. The client is on continuous cardiac monitoring and oxygen at 2 L/min by nasal cannula. Blood cultures were drawn, and empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics were started per provider order/facility protocol.

Repeat vital signs: T 38.4 °C, HR 112, BP 106/68, RR 20, SpO2 97% on 2 L oxygen. Chest pressure is about the same. The client is fatigued but alert and oriented.

Echocardiogram: LVEF 45% with mild global hypokinesis and a small pericardial effusion, consistent with acute myocarditis. Cardiology recommends supportive care, serial troponins every 6 hours, and transfer to telemetry if hemodynamic status worsens.

Repeat labs: ANC 940/µL, troponin I 0.52 ng/mL, CRP 72 mg/L. Clozapine remains held. Neutropenic precautions are implemented per facility protocol: private room, visitor illness screening, reinforced hand hygiene, no fresh flowers or raw fruits/vegetables, and instructions to report fever, chills, or other infection symptoms immediately.

Medication start: clozapine 12.5 mg PO once daily on Day 1, with slow titration. Facility policy: hold clozapine and notify the provider for fever ≥38.5 °C, ANC in the moderate/severe neutropenia range, or suspected myocarditis.

Baseline assessment: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 132/84 sitting and 126/80 standing, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 104 kg. Cardiac rhythm regular; lungs clear; abdomen soft and mildly distended with hypoactive bowel sounds. No chest pain, shortness of breath, sore throat, or fever. AIMS score 0.

Baseline labs: WBC 7,200/µL, ANC 4,100/µL, Hgb 14.8 g/dL, platelets 238,000/µL. Fasting glucose 112 mg/dL, total cholesterol 218 mg/dL, LDL 142 mg/dL, triglycerides 190 mg/dL. Troponin I <0.01 ng/mL, CRP 2.1 mg/L. ECG: normal sinus rhythm, QTc 412 ms.

options_or_rows_en.text: The ANC decline is best explained by myocarditis alone, so neutropenia-specific precautions can wait.
All findings should be treated as anxiety or somatic delusions because the client is on an inpatient psychiatric unit.
Troponin 0.48 ng/mL, CRP 68 mg/L, BNP 420 pg/mL, tachycardia, S3, crackles, and edema support suspected clozapine-associated myocarditis.
The improving hallucinations make clozapine toxicity unlikely.
The abnormal cardiac biomarkers and heart-failure signs are not explained by the falling ANC alone.
The ANC of 980/µL with fever and sore throat supports moderate neutropenia with high infection risk.

rationale_en: The data support two concurrent clozapine emergencies: febrile moderate neutropenia and suspected myocarditis. The falling ANC with fever and sore throat creates high infection risk. The troponin/CRP/BNP elevations with tachycardia, S3, crackles, edema, and chest pressure point to myocardial inflammation and early heart-failure physiology. These processes overlap clinically but require distinct management pathways.

options_or_rows_en.text: Priority: improved hallucinations; action: administer the next clozapine dose to preserve psychiatric gains.
Priority: constipation and drooling; action: request an anticholinergic medication before addressing the fever.
Priority: anxiety-related chest discomfort; action: provide reassurance and reassess after the next group session.
Priority: concurrent life-threatening clozapine toxicities; action: hold clozapine immediately per policy and notify the provider.
Priority: metabolic syndrome risk; action: focus first on diet teaching for rising glucose and triglycerides.

rationale_en: The immediate priority is the combined acute toxicity: febrile moderate neutropenia and suspected myocarditis. Holding clozapine stops ongoing exposure to the causative medication and is authorized by the facility policy. Provider notification is essential because blood cultures, empiric antibiotics, echocardiography, cardiology consultation, and higher-level monitoring require orders or coordinated escalation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Implement neutropenic precautions according to facility protocol and reinforce hand hygiene/visitor screening.
Delay antibiotics until the ANC has recovered to at least 1,500/µL to avoid masking the trend.
Draw blood cultures as ordered before starting prescribed empiric antibiotics.
Administer the next clozapine dose with acetaminophen so the fever decreases while psychiatric symptoms stay controlled.

rationale_en: This client needs simultaneous febrile-neutropenia and myocarditis management. Neutropenic precautions reduce exposure to new pathogens. Cultures should be obtained before prescribed empiric antibiotics whenever this can be done without delaying therapy. Continuous cardiac monitoring, oxygen, hemodynamic monitoring, serial biomarkers, ECG/echo assessment, and cardiology involvement track progression and identify deterioration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Worsening hypotension, increasing tachycardia, new arrhythmias, decreasing urine output, worsening dyspnea, or new infection signs require escalation.
Clozapine can be restarted now because the hallucinations had improved before the adverse reaction.
Falling troponin/CRP values, symptom improvement, and improved LVEF on repeat echocardiography would support myocarditis recovery.
A rising ANC trend over the next days would support hematologic recovery.
The mild improvement in HR and oxygen saturation proves the toxicities have resolved, so monitoring can be relaxed.
The client is hemodynamically stable but not yet improving because troponin and CRP are still rising and ANC remains low.

rationale_en: Four hours after holding clozapine is too early to declare resolution. HR and oxygenation are slightly better with rest and oxygen, but ANC remains in the moderate neutropenia range and troponin/CRP are still rising. Recovery should be evaluated with serial ANC, troponin, CRP, symptoms, and cardiac function. Any hemodynamic, rhythm, respiratory, renal-perfusion, or infection deterioration requires escalation.

options_or_rows_en.content: A 72-year-old client with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction arrives with 2 days of worsening dyspnea and orthopnea. The client missed furosemide for 3 days after running out of tablets. Vital signs: T 37.1° C, HR 112/min, RR 26/min, BP 168/94 mm Hg, SpO2 89% on room air. Lung sounds: bibasilar crackles. 2+ bilateral ankle edema. Reports needing 4 pillows to sleep.

options.text: acute pulmonary edema with respiratory failure
ankle edema without a respiratory change
a normal potassium and a non-elevated troponin
pink frothy sputum, worsening hypoxemia, and crackles extending upward

stem_en: Calculate the mean arterial pressure (MAP) for BP 78/40 mm Hg using MAP = (SBP + 2DBP) / 3. Round to the nearest whole number.

options_or_rows_en.title: Urgent care presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: A 59-year-old client with autoimmune adrenal insufficiency arrives with vomiting, weakness, abdominal pain, and dizziness after a febrile gastroenteritis illness. The client states, 'I could not keep my hydrocortisone pills down for two days.' Vital signs: T 38.4° C, HR 122/min, RR 24/min, BP 84/46 mm Hg. Skin is cool and mottled.

rationale_en: MAP = (78 + 2[40]) / 3 = 158 / 3 = 52.7, rounded to 53 mm Hg. This is below the usual perfusion target and supports shock-level urgency.

rationale_en: MAP = (78 + 2[40]) / 3 = 158 / 3 = 52.7, rounded to 53 mm Hg. This is below the usual perfusion target and supports shock-level urgency.

The client states, 'I could not keep my hydrocortisone pills down for two days.' Vital signs: T 38.4° C, HR 122/min, RR 24/min, BP 84/46 mm Hg. Skin is cool and mottled.

options_or_rows_en.text: Could not keep hydrocortisone down for two days
BP 84/46 mm Hg with mottled skin
Glucose 52 mg/dL
Potassium 5.9 mEq/L and sodium 126 mEq/L
Abdominal pain after gastroenteritis
Adrenal crisis cue
Less urgent/less specific

rationale_en: Missed steroid replacement during illness plus shock, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia strongly supports adrenal crisis. Abdominal pain can occur but is less specific than the instability cues.

options_or_rows_en.text: Activate rapid response/provider notification for suspected adrenal crisis with shock.
Administer prescribed IV hydrocortisone, isotonic saline, and dextrose without delaying for cortisol results.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, and bedside glucose.
Maintain IV access, apply cardiac monitoring, and place the client supine with legs elevated if tolerated.
Reassess blood pressure, mental status, glucose, potassium, sodium, and urine output.

rationale_en: Immediate assessment identifies shock and hypoglycemia, supportive measures reduce collapse risk, urgent escalation obtains emergency therapy, and treatment should not wait for confirmatory cortisol testing when crisis is suspected.

dry mucous membranes without ECG changes

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 66-year-old client is admitted after 4 days of vomiting and poor oral intake from viral gastroenteritis. History: hypertension and osteoarthritis. Home medications include lisinopril and ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for knee pain. Vital signs: T 37.6° C, HR 108/min, BP 96/58 mm Hg. Mucous membranes are dry. rationale_en: Urine output is 120 mL ÷ 80 kg ÷ 8 hr = 0.1875 mL/kg/hr, rounded to 0.19 mL/kg/hr. This is oliguria and supports ongoing AKI risk.

options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old client is receiving a continuous unfractionated heparin infusion for an acute pulmonary embolism. Baseline platelet count was 226,000/mm3 and hemoglobin was 13.2 g/dL. Current vital signs: HR 96/min, BP 118/70 mm Hg, SpO2 95% on 2 L/min. options.text: new flank/abdominal pain, hypotension, tachycardia, hemoglobin drop, and aPTT >150 seconds
improving oxygen saturation and correct pump settings

rationale_en: Sudden flank or abdominal pain with shock signs and a large hemoglobin drop during excessive heparin effect suggests occult internal bleeding such as retroperitoneal hemorrhage.

stem_en: Calculate the percent decrease in platelet count from baseline 226,000/mm3 to 96,000/mm3. Round to the nearest whole percent.

options_or_rows_en.title: Hospital day 2
options_or_rows_en.content: A 63-year-old client is receiving a continuous unfractionated heparin infusion for an acute pulmonary embolism. Baseline platelet count was 226,000/mm3 and hemoglobin was 13.2 g/dL. Current vital signs: HR 96/min, BP 118/70 mm Hg, SpO2 95% on 2 L/min. The client reports mild pleuritic chest pain that is improving.

rationale_en: The decrease is 226,000 - 96,000 = 130,000. 130,000 ÷ 226,000 × 100 = 57.5%, rounded to 58%. A fall of about 50% or more during heparin therapy requires HIT evaluation while bleeding is also addressed.

Client: 68-year-old discharged 6 days ago after hospitalization for worsening chronic heart failure. The client reads at about a fifth-grade level and says, “I know I have to take the little water pill, but I do not know what numbers matter.”
Weight log: Monday 182 lb, Tuesday 183 lb, Wednesday 185 lb, Thursday 187 lb. The client reports new ankle swelling and sleeping on 3 pillows last night. The client denies chest pain, severe shortness of breath at rest, fainting, or confusion.
The client lives alone and buys canned soups because they are inexpensive. A working scale and the clinic phone number are in the home.

Client says, “I do not know what numbers matter.”
Client buys canned soups because they are inexpensive.
Working scale and clinic number are in the home.

Self-management barrier
Same-day clinic follow-up cue
Self-management support

The client lacks reliable access to food, prescriptions, and transportation for follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-health nurse note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 79-year-old with moderate dementia discharged 5 days ago after observation for dehydration. Lives with adult daughter, who works remotely.

options_or_rows_en.text: “You are doing the right thing by keeping her at home, so try to take it one day at a time.”
“You sound overwhelmed. Tell me what has been hardest since your mother came home.”
“A facility placement would break your promise, so we should avoid discussing that today.”
“I will give you a list of agencies, and you can decide which one to call later.”

rationale_en: The best response validates the caregiver's distress and invites further assessment without judgment. It opens the door to discussing safety, respite, and realistic supports.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over”?

options_or_rows_en.title: Nephrology clinic nurse note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 46-year-old recently told they have stage 4 chronic kidney disease and may need renal replacement education. The client came with a spouse after missing the first education class because of work.
Client states, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over.” The client denies thoughts of self-harm. Spouse says, “We cannot tell our teenagers. I will handle all the decisions so they do not worry.”
The family reports difficulty paying for fresh foods and confusion about which diet changes matter first.

options_or_rows_en.text: “Your spouse can make the decisions until you feel ready.”
“Hearing that word felt overwhelming. Tell me what dialysis means to you right now.”
“Let me explain the entire dialysis schedule so you can stop worrying.”
“Many people do fine on dialysis, so try not to think about the worst.”

rationale_en: The best response acknowledges the emotion, invites the client to explain their understanding, and keeps the client involved in decisions. It does not minimize the feeling, overload the client with details, or transfer decision-making to the spouse.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, “I heard dialysis and stopped listening. I think my life is over”?

The client prefers information in small sections and wants the spouse present if the client agrees before each discussion.

stem_en: What is the nurse’s priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Primary care screening visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 66-year-old seen for a wellness visit 3 months after spouse died. The client reports sleeping 3–4 hours, losing 8 lb, drinking 2–3 alcoholic drinks most nights, and stopping church and walking group activities.
PHQ-9 score: 18. Item 9 (“thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself”) marked “several days.”
Client says quietly, “Some mornings I wish I would not wake up.” When asked about a plan, the client says, “No plan. I just feel empty.” The spouse’s handgun is locked in a closet at home. Adult daughter calls weekly and offered to come to appointments.

options_or_rows_en.text: Call the daughter immediately and disclose all screening results before speaking further with the client.
Schedule a routine follow-up visit in 1 month to repeat the PHQ-9.
Ask directly about suicidal thoughts, intent, plan, and access to lethal means, and notify the provider or crisis clinician before the client leaves.
Reassure the client that grief commonly lasts longer than 3 months and provide a brochure.

rationale_en: The exhibit includes passive death wishes, a positive PHQ-9 item 9, alcohol use, withdrawal, weight loss, and firearm access. The priority is direct suicide risk assessment and escalation according to clinic policy before the client leaves.

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 3 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client has a new descending colostomy after surgery for bowel obstruction. Stoma is moist and beefy red. Peristomal skin is intact this morning.
Pouch output: soft brown stool. Client has been watching staff empty the pouch but has not done it independently.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stoma is moist and beefy red.
Client says, “If the stoma turns dark purple or black, I will wait until the clinic visit.”
Pouch leaked twice overnight and the skin feels like it is burning.
Client says, “I feel embarrassed when I look at the pouch.”
Client cannot state when to empty the pouch.
Requires follow-up before discharge
Expected/therapeutic support

rationale_en: A moist red stoma is expected early after surgery. A dusky stoma, repeated leakage with skin burning, and inability to explain pouch emptying need follow-up. Embarrassment requires supportive communication but is not the same as a physiologic complication.

stem_en: Which client statement indicates that discharge teaching about pouch emptying was effective?

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 3 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client has a new descending colostomy after surgery for bowel obstruction. Stoma is moist and beefy red. Peristomal skin is intact this morning.
Pouch output: soft brown stool. Client has been watching staff empty the pouch but has not done it independently.

options_or_rows_en.text: “I will wait until the pouch is completely full so I do not waste supplies.”
“I will empty the pouch when it is about one-third to one-half full.”
“I should scrub the stoma if it looks red and moist.”
“I only need to empty the pouch once each morning.”

rationale_en: The correct statement reflects practical pouch management: emptying before the pouch becomes heavy helps prevent leakage and skin injury. The other statements delay care or show misunderstanding of normal care.

stem_en: Which actions should the nurse take before discharge? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Postoperative day 3 assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client has a new descending colostomy after surgery for bowel obstruction. Stoma is moist and beefy red. Peristomal skin is intact this morning.
Pouch output: soft brown stool. Client has been watching staff empty the pouch but has not done it independently.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask permission to include a chosen support person in teaching.
Teach the adult child outside the room because the client is embarrassed.
Ask the client to demonstrate emptying and securing the pouch before leaving.
Show severe skin-breakdown photos to make the client take pouching seriously.
Replace dense written instructions with plain-language steps and pictures.
Confirm the client has starter supplies, delivery arrangements, and a number to call for leakage or skin breakdown.

rationale_en: Teaching should be adapted to the client's literacy needs, privacy preferences, and actual ability to perform care. The nurse should verify supply access and escalation instructions. Avoid excluding the client or relying on shame-based teaching.

stem_en: Complete the clinical judgment statement for the deterioration.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 48-year-old client with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back is admitted with suspected gallstone pancreatitis. Pain 9/10, persistent vomiting. Vital signs: T 38.0° C, HR 116/min, RR 24/min, BP 104/62 mm Hg. Abdomen is distended and tender. NPO status is initiated.

options.text: NPO status and opioid administration only
third spacing, hemoconcentration, hypotension, and falling urine output

rationale_en: Severe pancreatitis can shift large volumes out of the vascular space. Hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria, rising hematocrit, and rising BUN indicate poor perfusion.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 48-year-old client with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back is admitted with suspected gallstone pancreatitis. Pain 9/10, persistent vomiting. Vital signs: T 38.0° C, HR 116/min, RR 24/min, BP 104/62 mm Hg. Abdomen is distended and tender. NPO status is initiated.

rationale_en: Minimum expected urine output is 76 kg × 0.5 mL/kg/hr = 38 mL/hr. The current 38 mL/hr is just at the minimum and requires continued trending.

Complication or high-risk cue

rationale_en: Hemoconcentration, hypocalcemia, and possible biliary obstruction signal complications or higher severity. Elevated lipase and back-radiating epigastric pain support the diagnosis but are expected cues.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: A 48-year-old client with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back is admitted with suspected gallstone pancreatitis. Pain 9/10, persistent vomiting. Vital signs: T 38.0° C, HR 116/min, RR 24/min, BP 104/62 mm Hg. Abdomen is distended and tender. NPO status is initiated.

options_or_rows_en.text: Prepare large-bore IV access and administer prescribed isotonic fluid resuscitation.
Monitor response: blood pressure, lung sounds, oxygenation, urine output, BUN/hematocrit, and calcium.
Apply oxygen and raise the head of the bed while reassessing respiratory effort.
Assess airway, breathing, circulation, pain, abdomen, mental status, and urine output trend.
Activate rapid response/provider notification for hypotension, hypoxemia, and oliguria.

rationale_en: The nurse first verifies ABC and perfusion status, supports breathing, escalates for shock, implements prescribed resuscitation, and evaluates for both improved perfusion and fluid overload/electrolyte complications.

stem_en: Classify each cue from the home visit.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-health rehabilitation visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 74-year-old, 3 weeks after discharge from inpatient rehabilitation following a left-hemisphere stroke. The client has expressive aphasia and right-sided facial weakness. The client points accurately to pictures and follows one-step commands.
Caregiver reports giving thin water through a straw because the client “hates thickener.” During lunch, the nurse notes coughing after sips of water, a wet-sounding voice, and food pocketed in the right cheek. The client has lost 4 lb since discharge.
Two outpatient speech therapy visits were canceled because the caregiver could not arrange transportation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Coughing after thin water through a straw
Client points accurately to pictures
Two speech therapy visits canceled because transportation was unavailable
Food pocketed in the right cheek
Lost 4 lb since discharge
Care-coordination barrier
Swallowing or nutrition risk
Communication support

rationale_en: Coughing with thin liquids, wet voice, pocketing, and weight loss indicate swallowing/nutrition risk. Accurate picture pointing and one-step command following show useful communication supports. Missed speech therapy due to transportation is a care-coordination barrier.

stem_en: Classify each cue from the home visit.

options_or_rows_en.content: Current swallowing plan: minced-and-moist foods; mildly thick liquids; upright at 90 degrees for all oral intake; small bites and sips; no straws; check right cheek for pocketed food; stop feeding and contact the rehabilitation team for coughing, wet voice, fever, or reduced intake. Communication plan: use picture board, yes/no questions, one-step instructions, and extra response time.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-health rehabilitation visit
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client: 74-year-old, 3 weeks after discharge from inpatient rehabilitation following a left-hemisphere stroke. The client has expressive aphasia and right-sided facial weakness. The client points accurately to pictures and follows one-step commands.
Caregiver reports giving thin water through a straw because the client “hates thickener.” During lunch, the nurse notes coughing after sips of water, a wet-sounding voice, and food pocketed in the right cheek. The client has lost 4 lb since discharge.
Two outpatient speech therapy visits were canceled because the caregiver could not arrange transportation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Check the right cheek and listen for coughing or a wet-sounding voice during the meal.
Stop feeding and contact the rehabilitation team if coughing, wet voice, fever, or reduced intake occurs.
Offer small bites and sips without using a straw.
Position the client upright at 90 degrees before oral intake.
Confirm the client is alert and that minced-and-moist foods and mildly thick liquids are prepared.

rationale_en: The caregiver should first verify the client is alert and the correct diet/liquid level is available, then position upright, provide small bites/sips without a straw, monitor for pocketing and wet voice/coughing, and stop/contact the team if warning signs occur.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use the picture board, yes/no questions, one-step instructions, and extra response time.

Offer thin water by straw when the client refuses thickened liquids.

stem_en: For each finding, select the prevention concern it most directly supports.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin-risk huddle note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Resident is 86 years old, admitted for long-term care after functional decline. Uses wheelchair for meals and needs moderate assistance to turn in bed.
Sacral skin has blanchable redness after sitting for breakfast. Both heels rest directly on mattress when supine.
Brief was wet at 0700; loose stool noted near sacrum. Resident reports, “My bottom burns sometimes.”
Ate 25% of meals yesterday and drank little fluid.

options_or_rows_en.text: Resident cannot reposition independently in bed.
Brief is wet; loose stool noted near sacrum.
Ate 25% of meals yesterday and drank little fluid.
Both heels rest directly on the mattress.
Sacral redness blanches when gently pressed.
Nutrition/hydration
Moisture/skin protection
Pressure/mobility

rationale_en: Immobility and heel pressure require offloading and repositioning; moisture requires incontinence care and barrier protection; low intake requires nutrition/hydration support; blanchable redness requires pressure relief and monitoring, not staging as an open injury.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin-risk huddle note
options_or_rows_en.content:
Resident is 86 years old, admitted for long-term care after functional decline. Uses wheelchair for meals and needs moderate assistance to turn in bed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Consult the dietitian for poor intake and protein/calorie needs.
Provide prompt incontinence care and apply moisture-barrier product as ordered or per protocol.
Massage reddened areas over bony prominences each shift.
Offload the heels so they do not rest directly on the mattress.
Request evaluation for a pressure-redistribution mattress or overlay.
Create an individualized repositioning schedule based on skin tolerance, support surface, and mobility.

rationale_en: The plan should individualize repositioning, offload heels, manage moisture promptly, use a pressure-redistribution surface as indicated, and consult dietary support. Massage, donut cushions, and delaying incontinence care increase harm.

options_or_rows_en.title: Preventive screening record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is an average-risk adult who completed a clinic-ordered home stool-based colorectal cancer screening test. Result released May 3: positive.
The provider placed a referral for diagnostic follow-up. The client has not scheduled the appointment.
Two clinic calls from unknown numbers were not answered; one voicemail was left in English. The client reads short English phrases but prefers health information in Spanish.

options.text: navigation barriers after an abnormal screening result
complete understanding of the follow-up plan

rationale_en: A positive stool-based screening test is not treated by repeating the same home test; it requires diagnostic follow-up. The nurse should focus on navigation barriers such as scheduling, transportation, language access, and fear.

options_or_rows_en.title: Successful nurse call
options_or_rows_en.content:
On May 8, nurse reaches the client. Client says, “I thought maybe the test was wrong. I heard the follow-up test is painful, and I do not have anyone to drive me.”
Client says the daughter may help with scheduling if the clinic explains what is needed. Diagnostic follow-up appointment is scheduled for May 20 with transportation assistance pending confirmation.

options.text: navigation barriers after an abnormal screening result
complete understanding of the follow-up plan

rationale_en: A positive stool-based screening test is not treated by repeating the same home test; it requires diagnostic follow-up. The nurse should focus on navigation barriers such as scheduling, transportation, language access, and fear.

options_or_rows_en.text: Explain in plain language that an abnormal screening result needs diagnostic follow-up.
Send another home stool test first because the client is afraid.
Ask the client whether the daughter may help with scheduling and transportation.
Confirm the appointment date, transportation plan, and a number to call if the ride falls through.
Offer qualified interpreter services for health information and scheduling.
Ask what the client has heard about the test and what worries them most.

rationale_en: The nurse should explain the abnormal result in plain language, use qualified interpreter services if needed, assess concerns, obtain permission before involving family, confirm scheduling and transportation, and provide clear call-back instructions. Repeating the screening test, using family as an interpreter by default, or documenting refusal without assessment undermines follow-up.
