stem_en: Place the nursing actions in the safest order for the first assisted transfer today.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is postoperative day 6 after internal fixation of a right hip fracture. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker.
PT note: requires one-person assist for transfers; ambulated 15 ft yesterday with walker, stopped due to fatigue. Goal today: chair for meals and short assisted walks before meals as tolerated.
Nursing note: attempted to stand twice without calling. Reports dizziness when first sitting up. Pain 7/10 before therapy and 3/10 about 45 minutes after prescribed pain medicine.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply the gait belt snugly over clothing while the client is sitting upright.
Clear the path, lock the bed and wheelchair, place nonskid footwear, and position the walker.
Use the gait belt and walker to assist the client to stand and pivot to the chair.
Review the activity order, pain level, recent analgesic timing, and dizziness history.
Assist the client to sit at the bedside and assess dizziness before standing.

rationale_en: Before mobility, the nurse reviews activity orders and pain/orthostatic risk, prepares the environment and assistive devices, has the client sit and assess dizziness, then applies the gait belt and transfers with assistance.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is postoperative day 6 after internal fixation of a right hip fracture. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker.
PT note: requires one-person assist for transfers; ambulated 15 ft yesterday with walker, stopped due to fatigue. Goal today: chair for meals and short assisted walks before meals as tolerated.
Nursing note: attempted to stand twice without calling. Reports dizziness when first sitting up. Pain 7/10 before therapy and 3/10 about 45 minutes after prescribed pain medicine.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ambulate with walker and one-person assist before meals and at bedtime as tolerated.
Keep the client in bed except for therapy sessions to avoid falls.
Coordinate pain medication so the client can participate in therapy while monitoring sedation.
Use a bedside commode with staff assist overnight because urgency occurs after diuretic use.
Raise all four side rails whenever the client is alone.
Supports safe progression
Revise plan

rationale_en: Safe progression uses scheduled assistance, pain control, proper assistive devices, and call-light reinforcement. Restraint-like strategies and avoidance of all mobility increase harm.

stem_en: Which statements by the client indicate understanding of fall-prevention teaching? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Client is postoperative day 6 after internal fixation of a right hip fracture. Weight bearing as tolerated with walker.
PT note: requires one-person assist for transfers; ambulated 15 ft yesterday with walker, stopped due to fatigue. Goal today: chair for meals and short assisted walks before meals as tolerated.
Nursing note: attempted to stand twice without calling. Reports dizziness when first sitting up. Pain 7/10 before therapy and 3/10 about 45 minutes after prescribed pain medicine.

options_or_rows_en.text: “At home, I can hold on to furniture instead of using the walker.”
“I will sit at the bedside and tell staff if I feel dizzy before walking.”
“I should wear the nonskid socks and use the walker every time I get up.”
“I will press the call light before I stand, even if I only need the bathroom.”
“I will keep my phone, water, and call light where I can reach them.”
“If I feel embarrassed asking for help, I will move quickly so I do not bother anyone.”

rationale_en: Effective teaching includes calling before standing, using nonskid footwear and the walker, pausing for dizziness, and keeping needed items within reach. Rushing to the bathroom or using furniture instead of the walker is unsafe.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 data, classify each finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 7.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves and widening QRS
Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL with calcium 6.8 mg/dL and fingertip tingling
Creatinine increased from 1.1 to 2.4 mg/dL with 80 mL urine output in 4 hours
Uric acid decreased from 9.2 to 4.1 mg/dL after rasburicase
Temperature 37.4°C
LDH increased from 1,450 to 3,200 U/L
Expected treatment effect
Danger cue consistent with TLS
Not a priority TLS cue

rationale_en: TLS causes rapid release of intracellular potassium and phosphate, secondary hypocalcemia, kidney injury, and high cell-turnover markers. The falling uric acid is expected after rasburicase and should not reassure the nurse when the client has dangerous hyperkalemia and oliguria. A temperature of 37.4°C is not the priority cue in this pattern.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 data, classify each finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response data
options_or_rows_en.content: After IV calcium gluconate, insulin with dextrose, and initiation of dialysis, the client is more alert. HR 96/min, BP 126/78 mm Hg, RR 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air. ECG shows narrowing QRS complexes and less prominent T waves. Labs: potassium 5.4 mEq/L, phosphorus 6.2 mg/dL, calcium 7.5 mg/dL, creatinine 2.0 mg/dL, uric acid 2.8 mg/dL. Urine output is 110 mL over the past 4 hours.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 7.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves and widening QRS
Phosphorus 8.4 mg/dL with calcium 6.8 mg/dL and fingertip tingling
Creatinine increased from 1.1 to 2.4 mg/dL with 80 mL urine output in 4 hours
Uric acid decreased from 9.2 to 4.1 mg/dL after rasburicase
Temperature 37.4°C
LDH increased from 1,450 to 3,200 U/L
Expected treatment effect
Danger cue consistent with TLS
Not a priority TLS cue

rationale_en: TLS causes rapid release of intracellular potassium and phosphate, secondary hypocalcemia, kidney injury, and high cell-turnover markers. options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options.text: it creates immediate risk for ventricular dysrhythmia and cardiac arrest
it confirms that chemotherapy should be continued without interruption
it means the client is no longer at risk for kidney injury
it indicates the client has developed an infectious emergency
evidence that the ECG changes are unrelated to TLS
a sign that dialysis is no longer needed regardless of potassium
an expected effect of rasburicase, not resolution of the emergency
a reason to restart allopurinol immediately

rationale_en: The priority is hyperkalemia with ECG changes because it threatens circulation immediately. Hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, and kidney injury are serious, but the widened QRS and loss of P waves point to imminent dysrhythmia. Rasburicase can lower uric acid even while other TLS derangements worsen.

stem_en: The provider enters emergency hyperkalemia protocol orders while dialysis access is being arranged. Place the nursing actions in the safest priority order.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission data
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.
Assessment: T 37.0°C, HR 88/min, BP 128/80 mm Hg, RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Alert and oriented; no edema; no muscle twitching or paresthesias. Urine output adequate.
Labs: WBC 48,000/µL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, platelets 98,000/µL, BUN 22 mg/dL, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, phosphorus 4.9 mg/dL, calcium 8.9 mg/dL, uric acid 9.2 mg/dL, LDH 1,450 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Verify continuous cardiac monitoring and obtain/print the current ECG tracing.
Prepare the client for temporary dialysis catheter placement and emergent hemodialysis.
Administer IV calcium gluconate as prescribed to stabilize the myocardium.
Administer regular insulin with dextrose as prescribed to shift potassium into cells.
Monitor blood glucose, repeat potassium results, ECG pattern, and strict intake/output after interventions begin.

rationale_en: With severe hyperkalemia and ECG changes, the nurse first ensures continuous ECG surveillance, then administers prescribed calcium to stabilize the myocardium, then prescribed insulin/dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly. Dialysis preparation follows because dialysis removes potassium definitively. Ongoing monitoring evaluates safety and response, including hypoglycemia after insulin.

options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 34-year-old client with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma; CT shows 14-cm abdominal mass. Orders include normal saline at 200 mL/hr and rasburicase; allopurinol is held because rasburicase is ordered. First chemotherapy cycle begins this morning.

rationale_en: Eighty mL divided by 4 hours equals 20 mL/hr. This is below the common adult minimum benchmark of about 30 mL/hr and supports oliguria in the setting of TLS-associated kidney injury.

options_or_rows_en.text: Activate the sepsis/rapid response pathway and communicate suspected urinary source with obstruction.
Administer prescribed broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly.
Prepare for vasopressor support and urgent source-control procedures if hypotension persists.
Ensure blood and urine cultures and lactate are obtained or verified without delaying antibiotics.
Administer prescribed isotonic fluid resuscitation and monitor lung sounds and perfusion.

rationale_en: The nurse escalates immediately, verifies cultures/lactate as part of the sepsis bundle, gives antibiotics promptly, supports perfusion with ordered fluids, and anticipates vasopressors/source control when shock persists.

stem_en: Which events from the morning require privacy follow-up by RN Jordan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline client profile and facility policy
options_or_rows_en.content: Mrs. Torrance is postoperative day 1 after ORIF of a left tibial plateau fracture. She is alert, oriented, and participates in care. Emergency contact/authorized contact listed in the chart: adult daughter Megan Torrance only. The facility policy requires the nurse who discovers or witnesses a potential privacy breach to immediately mitigate exposure, notify the charge nurse, complete an electronic incident report, and ensure same-shift notification to the unit manager and Privacy Officer. Incident reports are quality-improvement documents and must not be referenced in the medical record.

options_or_rows_en.text: Mrs. Torrance reports pain of 4 out of 10 while the nurse performs the routine morning assessment.
RN Casey opens Mrs. Torrance's operative report and medication record out of curiosity without a care role.
A caller who is not listed as an authorized contact asks for Mrs. Torrance's laboratory results and discharge timing.
A nursing student photographs the wound on a personal phone with the hospital identification band visible.
The employer faxes the nursing unit requesting admission records and operative reports without a signed authorization.
Two nurses discuss another client's HIV status by name in the hallway where a visitor can hear.

rationale_en: The unverified caller, curiosity-driven chart access, unauthorised employer request, identifiable photo on a personal device, and hallway report all create actual or potential privacy violations. The pain report during routine assessment is ordinary patient-care information used by the assigned nurse and is not itself a privacy event.

stem_en: Which events from the morning require privacy follow-up by RN Jordan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Privacy Officer review and safeguards
options_or_rows_en.content: At 1430, the Privacy Officer states RN Casey's access will be investigated through the formal breach-determination process using the EHR audit trail. The student's photo is also reviewed as a potential breach. At 1500, HIM confirms no employer records will be released without a signed authorization or valid legal basis. At 1530, Mrs. Torrance asks about a concern with her chart; RN Jordan acknowledges the facility is aware of a concern and the Privacy Officer is handling it, without naming staff or disclosing investigation details. At 1700, safeguards under review include fax-machine signage to redirect records requests to HIM, instructor reinforcement of the no-identifiable-photo policy, an in-service on minimum-necessary access and audit trails, and staff review of private spaces for report. By 1900, the client remains clinically stable.

options_or_rows_en.text: RN Casey's curiosity-based EHR access
Student's identifiable wound photograph on a personal phone
Employer fax asking for records without authorization
Caller claiming to be the patient's husband asks for results
Hallway report about another patient's HIV status
Completed impermissible access or identifiable record as it occurred
Conditionally permissible only after authorization or legal basis is verified through the proper process
Verbal incidental disclosure risk because reasonable safeguards were not used

rationale_en: RN Casey's access and the student's identifiable photo are completed impermissible events as they occurred. The employer and phone requests might be handled only through proper authorization or legal authority, but they are not disclosures the unit nurse can make as presented. The hallway report is a verbal disclosure risk because staff failed to use reasonable safeguards in a public area.

options_or_rows_en.text: Secure the employer fax and redirect the request to HIM rather than releasing records from the nursing unit.
Have the student delete the identifiable photo, including recently deleted storage, in the presence of RN Jordan and the instructor; report for Privacy Officer follow-up.
Document in Mrs. Torrance's medical record that incident reports were filed for the privacy breaches.
Interrupt the hallway report and move the nurses to a private space to finish patient-specific communication.
Release no information to Robert; ask Mrs. Torrance whether she authorizes him and document her restriction if she declines.
Ask RN Casey to exit the chart, notify the charge nurse, complete the incident report, and keep the report out of the medical record.

rationale_en: The correct response combines immediate mitigation, proper reporting, and prevention of further disclosure for each event. The nurse should not reference the incident report in the patient's medical record because the incident report is a quality-improvement/risk-management document kept separate from clinical documentation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline client profile and facility policy
options_or_rows_en.content: Mrs. Torrance is postoperative day 1 after ORIF of a left tibial plateau fracture. She is alert, oriented, and participates in care. The facility policy requires the nurse who discovers or witnesses a potential privacy breach to immediately mitigate exposure, notify the charge nurse, complete an electronic incident report, and ensure same-shift notification to the unit manager and Privacy Officer. Incident reports are quality-improvement documents and must not be referenced in the medical record.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask RN Casey to close the record or log out immediately, then notify the charge nurse and follow the breach-reporting chain.
Tell RN Casey that discipline will be imposed and require her to write an apology to the patient.
Complete the electronic incident report first, then tell the charge nurse after the report is submitted.
Wait until the end of the shift so all privacy events can be reported together.

rationale_en: The first step is immediate mitigation: stop the active unauthorized access by having RN Casey leave the record. The nurse then notifies the charge nurse and follows the facility's reporting chain. Discipline and patient notification are handled through management and the Privacy Officer's process, not by the discovering nurse acting independently.

stem_en: Stage 1: Classify each nursing-student behavior in Client A's Clostridioides difficile room.

options_or_rows_en.title: Unit infection-prevention policy
options_or_rows_en.content:
Standing rules at each doorway and reviewed at shift huddle:
1. Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the default hand-hygiene method except when hands are visibly soiled or the client has a known or suspected spore-forming organism; then soap and water is required.
2. Dedicated or single-use equipment is required for clients on contact precautions. If a device must be shared, clean visible soil, disinfect with an appropriate agent, and allow the manufacturer-specified wet-contact time before the device enters the next room.
3. PPE is donned outside the room and doffed inside the room, gloves first and then gown, before exiting; perform hand hygiene immediately after removal.
4. Visitors are offered a precautions orientation at the first visit of each shift, including hand hygiene and required PPE.
5. Soiled linen from any isolation room is placed in a water-soluble inner bag and then into a labeled outer bag at the point of use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Entering the contact-precautions room wearing gloves but no gown
Using the blood-pressure cuff dedicated to Room 12
Removing gloves and failing to perform hand hygiene immediately afterward
Touching the door handle with bare, unwashed hands after client contact
Breach
Not a breach

rationale_en: Contact precautions require gown and gloves before room entry. The dedicated cuff belongs in Room 12 and is appropriate to use. The major failures are missing the gown, missing immediate hand hygiene after glove removal, and touching a shared surface with contaminated bare hands; in a C. difficile room, the required hand-hygiene method is soap and water.

stem_en: Stage 1: Classify each nursing-student behavior in Client A's Clostridioides difficile room.

options_or_rows_en.title: Focused debrief and end-of-shift status
options_or_rows_en.content:
1215: The nurse debriefs the student about the Room 12 entry without a gown, missed hand hygiene after glove removal, door-handle contact, the linen-bagging error, and the stethoscope found in the clean utility room.
1230: The charge nurse asks which identified breach posed the greatest cross-contamination risk to other clients and why.
1300: Client D's repeat sputum AFB smear remains positive (2+); the provider confirms airborne precautions and AIIR must continue. The nurse teaches the patient and mother that non-essential trips outside the AIIR are not permitted while smear-positive; a surgical mask is used on the patient only for medically necessary transport, and visitors follow the facility airborne-precautions policy with instruction and offering of an N95 respirator.
End of shift: Client C's daughter correctly demonstrates gown and glove donning and doffing. The contaminated stethoscope is retrieved; clean-utility surfaces it may have touched are disinfected; potentially contaminated nearby supplies are removed or handled per policy; the stethoscope is disinfected with full wet-contact time and returned to Room 16. Client A's CDI diarrhea continues; contact precautions remain. Client D remains smear-positive in AIIR. The student requires follow-up education and supervised return demonstration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Entering the contact-precautions room wearing gloves but no gown
Using the blood-pressure cuff dedicated to Room 12
Removing gloves and failing to perform hand hygiene immediately afterward
Touching the door handle with bare, unwashed hands after client contact
Breach
Not a breach

rationale_en: Contact precautions require gown and gloves before room entry. The dedicated cuff belongs in Room 12 and is appropriate to use. The major failures are missing the gown, missing immediate hand hygiene after glove removal, and touching a shared surface with contaminated bare hands; in a C. difficile room, the required hand-hygiene method is soap and water.

stem_en: Stage 1: Put the steps in the correct order for handling the portable bladder scanner after use on Client A before taking it to Client B.

options_or_rows_en.title: Unit infection-prevention policy
options_or_rows_en.content:
Standing rules at each doorway and reviewed at shift huddle:
1. Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the default hand-hygiene method except when hands are visibly soiled or the client has a known or suspected spore-forming organism; then soap and water is required.
2. Dedicated or single-use equipment is required for clients on contact precautions. If a device must be shared, clean visible soil, disinfect with an appropriate agent, and allow the manufacturer-specified wet-contact time before the device enters the next room.
3. PPE is donned outside the room and doffed inside the room, gloves first and then gown, before exiting; perform hand hygiene immediately after removal.
4. Visitors are offered a precautions orientation at the first visit of each shift, including hand hygiene and required PPE.
5. Soiled linen from any isolation room is placed in a water-soluble inner bag and then into a labeled outer bag at the point of use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform hand hygiene with soap and water immediately after doffing.
Doff gloves first and then gown inside Room 12 before exiting.
Clean any visible soil from the scanner surfaces while still in Room 12 and wearing PPE.

Disinfect the probe, cord, screen or controls, handle, and touched surfaces with an EPA-registered sporicidal disinfectant effective against C. difficile, keeping surfaces wet for the label contact time.
Transport the disinfected scanner to Client B's room with clean hands.

rationale_en: Because the scanner was used in a CDI contact-precautions room and must be shared with a standard-precautions client, it must be cleaned, disinfected with a sporicidal product, and allowed full wet-contact time before moving to the next room. Doffing and soap-and-water hand hygiene after the device is disinfected prevent the nurse from recontaminating it during transport.

stem_en: Stage 3: The charge nurse asks which identified breach posed the greatest cross-contamination risk to other clients on the unit. Which breach should the nurse identify as the priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: Unit infection-prevention policy
options_or_rows_en.content:
Standing rules at each doorway and reviewed at shift huddle:
1. Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the default hand-hygiene method except when hands are visibly soiled or the client has a known or suspected spore-forming organism; then soap and water is required.
2. Dedicated or single-use equipment is required for clients on contact precautions. If a device must be shared, clean visible soil, disinfect with an appropriate agent, and allow the manufacturer-specified wet-contact time before the device enters the next room.
3. PPE is donned outside the room and doffed inside the room, gloves first and then gown, before exiting; perform hand hygiene immediately after removal.
4. Visitors are offered a precautions orientation at the first visit of each shift, including hand hygiene and required PPE.
5. Soiled linen from any isolation room is placed in a water-soluble inner bag and then into a labeled outer bag at the point of use.

options_or_rows_en.text: The nursing student's contaminated bare-hand contact with the Room 12 door handle
The standard linen bag used for Client A's soiled linens
Client B's wife stopping at the open doorway of Room 12 to ask a question
Client C's dedicated stethoscope placed on the clean-utility-room counter near the clean supply cart

rationale_en: The stethoscope breach has the widest and most direct cross-contamination pathway: a device from a draining MRSA contact-precautions room was placed in a clean supply area used for multiple clients. It could contaminate surfaces, supplies, and staff hands. The door handle and linen bagging errors are serious, but their exposure radius is more contained.

stem_en: Stage 2: Client D has smear-positive pulmonary TB in a functioning AIIR. Her mother asks to take her outside in a wheelchair for fresh air. Which response is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Unit infection-prevention policy
options_or_rows_en.content:
Standing rules at each doorway and reviewed at shift huddle:
1. Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the default hand-hygiene method except when hands are visibly soiled or the client has a known or suspected spore-forming organism; then soap and water is required.
2. Dedicated or single-use equipment is required for clients on contact precautions. If a device must be shared, clean visible soil, disinfect with an appropriate agent, and allow the manufacturer-specified wet-contact time before the device enters the next room.
3. PPE is donned outside the room and doffed inside the room, gloves first and then gown, before exiting; perform hand hygiene immediately after removal.
4. Visitors are offered a precautions orientation at the first visit of each shift, including hand hygiene and required PPE.
5. Soiled linen from any isolation room is placed in a water-soluble inner bag and then into a labeled outer bag at the point of use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the mother she does not need respiratory protection inside the AIIR because negative pressure protects everyone in the room.

Explain that a non-essential trip outside the AIIR is not permitted while AFB smears remain positive; offer in-room comfort alternatives and teach the mother to follow the facility airborne-precautions visitor policy, including instruction and offering of an N95 respirator.
Delay answering until the provider makes rounds because nurses do not teach airborne-precautions restrictions.
Allow a brief outdoor trip if the patient wears a surgical mask the entire time.
Give the mother a surgical mask and explain that this reliably protects visitors from airborne TB while inside the room.

rationale_en: The patient is still AFB smear-positive, and the case states the AIIR is functioning and precautions must continue. A non-essential outdoor trip would bypass the engineering control. A surgical mask on the patient is used only when medically necessary transport cannot be avoided; it is not a substitute for the AIIR. The nurse should respond compassionately, offer alternatives, and teach visitor respiratory-protection expectations under facility policy.

stem_en: Stage 3: Which statements show an accurate evaluation of whether the infection-control interventions were effective? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Unit infection-prevention policy
options_or_rows_en.content:
Standing rules at each doorway and reviewed at shift huddle:
1. Alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) is the default hand-hygiene method except when hands are visibly soiled or the client has a known or suspected spore-forming organism; then soap and water is required.
2. Dedicated or single-use equipment is required for clients on contact precautions. If a device must be shared, clean visible soil, disinfect with an appropriate agent, and allow the manufacturer-specified wet-contact time before the device enters the next room.
3. PPE is donned outside the room and doffed inside the room, gloves first and then gown, before exiting; perform hand hygiene immediately after removal.
4. Visitors are offered a precautions orientation at the first visit of each shift, including hand hygiene and required PPE.
5. Soiled linen from any isolation room is placed in a water-soluble inner bag and then into a labeled outer bag at the point of use.

options_or_rows_en.text: Client C's daughter demonstrating correct gown and glove donning and doffing supports that visitor teaching changed behavior.
The student's isolation-room errors require follow-up education and supervised return demonstration before independent isolation-room contact.
Client D's continued AFB smear positivity means airborne precautions and AIIR confinement remain necessary; the outdoor trip remains inappropriate.
Client A's potassium correction to 3.6 mEq/L means CDI contact precautions can be discontinued.

Retrieving the stethoscope, disinfecting contacted clean-utility surfaces, handling nearby supplies per policy, and completing wet-contact disinfection before return supports containment of the environmental breach.
Because no new infections were diagnosed by the end of the shift, all interventions can be declared fully effective.

rationale_en: Effective infection-control evaluation during the same shift relies on process indicators: observed PPE compliance, return demonstration, correction of environmental contamination, and recognition of ongoing isolation needs. A same-day absence of new infections is not proof of effectiveness, and lab correction does not end CDI precautions.

stem_en: Stage 1: Highlight the specific cues Marcus should identify as requiring charge-nurse intervention because they show lateral incivility, a safety-relevant handoff omission, or a receiving-nurse response that needs coaching.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client background and active orders
options_or_rows_en.content: Admitted 2 days ago for hypertensive urgency; arrival BP 218/114. History: CKD stage 3b, baseline creatinine 1.5 mg/dL/eGFR 37 mL/min; HFpEF; type 2 diabetes. Orders updated at 1400: amlodipine 10 mg PO daily, increased from 5 mg after BP trend 168-174/92-96; lisinopril 20 mg PO daily; metformin 500 mg PO twice daily held per pharmacy renal-dose flag pending repeat labs; sliding-scale insulin per protocol. Telemetry in place. Plan: observe BP response to amlodipine increase over the next 24 hours before discharge consideration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Renee speaks rapidly during the bedside handoff.
Renee stands with crossed arms and does not make eye contact.
Renee says, "Meds are on the MAR — you can read."
Renee visibly rolls her eyes when Priya asks whether any medications changed, and Mrs. Vega sees it.
Renee says, "I'm not going to spoon-feed you."
Renee leaves the room without allowing time for questions.
Renee does not verbally communicate that amlodipine was increased from 5 mg to 10 mg and given at 1700.
Renee does not verbally communicate the hospitalist's instruction to monitor BP q4h for 24 hours.
In the hallway Renee says, "She'll be calling you every five minutes to hold her hand."
Priya accepts the incomplete report and moves on without clarifying or escalating.

rationale_en: The key cues are the dismissive statements, the eye-roll in front of the patient, the failure to allow questions, the omitted medication-change and monitoring-order information, the public belittling comment, and Priya's failure to advocate for a complete report. Rapid speech and crossed arms contribute to tone but are less specific as isolated cues.

stem_en: Stage 1: Highlight the specific cues Marcus should identify as requiring charge-nurse intervention because they show lateral incivility, a safety-relevant handoff omission, or a receiving-nurse response that needs coaching.

options_or_rows_en.title: Conduct and systems follow-up
options_or_rows_en.content: After the patient is stabilized, Marcus speaks privately with Renee by phone, names the observed behaviors and their impact, and states that the event will be documented through the civility/professional-conduct pathway. Marcus also speaks privately with Priya, acknowledges the inadequate handoff, and coaches her to ask direct questions, request structured SBAR, or escalate incomplete reports to the charge nurse. Priya documents objective clinical facts in the patient chart. Marcus completes a safety report for the communication failure and missed monitoring, separately documents the incivility details through the professional-conduct pathway, and notifies the nurse manager.

Patient-safety communication failure/professional lapse
Lateral incivility
Assertive/acceptable communication or not incivility by itself

rationale_en: Incivility is defined by demeaning or disrespectful behavior toward a colleague. The omitted medication and monitoring information is a patient-safety communication failure. Rapid speech alone may reflect workload, and a private, respectful redirect is assertive professional communication.

rationale_en: The first action is to ensure the hospitalist is notified now because the stated SBP > 180 parameter has been crossed and the client has a headache. Supporting Priya through the call preserves patient safety and builds her competence.

stem_en: Stage 3: Which findings best show that Marcus's interventions addressed both the care gap and the communication breakdown? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client background and active orders
options_or_rows_en.content: Admitted 2 days ago for hypertensive urgency; arrival BP 218/114. History: CKD stage 3b, baseline creatinine 1.5 mg/dL/eGFR 37 mL/min; HFpEF; type 2 diabetes. Orders updated at 1400: amlodipine 10 mg PO daily, increased from 5 mg after BP trend 168-174/92-96; lisinopril 20 mg PO daily; metformin 500 mg PO twice daily held per pharmacy renal-dose flag pending repeat labs; sliding-scale insulin per protocol. Telemetry in place. Plan: observe BP response to amlodipine increase over the next 24 hours before discharge consideration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Renee and Priya become personally friendly and socialize together during breaks.
Subsequent handoffs include verbal medication changes, monitoring orders, and time for clarifying questions.
Mrs. Vega's BP decreases from 186/102 to 162/90 and then 154/86, and her headache resolves.
No further care omissions related to handoff occur, and nurse-manager follow-up is documented.
Priya documents the q4h BP checks and updates the care plan with the current amlodipine dose and monitoring schedule.
Mrs. Vega's serum creatinine trend improves during the admission.

rationale_en: Resolution requires clinical and process evidence: BP improves and symptoms resolve, monitoring is performed and documented, the care plan reflects the order, handoffs become complete and professional, and manager follow-up occurs. Friendship and creatinine trends do not evaluate the communication breakdown.

options_or_rows_en.title: Conduct and systems follow-up
options_or_rows_en.content: After the patient is stabilized, Marcus speaks privately with Renee by phone, names the observed behaviors and their impact, and states that the event will be documented through the civility/professional-conduct pathway. Marcus also speaks privately with Priya, acknowledges the inadequate handoff, and coaches her to ask direct questions, request structured SBAR, or escalate incomplete reports to the charge nurse. Priya documents objective clinical facts in the patient chart. Marcus completes a safety report for the communication failure and missed monitoring, separately documents the incivility details through the professional-conduct pathway, and notifies the nurse manager.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 triage-point findings, assign the correct START triage category to each casualty.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline protocol and resources
options_or_rows_en.content:
Facility protocol: This 22-bed community ED is in Level 1 resource-constrained MCI mode when acute casualties exceed available treatment spaces. The protocol requires START triage at the point of entry; full external decontamination of any chemically contaminated casualty before ED entry; red/immediate before yellow/delayed before green/minor; black/expectant casualties receive comfort measures only when resources allow. The decontamination station outside the ambulance entrance is functional.

Resources at 1854: three RNs, one emergency physician, one PA; one trauma bay and two resuscitation bays; two ventilators, with one already in use for an ICU-hold patient; four O-negative PRBC units immediately available; standard airway, albuterol, burn-care, and resuscitation supplies available. Two additional nurses have been called in but are not yet present.

Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: A: apneic after airway repositioning, pulseless
B: ambulatory, alert, isolated forearm laceration
C: nonambulatory, RR 32, capillary refill 4 seconds, altered mental status
D: nonambulatory, RR 32, stridor, chemical contamination
E: nonambulatory burn casualty, RR 26, pulse present, follows commands
F: ambulatory, alert, mild headache and tinnitus
G: apneic after airway repositioning, chemically contaminated
Black / expectant
Green / minor
Red / immediate
Yellow / delayed

rationale_en: START sorts by walking, breathing, perfusion, and mental status. A and G remain apneic after airway repositioning, so they are black/expectant in this MCI. B and F can walk and have minor complaints, so they are green. C is red because RR is greater than 30, perfusion is poor, and mental status is abnormal. D is red because she is nonambulatory with RR greater than 30 and airway compromise. E is yellow because he is serious but currently breathing under 30, perfusing, and following commands.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 update
options_or_rows_en.content: 1934-2009: C returns after emergent external fixation and hemorrhage control. HR 108, BP 98/64, RR 20, SpO2 97% on 4 L nasal cannula. He received four units PRBCs total and is now responsive to voice and following commands; urine output is 25 mL in the past hour. D improves after additional albuterol and dexamethasone: stridor diminished, SpO2 94% on oxygen, RR 24, speaking in full sentences. Because severe ammonia exposure can worsen after arrival, she remains under continued observation/admission-level monitoring for recurrent airway compromise and noncardiogenic pulmonary edema. E is accepted by the burn center, hemodynamically stable, pain 4/10, awaiting helicopter transport. B's laceration is irrigated and sutured, tetanus prophylaxis given, and she is discharged with wound-care instructions. F has intact tympanic membranes, normal neurologic exam, and improving headache; she is discharged after observation with blast-injury precautions. At 2000 the charge nurse performs a re-triage sweep: five treated, two deceased (A and G), two discharged (B and F), one awaiting transfer (E), and two improving in the ED (C and D).

Resources at 1854: three RNs, one emergency physician, one PA; one trauma bay and two resuscitation bays; two ventilators, with one already in use for an ICU-hold patient; four O-negative PRBC units immediately available; standard airway, albuterol, burn-care, and resuscitation supplies available. Two additional nurses have been called in but are not yet present.

Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: Chemical contamination alone makes a casualty ineligible for treatment, regardless of breathing status or resources.
In this resource-limited MCI, casualties who remain apneic after airway repositioning are expectant so resuscitation resources can be directed to salvageable casualties.
They are deprioritized mainly because they are older than the other casualties and younger clients should always be treated first.
Pulseless adults in the ED must always receive CPR first, even during an activated MCI protocol.

rationale_en: Disaster triage shifts from routine one-patient rescue to greatest good for the greatest number. A and G meet the facility's START black criteria after airway repositioning. CPR, ventilator use, and one-to-one nursing would consume scarce resources needed for C, D, and E, who are more salvageable with timely intervention.

stem_en: Two red-category casualties are present at the start of Stage 1: Casualty C with hemorrhagic shock and no contamination, and Casualty D with stridor and ammonia contamination. Which casualty should enter the ED treatment area first?

Resources at 1854: three RNs, one emergency physician, one PA; one trauma bay and two resuscitation bays; two ventilators, with one already in use for an ICU-hold patient; four O-negative PRBC units immediately available; standard airway, albuterol, burn-care, and resuscitation supplies available. Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: Move Casualty E first because burns over 18% TBSA are visually severe and need fluid resuscitation.
Move Casualty C into the trauma bay while Casualty D completes decontamination outside the ED.
Wait until all seven casualties have full head-to-toe assessments before assigning the trauma bay.
Bring Casualty D directly into the ED because airway problems always override decontamination barriers.

rationale_en: C can receive life-saving hemorrhage treatment immediately and is deteriorating. D is also red, but the facility protocol prohibits ED entry until decontamination and re-triage are complete. Using the trauma bay for C during D's required decontamination interval maximizes treatment of salvageable casualties without contaminating the ED.

stem_en: During Stage 2, which actions are appropriate disaster-phase nursing actions? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline protocol and resources
options_or_rows_en.content:

The decontamination station outside the ambulance entrance is functional.

Resources at 1854: three RNs, one emergency physician, one PA; one trauma bay and two resuscitation bays; two ventilators, with one already in use for an ICU-hold patient; four O-negative PRBC units immediately available; standard airway, albuterol, burn-care, and resuscitation supplies available. Two additional nurses have been called in but are not yet present.

Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: Initiate the ordered massive transfusion protocol for Casualty C.
Apply high-flow humidified oxygen, administer ordered bronchodilator therapy, and prepare difficult-airway equipment for Casualty D after decontamination.
Continue ordered IV burn resuscitation and transfer planning for stable Casualty E.
Assign one nurse to start CPR on Casualty A or G while the red casualties are being treated.
Keep Casualties B and F deferred with basic self-care instructions until reinforcement nurses can assess them.
Require complete head-to-toe assessments and full routine charting on all casualties before starting treatment.

rationale_en: Appropriate MCI actions address immediate or near-immediate threats in salvageable casualties using proportionate resources: hemorrhage control and transfusion for C, airway/respiratory treatment for decontaminated D, protocol burn stabilization and transfer for E, and safe deferral of green casualties. CPR on black casualties and routine full assessment before treatment would divert scarce nurse-minutes from salvageable casualties.

options_or_rows_en.content:

Two additional nurses have been called in but are not yet present.

Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform copious water irrigation for the full facility-protocol duration, including eyes and exposed skin.
Initiate definitive airway and respiratory treatment with monitoring in the ED.
Don appropriate PPE before approaching the contaminated casualty.
Remove and bag contaminated clothing and personal items.
Move the casualty into the ED treatment area only after decontamination is complete.
Perform post-decontamination reassessment and re-triage.

rationale_en: PPE protects the nurse before contact. Clothing removal reduces ongoing contamination and prevents transfer. Irrigation completes the facility decontamination requirement, with eyes and skin addressed. Reassessment and re-triage confirm the current status before ED entry. Only then can the patient enter the treatment area for monitored oxygen, medications, and airway backup.

stem_en: At Stage 3, which findings indicate the disaster triage and resource allocation were effective? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline protocol and resources
options_or_rows_en.content:
Facility protocol: This 22-bed community ED is in Level 1 resource-constrained MCI mode when acute casualties exceed available treatment spaces. The decontamination station outside the ambulance entrance is functional.

Resources at 1854: three RNs, one emergency physician, one PA; one trauma bay and two resuscitation bays; two ventilators, with one already in use for an ICU-hold patient; four O-negative PRBC units immediately available; standard airway, albuterol, burn-care, and resuscitation supplies available. Two additional nurses have been called in but are not yet present.

Incident: Industrial explosion and anhydrous-ammonia release at a fertilizer-blending facility. Seven casualties arrive within eight minutes by ambulance and private vehicle.

options_or_rows_en.text: The salvageable red-category casualties, C and D, are alive and clinically improving after timely treatment.
Effectiveness is best shown by complete routine nursing charting for every casualty during the acute phase.
The green-category casualties, B and F, were safely deferred and then discharged without deterioration.
No staff or other casualties show evidence of secondary ammonia contamination inside the ED.
The response is effective only if all seven casualties survive.
The yellow-category burn casualty, E, remains stable and has an accepted burn-center transfer.

rationale_en: MCI effectiveness is judged by survival and stabilization of salvageable casualties, safe delay of minor casualties, successful transfer of serious delayed casualties, and preservation of staff/environmental safety. Two deaths do not by themselves show failure when those patients met expectant criteria. Routine full documentation is important after the acute phase but is not the key outcome metric during active disaster triage.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 1 findings that are significant cues of a time-critical neutropenic fever emergency.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and standing orders
options_or_rows_en.content: Diagnosis: stage IIIB diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Treatment: cycle 2 day 10 R-CHOP. Oncology note: expected count nadir window. Admission reason: poor oral intake and dehydration. Allergies: none known. Line: triple-lumen PICC, flushed and patent. Baseline admission vitals: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% room air. Standing order: for any single oral temperature ≥ 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or oral temperature ≥ 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) sustained over one hour, obtain blood cultures (two peripheral sets and one PICC lumen per local order) and notify oncology immediately.

options_or_rows_en.text: At 1415 the client has visible rigors and says she feels suddenly cold and shaky.
Oral temperature is 38.9 °C (102.0 °F).
HR is 104/min.
BP is 108/68 mm Hg.
Fingertips are cool and slightly mottled with capillary refill of 3 seconds.
Lung sounds are clear bilaterally.
The PICC insertion site is clean, dry, intact, and non-tender.
The left buccal mucosa has a small intact area of oral mucositis.
WBC is 0.6 × 10³/µL with ANC 180/µL during the expected chemotherapy nadir.
Lactate is 1.4 mmol/L.

rationale_en: The fever exceeds the standing single-reading threshold, and rigors, tachycardia, severe neutropenia during the nadir, and early peripheral vasoconstriction make this a time-critical emergency. Clear lungs, a normal-appearing PICC site, and normal lactate do not rule out serious infection in a neutropenic client.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 1 findings that are significant cues of a time-critical neutropenic fever emergency.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 clinical course
options_or_rows_en.content: After the bolus and broadened antibiotics: 1930 vitals T 38.6 °C, HR 106, BP 100/64, RR 20, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. More alert and conversational. Urine output 1800-2000: 90 mL. Lactate at 2100: 2.0 mmol/L. Capillary refill 3 seconds. 2200 vitals T 38.1 °C, HR 96, BP 106/68, RR 18, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L; urine output 2000-2200: 110 mL. Initial blood cultures pending. At 1030 next morning, microbiology reports a preliminary Gram-positive organism in the PICC lumen bottle at 20 hours, with speciation and sensitivities to follow. Oncology states the client will remain inpatient on IV antibiotics until afebrile for at least 48 hours and ANC shows early recovery. The nurse begins discharge-readiness teaching for future cycles.

Baseline admission vitals: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% room air. Standing order: for any single oral temperature ≥ 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or oral temperature ≥ 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) sustained over one hour, obtain blood cultures (two peripheral sets and one PICC lumen per local order) and notify oncology immediately.

options.text: after the antibiotic infusion is complete
only if a local source of infection is found
after culture results identify the organism
after the fever has persisted for another hour
neutropenic clients usually produce obvious localizing infection signs
antibiotics are unsafe until all imaging is completed
cultures preserve diagnostic yield while rapid empiric therapy limits progression

rationale_en: Cultures before antibiotics preserve the chance of identifying the organism, but this should take minutes, not delay treatment. The provider order pairs rapid cultures with prompt empiric antibiotics because the severely neutropenic client has little innate immune defense.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 clinical course
options_or_rows_en.content: After the bolus and broadened antibiotics: 1930 vitals T 38.6 °C, HR 106, BP 100/64, RR 20, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. More alert and conversational. Urine output 1800-2000: 90 mL. Lactate at 2100: 2.0 mmol/L. Capillary refill 3 seconds. 2200 vitals T 38.1 °C, HR 96, BP 106/68, RR 18, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L; urine output 2000-2200: 110 mL. Initial blood cultures pending. At 1030 next morning, microbiology reports a preliminary Gram-positive organism in the PICC lumen bottle at 20 hours, with speciation and sensitivities to follow. Oncology states the client will remain inpatient on IV antibiotics until afebrile for at least 48 hours and ANC shows early recovery. The nurse begins discharge-readiness teaching for future cycles.

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula to support tissue oxygenation during the perfusion crisis.
Reposition the client supine and start the ordered isotonic crystalloid bolus while reassessing perfusion and lung status.
Redraw blood cultures from new sites before the antibiotic regimen is broadened.
Activate the rapid response team and notify the oncologist with the BP drop, HR rise, urine-output decline, and lactate rise.

rationale_en: The Stage 2 findings show progression toward sepsis with hypotension, rising lactate, oliguria, tachycardia, and slower responses. Restoring perfusion and escalating the team come first; oxygen supports the oxygen-delivery problem, and repeat cultures should precede the antibiotic change without delaying resuscitation.

stem_en: Which nursing interventions are appropriate modifications for this severely neutropenic client? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and standing orders
options_or_rows_en.content: Diagnosis: stage IIIB diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Treatment: cycle 2 day 10 R-CHOP. Oncology note: expected count nadir window. Admission reason: poor oral intake and dehydration. Allergies: none known. Line: triple-lumen PICC, flushed and patent. Baseline admission vitals: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% room air. Standing order: for any single oral temperature ≥ 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or oral temperature ≥ 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) sustained over one hour, obtain blood cultures (two peripheral sets and one PICC lumen per local order) and notify oncology immediately.

options_or_rows_en.text: Allow fresh flowers if the water is changed daily and the vase is kept away from the bed.
Follow the facility low-microbial/food-safety protocol for food brought to the client.
Require hand hygiene before entry and restrict visitors who are ill.
Minimize unnecessary invasive procedures and use meticulous aseptic technique for every PICC access.
Use gentle oral care with a soft toothbrush and non-alcohol rinse for the mucositis site.
Avoid rectal temperatures, rectal medications, enemas, and rectal assessment unless specifically ordered for an overriding reason.

rationale_en: Severe neutropenia requires reducing microbial exposure and avoiding preventable mucosal or skin breaks. Rectal instrumentation is avoided, visitors and food are controlled by protocol, oral care should protect fragile mucosa, and all line access must be aseptic and necessary.

Standing order: for any single oral temperature ≥ 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or oral temperature ≥ 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) sustained over one hour, obtain blood cultures (two peripheral sets and one PICC lumen per local order) and notify oncology immediately.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature trend: 39.4 → 38.6 → 38.1 °C
Heart rate trend: 118 → 106 → 96/min
Blood pressure trend: 92/58 → 100/64 → 106/68 mm Hg
Urine output improves from 15 mL/hr to 45-55 mL/hr
Lactate decreases from 3.2 to 2.0 mmol/L
Mental status improves from slower responses to alert and conversational
Preliminary Gram-positive organism is reported in the PICC lumen bottle
Needs continued follow-up/communication
Supports improving response
Indicates continued deterioration

rationale_en: The overall trajectory is improving because perfusion and systemic response markers are reversing: lower temperature and heart rate, recovering blood pressure, better urine output, falling lactate, and improved alertness. The positive PICC-lumen culture is important and must be reported, but it does not erase the improving hemodynamic trend.

stem_en: Which discharge-readiness teaching should the nurse give about seeking emergency care during future chemotherapy nadir periods? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and standing orders
options_or_rows_en.content: Diagnosis: stage IIIB diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Treatment: cycle 2 day 10 R-CHOP. Oncology note: expected count nadir window. Admission reason: poor oral intake and dehydration. Allergies: none known. Line: triple-lumen PICC, flushed and patent. Baseline admission vitals: T 36.8 °C, HR 78, BP 118/72, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% room air. Standing order: for any single oral temperature ≥ 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or oral temperature ≥ 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) sustained over one hour, obtain blood cultures (two peripheral sets and one PICC lumen per local order) and notify oncology immediately.

options_or_rows_en.text: Take acetaminophen or ibuprofen first, then call the clinic the next morning if the fever returns.
Go for immediate evaluation for rigors, shaking chills, sudden lightheadedness, confusion, or rapid heartbeat even if a fever has not yet been confirmed.
Use an oral thermometer for temperature checks and do not take rectal temperatures.
If there are no localizing symptoms, wait unless the fever is above 39 °C (102.2 °F).
Seek emergency care immediately for any single oral temperature of 38.3 °C (101.0 °F) or higher.
Seek emergency care for oral temperature of 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) or higher that persists for one hour, per oncology instructions.

rationale_en: Teaching must be specific and action-oriented because fever during the nadir can be the only early sign of life-threatening infection. The client should use oral temperature thresholds from the oncology plan, avoid rectal measurements, avoid masking fever with antipyretics before evaluation, and seek care for systemic illness cues even before a temperature is confirmed.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 1 findings that require nursing safety follow-up. Do not highlight normal caregiver or developmental findings.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline client record
options_or_rows_en.content: Age: 9 months. Weight: 8.9 kg (about 19.6 lb). Length: 72 cm. Head circumference: 45 cm. Temperature 37.1 C tympanic, HR 128/min, RR 30/min, SpO2 99% on room air. Skin intact without bruises, burns, or unusual marks. Anterior fontanelle open and flat. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur; abdomen soft and non-tender. Liam sits independently, pulls to stand, transfers objects hand to hand, babbles consonant sounds, and shows stranger anxiety. ASQ-3 is within normal limits. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; lead level 2 mcg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Liam is chewing on car keys with a small decorative charm about 1.5 cm in diameter.
The diaper bag contains a propped bottle of whole cow's milk mixed with cereal.
An open ziplock bag of popcorn is visible in the diaper bag.
A small unlocked cosmetics pouch is accessible at the top of the diaper bag.
Danielle recently turned Liam's rear-facing infant car seat forward-facing because he screams when he cannot see her.
Danielle says Liam sleeps in her bed because he will not stay in the crib.
The apartment has an open staircase and no baby gate because Danielle thinks Liam has not figured out the stairs yet.
Cleaning supplies are kept under the kitchen sink without a cabinet lock.
Danielle offers Liam small pieces of hot dog and grapes as finger foods.
Danielle asks whether she can give diphenhydramine at night so Liam will sleep through.
Danielle makes eye contact, responds to Liam's vocalizations, and repositions him when he squirms.
Danielle asks several questions and appears genuinely interested in doing the right thing.
Liam turns toward Danielle and fusses when the nurse reaches for him.

rationale_en: The safety-relevant cues are the mouthing hazard, unsafe feeding practices, choking hazards, forward-facing car seat, bed-sharing, fall risk, poisoning risks, and proposed diphenhydramine use. Danielle's warmth, questions, and Liam's stranger anxiety are reassuring or developmentally expected and should not be treated as red flags.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 1 findings that require nursing safety follow-up. Do not highlight normal caregiver or developmental findings.

options_or_rows_en.title: Approximately 40 minutes into the visit
options_or_rows_en.content: After structured teaching, Danielle correctly states that Liam should remain rear-facing until he reaches the rear-facing height or weight limit for his specific seat, should sleep alone on his back in a crib with no loose bedding, and should not receive diphenhydramine for sleep. For acetaminophen, she says she will use the syringe that came with the medicine and give 5 mL. When asked about foods, she says she will cut hot dogs into small circles and does not mention grapes. For possible ingestion, she says she will call 911 but does not mention Poison Control. She says she will try to get gates and cabinet locks this weekend. The nurse provides written teaching, a Poison Control magnet, community resources, and a follow-up appointment in one month.

stem_en: Which interpretation best explains the Stage 1 pattern of unsafe infant-care practices?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline client record
options_or_rows_en.content: Age: 9 months. Weight: 8.9 kg (about 19.6 lb). Length: 72 cm. Head circumference: 45 cm. Temperature 37.1 C tympanic, HR 128/min, RR 30/min, SpO2 99% on room air. Skin intact without bruises, burns, or unusual marks. Anterior fontanelle open and flat. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur; abdomen soft and non-tender. Liam sits independently, pulls to stand, transfers objects hand to hand, babbles consonant sounds, and shows stranger anxiety. ASQ-3 is within normal limits. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; lead level 2 mcg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Caregiver knowledge deficit with reliance on informal advice
Intentional neglect requiring an immediate protective-services report based only on these facts
Infant developmental delay causing caregiver stress
Willful disregard because Danielle already knows the practices are dangerous

rationale_en: The pattern is most consistent with a knowledge deficit. Danielle relies on a neighbor, partner, and online videos, has no consistent pediatric medical home, openly discloses practices, asks questions, and shows affectionate engagement with Liam. The risks still require documentation, teaching, and follow-up, but the evidence does not support labeling the situation as intentional neglect based on the current facts alone.

stem_en: At Stage 2, Liam is actively pulling out the cosmetics pouch that contains an uncapped bottle of infant acetaminophen. Which risk requires the nurse's immediate intervention right now?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline client record
options_or_rows_en.content: Age: 9 months. Weight: 8.9 kg (about 19.6 lb). Length: 72 cm. Head circumference: 45 cm. Temperature 37.1 C tympanic, HR 128/min, RR 30/min, SpO2 99% on room air. Skin intact without bruises, burns, or unusual marks. Anterior fontanelle open and flat. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur; abdomen soft and non-tender. Liam sits independently, pulls to stand, transfers objects hand to hand, babbles consonant sounds, and shows stranger anxiety. ASQ-3 is within normal limits. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; lead level 2 mcg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Remove the accessible pouch containing uncapped acetaminophen from Liam's reach.
Call protective services immediately because the partner suggested diphenhydramine.
Focus first on stopping bed-sharing because safe sleep is a well-child priority.
Address whole cow's milk before 12 months before moving objects in the room.
Teach rear-facing car seat use first because vehicle crashes are a major cause of injury.

rationale_en: The accessible, uncapped medication is the immediate danger in the room. The nurse should remove it from reach before teaching, then assess and address the reported dosing error. Car seat, sleep, nutrition, home safety, and partner-related concerns matter, but they are not as immediate as a medication Liam could ingest within seconds.

options_or_rows_en.text: Connect the family with parenting support, safety-equipment resources, a medical home, and follow-up.
Acknowledge Danielle's distress and name her strengths before giving corrections.
Use demonstration, return demonstration, written instructions, and teach-back.
Avoid discussing safety risks today because Danielle is tearful.
Prioritize teaching by severity, starting with medication safety and choking hazards.
Correct every unsafe practice rapidly in one directive lecture so no topic is missed.

rationale_en: Danielle is emotionally overwhelmed but engaged. The nurse should use strengths-based, nonjudgmental teaching; prioritize the highest-severity risks; use demonstration and teach-back; and connect her with ongoing supports. A rapid lecture can overwhelm her, while avoiding safety teaching leaves Liam at risk.

stem_en: Danielle reports giving Liam about 1 tablespoon (15 mL) of 160 mg/5 mL acetaminophen when he has a fever. The clinic/package table in the record lists 5 mL for Liam's weight band. Which actions should the nurse take before the family leaves? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Approximately 40 minutes into the visit
options_or_rows_en.content: After structured teaching, Danielle correctly states that Liam should remain rear-facing until he reaches the rear-facing height or weight limit for his specific seat, should sleep alone on his back in a crib with no loose bedding, and should not receive diphenhydramine for sleep. For acetaminophen, she says she will use the syringe that came with the medicine and give 5 mL. When asked about foods, she says she will cut hot dogs into small circles and does not mention grapes. For possible ingestion, she says she will call 911 but does not mention Poison Control. She says she will try to get gates and cabinet locks this weekend. The nurse provides written teaching, a Poison Control magnet, community resources, and a follow-up appointment in one month.

options_or_rows_en.content: Age: 9 months. Weight: 8.9 kg (about 19.6 lb). Length: 72 cm. Head circumference: 45 cm. Temperature 37.1 C tympanic, HR 128/min, RR 30/min, SpO2 99% on room air. Skin intact without bruises, burns, or unusual marks. Anterior fontanelle open and flat. Lungs clear; heart regular without murmur; abdomen soft and non-tender. Liam sits independently, pulls to stand, transfers objects hand to hand, babbles consonant sounds, and shows stranger anxiety. ASQ-3 is within normal limits. Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL; hematocrit 33%; lead level 2 mcg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: States Liam should remain rear-facing until he reaches the rear-facing height or weight limit of the specific seat.
States Liam should sleep alone on his back in a crib without loose bedding.
States she should not give diphenhydramine to make Liam sleep.

Adequate understanding
Knowledge present; problem-solve resource barrier
Needs immediate re-teaching

rationale_en: Danielle accurately restates rear-facing car seat use, safe sleep, avoiding diphenhydramine for sleep, and the stated acetaminophen dose. Hot dogs cut into circles and unmentioned grapes require immediate re-teaching because the round/cylindrical shape remains dangerous. Calling 911 for every ingestion is incomplete; Poison Control should be used for a conscious, breathing child, while 911 is for severe symptoms such as unresponsiveness, breathing trouble, or seizure. Her wording about gates and locks suggests a resource/access barrier, not failure to understand the need.

stem_en: Stage 1: Which findings are part of the safety concern with the potassium order as written? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1900 baseline client record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 71-year-old woman on a medical-surgical unit, post-operative day 2 after open cholecystectomy complicated by bile leak requiring a surgical drain. History: hypertension, type 2 diabetes; stage 3b chronic kidney disease. Metformin is held perioperatively.
IV access/fluids: One 20-gauge peripheral IV in the left forearm; 0.9% normal saline infusing at 75 mL/hr. No central venous access.
Drain/diet: Surgical drain with about 200 mL bile-tinged output over the past 12 hr. Tolerating clear liquids; diet expected to advance tomorrow.
Unit electrolyte protocol: Before potassium replacement, verify most recent creatinine/eGFR. For peripheral IV potassium chloride, maximum concentration is 10 mEq per 100 mL and maximum rate is 10 mEq/hr. Central venous access is required for concentrations or rates above that threshold. Hold and clarify any potassium order that does not conform to protocol parameters.
1900 assessment: Alert and oriented; mild incisional soreness and fatigue; denies palpitations, muscle cramps, or weakness. T 37.1 C, HR 82 regular, BP 134/78, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air. Cardiac monitor: normal sinus rhythm without ectopy. Lungs clear. Abdomen soft, mildly tender near intact incision; drain functioning. Left forearm IV site without redness, swelling, tenderness, or infusion difficulty. No peripheral edema.
1600 labs: Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.1 mEq/L, Cl 101 mEq/L, HCO3 24 mEq/L, BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.3), eGFR 34 mL/min (baseline 42-44), Mg 1.9 mg/dL, phosphorus 3.4 mg/dL, glucose 142 mg/dL. CBC: WBC 9,200/uL, Hgb 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/uL. POD1 potassium was 3.8 mEq/L and creatinine was 1.4 mg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client asks when she can go home.
The cardiac monitor shows normal sinus rhythm without ectopy.
The creatinine is 1.6 mg/dL with eGFR 34 mL/min, worse than baseline.
The order is potassium chloride 40 mEq IV over 1 hour.
The order places 40 mEq potassium chloride in 100 mL normal saline.
The client has only a 20-gauge peripheral IV and no central venous access.

rationale_en: The unsafe pattern is the combination of excessive peripheral potassium rate, excessive concentration for the stated protocol, absence of central access, and worsened renal function. The client's stable rhythm and discharge question are real assessment findings, but they do not make the order safe or unsafe.

stem_en: Stage 1: Which findings are part of the safety concern with the potassium order as written? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Resolution and monitoring data
options_or_rows_en.content: The chief resident reviews the situation and enters a revised order: potassium chloride 10 mEq in 100 mL normal saline IV over 1 hour via the peripheral line, repeat for a total of four doses, with a serum potassium recheck after the second dose and a basic metabolic panel at 0600. The covering resident later calls back, acknowledges the protocol, and asks to be updated with the repeat result. The nurse administers the first dose at 2045. At 2245, after the second dose, a stat potassium drawn from the opposite arm returns at 3.4 mEq/L. The rhythm remains normal sinus without ectopy. The client denies IV site discomfort, palpitations, or muscle symptoms. The nurse continues the third dose per the revised order and documents the original order, protocol concern, escalation, revised order, doses administered, repeat potassium, and follow-up communication.

options_or_rows_en.text: A genuine patient-safety conflict based on a protocol discrepancy and renal-risk concern
A clinical-preference difference where either potassium administration approach is equally acceptable
A scope-of-practice conflict because the nurse is trying to prescribe medication
A personality clash caused mainly by the resident's irritated tone

rationale_en: The disagreement is driven by an objective safety issue: the order exceeds the stated peripheral potassium protocol and the client's renal function is worse than baseline. The resident's tone is a barrier, but it is not the root cause.

options_or_rows_en.text: Preserve the working relationship by avoiding further challenge to the resident
Protect the client from the unsafe rate and concentration while advocating for safe potassium replacement
Refuse all potassium replacement until the attending evaluates the client in person

rationale_en: The client needs potassium replacement, but the replacement must be delivered safely. The nurse's priority is not to choose between treatment and no treatment; it is to obtain a safe, protocol-consistent treatment plan.

options_or_rows_en.text: Request a modified order or an authorized policy-consistent override through the appropriate pathway.
Ask another nurse to hang the potassium so the assigned nurse is not the one administering it.
Withhold the potassium as written and escalate through the chain of command if the concern remains unresolved.

rationale_en: The correct pathway is graded assertive communication, pursuit of a safe revised or properly authorized order, withholding the unsafe order while escalating if needed, and factual documentation. Compliance or transferring the task to another nurse does not resolve the safety problem.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Marcus says, “Sometimes I think it would be easier if I just didn’t have to deal with any of this anymore.”
He clarifies, “I’m not going to hurt myself.”
He says recovery is exhausting and he feels “one bad day away from using again.”
Pain is 6/10 after ketorolac and acetaminophen.
HR and BP improved to HR 94 and BP 138/86.
He is tearful, fidgeting, and emotionally distressed.
He has not contacted Tanya or his sponsor.
He says he does not want to “bother” his support people.
He fears any fentanyl or opioid exposure will put him “right back where I started.”
Urine drug screen is positive for prescribed buprenorphine and negative for illicit substances.

rationale_en: The significant cues are the ambiguous “easier if I did not have to deal with this” statement, catastrophic relapse framing, tearful emotional distress, avoidance of Tanya and the sponsor, framing support contact as “bothering” others, and fear that opioid exposure will trigger relapse. The denial of self-harm is important but does not erase the need to monitor; improved vital signs, moderate pain, and a negative illicit UDS are not the primary psychosocial cues.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 1 findings that are significant psychosocial relapse-risk cues requiring nursing attention.

options_or_rows_en.title: Discharge readiness update
options_or_rows_en.content: The nerve block is beginning to wear off. Marcus reports left wrist pain increasing to 5/10. Vitals: HR 88, BP 132/82, RR 16, SpO2 99%. He took the evening buprenorphine/naloxone dose 30 minutes ago and has taken acetaminophen and ibuprofen as scheduled. He is restless and says, “This is exactly the kind of thing that made me use before — being in pain, feeling like crap, not being able to work. I know what would make this pain go away in five minutes.” He pauses and adds, “I’m not going to do it. But I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t thinking about it.” Tanya is present and alarmed. Marcus has not called his sponsor, is unsure he will attend tomorrow’s NA meeting because he cannot drive with the cast, and has not confirmed his MAT follow-up appointment. He asks whether ibuprofen will be enough, what to do if pain becomes severe overnight, and whether Tanya’s approximately 1-year-old naloxone kit still works.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Primary cardiovascular instability from persistent hypertension and tachycardia
Drug-seeking behavior because he is asking many questions about sedation
Active opioid withdrawal requiring withdrawal-protocol medication
Acute suicidal crisis requiring involuntary safety hold based only on the first statement
Acute relapse risk from converging pain, fear, shame, and self-isolation

rationale_en: The cluster best represents acute relapse risk: early recovery, prior relapse and overdose, acute fracture pain, fear of opioid exposure, emotional distress, and withdrawal from supports. There are no objective withdrawal signs; his anxiety is about avoiding opioids rather than obtaining them. He denied suicidal intent and plan, so the nurse monitors and reassesses rather than jumping to an involuntary hold.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which concern should the nurse prioritize?

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Call the MAT prescriber immediately because the buprenorphine plan has not been addressed.
Focus on Marcus’s escalating hopelessness, emotional distress, and isolation using a therapeutic interaction and safety reassessment.
Repeat the urine drug screen before speaking with Marcus to verify whether Tanya’s concern is true.
Complete medication discharge teaching first because pain control is the concrete discharge task.
Answer Tanya’s hallway question directly because she is frightened and has overdose history context.

rationale_en: Marcus has shifted from anxious but engaged to hopeless, ashamed, and isolated. That trajectory is the most immediate destabilizing concern because it can precede relapse or self-harm. Tanya’s fear should be validated without violating confidentiality, and she can be included in planning with Marcus’s consent. Medication teaching remains important, but teaching will be poorly retained if the patient is in emotional crisis. The MAT plan has already been clarified.
