stem_en: Which nursing responses are appropriate for Marcus’s Stage 2 statement, “What’s the point of staying clean if everyone still treats you like a junkie?” Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Offer concrete help contacting his sponsor or another recovery support before discharge.
Tell Marcus that Tanya reported the 2 AM car incident so he understands why she is worried.
Ask Marcus for permission to include Tanya in safety planning while keeping private information confidential.
Explore “what’s the point” and reassess for suicidal thoughts if his response suggests passive ideation.
Use reassurance such as, “You’re doing great; just stay positive and don’t think about using.”
Reflect the feeling without judgment: “It sounds like you feel hurt and exhausted by being distrusted.”

rationale_en: Therapeutic communication should validate the feeling without endorsing the distortion that recovery is pointless. The nurse should explore the phrase “what’s the point,” reassess safety if passive ideation is suggested, offer concrete support-contact help, and include Tanya only with Marcus’s consent. Disclosing Tanya’s hallway comments or using platitudes would worsen shame and damage trust.

stem_en: At Stage 3, which actions should the nurse include in discharge teaching and safety planning? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Teach scheduled acetaminophen and ibuprofen use, acetaminophen daily limit/duplicate-product safety, and avoiding NSAID overlap after ketorolac.
Reinforce the ordered buprenorphine/naloxone plan and explain that it supports OUD treatment and contributes to analgesia.
Thank him for disclosing craving and reinforce that cravings can occur without meaning relapse is inevitable.
Tell him to avoid returning to the ED for uncontrolled pain because any opioid discussion could threaten recovery.
Help him call his sponsor, confirm the MAT follow-up, and problem-solve transportation or remote attendance for the NA meeting before discharge.
Review naloxone with Marcus and Tanya: check the printed expiration date, replace expired kits, call 911, administer available naloxone in an overdose emergency, and review overdose signs.

rationale_en: The discharge plan must integrate relapse prevention, pain control, harm reduction, and concrete logistics. Marcus needs validation for honest craving disclosure, a clear scheduled multimodal pain plan, continuation of ordered buprenorphine/naloxone, activated recovery supports, confirmed follow-up, transportation planning, and naloxone/overdose teaching with Tanya. He should have a supervised escalation pathway if pain becomes uncontrolled; avoiding care would increase suffering and relapse risk.

stem_en: Classify each Stage 3 finding as supporting safe discharge with the current plan or requiring escalation before discharge.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: Marcus Delaney is a 38-year-old man with opioid use disorder (OUD) in recovery for about 5 months. He takes buprenorphine/naloxone 16 mg/4 mg sublingual daily through an outpatient medication-assisted treatment (MAT) program and attends weekly group counseling. He has two prior relapses; the most recent was 8 months ago and involved IV heroin use with a nonfatal overdose reversed by paramedics with naloxone. His recovery supports include a Narcotics Anonymous sponsor, his girlfriend Tanya, and his mother. Tanya has been trained to administer naloxone and keeps a kit at home.

options_or_rows_en.text: Marcus contacted his sponsor before leaving and has a confirmed MAT appointment within 48 hours.
He verbalizes the scheduled pain plan and knows whom to call or where to return if pain is uncontrolled.
Marcus and Tanya can describe opioid overdose signs and naloxone administration steps.
He denies suicidal ideation on reassessment and is engaged in planning for tonight and tomorrow.
Marcus says he intends to use heroin tonight if the pain becomes severe.
He refuses to contact any support, refuses follow-up, and says there is no reason to stay in recovery.
Tanya reports recent self-harm statements and access to a firearm at home.
On reassessment, he says, “I might be better off not waking up,” and cannot identify protective reasons to stay safe.
Escalate before discharge
Supports discharge plan

rationale_en: Safe discharge is supported when the safety net is active: recovery support contacted, MAT follow-up confirmed, pain and naloxone plans understood, suicidal ideation denied, and the patient is engaged and future-oriented. Escalation is required for intent to use, refusal of all supports/follow-up, loss of protective factors, new suicidal ideation, or collateral information suggesting self-harm risk or weapon access.

stem_en: At initial presentation, before imaging results are available, which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial emergency department record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 22-month-old boy brought by mother's boyfriend, who reports the child fell off a couch onto carpet about 1 hour ago and will not stop crying. The mother is at work and has not yet been contacted. The boyfriend reports being the primary daytime caregiver for about 3 months. No known bleeding disorder, metabolic bone disease, or significant past medical history. Immunizations are up to date per state registry. No prior CPS involvement is documented.

Orders placed: skeletal survey, CT head without contrast, BMP, CBC with differential, coagulation studies, AST/ALT, lipase, urinalysis, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, and peripheral IV.

Caregiver statements: The boyfriend says the child is "always a crybaby" and "does this for attention." He reports no loss of consciousness, vomiting, or seizure activity and initially declines to call the mother.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue pain assessment and neurovascular checks of the injured left arm while avoiding unnecessary manipulation.
Tell the boyfriend that the bruises prove child abuse and that he caused the injuries.
Communicate to the ED physician that the patterned bruising and locations are inconsistent with the reported short fall.
Delay documentation until the skeletal survey confirms whether fractures are present.
Document each bruise objectively, including size, shape, color, and exact anatomic location, using a body map if available.
Accept the caregiver's explanation because toddlers commonly fall and bruise during play.

rationale_en: The initial bruising pattern is a safety and reporting cue even before imaging. Objective documentation, prompt communication to the provider, and ongoing pain/neurovascular assessment protect the child and preserve a clear record without assigning legal guilt.

stem_en: At initial presentation, before imaging results are available, which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Child abuse team evaluation and discharge planning
options_or_rows_en.content:
The child abuse pediatrician documents multiple injuries in various stages of healing, a developmentally inconsistent injury pattern, and a history inconsistent with the injury mechanism. The findings are described as highly concerning for inflicted injury/nonaccidental trauma. A follow-up skeletal survey is ordered for 2 weeks per child abuse team protocol to identify additional occult fractures not visible on initial imaging.

The mother is at the bedside and asks, "What's going to happen to my son? Will they take him away from me?" She is tearful and says she feels guilty for not protecting him. The child is resting comfortably. HR 110, BP 90/54, RR 22, SpO2 99%, FLACC score 2. He is taking oral fluids and small amounts of food.

CPS informs nursing staff that a temporary safety plan is established: the boyfriend is not permitted to have unsupervised contact with the child, and the mother has agreed to a safety plan that includes a temporary protective order. CPS asks the nurse to document the child's behavioral responses to each visitor and to notify CPS immediately if the boyfriend attempts to visit. Discharge planning is initiated with the social worker, CPS, and the mother.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue pain assessment and neurovascular checks of the injured left arm while avoiding unnecessary manipulation.
Tell the boyfriend that the bruises prove child abuse and that he caused the injuries.
Communicate to the ED physician that the patterned bruising and locations are inconsistent with the reported short fall.
Delay documentation until the skeletal survey confirms whether fractures are present.
Document each bruise objectively, including size, shape, color, and exact anatomic location, using a body map if available.
Accept the caregiver's explanation because toddlers commonly fall and bruise during play.

rationale_en: The initial bruising pattern is a safety and reporting cue even before imaging. Objective documentation, prompt communication to the provider, and ongoing pain/neurovascular assessment protect the child and preserve a clear record without assigning legal guilt.

options_or_rows_en.text: Healing posterior left 8th rib fracture with callus formation
Healing classic metaphyseal lesion of the right distal tibia
Multiple fractures in different stages of healing with vague, shifting explanations
Negative CT head for intracranial hemorrhage or skull fracture
Normal PT/INR/PTT and normal serum calcium
Does not explain away the concern or justify delaying the report
Supports reasonable suspicion and need for safety/reporting actions

rationale_en: Posterior rib fracture, classic metaphyseal lesion, multiple injuries in different healing stages, and inconsistent explanations together support reasonable suspicion and safety/reporting actions. A negative head CT and normal coagulation/calcium results do not erase the concern created by the skeletal findings and history.

stem_en: During Stage 2, the mother discloses fear of the boyfriend and asks the nurse not to tell him what she said. Which nursing responses are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial emergency department record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 22-month-old boy brought by mother's boyfriend, who reports the child fell off a couch onto carpet about 1 hour ago and will not stop crying. The mother is at work and has not yet been contacted. The boyfriend reports being the primary daytime caregiver for about 3 months. No known bleeding disorder, metabolic bone disease, or significant past medical history. Immunizations are up to date per state registry. No prior CPS involvement is documented.

Orders placed: skeletal survey, CT head without contrast, BMP, CBC with differential, coagulation studies, AST/ALT, lipase, urinalysis, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, and peripheral IV.

Caregiver statements: The boyfriend says the child is "always a crybaby" and "does this for attention." He reports no loss of consciousness, vomiting, or seizure activity and initially declines to call the mother.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the boyfriend what the mother disclosed so he can respond to the allegation directly.
Explain that the team will protect her confidentiality to the greatest extent possible and will not share her specific statements with the boyfriend.
Acknowledge her fear and validate that it was important to disclose safety concerns.
Offer domestic violence resources and continue safety-focused, nonjudgmental support.
Advise the mother to confront the boyfriend in the hospital while security is nearby.
Ensure the social worker and CPS investigator know about the domestic violence concern so the safety plan addresses both the child and the mother.

rationale_en: The mother may be a protective caregiver and a victim at risk. The nurse should respond therapeutically, protect confidentiality from the alleged perpetrator as much as possible, involve the appropriate safety-planning team, and provide resources. Confrontation or disclosure to the boyfriend can increase danger.

options_or_rows_en.title: Child abuse team evaluation and discharge planning
options_or_rows_en.content:
The child abuse pediatrician documents multiple injuries in various stages of healing, a developmentally inconsistent injury pattern, and a history inconsistent with the injury mechanism. The findings are described as highly concerning for inflicted injury/nonaccidental trauma. A follow-up skeletal survey is ordered for 2 weeks per child abuse team protocol to identify additional occult fractures not visible on initial imaging.

The mother is at the bedside and asks, "What's going to happen to my son? Will they take him away from me?" She is tearful and says she feels guilty for not protecting him. The child is resting comfortably. HR 110, BP 90/54, RR 22, SpO2 99%, FLACC score 2. He is taking oral fluids and small amounts of food.

CPS informs nursing staff that a temporary safety plan is established: the boyfriend is not permitted to have unsupervised contact with the child, and the mother has agreed to a safety plan that includes a temporary protective order. CPS asks the nurse to document the child's behavioral responses to each visitor and to notify CPS immediately if the boyfriend attempts to visit. Discharge planning is initiated with the social worker, CPS, and the mother.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tell the boyfriend what the mother disclosed so he can respond to the allegation directly.
Explain that the team will protect her confidentiality to the greatest extent possible and will not share her specific statements with the boyfriend.
Acknowledge her fear and validate that it was important to disclose safety concerns.
Offer domestic violence resources and continue safety-focused, nonjudgmental support.
Advise the mother to confront the boyfriend in the hospital while security is nearby.
Ensure the social worker and CPS investigator know about the domestic violence concern so the safety plan addresses both the child and the mother.

rationale_en: The mother may be a protective caregiver and a victim at risk. The nurse should respond therapeutically, protect confidentiality from the alleged perpetrator as much as possible, involve the appropriate safety-planning team, and provide resources. Confrontation or disclosure to the boyfriend can increase danger.

stem_en: Complete the statement describing the nurse's appropriate response to the agitated caregiver.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial emergency department record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 22-month-old boy brought by mother's boyfriend, who reports the child fell off a couch onto carpet about 1 hour ago and will not stop crying. The mother is at work and has not yet been contacted. The boyfriend reports being the primary daytime caregiver for about 3 months. No known bleeding disorder, metabolic bone disease, or significant past medical history. Immunizations are up to date per state registry. No prior CPS involvement is documented.

Orders placed: skeletal survey, CT head without contrast, BMP, CBC with differential, coagulation studies, AST/ALT, lipase, urinalysis, continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring, and peripheral IV.

Caregiver statements: The boyfriend says the child is "always a crybaby" and "does this for attention." He reports no loss of consciousness, vomiting, or seizure activity and initially declines to call the mother.

options.text: reasonable suspicion and a legal reporting obligation, not a hospital determination of guilt
the mother's permission to involve protective services
definitive proof that the caregiver intentionally injured the child
asking security for assistance if behavior threatens safety
debating guilt, confronting him with accusations, or attempting a forensic interview
documenting his statements verbatim in the medical record

rationale_en: Mandatory reporting is triggered by reasonable suspicion and safety concerns; it is not the nurse's role to prove abuse, determine guilt, or conduct an interrogation. The nurse should remain calm, explain the duty to report, document objectively, and preserve safety.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial emergency department record
options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Teach the mother the need for the follow-up skeletal survey in 2 weeks and orthopedic follow-up for the humeral fracture.
Give honest, supportive information that CPS's goal is child safety and reinforce the mother's protective actions.
Limit discharge teaching to splint care because CPS is responsible for all safety and psychosocial issues.
Document the mother's emotional state, her statements, and the child's behavioral responses to visitors.
Coordinate with social work and CPS so community resources and the safety plan are confirmed before discharge.
Reassure the mother that CPS will not remove the child as long as she cooperates with the plan.

rationale_en: Discharge planning must address the medical injury, scheduled reevaluation for occult fractures, psychosocial support, objective documentation, and the safety plan. The nurse should support the mother without making promises about CPS decisions and should not narrow teaching to orthopedic care alone.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the active MAR and sees loperamide 2 mg PO PRN for diarrhea. Which action is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Give loperamide only after the stool specimen is collected so the medication does not interfere with testing.
Withhold the loperamide and notify the provider that the order should be discontinued or held for suspected CDI.
Administer loperamide and increase oral fluids before contacting the provider.
Request diphenoxylate-atropine instead because it is stronger than loperamide for severe diarrhea.
Administer loperamide now because reducing stool frequency will prevent dehydration and skin injury.

rationale_en: The client has suspected CDI with fever, leukocytosis, abdominal distension, recent high-risk antibiotics, and frequent watery stools. Antimotility agents can worsen toxin retention and may contribute to ileus or toxic megacolon, so the nurse should hold loperamide and notify the provider.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the active MAR and sees loperamide 2 mg PO PRN for diarrhea. Which action is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: Hospital days 17–18 discharge planning
options_or_rows_en.content: After three full days of oral vancomycin, she has two to three semi-formed stools per day, no cramping, improving appetite, and no fever for 48 hours. Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 76, BP 126/74, RR 14, SpO₂ 98% on room air. Labs: WBC 11,200/µL, potassium 4.0 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, BUN 18 mg/dL, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, lactate 0.8 mmol/L. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended. Discharge plan: complete a 10-day oral vancomycin course with 6 days remaining at home, continue prednisone, keep methotrexate held pending rheumatology follow-up, home PT, and no repeat C. difficile test if symptoms remain resolved.

stem_en: Which nursing actions are appropriate during the first hour of care? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Delay notifying environmental services until the client has a negative follow-up stool test.
Use alcohol-based hand rub as the preferred method because gloves were worn during care.
Initiate contact precautions while CDI is suspected rather than waiting for a confirmed result.
Send an unformed stool specimen for C. difficile testing per facility protocol once ordered.
Perform hand hygiene with soap and water after removing gloves and before leaving the room.
Place a dedicated commode and dedicated assessment equipment in the room.

rationale_en: Suspected CDI requires early contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, appropriate testing of unformed stool, and dedicated equipment to reduce environmental spread. Alcohol-based hand rub is not the preferred exit hand hygiene for CDI, and environmental cleaning should not be delayed.

stem_en: Complete the teaching statement about why the prescribed vancomycin route is appropriate.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

rationale_en: CDI is a luminal colonic infection. Oral vancomycin is minimally absorbed, allowing high concentrations to remain in the intestinal lumen. IV vancomycin produces systemic levels but does not reliably deliver therapeutic drug into the colonic lumen.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. options_or_rows_en.text: Stool frequency decreases gradually while the abdomen is softer and vital signs are stable.
Diarrhea abruptly stops while abdominal distension increases and bowel sounds become absent.
WBC decreases from 21,400/µL to 18,600/µL with improving temperature and blood pressure.
New abdominal rigidity, guarding, and rebound tenderness develop.
Blood pressure falls to 86/50, heart rate rises to 118, and lactate increases to 3.0 mmol/L.
Stools become semi-formed, creatinine returns to baseline, and abdominal pain resolves.
Immediate escalation
Expected improvement

rationale_en: Improvement is gradual and paired with softer abdomen, better vital signs, improving labs, and more formed stool. Sudden cessation of diarrhea with worsening distension, peritoneal signs, hypotension, tachycardia, or rising lactate suggests ileus, toxic megacolon, perforation risk, or shock and requires immediate escalation.

options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. options_or_rows_en.text: Initiate contact precautions, soap-and-water hand hygiene, and dedicated equipment while CDI is suspected.

Collect and send an unformed stool specimen as ordered using appropriate precautions, then administer ordered fluids, potassium replacement, and oral vancomycin; continue stool counts, intake/output, abdominal checks, and skin protection.
Perform a focused assessment and recognize the CDI risk pattern from new watery diarrhea, fever, leukocytosis, recent antibiotics, immunosuppression, and abdominal distension.
Notify the provider using SBAR and request stool testing, fluids, electrolyte replacement, and CDI-directed therapy.
Hold the PRN loperamide and flag the order for provider review.

rationale_en: The nurse first recognizes the risk pattern and assesses severity. Because CDI may spread before confirmation, infection-control measures begin immediately. The unsafe PRN antimotility medication is held before provider communication. SBAR then obtains orders for testing and treatment; the stool specimen is collected as ordered before or alongside therapy, and ordered fluids, electrolytes, oral vancomycin, monitoring, and skin protection follow.

stem_en: Which discharge teaching statement by the nurse is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Hospital day 14. Age 74. Recent hospital-acquired pneumonia caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa treated with piperacillin-tazobactam for 10 days and IV levofloxacin for 7 days; antibiotics completed within the past week. Rheumatoid arthritis is managed with methotrexate 15 mg weekly and prednisone 7.5 mg daily for 3 years. Home omeprazole was continued after IV pantoprazole during the acute pneumonia phase. Baseline mild CKD: creatinine 1.1 mg/dL, eGFR 54 mL/min. No drug allergies. Previously independent, now deconditioned and using a rolling walker with standby assistance.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use hand sanitizer after toileting because it is less irritating than frequent soap-and-water handwashing.
Restart methotrexate at home as soon as the diarrhea improves so rheumatoid arthritis does not flare.
You do not need a repeat C. difficile test if symptoms have resolved. Finish the oral vancomycin course and call the provider if watery diarrhea, fever, or worsening abdominal pain returns.
A repeat PCR must be negative before you can safely stop contact precautions at home.
Stop oral vancomycin when stools become formed for 24 hours because taking extra doses increases resistance.

rationale_en: Resolution is judged clinically. Repeat C. difficile testing is not recommended when symptoms have resolved because colonization or residual genetic material may persist. The client should complete the prescribed oral vancomycin course, use soap-and-water hand hygiene after toileting, follow the plan for holding methotrexate, and report recurrent watery diarrhea, fever, or worsening abdominal pain.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are relevant to planning the nurse's communication approach for this visit.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history
options_or_rows_en.content:
52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: During intake, the nurse notes:
Maria left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."
Newest Vital Sign score is 1 out of 6.
She reads the pamphlet slowly, holds it at arm's length, and sets it down.
She fidgets with her purse strap and avoids eye contact.
She says a previous provider used words she did not understand and she just nodded along.
Blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg.
Non-fasting fingerstick glucose is 118 mg/dL.
There is mild right supraspinatus tenderness without reduced range of motion.

rationale_en: Blank forms, a very low health-literacy score, difficulty using the pamphlet, discomfort behaviors, and a statement about nodding along when she did not understand all directly shape the communication plan. They support plain language, pacing, and teach-back. Normal vital signs, a single non-fasting glucose of 118 mg/dL, and mild shoulder tenderness are baseline findings but do not drive the immediate communication strategy.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are relevant to planning the nurse's communication approach for this visit.

options_or_rows_en.title: Follow-up results and second teach-back
options_or_rows_en.content:
Maria reports that the financial counselor confirmed the colonoscopy will be fully covered as a screening procedure. Colonoscopy is scheduled for the following month when her daughter can stay with her mother. Fasting labs: glucose 104 mg/dL, total cholesterol 212 mg/dL, LDL 134 mg/dL, HDL 52 mg/dL, triglycerides 130 mg/dL. Provider plans lifestyle counseling and no medication today. Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 72, BP 122/76, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air. The nurse administers the first recombinant zoster vaccine dose in the right deltoid, schedules Pap with HPV co-testing for the same day as the colonoscopy-prep teaching visit, and schedules the second zoster dose 2-6 months later.
Teach-back: Maria explains that colonoscopy uses a camera to look for intestinal growths before cancer develops and that she is doing it because her mother had colon cancer. options_or_rows_en.text: During intake, the nurse notes:
Maria left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."
Newest Vital Sign score is 1 out of 6.
She reads the pamphlet slowly, holds it at arm's length, and sets it down.
She fidgets with her purse strap and avoids eye contact.
She says a previous provider used words she did not understand and she just nodded along.
Blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg.
Non-fasting fingerstick glucose is 118 mg/dL.
There is mild right supraspinatus tenderness without reduced range of motion.

rationale_en: Blank forms, a very low health-literacy score, difficulty using the pamphlet, discomfort behaviors, and a statement about nodding along when she did not understand all directly shape the communication plan. They support plain language, pacing, and teach-back. Normal vital signs, a single non-fasting glucose of 118 mg/dL, and mild shoulder tenderness are baseline findings but do not drive the immediate communication strategy.

stem_en: Based on the clinic preventive-care protocol and the Stage 1 data, which overdue screening is the highest priority for the nurse to address first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history

52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: Schedule colorectal cancer screening with colonoscopy.
Schedule cervical cancer screening with Pap/HPV co-testing.
Complete fasting glucose and lipid testing before any cancer screening is scheduled.
Schedule screening mammography.
Administer the influenza vaccine today.

rationale_en: Maria's mother is a first-degree relative diagnosed with colorectal cancer before age 60. Under the clinic protocol, Maria should have begun colonoscopy at age 40 or 10 years before her mother's diagnosis, whichever was earlier; age 40 is earlier than age 48. At age 52 with no prior colonoscopy, she is 12 years beyond the risk-stratified start point. Cervical and breast screening are also important, and influenza vaccine can be completed today, but colonoscopy is the highest-priority screening risk to address.

stem_en: When colonoscopy is mentioned, Maria says she does not want to know if she has cancer and cannot take a day off work. She crosses her arms and looks toward the door. Which response by the nurse is best first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history
options_or_rows_en.content:
52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: "Colonoscopy is very safe, and survival is much better when cancer is found early."
"Your health has to come first, even if taking time off is hard."
"I will give you the scheduling number so you can book the procedure when you are ready."
"You have no symptoms, so you should not worry about colon cancer right now."
"Your mother's experience sounds frightening, and losing work time is a real concern. Let's talk through both before we decide next steps."

rationale_en: Maria is showing fear and a defensive posture. The nurse should first acknowledge the emotional barrier and validate the practical work barrier without judgment. This builds trust and makes later education possible. Facts, reassurance, or scheduling instructions given before the emotion is addressed are less likely to be heard and may increase avoidance.

stem_en: Classify each part of Maria's Stage 2 teach-back as either adequate understanding or needing targeted reteaching/follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history
options_or_rows_en.content:
52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: She correctly connects the Pap smear, mammogram, and colonoscopy with the appropriate body areas and recognizes the family-history link for colonoscopy.
She says each cancer screening is needed because she might already have cancer.
She states that the influenza vaccine is to help prevent influenza.
She omits the recombinant zoster vaccine from the plan.
She says her blood sugar is fine, so she does not need to worry about anything else.
Needs targeted reteaching/follow-up
Adequate understanding

rationale_en: Teach-back is partially correct. Maria can match the screenings to the correct body areas and understands the family-history reason for colonoscopy, and she understands the influenza vaccine. However, she still equates screening with suspected cancer, omits the zoster vaccine, and incorrectly concludes that no other follow-up is needed because the non-fasting glucose was not alarming. These gaps require focused reteaching rather than repeating the entire visit from the beginning.

stem_en: Which nursing actions best address Maria's barriers while sequencing the remaining preventive-care plan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history
options_or_rows_en.content:
52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: Offer fasting labs through a walk-in lab or other option that fits her work schedule.
Schedule the colonoscopy, Pap/HPV test, vaccines, and labs on separate days so each topic can be discussed in isolation.
Use plain-language teaching and teach-back at each encounter, including vaccine and follow-up instructions.
Help Maria identify caregiver coverage for her mother on appointment or procedure days.
Consolidate appointments when possible, such as pairing Pap/HPV testing with the colonoscopy-prep teaching visit.
Connect Maria with the financial counselor before colonoscopy scheduling is finalized.

rationale_en: Maria's barriers are practical and communication-related: rotating work, caregiver duties, cost concerns, and limited health literacy. The plan should reduce visit burden, address insurance questions before scheduling, make labs feasible, arrange caregiver coverage, and preserve plain-language teach-back. Scheduling everything on separate days increases the number of days off and caregiver arrangements required, which undermines follow-through.

options_or_rows_en.title: Client profile and history
options_or_rows_en.content:
52-year-old woman; new-patient primary care visit. No primary care for about 6 years. Works full-time as a hotel housekeeper on rotating shifts, including weekends. Primary caregiver for 78-year-old mother.
Medication: ibuprofen as needed for work-related shoulder pain. No known drug allergies.
Preventive history: Pap smear about 10 years ago; never had a mammogram or colonoscopy; uncertain vaccination history; possible tetanus shot 5-6 years ago; no recalled shingles vaccine.
Family history: mother diagnosed with colon cancer at age 58; father died of myocardial infarction at age 71; sister age 56 healthy.
Insurance/social barriers: employer-sponsored high-deductible plan; concerned about cost. Speaks English fluently and completed high school, but left several intake sections blank and wrote, "not sure what this means."

options_or_rows_en.text: It is incomplete because she must be taught to call the clinic promptly for new symptoms, then repeat teach-back.
It indicates the nurse should request FIT instead of colonoscopy because Maria remains anxious.
It indicates she needs detailed cancer survival statistics to motivate faster follow-through.
It indicates she should cancel the screening colonoscopy and wait until symptoms develop.
It is adequate because she understands the purpose of the colonoscopy and has already scheduled it.

rationale_en: Maria understands the purpose of screening but has a critical safety gap: she would wait to report symptoms that could require earlier diagnostic evaluation. The nurse should correct the misunderstanding calmly, explain that new symptoms change the situation from routine screening to diagnostic follow-up, and repeat teach-back to confirm understanding.

a warning that refusal means cancer is likely
all possible guidelines without checking understanding

Schedule follow-up colonoscopy and explain that a positive screening test needs diagnostic evaluation.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's therapeutic response.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home health visit
options_or_rows_en.content: A 68-year-old client with Parkinson disease lives with a spouse who provides most daily care. The client needs help bathing, dressing, and transferring but can feed self slowly. During a home visit, the spouse says, 'I am awake most nights. I have not seen my friends in months. Yesterday I yelled when he froze in the doorway. I hate that I did that.' The spouse denies wanting to harm the client but says, 'I am afraid I will lose control if nothing changes.'

options.text: focus only on the client's motor symptoms
tell the spouse that yelling is unacceptable and move on
acknowledge the spouse's exhaustion without judgment
assess immediate safety and arrange concrete support options
ask the daughter to take over all care immediately
advise the spouse to wait until the next routine visit

rationale_en: The response should validate distress, assess safety, and move toward specific supports such as respite or shared care.

Yesterday I yelled when he froze in the doorway. I hate that I did that.' The spouse denies wanting to harm the client but says, 'I am afraid I will lose control if nothing changes.'

Client can feed self slowly
Spouse has not seen friends in months
Spouse denies intent to harm the client
Preserved client ability or protective cue
Caregiver strain cue

options_or_rows_en.title: Diabetes self-management visit
options_or_rows_en.content: A 59-year-old client with type 2 diabetes attends a nurse-led self-management visit. A1c is 8.8%. The client works nights, eats one large meal after the shift, and says, 'I know what I should do, but the plan never fits my life.' The client checks blood glucose irregularly, has decreased sensation in both feet, and has no open wounds. The client asks whether a daughter can join education sessions by video.

options.text: a list of complications to scare the client
a perfect daily schedule starting tomorrow
one or two realistic behavior goals

rationale_en: Self-management plans are more effective when goals are realistic and linked to the client's actual routine.

options_or_rows_en.title: Diabetes self-management visit
options_or_rows_en.content: A 59-year-old client with type 2 diabetes attends a nurse-led self-management visit. A1c is 8.8%. The client works nights, eats one large meal after the shift, and says, 'I know what I should do, but the plan never fits my life.' The client checks blood glucose irregularly, has decreased sensation in both feet, and has no open wounds. The client asks whether a daughter can join education sessions by video.

options_or_rows_en.text: I should walk only on days when my blood glucose is exactly normal.
My daughter should decide which goals I follow because she is helping me.
If my feet looked fine last week, I can skip checks when I am tired.
I will inspect my feet every day and report any open area, blister, or redness that does not go away.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's interpretation of the day-3 finding.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation admission
options_or_rows_en.content: A 54-year-old client is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation after an incomplete spinal cord injury. The client uses a wheelchair for most mobility and reports decreased sensation over the sacrum and heels. Braden score is 13. Skin is intact on admission except for blanchable redness on the coccyx after sitting for 3 hours. The client says, 'I do not feel pain there, so I thought it was fine.' Appetite is fair, and occasional urinary leakage is reported.

options.text: offload pressure and update the prevention plan
massage the reddened area to improve circulation
continue the same sitting schedule because the skin is intact

rationale_en: Intact, nonblanchable redness over a pressure point is consistent with stage 1 pressure injury and requires pressure relief, not massage.

options.text: offload pressure and update the prevention plan
massage the reddened area to improve circulation
continue the same sitting schedule because the skin is intact

stem_en: Complete the nurse's teaching strategy.

options_or_rows_en.title: Primary care follow-up
options_or_rows_en.content: A 62-year-old client with newly diagnosed hypothyroidism returns for follow-up after 6 weeks of treatment. The client brings three medicine bottles in a plastic bag and says, 'I take the thyroid pill when I remember. The label has too many words.' The client reads at an elementary level, uses a basic cell phone, and prefers Spanish but understands some English. The client says breakfast is at 6 AM before work and asks, 'Can you just tell my nephew what to do?'

options.text: a complex chart of thyroid physiology
the full pharmacy label read aloud once in English
a plain-language picture schedule in the client's preferred language
teach-back using the client's own words

rationale_en: Plain-language, language-concordant visual teaching plus teach-back addresses health literacy and confirms practical understanding.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's teaching strategy.

options_or_rows_en.content: At a nurse call 4 weeks later, the client says, 'The picture schedule helped. I take the thyroid pill on the table when I wake up, then I eat later at work. I missed two doses when the refill ran out.' TSH is improved but still above goal. The client gives permission for the nephew to help set up refills but wants to speak directly with the nurse about health decisions.

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-Health Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 74-year-old client with moderate dementia lives with the spouse in a two-story home. The spouse says, "He wakes up at night looking for his mother and tries to leave the house. Yesterday I yelled and held his wrists to stop him. I am scared I will lose control." The spouse reports sleeping 3 to 4 hours per night and has not attended a caregiver support group.

options.text: sleep deprivation and escalating wandering behavior
lack of interest in the client's diagnosis
teaching the spouse to physically block exits
delaying referrals until bruising is observed

rationale_en: The spouse describes exhaustion, fear of losing control, and unsafe escalation while managing nighttime wandering. Respite and safety planning are the priority because they address both caregiver strain and immediate risk.

stem_en: The spouse says, "I feel like a terrible person. I cannot keep doing this." Which response by the nurse is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Home-Health Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 74-year-old client with moderate dementia lives with the spouse in a two-story home. The spouse says, "He wakes up at night looking for his mother and tries to leave the house. Yesterday I yelled and held his wrists to stop him. I am scared I will lose control." The spouse reports sleeping 3 to 4 hours per night and has not attended a caregiver support group.

options_or_rows_en.text: "Most families go through this, so try not to feel guilty."
"You are exhausted and trying to keep him safe. Let's talk about what happened and what support is needed today."
"You must promise that you will never grab him again, or he may need to be placed immediately."
"Because there are no bruises, we can focus on medication reminders instead."

rationale_en: The best response validates distress without excusing unsafe behavior, invites assessment, and moves toward immediate support and safety planning.

stem_en: Complete the statement about the priority teaching need.

options_or_rows_en.title: Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 58-year-old client arrives for follow-up 2 weeks after starting glipizide in addition to metformin. The client says, 'My wife reads the bottles for me. I take the small white pill when I feel shaky because I think it lowers the sugar faster.' The client reports skipping breakfast on workdays and keeping medications in a pillbox without labels. The spouse says, 'I am afraid to leave him alone because he does not know what to do if his sugar drops.'

rationale_en: Shakiness with low fasting readings indicates hypoglycemia risk. The immediate teaching priority is to use fast-acting carbohydrate rather than taking more glucose-lowering medication.

options_or_rows_en.content: The provider asks the nurse to reinforce medication safety, assess barriers to meal consistency, and identify whether the spouse needs additional support. The clinic has a diabetes educator, dietitian, social worker, and bilingual printed teaching materials.

The nurse notes bruising on the upper arm in different stages of healing. The client is alert, oriented, and reports pain 5/10. Distal pulse, movement, and sensation are intact.

options.text: objective documentation of the injury assessment
asking whether the client has a safe contact
sending domestic violence resources through the portal or printing obvious instructions
a discreet safety card and neutral follow-up wording
a voicemail on the shared phone
a confrontation with the partner before discharge

rationale_en: When communication is monitored, routine electronic messages or obvious printed resources can increase danger. A discreet resource and neutral follow-up plan better protect confidentiality while preserving access to help.

stem_en: Which response by the nurse is most therapeutic after the client says, "I'm not ready to leave"?

options_or_rows_en.title: Triage Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 34-year-old client arrives with right wrist pain after "falling at home." The partner answers most questions, keeps a hand on the client's shoulder, and says, "We are in a hurry. Just wrap it." The client avoids eye contact. The nurse notes bruising on the upper arm in different stages of healing. The client is alert, oriented, and reports pain 5/10. Distal pulse, movement, and sensation are intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I will bring your partner in so we can hear both sides of the story."
"You are the expert on your safety. I am concerned about what happened, and we can talk about options that do not require you to decide today."
"You should promise me you will call the police as soon as you get home."
"If you do not leave now, there is nothing useful we can do for you today."

rationale_en: The therapeutic response validates autonomy, expresses concern, and offers options without pressuring the client to leave immediately. Pressuring, blaming, or involving the partner can increase danger and reduce trust.

options_or_rows_en.content: A 34-year-old client arrives with right wrist pain after "falling at home." The partner answers most questions, keeps a hand on the client's shoulder, and says, "We are in a hurry. Just wrap it." The client avoids eye contact. The nurse notes bruising on the upper arm in different stages of healing. The client is alert, oriented, and reports pain 5/10. Distal pulse, movement, and sensation are intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assess immediate danger, suicidal thoughts, weapons, and whether the client has a safe place or contact.
Maintain privacy and ensure the partner does not re-enter the room during the conversation.
Offer private access to the social worker or domestic violence advocate.
Validate the disclosure and tell the client the abuse is not the client's fault.
Create a discreet discharge and follow-up plan that avoids unsafe portal messages or obvious printed materials.

rationale_en: The nurse first preserves privacy, then assesses immediate safety risks, validates the client, offers specialized support, and creates a discreet follow-up plan that does not increase danger after discharge.

stem_en: Complete the exposure-management statement.

options_or_rows_en.title: Incident Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A nurse sustains a puncture wound to the left index finger while clearing a tray after giving an adult vaccine. The safety device on the used needle had not been activated. The source client has left the injection area but registration has the client's name and phone number. The nurse says, "It is barely bleeding. I will finish the line first."

options.text: wash the puncture site with soap and water
squeeze the finger until bleeding stops
contact employee health for risk assessment
discard all documentation to protect the source client's privacy
wait until symptoms develop before reporting

rationale_en: A needlestick is a percutaneous exposure. The nurse should perform immediate wound care and promptly contact employee health for risk assessment and recommendations.

stem_en: Complete the exposure-management statement.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic Observation
options_or_rows_en.content: The sharps container at one station is above the fill line. Staff have been carrying used syringes on trays to a shared container because two stations do not have point-of-use sharps containers. One staff member says some needles are recapped when the safety device is hard to activate quickly. The exposed nurse completed a hepatitis B vaccine series years ago but does not know whether a post-vaccination titer was documented.

options_or_rows_en.title: Incident Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A nurse sustains a puncture wound to the left index finger while clearing a tray after giving an adult vaccine. The safety device on the used needle had not been activated. The source client has left the injection area but registration has the client's name and phone number. The nurse says, "It is barely bleeding. I will finish the line first."

options_or_rows_en.text: Place an approved sharps container at each injection station.
Replace sharps containers before they exceed the fill line.
Require staff to recap needles before carrying them to the shared container.
Teach staff to activate the safety device immediately after use as designed.
Use nonpunitive reporting to identify workflow barriers and near misses.

rationale_en: Point-of-use disposal, timely replacement of full containers, correct safety-device activation, and nonpunitive reporting address system causes of sharps injuries. Recapping and storing used syringes on trays increase risk.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinic Intake Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 52-year-old client comes to a community clinic for a school volunteer clearance form. The client says, "I feel fine, so I do not understand why I need all these tests. I cannot miss work for a colonoscopy." The client has not had a primary care visit in 7 years. The client reports no colorectal cancer screening, no mammogram in 8 years, no shingles vaccine, and last tetanus booster more than 10 years ago. The client receives influenza vaccine only in some years.

options.text: within the routine adult screening age range
too young for any colorectal cancer screening

rationale_en: A 52-year-old adult who has not been screened should be offered colorectal cancer screening. Stool-based testing may reduce access barriers, but a positive stool-based result needs colonoscopy follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask what the client already knows and what worries the client most about screening and vaccines.
Schedule referrals or appointments and document barriers that still need follow-up.
Prioritize one or two overdue preventive actions that the client is willing to complete first.
Ask the client to teach back the selected plan, including when and where each step will happen.
Use large-print, plain-language materials to explain options, benefits, and next steps.

rationale_en: The nurse first assesses understanding and concerns, then gives accessible information, helps the client choose a realistic starting plan, verifies understanding with teach-back, and finally schedules and documents follow-up needs.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use teach-back to confirm how the client will complete the selected screening steps.
Offer a stool-based colorectal cancer screening kit with picture-based instructions.

stem_en: Complete the prevention plan statement.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 76-year-old client is postoperative day 5 after open reduction and internal fixation of a left hip fracture. The client can stand with assistance and a walker but tires quickly. The client says, 'I avoid drinking because I do not want to wet the bed.' The adult daughter says, 'I work nights and I am scared Mom will fall if she tries to go to the bathroom alone.'

options.text: limited mobility and moisture from urinary leakage
keeping the head of bed elevated at all times
limiting oral fluids until urinary leakage stops
scheduled repositioning, moisture management, and pressure redistribution

rationale_en: Limited mobility, moisture, and early sacral color changes place the client at risk for pressure injury. Prevention should combine repositioning, moisture control, and pressure redistribution rather than fluid restriction or constant head elevation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Team Plan
options_or_rows_en.content: Physical therapy will evaluate gait endurance this afternoon. Occupational therapy is available for bathroom equipment training. The provider prescribed scheduled stool softener and as-needed laxative. The unit has a toileting schedule protocol and pressure-injury prevention bundle.

options_or_rows_en.content: A 76-year-old client is postoperative day 5 after open reduction and internal fixation of a left hip fracture. rationale_en: Eight ounces equals 240 mL, and 0.5 L equals 500 mL. Total intake is 600 + 240 + 500 = 1340 mL.

stem_en: The client needs to transfer from bed to a chair with a walker. Place the nursing actions in the best order.

options_or_rows_en.title: Rehabilitation Nurse's Note
options_or_rows_en.content: A 76-year-old client is postoperative day 5 after open reduction and internal fixation of a left hip fracture. The client can stand with assistance and a walker but tires quickly. The client says, 'I avoid drinking because I do not want to wet the bed.' The adult daughter says, 'I work nights and I am scared Mom will fall if she tries to go to the bathroom alone.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Apply nonskid footwear and a gait belt.
Assist the client to sit at the bedside and assess dizziness before standing.
Ensure the call light and needed items are within reach after the client sits.
Confirm the chair is locked, close to the bed, and positioned on the client's stronger side when possible.
Cue the client to push from the bed, stand, grasp the walker, and pivot toward the chair.

rationale_en: The nurse first prepares the environment, then prepares the client, assesses tolerance in sitting, assists the stand-pivot transfer safely, and finally restores access to the call light and needed items.

stem_en: Using the baseline and Stage 1 data, highlight the findings that require nursing attention now.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record: evening of surgery, 1900
options_or_rows_en.content: 82-year-old woman after right hip hemiarthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fracture. Lives alone; recently eating less, lost about 7 lb, and naps frequently. History includes osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, GERD, mild cognitive impairment, chronic constipation, and well-controlled hypertension. Orders include posterior hip precautions, PT evaluation on postoperative day 1, incentive spirometry q2h while awake, SCDs in bed, enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous daily per facility VTE protocol, clear liquids advancing as tolerated, and Foley removal on postoperative day 1 per CAUTI bundle. Braden score 14: moderate risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Coccygeal skin is intact with a 2 × 3 cm non-blanchable erythematous area that is warm to touch.
The perineum is moist with mild erythema in the gluteal fold.
Pain is 7/10 at rest and she says she is afraid to move because everything hurts.
After IV hydromorphone, she is more sedated, drifts off mid-sentence, RR is 12/min, and SpO2 is 94% on 2 L nasal cannula.
She has not used the incentive spirometer since arrival on the unit.
Her daughter reports recent unintentional weight loss of about 7 lb and poor intake.
Mucous membranes are dry and she has taken only two sips of ginger ale.
Albumin is 2.8 g/dL and prealbumin is 14 mg/dL.
Mild edema is present in the right foot and ankle, with warmth, intact sensation, and a palpable 2+ pedal pulse.
Bowel sounds are hypoactive after surgery.
Temperature is 36.8 °C.
Hemoglobin decreased from 11.8 g/dL preoperatively to 10.4 g/dL postoperatively.
Urine output is 350 mL over about 5 hours since surgery.

rationale_en: The actionable cues show an existing Stage 1 coccygeal pressure injury, moisture-associated skin risk, pain-driven immobility, opioid-related sedation/respiratory monitoring needs, pulmonary hygiene risk, and nutrition/fluid-risk markers. The mild foot edema with intact neurovascular status, hypoactive bowel sounds, normal temperature, stable postoperative hemoglobin change, and adequate urine output are expected or less urgent.

options_or_rows_en.content: 82-year-old woman after right hip hemiarthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fracture. Lives alone; recently eating less, lost about 7 lb, and naps frequently. History includes osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, GERD, mild cognitive impairment, chronic constipation, and well-controlled hypertension. Orders include posterior hip precautions, PT evaluation on postoperative day 1, incentive spirometry q2h while awake, SCDs in bed, enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous daily per facility VTE protocol, clear liquids advancing as tolerated, and Foley removal on postoperative day 1 per CAUTI bundle. Braden score 14: moderate risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: High and escalating risk for progressive pressure injury, with an existing Stage 1 coccygeal injury and compounding moisture, nutrition, immobility, and shear risks.
Expected postoperative redness only; no pressure injury is present because the coccygeal skin surface is intact.
Surgical-site infection, because low albumin and poor intake indicate the incision is infected.
Postoperative hemorrhage as the unifying problem, because hemoglobin decreased after surgery.
Deep vein thrombosis, because postoperative immobility and mild right foot swelling explain the skin findings.

rationale_en: The findings indicate escalating pressure-injury risk with tissue damage already present. Sustained pressure from immobility, moisture, nutrition risk, and friction/shear converge to reduce tissue tolerance. Low albumin and prealbumin reinforce adverse outcome risk but are not direct diagnostic proof of malnutrition.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which interventions should the nurse include in the integrated care plan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record: evening of surgery, 1900
options_or_rows_en.content: 82-year-old woman after right hip hemiarthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fracture. Lives alone; recently eating less, lost about 7 lb, and naps frequently. History includes osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, GERD, mild cognitive impairment, chronic constipation, and well-controlled hypertension. Orders include posterior hip precautions, PT evaluation on postoperative day 1, incentive spirometry q2h while awake, SCDs in bed, enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneous daily per facility VTE protocol, clear liquids advancing as tolerated, and Foley removal on postoperative day 1 per CAUTI bundle. Braden score 14: moderate risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue q2h repositioning, maintain the pressure-redistribution overlay, keep linens dry and smooth, apply barrier cream after moisture exposure, and verify heel suspension.
Before standing, dangle at the bedside, rise slowly, monitor symptoms/vital signs, use gait belt and assistive device, and keep the call light within reach.
Request dietary consultation, offer small frequent high-protein supplements, track intake/output, and encourage preferred foods.
Use prompted voiding every 2–3 hours with the raised bedside commode to prevent urgency-driven unsafe transfers.
Premedicate 20–30 minutes before physical therapy or transfers and use pillows, ordered ice, distraction, and hearing aids to improve participation.
Keep the head of bed above 45 degrees whenever she is supine to reduce aspiration and improve breathing.

rationale_en: The plan must address interdependent risks: pain limiting mobility, orthostatic fall risk, poor intake, pressure and moisture exposure, and urgency-driven unsafe toileting. Elevating the head of bed too high while supine can worsen sacral shear.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline record: evening of surgery, 1900
options_or_rows_en.content: 82-year-old woman after right hip hemiarthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fracture. Lives alone; recently eating less, lost about 7 lb, and naps frequently. Braden score 14: moderate risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Notify the provider or wound-care pathway per facility protocol and add the heel to q2h skin checks.
Document size, exact location, color, warmth, and intact skin, and identify the finding as a new Stage 1 device-related pressure injury.
Check the opposite heel and teach the nursing assistant to verify device position at every repositioning.
Massage the reddened heel, tighten the strap so the boot will not shift, and continue routine monitoring.
Correct offloading by reapplying a properly fitted boot with no strap pressure or by using pillow suspension under the calf to fully float the heel.
Remove the boot and inspect the entire right heel and surrounding skin.

rationale_en: The boot strap caused a new Stage 1 device-related pressure injury. The nurse must remove and inspect the device site, document and stage the injury, correct offloading, notify per protocol, check the opposite heel, and communicate device-position checks to assistive staff.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial surgical-unit record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 62-year-old woman, post-operative after open Roux-en-Y revision for chronic gastrojejunal anastomotic stricture. Negligible oral/enteral intake for approximately 5 weeks. Weight 46 kg; BMI 17.1; 11-kg weight loss over 2 months. Type 2 diabetes; metformin held perioperatively. No alcohol use.

Current nutrition orders: PPN via 20-gauge peripheral IV at 40 mL/hr, started in PACU. PICC placement planned the next morning for central TPN. Nutrition consult: high risk for refeeding syndrome; initiate TPN at no more than 10 kcal/kg/day and advance slowly over 5-7 days. Thiamine 200 mg IV daily for 5-7 days ordered; first dose given before dextrose-containing PPN. Monitor BMP plus magnesium and phosphorus every 12 hours for 48 hours, then daily.

Unit electrolyte replacement protocol: KCl 20 mEq IV over 1 hr for K 3.0-3.4 mEq/L; KCl 40 mEq IV over 2 hr for K < 3.0 mEq/L. Sodium phosphate 15 mmol IV over 2 hr for phosphorus 1.5-2.0 mg/dL; sodium phosphate 30 mmol IV over 4 hr for phosphorus < 1.5 mg/dL. Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 2 hr for Mg < 1.5 mg/dL.

Initial assessment at start of night shift: drowsy but arousable; oriented to person, place, and time. Mild incisional pain 3/10 and mild nausea. No oral intake. PPN infusing freely through left forearm peripheral IV without redness, swelling, or tenderness. Foley catheter draining clear yellow urine. Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 92, BP 108/64, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air. Thin appearance with temporal wasting and dry mucous membranes. Midline dressing clean/dry/intact; JP drain with 40 mL serosanguineous output. Bowel sounds absent. No edema; neurologic exam grossly intact; DTRs 2+ and symmetric. Point-of-care glucose 142 mg/dL. Foley output over first 8 post-operative hours: 280 mL (about 35 mL/hr).

PACU labs 6 hours earlier: Na 138, K 3.5, Cl 102, bicarbonate 24, BUN 18, creatinine 0.8, glucose 156, calcium 8.4, phosphorus 2.8, magnesium 1.8, albumin 2.1, prealbumin 8, WBC 9,200, Hgb 11.0, platelets 210,000. AST 28, ALT 22, total bilirubin 0.6.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stage 1 findings:
Phosphorus decreased from 2.8 to 1.8 mg/dL.
Potassium decreased from 3.5 to 3.1 mEq/L.
Magnesium decreased from 1.8 to 1.4 mg/dL.
Point-of-care glucose is 204 mg/dL, increased from the earlier glucose of 156 mg/dL.
Heart rate is 104/min, increased from 92/min.
The client reports shakiness and mild shortness of breath.
New generalized weakness, paresthesias, and increasing fatigue are present.
Urine output declined from about 35 mL/hr to 25 mL/hr.
Temperature is 37.1 C.
The PICC dressing is clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or drainage.

rationale_en: The significant cue cluster is the simultaneous decline in phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium after PN initiation, plus rising glucose, tachycardia, neuromuscular symptoms, mild dyspnea, and lower urine output. Together these findings suggest evolving refeeding syndrome and early organ stress. The stable temperature and benign PICC site are not priority cues at Stage 1.

stem_en: At Stage 1, highlight the findings that are significant cues requiring nursing attention.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response after rate reduction, aggressive replacement, and infection workup
options_or_rows_en.content:
At Stage 2, the provider was notified urgently of continued electrolyte decline despite repletion, cardiac rhythm changes, respiratory compromise, and PICC-site findings. Orders: reduce TPN rate by 50% without abrupt discontinuation; administer higher-tier electrolyte repletion (KCl 40 mEq IV over 2 hr, sodium phosphate 30 mmol IV over 4 hr, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 2 hr with repeat magnesium in 4 hr if still < 1.5); obtain blood cultures from the PICC and a peripheral site before vancomycin 1 g IV; obtain a 12-lead ECG; increase glucose monitoring to every 4 hours with insulin coverage.

After interventions, repeat labs: potassium 3.4 mEq/L, phosphorus 1.9 mg/dL, magnesium 1.6 mg/dL, glucose 168 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Vital signs: T 37.6 C, HR 96, BP 110/66, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm without PVCs; QT interval has shortened. The client is more alert, oriented to person and place, and can lift her arms above her shoulders. PICC-site erythema has not expanded; cultures are pending. TPN continues at the reduced rate. Nutrition plans to hold the reduced rate for 48 hours, then advance by 5 kcal/kg/day increments with electrolytes every 12 hours.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stage 1 findings:
Phosphorus decreased from 2.8 to 1.8 mg/dL.
Potassium decreased from 3.5 to 3.1 mEq/L.
Magnesium decreased from 1.8 to 1.4 mg/dL.
Point-of-care glucose is 204 mg/dL, increased from the earlier glucose of 156 mg/dL.
Heart rate is 104/min, increased from 92/min.
The client reports shakiness and mild shortness of breath.
New generalized weakness, paresthesias, and increasing fatigue are present.
Urine output declined from about 35 mL/hr to 25 mL/hr.
Temperature is 37.1 C.
The PICC dressing is clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or drainage.

rationale_en: The significant cue cluster is the simultaneous decline in phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium after PN initiation, plus rising glucose, tachycardia, neuromuscular symptoms, mild dyspnea, and lower urine output. Together these findings suggest evolving refeeding syndrome and early organ stress. The stable temperature and benign PICC site are not priority cues at Stage 1.

options.text: insulin-mediated intracellular shifting of phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium
renal wasting from acute kidney injury
the primary cause of the falling phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium
evidence that infection is already the dominant problem
a concurrent PN complication that does not explain the electrolyte triad

rationale_en: The Stage 1 pattern is best analyzed as refeeding syndrome: severe malnutrition followed by carbohydrate-containing PN, then simultaneous declines in phosphorus, potassium, and magnesium within the expected early window. Hyperglycemia is common during PN and needs treatment, but it does not explain the electrolyte triad. A central-line infection is unlikely at Stage 1 because there is no fever and the PICC site is benign.

stem_en: At Stage 1, use the client's history, new laboratory pattern, and glucose trend to identify the most likely clinical problem.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response after rate reduction, aggressive replacement, and infection workup
options_or_rows_en.content:
At Stage 2, the provider was notified urgently of continued electrolyte decline despite repletion, cardiac rhythm changes, respiratory compromise, and PICC-site findings. Orders: reduce TPN rate by 50% without abrupt discontinuation; administer higher-tier electrolyte repletion (KCl 40 mEq IV over 2 hr, sodium phosphate 30 mmol IV over 4 hr, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 2 hr with repeat magnesium in 4 hr if still < 1.5); obtain blood cultures from the PICC and a peripheral site before vancomycin 1 g IV; obtain a 12-lead ECG; increase glucose monitoring to every 4 hours with insulin coverage.

After interventions, repeat labs: potassium 3.4 mEq/L, phosphorus 1.9 mg/dL, magnesium 1.6 mg/dL, glucose 168 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. Vital signs: T 37.6 C, HR 96, BP 110/66, RR 18, SpO2 97% on room air. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm without PVCs; QT interval has shortened. The client is more alert, oriented to person and place, and can lift her arms above her shoulders. PICC-site erythema has not expanded; cultures are pending. TPN continues at the reduced rate. Nutrition plans to hold the reduced rate for 48 hours, then advance by 5 kcal/kg/day increments with electrolytes every 12 hours.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which problem is the most immediately life-threatening and requires priority intervention?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial surgical-unit record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 62-year-old woman, post-operative after open Roux-en-Y revision for chronic gastrojejunal anastomotic stricture. Negligible oral/enteral intake for approximately 5 weeks. Weight 46 kg; BMI 17.1; 11-kg weight loss over 2 months. Type 2 diabetes; metformin held perioperatively. No alcohol use.

Current nutrition orders: PPN via 20-gauge peripheral IV at 40 mL/hr, started in PACU. PICC placement planned the next morning for central TPN. Nutrition consult: high risk for refeeding syndrome; initiate TPN at no more than 10 kcal/kg/day and advance slowly over 5-7 days. Thiamine 200 mg IV daily for 5-7 days ordered; first dose given before dextrose-containing PPN. Monitor BMP plus magnesium and phosphorus every 12 hours for 48 hours, then daily.

Unit electrolyte replacement protocol: KCl 20 mEq IV over 1 hr for K 3.0-3.4 mEq/L; KCl 40 mEq IV over 2 hr for K < 3.0 mEq/L. Sodium phosphate 15 mmol IV over 2 hr for phosphorus 1.5-2.0 mg/dL; sodium phosphate 30 mmol IV over 4 hr for phosphorus < 1.5 mg/dL. Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 2 hr for Mg < 1.5 mg/dL.

Initial assessment at start of night shift: drowsy but arousable; oriented to person, place, and time. Mild incisional pain 3/10 and mild nausea. No oral intake. PPN infusing freely through left forearm peripheral IV without redness, swelling, or tenderness. Foley catheter draining clear yellow urine. Vital signs: T 36.8 C, HR 92, BP 108/64, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air. Thin appearance with temporal wasting and dry mucous membranes. Midline dressing clean/dry/intact; JP drain with 40 mL serosanguineous output. Bowel sounds absent. No edema; neurologic exam grossly intact; DTRs 2+ and symmetric. Point-of-care glucose 142 mg/dL. Foley output over first 8 post-operative hours: 280 mL (about 35 mL/hr).

PACU labs 6 hours earlier: Na 138, K 3.5, Cl 102, bicarbonate 24, BUN 18, creatinine 0.8, glucose 156, calcium 8.4, phosphorus 2.8, magnesium 1.8, albumin 2.1, prealbumin 8, WBC 9,200, Hgb 11.0, platelets 210,000. AST 28, ALT 22, total bilirubin 0.6.

options_or_rows_en.text: Post-operative fever requiring routine antipyretic administration before other actions
Parenteral-nutrition hyperglycemia with glucose 226 mg/dL
Electrolyte-driven cardiac and respiratory compromise from worsening refeeding syndrome
Suspected PICC-related bloodstream infection from erythema and purulent drainage

rationale_en: The immediate threat is the worsening electrolyte derangement with active organ effects: phosphorus 1.3 mg/dL, potassium 2.9 mEq/L, magnesium 1.3 mg/dL, frequent PVCs, prolonged QT, shallow respirations, weakness, and altered mental status. These findings carry near-term risk of lethal dysrhythmia and respiratory failure. The PICC site must be cultured and treated, but it does not explain the established electrolyte triad or current rhythm instability.
