options_or_rows_en.text: Draw blood cultures from the PICC and a peripheral site before starting the first ordered vancomycin dose.
Treat the glucose of 226 mg/dL as the first priority with aggressive insulin before correcting potassium and magnesium.
Stop the TPN immediately without a provider order or dextrose/glucose-monitoring backup plan.
Administer higher-tier potassium, phosphate, and magnesium replacement with continuous cardiac monitoring as ordered.
Anticipate reducing the TPN rate as ordered rather than abruptly stopping it independently.
Notify the provider urgently about the continued electrolyte decline, rhythm changes, respiratory findings, and PICC-site drainage.

rationale_en: The nurse should urgently escalate the worsening refeeding syndrome, rhythm changes, respiratory compromise, and suspected line infection. Anticipated management is to reduce caloric delivery rather than abruptly stop the high-dextrose infusion independently, replace potassium/phosphate/magnesium aggressively with monitoring, and obtain paired cultures before vancomycin. Hyperglycemia still needs q4h checks and insulin coverage, but aggressive insulin as the first priority could worsen hypokalemia.

Begin prescribed higher-tier potassium, phosphate, and magnesium repletion with telemetry.

stem_en: Highlight the admission and Stage 1 findings that are significant cues of impaired venous return through the superior vena cava.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Background: 58-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and non-small cell lung cancer with a large right mediastinal mass. No history of DVT or heart failure. Orders include normal saline at 75 mL/hr, oxygen by nasal cannula to maintain SpO2 above 92%, head of bed at least 45 degrees, twice-daily weights, strict intake and output, and oncology consult. A right forearm peripheral IV is present.

Presentation and assessment: progressive facial puffiness for two weeks, worse in the morning; dyspnea on exertion; shirt collars feel tight; wedding ring still fits. Alert and oriented, speaking in full sentences, mild respiratory effort. Facial plethora and bilateral periorbital edema. Bilateral external jugular veins are distended and do not collapse with inspiration or with the head of bed at 45 degrees. Faint dilated superficial venous collaterals are present across the anterior chest wall. No lower-extremity edema. Hands and fingers are not edematous. Lung sounds are diminished in the right upper lobe with no wheezes, crackles, or stridor. Neurologic assessment is normal. Neck circumference at the cricoid cartilage is 44 cm.

Vital signs/labs: T 37.1 °C, HR 92/min, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 20/min, SpO2 94% on 2 L nasal cannula. WBC 11,200/µL, Hgb 13.8 g/dL, platelets 268,000/µL, Na 139 mEq/L, K 4.2 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, INR 1.0, PTT 28 seconds, D-dimer 0.8 µg/mL. ABG on 2 L nasal cannula: pH 7.42, PaCO2 36 mmHg, PaO2 72 mmHg, HCO3 23 mEq/L, SaO2 94%.

options_or_rows_en.text: Facial plethora
Bilateral periorbital edema
Bilateral external jugular veins that do not collapse with inspiration or 45-degree head elevation
Faint dilated superficial venous collaterals across the anterior chest wall
Worse head fullness and desaturation when the head of bed is lowered
Sluggish flow through a right forearm IV
Diminished breath sounds in the right upper lobe with a known right mediastinal mass
No lower-extremity edema
Hands and fingers are non-edematous at admission
Normal neurological findings and no headache
Temperature 37.1 °C
Capillary refill under 2 seconds bilaterally

rationale_en: The key pattern is congestion above the diaphragm: facial plethora, periorbital edema, non-collapsing jugular venous distention, chest-wall venous collaterals, position-dependent worsening, impaired upper-extremity IV flow, and findings consistent with the known mediastinal mass. Normal lower extremities and stable nonspecific findings do not rule out SVC obstruction.

stem_en: Highlight the admission and Stage 1 findings that are significant cues of impaired venous return through the superior vena cava.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 response to escalation
options_or_rows_en.content: The nurse escalated to the provider, oxygen was increased to high-flow nasal cannula at 40 L/min with FiO2 0.50 per order, and the head of bed remains at 45 degrees. Oncology recommends urgent treatment; dexamethasone 4 mg IV every 6 hours is ordered and the first dose is administered. Interventional radiology is consulted for possible SVC stenting. At hour 24: SpO2 93% on high-flow nasal cannula, RR 24/min, HR 102/min, BP 130/80 mmHg. The client again speaks in short-to-moderate phrases. Stridor is no longer audible over the trachea. Neck circumference is 46 cm, down from 47 cm but still above the 44 cm baseline. Facial edema remains but is slightly less tense. Upper-extremity edema is unchanged. Urine output over the preceding 6 hours is 320 mL. The client feels a little better but is still short of breath with movement. A new peripheral IV has been placed in the left lower extremity after coordination with the team and per facility policy.

Bilateral external jugular veins that do not collapse with inspiration or 45-degree head elevation
Faint dilated superficial venous collaterals across the anterior chest wall
Worse head fullness and desaturation when the head of bed is lowered

Diminished breath sounds in the right upper lobe with a known right mediastinal mass

Normal neurological findings and no headache
Temperature 37.1 °C
Capillary refill under 2 seconds bilaterally

Normal lower extremities and stable nonspecific findings do not rule out SVC obstruction.

stem_en: At Stage 1, which condition best explains the client’s cue cluster: upper-body venous congestion sparing the lower extremities, worsening when the head of bed is lowered, known right mediastinal mass, chest-wall venous collaterals, and a mildly elevated D-dimer?

options_or_rows_en.text: Lower the head of bed to facilitate a full chest assessment.
Confirm the head of bed remains at 45 degrees or higher.
Activate rapid response or the facility emergency escalation process and notify the provider of new stridor.
Increase oxygen delivery using available respiratory distress equipment or protocol.
Bring emergency airway equipment, suction, bag-valve-mask, and oxygen escalation supplies to the bedside.
Attempt a new left forearm IV before calling for help.

rationale_en: The client has new stridor with worsening hypoxemia and upper-body edema, so immediate nursing priorities are oxygenation, upright positioning, emergency escalation/provider notification, and readiness for airway deterioration. Vascular access is important after the airway response is underway, but an upper-extremity attempt before calling help delays the priority response and is likely to fail.

options_or_rows_en.text: Coordinate non–upper-extremity peripheral access per facility policy and provider direction.
Place a new right antecubital IV because it is closer to central circulation.
Request an upper-extremity PICC line as the fastest reliable access.
Place a peripheral IV in the left hand because the right forearm IV failed.

rationale_en: Upper-extremity venous return drains through the obstructed SVC, so either arm can have high back-pressure, unreliable flow, and infiltration risk. Non–upper-extremity access, such as lower-extremity access when allowed by policy and ordered/arranged by the team, drains through the IVC and is more appropriate for this anatomy.

SpO2 is 93%, but only on high-flow nasal cannula at 40 L/min and FiO2 0.50.

Supports early improvement

rationale_en: The absent stridor is the strongest early improvement because it suggests less airway narrowing. However, the client still requires much higher oxygen support, has persistent neck edema above baseline, and remains dyspneic with movement, so the obstruction is not resolved. Urine output is useful for general perfusion but does not track the primary SVC threat.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 2 findings that are significant new cues compared with the pre-transfusion baseline.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and transfusion orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: chronic kidney disease stage 3b with baseline eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m2; compensated heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, EF 52%, grade I diastolic dysfunction; type 2 diabetes. Home medications include furosemide 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime.

Reason for admission: progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea on one flight of stairs, lightheadedness, outpatient hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. No prior transfusion reactions.

Orders/protocol: two units packed red blood cells sequentially; each unit over 2 hours, not to exceed 4 hours per unit. Vital signs at baseline, 15 minutes after initiation, every 30 minutes during infusion, and at completion. Furosemide 20 mg IV between units if signs of fluid overload occur. Blood product verification by two RNs: client identity, ABO/Rh, unit number, crossmatch information, and expiration date.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client reports acute shortness of breath and cannot get comfortable.
Temperature is 37.2 C, compared with 36.8 C before transfusion.
Heart rate is 110/min, increased from 92/min.
Blood pressure is 168/96 mm Hg, increased from 138/82 mm Hg.
Respiratory rate is 28/min, increased from 16/min.
SpO2 is 89% on room air, decreased from 96%.
Bilateral crackles now extend from the bases to the mid-lung fields.
Faint bibasilar crackles were present at baseline.
A new S3 gallop is audible.
JVD is visible at 45 degrees.
Lower-extremity edema has increased from trace to 2+ bilateral pitting.
The client's face is flushed without urticaria, hives, angioedema, or wheezing.
Urine output is 15 mL over the past 45 minutes.
The client has a new nonproductive cough.

rationale_en: The significant new cues are acute dyspnea, tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnea, oxygen desaturation, crackles extending beyond baseline, new S3, new JVD, worsening edema, oliguria, and new cough. The small temperature change and isolated flushing without allergic features are not the main cue cluster.

stem_en: Highlight the Stage 2 findings that are significant new cues compared with the pre-transfusion baseline.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response to continued treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: A second dose of furosemide 20 mg IV was given 2 hours after the first dose for continued but improving fluid overload. Current vital signs: T 36.9 C, HR 84, BP 136/78, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 4 L nasal cannula after weaning from the non-rebreather. The client is resting comfortably at 45 degrees and says she can breathe much more easily. Lung sounds have returned to faint bibasilar crackles only, similar to admission baseline. JVD is no longer visible. Edema has returned to trace bilaterally. S3 is no longer audible. Total urine output since the initial furosemide dose: 1,400 mL over 4 hours. Repeat BNP: 420 pg/mL. Provider documentation: transfusion-associated circulatory overload; hold remaining packed red blood cells pending reassessment; repeat hemoglobin in 6 hours; consult nephrology for future transfusion planning such as slower rate, pre-transfusion diuretic, and single-unit strategy. Transfusion reaction report completed per policy.

Home medications include furosemide 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime.

options.text: angioedema, wheezing, urticaria, hypotension, and throat tightness
hypertension with JVD, new S3, worsening edema, pulmonary crackles, oliguria, and high-risk cardiac/renal history
hypoxemia with bilateral infiltrates but no evidence of circulatory overload
fever and chills without respiratory compromise

rationale_en: The client has acute respiratory distress during transfusion, but the differentiating pattern is circulatory overload: hypertension, JVD, S3, worsening edema, decreased urine output, crackles, older age, CKD, heart failure history, and low albumin. TRALI remains a competing respiratory transfusion reaction, but the presence of multiple volume-overload signs points away from isolated noncardiogenic lung injury.

stem_en: Which actions belong in the management plan for this suspected TACO episode? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and transfusion orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: chronic kidney disease stage 3b with baseline eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m2; compensated heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, EF 52%, grade I diastolic dysfunction; type 2 diabetes. Home medications include furosemide 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime.

Reason for admission: progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea on one flight of stairs, lightheadedness, outpatient hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. No prior transfusion reactions.

Orders/protocol: two units packed red blood cells sequentially; each unit over 2 hours, not to exceed 4 hours per unit. Vital signs at baseline, 15 minutes after initiation, every 30 minutes during infusion, and at completion. Furosemide 20 mg IV between units if signs of fluid overload occur. Blood product verification by two RNs: client identity, ABO/Rh, unit number, crossmatch information, and expiration date.

options_or_rows_en.text: Notify the provider, anticipate ordered IV furosemide, and monitor intake and output closely.
Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line patent with normal saline using new tubing at a keep-vein-open rate.
Place the client in high Fowler's position.
Apply supplemental oxygen and monitor pulse oximetry.
Restart the unit at a slower rate once the SpO2 begins to improve.
Administer a rapid IV fluid bolus to support circulation.

rationale_en: TACO management begins with stopping the transfusion, maintaining IV access with new tubing, positioning upright, supporting oxygenation, notifying the provider, and using ordered diuresis with close intake/output monitoring. Restarting the transfusion or giving a fluid bolus would worsen the volume problem.

stem_en: Which actions belong in the management plan for this suspected TACO episode? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response to continued treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: A second dose of furosemide 20 mg IV was given 2 hours after the first dose for continued but improving fluid overload. Current vital signs: T 36.9 C, HR 84, BP 136/78, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 4 L nasal cannula after weaning from the non-rebreather. The client is resting comfortably at 45 degrees and says she can breathe much more easily. Lung sounds have returned to faint bibasilar crackles only, similar to admission baseline. JVD is no longer visible. Edema has returned to trace bilaterally. S3 is no longer audible. Total urine output since the initial furosemide dose: 1,400 mL over 4 hours. Repeat BNP: 420 pg/mL. Provider documentation: transfusion-associated circulatory overload; hold remaining packed red blood cells pending reassessment; repeat hemoglobin in 6 hours; consult nephrology for future transfusion planning such as slower rate, pre-transfusion diuretic, and single-unit strategy. Transfusion reaction report completed per policy.

Place the client in high Fowler's position.
Apply supplemental oxygen and monitor pulse oximetry.
Restart the unit at a slower rate once the SpO2 begins to improve.
Administer a rapid IV fluid bolus to support circulation.

rationale_en: TACO management begins with stopping the transfusion, maintaining IV access with new tubing, positioning upright, supporting oxygenation, notifying the provider, and using ordered diuresis with close intake/output monitoring. Restarting the transfusion or giving a fluid bolus would worsen the volume problem.

stem_en: At Stage 2, classify each action as part of the first-priority concurrent RN response or as a next/protocol step after immediate stabilization begins.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and transfusion orders
options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Stop the transfusion immediately.
Position the client in high Fowler's.
Apply high-flow oxygen for SpO2 89% with dyspnea.
Notify the provider for assessment and diuretic orders.
Notify the blood bank and send the blood product, tubing, and ordered samples per protocol.
Complete the transfusion reaction report.
First-priority concurrent response
Next/protocol step

rationale_en: The first-priority concurrent RN actions are stopping the transfusion, placing the client upright, and applying oxygen because they require no new order and directly address ongoing exposure and hypoxemia. Provider notification, blood bank notification/sample handling, and reaction reporting are essential, but they do not replace immediate stabilization.

stem_en: Which Stage 3 and Stage 4 findings best show that the TACO episode is resolving? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline history and transfusion orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: chronic kidney disease stage 3b with baseline eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73 m2; compensated heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, EF 52%, grade I diastolic dysfunction; type 2 diabetes. Home medications include furosemide 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, and insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime.

Reason for admission: progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea on one flight of stairs, lightheadedness, outpatient hemoglobin 6.4 g/dL. No prior transfusion reactions.

Orders/protocol: two units packed red blood cells sequentially; each unit over 2 hours, not to exceed 4 hours per unit. Vital signs at baseline, 15 minutes after initiation, every 30 minutes during infusion, and at completion. Furosemide 20 mg IV between units if signs of fluid overload occur. Blood product verification by two RNs: client identity, ABO/Rh, unit number, crossmatch information, and expiration date.

options_or_rows_en.text: Post-transfusion hemoglobin is 8.1 g/dL.
RR, HR, and BP trend back toward baseline.
BNP trends down from 890 pg/mL to 420 pg/mL after treatment.
JVD resolves, S3 is no longer audible, edema returns to trace, and lung sounds return to baseline.
SpO2 improves from 89% to 96% while oxygen is weaned from non-rebreather to nasal cannula.
Urine output reaches 1,400 mL over 4 hours after furosemide.

rationale_en: Resolution of TACO is shown by improving oxygenation with oxygen weaning, normalized respiratory and hemodynamic trends, brisk diuresis after furosemide, disappearance of congestion findings, and a falling BNP trend. Hemoglobin shows transfusion effect, not resolution of pulmonary congestion.

stem_en: The nurse identifies that the assigned patient load presents a significant safety concern. Which of the following findings from the initial rounds and shift report confirm this mismatch? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Assignment (1900)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Nurse Okonkwo arrives on the step-down unit and receives a verbal assignment from the charge nurse, Marcus Reilly, RN. This step-down unit is credentialed to manage selected low-dose vasoactive infusions and arterial lines when assigned to nurses validated in those competencies. He assigns her three patients:

Client A — a 58-year-old man, postoperative day one after coronary artery bypass graft, transferred from the cardiac ICU to step-down two hours ago on a low-dose norepinephrine drip at 4 mcg/min titrated to maintain MAP above 64 mmHg, with an arterial line in the left radial artery for continuous blood pressure monitoring. He also has mediastinal chest tubes to a closed drainage system.

Client B — a 62-year-old woman, eight hours post-carotid endarterectomy, requiring neurological assessments every one hour per the unit's post-carotid-endarterectomy protocol (pupil reactivity, facial symmetry, speech clarity, grip strength, swallow function, and blood pressure parameters to maintain systolic between 100 and 150 mmHg). She is on a nicardipine drip at 5 mg/hr, titrated per protocol to keep systolic blood pressure within that range.

Client C — a 71-year-old woman, post-operative day two after right total knee arthroplasty, transferred to the step-down unit because no medical-surgical beds were available. She is hemodynamically stable, on oral pain medication, receiving routine incision care, and ambulating with physical therapy. Her care needs are consistent with a medical-surgical level of acuity.

The charge nurse tells Nurse Okonkwo: "I know it's a lot, but we're short. You'll be fine — the drips are already running, and the settings are in the chart. Just keep an eye on things."

options_or_rows_en.text: Client A's blood pressure is monitored via an arterial line.
Client B requires an hourly neuro assessment with unfamiliar grading criteria.
All three clients are hemodynamically stable at the start of the shift.
Client C requires a continuous passive motion machine.
Client A requires active norepinephrine titration.

rationale_en: The significant cues are the mismatch between the nurse's documented competencies and the care requirements of Clients A and B. Specifically, the nurse is not validated to manage vasoactive titration orders, arterial lines, or the unit's unfamiliar structured neuro protocol.

stem_en: The nurse identifies that the assigned patient load presents a significant safety concern. Which of the following findings from the initial rounds and shift report confirm this mismatch? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Resolution and Escalation
options_or_rows_en.content:
At 2115, Supervisor Huang arrives on the unit. She reviews the assignment board, confirms Nurse Okonkwo's competency file does not include vasoactive titration or step-down neuro protocols, and restructures the assignments: a step-down-validated nurse, Keisha Williams, RN, takes over primary responsibility for Clients A and B. Nurse Okonkwo retains Client C and picks up a fourth patient — a stable post-operative cholecystectomy patient whose care needs are within Nurse Okonkwo's validated competencies.

At 2130, Nurse Okonkwo gives a bedside handoff report to Keisha for Clients A and B.

At 2145, Keisha reassesses Client B. She notes that Client B's speech now has a definite slurring quality compared to the immediate post-operative baseline documented in the recovery room note. Systolic blood pressure is 148 after the nicardipine increase. Keisha immediately notifies the surgeon per the post-carotid protocol and a STAT CT of the head is ordered.

At 2200, Nurse Okonkwo documents the events of the evening in a factual, objective manner according to the facility's assignment-objection and incident-reporting policies.

At 2230, Nurse Okonkwo completes an incident report per facility policy.

stem_en: The charge nurse tells Nurse Okonkwo, "The drips are already set — just don't change them. If something goes wrong, call me and I'll come help." Why does this instruction fail to resolve the safety concern?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Assignment (1900)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Nurse Okonkwo arrives on the step-down unit and receives a verbal assignment from the charge nurse, Marcus Reilly, RN. This step-down unit is credentialed to manage selected low-dose vasoactive infusions and arterial lines when assigned to nurses validated in those competencies. He assigns her three patients:

He also has mediastinal chest tubes to a closed drainage system.

Client B — a 62-year-old woman, eight hours post-carotid endarterectomy, requiring neurological assessments every one hour per the unit's post-carotid-endarterectomy protocol (pupil reactivity, facial symmetry, speech clarity, grip strength, swallow function, and blood pressure parameters to maintain systolic between 100 and 150 mmHg). She is on a nicardipine drip at 5 mg/hr, titrated per protocol to keep systolic blood pressure within that range.

Client C — a 71-year-old woman, post-operative day two after right total knee arthroplasty, transferred to the step-down unit because no medical-surgical beds were available. She is hemodynamically stable, on oral pain medication, receiving routine incision care, and ambulating with physical therapy. Her care needs are consistent with a medical-surgical level of acuity.

The charge nurse tells Nurse Okonkwo: "I know it's a lot, but we're short. You'll be fine — the drips are already running, and the settings are in the chart. Just keep an eye on things."

options_or_rows_en.text: Vasoactive drips are dynamic and require the primary nurse to independently recognize the need for and perform timely titrations.
Nurse Okonkwo is not willing to accept any assignment on the step-down unit.
The charge nurse does not have the authority to alter an assignment made by the house supervisor.
The float policy prohibits a float nurse from caring for any patient receiving an intravenous drip.

rationale_en: The charge nurse's statement incorrectly assumes vasoactive drips do not need adjustment. Because hemodynamic status fluctuates, the primary nurse must be competent to titrate the drips independently. Relying on a charge nurse for titrations introduces delays and fails to resolve the safety risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: The charge nurse's delayed response time to bedside pages.
Client A's increasing chest tube drainage.
The unsafe structure of the assignment leaving a non-validated nurse as primary caregiver.
Client B's potential neurological deterioration.

rationale_en: The priority problem is the unsafe assignment structure itself, not any single patient's current vital signs. The individual clinical events are symptoms of the underlying issue: assigning patients to a nurse who lacks the required competencies, resulting in a reactive and delayed pattern of care.

options_or_rows_en.text: Propose a safe alternative, such as reassignment of Clients A and B to a validated nurse.
Continue providing care for Client C.
Refuse all patient care and leave the unit immediately.
Call the on-call provider to complain about the staffing assignment.
Escalate the concern to the nursing supervisor.

rationale_en: The appropriate actions resolve the two conflicting obligations: continuing care within competency while escalating the structural problem. The nurse should continue caring for Client C to avoid abandonment, propose a safe reassignment alternative, and escalate the unresolved concern through the nursing chain of command.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I have not been validated to titrate vasoactive drips or manage arterial lines."
"Marcus is being dismissive and lazy because he doesn't want to change the assignment."
"I can continue caring for Client C, but Clients A and B require a validated nurse."
"There was a twelve-minute delay when I needed the charge nurse to evaluate a blood pressure parameter."
Appropriate
Inappropriate

rationale_en: Effective chain-of-command communication is factual, specific, and solution-oriented. Providing concrete evidence of the competency gap and its consequences is appropriate, whereas assigning emotional motives or blame is unprofessional.

At 2200, Nurse Okonkwo documents the events of the evening in a factual, objective manner according to the facility's assignment-objection and incident-reporting policies.

At 2230, Nurse Okonkwo completes an incident report per facility policy.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I have not been validated to titrate vasoactive drips or manage arterial lines."
"Marcus is being dismissive and lazy because he doesn't want to change the assignment."
"I can continue caring for Client C, but Clients A and B require a validated nurse."
"There was a twelve-minute delay when I needed the charge nurse to evaluate a blood pressure parameter."
Appropriate
Inappropriate

rationale_en: Effective chain-of-command communication is factual, specific, and solution-oriented. Providing concrete evidence of the competency gap and its consequences is appropriate, whereas assigning emotional motives or blame is unprofessional.

stem_en: At 2200, Nurse Okonkwo completes her documentation regarding the assignment concern. Which action is correct?

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial Assignment (1900)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Nurse Okonkwo arrives on the step-down unit and receives a verbal assignment from the charge nurse, Marcus Reilly, RN. This step-down unit is credentialed to manage selected low-dose vasoactive infusions and arterial lines when assigned to nurses validated in those competencies. He assigns her three patients:

Client A — a 58-year-old man, postoperative day one after coronary artery bypass graft, transferred from the cardiac ICU to step-down two hours ago on a low-dose norepinephrine drip at 4 mcg/min titrated to maintain MAP above 64 mmHg, with an arterial line in the left radial artery for continuous blood pressure monitoring. He also has mediastinal chest tubes to a closed drainage system.

Client B — a 62-year-old woman, eight hours post-carotid endarterectomy, requiring neurological assessments every one hour per the unit's post-carotid-endarterectomy protocol (pupil reactivity, facial symmetry, speech clarity, grip strength, swallow function, and blood pressure parameters to maintain systolic between 100 and 150 mmHg). She is on a nicardipine drip at 5 mg/hr, titrated per protocol to keep systolic blood pressure within that range.

Client C — a 71-year-old woman, post-operative day two after right total knee arthroplasty, transferred to the step-down unit because no medical-surgical beds were available. She is hemodynamically stable, on oral pain medication, receiving routine incision care, and ambulating with physical therapy. Her care needs are consistent with a medical-surgical level of acuity.

The charge nurse tells Nurse Okonkwo: "I know it's a lot, but we're short. You'll be fine — the drips are already running, and the settings are in the chart. Just keep an eye on things."

options_or_rows_en.text: Document in the patient chart that an incident report was filed regarding the unsafe assignment.
Document factual details of patient assessments, exact notification times, the delayed response time, and the handoff to the relieving nurse.
Document the patient findings but omit the 12-minute delay in charge nurse response to protect unit liability.
Complete a single comprehensive variance report covering all patients instead of charting the events in the individual patient medical records.

rationale_en: Patient medical records should contain objective clinical facts and a clear timeline of care. The nurse must chart accurate assessments, the times providers or charge nurses were notified, delayed responses, and handoff details. Subjective blame or omissions compromise the medical record.

stem_en: At 2200, Nurse Okonkwo completes her documentation regarding the assignment concern. Which action is correct?

options_or_rows_en.title: Resolution and Escalation
options_or_rows_en.content:
At 2115, Supervisor Huang arrives on the unit. She reviews the assignment board, confirms Nurse Okonkwo's competency file does not include vasoactive titration or step-down neuro protocols, and restructures the assignments: a step-down-validated nurse, Keisha Williams, RN, takes over primary responsibility for Clients A and B. Nurse Okonkwo retains Client C and picks up a fourth patient — a stable post-operative cholecystectomy patient whose care needs are within Nurse Okonkwo's validated competencies.

At 2130, Nurse Okonkwo gives a bedside handoff report to Keisha for Clients A and B.

At 2145, Keisha reassesses Client B. She notes that Client B's speech now has a definite slurring quality compared to the immediate post-operative baseline documented in the recovery room note. Systolic blood pressure is 148 after the nicardipine increase. Keisha immediately notifies the surgeon per the post-carotid protocol and a STAT CT of the head is ordered.

At 2200, Nurse Okonkwo documents the events of the evening in a factual, objective manner according to the facility's assignment-objection and incident-reporting policies.

At 2230, Nurse Okonkwo completes an incident report per facility policy.

Document factual details of patient assessments, exact notification times, the delayed response time, and the handoff to the relieving nurse.
Document the patient findings but omit the 12-minute delay in charge nurse response to protect unit liability.
Complete a single comprehensive variance report covering all patients instead of charting the events in the individual patient medical records.

rationale_en: Patient medical records should contain objective clinical facts and a clear timeline of care. The nurse must chart accurate assessments, the times providers or charge nurses were notified, delayed responses, and handoff details. Subjective blame or omissions compromise the medical record.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 information, highlight the findings that are red flags for unsafe discharge today.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and discharge context
options_or_rows_en.content: A 71-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and mild CKD was admitted 4 days ago for acute decompensated heart failure after running out of furosemide because she could not afford the refill. She was treated with IV furosemide, transitioned to new oral torsemide 40 mg daily, had lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily, and received a new carvedilol prescription. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment with no elevator and has no home scale. Her daughter lives 90 minutes away and works full-time. The attending entered a discharge order this morning with a target discharge time of 11:00 AM during bed-throughput pressure.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Resting RR is 22/min with SpO2 93% on room air.
Weight decreased from 84.6 kg on admission to 78.2 kg today.
Jugular venous distension is no longer visible at 45 degrees.
Bilateral pedal edema improved from 2+ on admission to trace today.
During hallway ambulation of about 40 feet, SpO2 decreased to 89% on room air and oxygen was needed for recovery.
The client cannot explain the purpose of torsemide, what to do for sudden weight gain, or which symptoms require calling the provider.
The new diuretic has a $95/month copay, and medication cost was the reason she stopped her prior furosemide.
She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment and has no home scale.
BNP decreased from 2,400 pg/mL on admission to 680 pg/mL today.
Creatinine is 1.8 mg/dL, above her outpatient baseline of 1.4 mg/dL.

rationale_en: The unsafe-discharge cues are residual respiratory compromise, exertional desaturation requiring oxygen recovery, failed teach-back, unaffordable diuretic therapy, unsafe home environment/no scale, and creatinine above baseline. Improving weight, edema, JVD, and BNP show treatment response but do not prove discharge readiness.

stem_en: Using the Stage 1 information, highlight the findings that are red flags for unsafe discharge today.

options_or_rows_en.title: Re-evaluation after interventions
options_or_rows_en.content: The provider orders potassium and magnesium repletion, repeat labs, and an additional loop-diuretic dose. Next morning resting vitals: HR 82, BP 112/70, RR 18, SpO2 95% on room air. Weight 77.4 kg. At 09:00, the client walks 150 feet and completes simulated stair climbing with HR 98, RR 22, SpO2 92% on room air; she recovers to baseline within 1 minute without oxygen. On repeat teach-back, she correctly states that the diuretic is her water pill, that she should weigh herself every morning, and that she should call for weight gain over 2 lb in 1 day or 3 lb in 1 week. She names increased leg swelling, waking up short of breath, and needing more pillows to sleep as warning signs. She states the medication names, doses, and timing, and confirms her daughter will stay for 2 days, home health will visit the day after discharge, a scale is being delivered, her prescription was revised, a 30-day diuretic supply is in hand, and cardiology follow-up is in 5 days. Labs: K 4.1, Mg 2.0, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL, BNP 520 pg/mL.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and discharge context
options_or_rows_en.content: A 71-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and mild CKD was admitted 4 days ago for acute decompensated heart failure after running out of furosemide because she could not afford the refill. She was treated with IV furosemide, transitioned to new oral torsemide 40 mg daily, had lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily, and received a new carvedilol prescription. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment with no elevator and has no home scale. Her daughter lives 90 minutes away and works full-time. The attending entered a discharge order this morning with a target discharge time of 11:00 AM during bed-throughput pressure.

options_or_rows_en.text: Give extra written instructions and avoid challenging the provider because the discharge order is active.
Advocate to hold or modify the discharge because the plan is unsafe despite improving biomarkers.
Proceed with discharge because the BNP is lower and the client has completed four hospital days.
Delay advocacy until bed management confirms whether an emergency department patient still needs the bed.

rationale_en: The priority concern is patient safety. The nurse should communicate the specific barriers and request that discharge be held or modified pending interdisciplinary evaluation. Bed pressure and improving biomarkers do not override unsafe functional, comprehension, medication-access, and home-environment findings.

Contact the case manager about transition needs, insurance coverage, and discharge resources.
Discharge the client as ordered to free the telemetry bed and rely on written instructions.

stem_en: The discharge order is active with a target discharge time of 11:00 AM. After identifying the unsafe discharge picture, what should the nurse do first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission background and discharge context
options_or_rows_en.content: A 71-year-old woman with HFrEF (EF 30%), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and mild CKD was admitted 4 days ago for acute decompensated heart failure after running out of furosemide because she could not afford the refill. She was treated with IV furosemide, transitioned to new oral torsemide 40 mg daily, had lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily, and received a new carvedilol prescription. She lives alone in a second-floor walk-up apartment with no elevator and has no home scale. Her daughter lives 90 minutes away and works full-time. The attending entered a discharge order this morning with a target discharge time of 11:00 AM during bed-throughput pressure.

options_or_rows_en.text: Escalate directly to the nursing supervisor before speaking with the attending physician.
Call the daughter first to see if she can solve the transportation and stair problem.
Call the attending physician with the objective safety concerns and request that the discharge order be held.
Page the social worker first because financial and home barriers are the root causes.

rationale_en: The first action is direct communication with the ordering provider to request a discharge hold. This stops the unsafe process and creates time for case management, social work, pharmacy, home health, and family coordination. Escalation follows if the provider refuses to address the safety concern.

stem_en: At 1030, warfarin 7.5 mg nightly appears on the home medication list. The INR is 5.2 and the client has active gingival oozing and new ecchymoses. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the scheduled warfarin because the client has a mechanical mitral valve.
Hold the warfarin, verify the hold order or obtain clarification, and notify the provider of the INR and bleeding findings.
Ask the wife whether she wants the warfarin held because she manages the pill organizer at home.
Give half of the warfarin dose and recheck the INR after the medication is absorbed.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold and clarify a high-risk medication when current objective data show likely harm. INR 5.2 with active mucocutaneous bleeding makes another warfarin dose unsafe; notifying the provider preserves the medication-order chain while preventing iatrogenic worsening.

stem_en: At 1030, warfarin 7.5 mg nightly appears on the home medication list. The INR is 5.2 and the client has active gingival oozing and new ecchymoses. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Orders, interventions, and 1200 status
options_or_rows_en.content:
Provider orders: vitamin K 10 mg IV diluted in normal saline and infused over 30 minutes; type and crossmatch 2 units PRBCs; administer 4-factor PCC per weight/INR protocol; hold all anticoagulation; GI consult; urology consult; noncontrast head CT; continue close neurologic and hemodynamic monitoring.
Nursing actions: vitamin K infused by pump over 30 minutes while monitoring for flushing, dyspnea, chest tightness, and hypotension; 4-factor PCC verified with a second nurse and administered per protocol; PRBC transfusion initiated; vital signs every 15 minutes during acute reversal; strict intake/output and hourly urine color assessment; neurologic checks continued. Head CT is negative for intracranial hemorrhage.
1100-1200 status: HR 88, BP 114/70, urine light pink and clearing, INR 3.8, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL after 1 unit PRBC with second unit infusing, abdominal tenderness mild and stable. GI and urology evaluations are pending. Warfarin has not yet been restarted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the scheduled warfarin because the client has a mechanical mitral valve.
Hold the warfarin, verify the hold order or obtain clarification, and notify the provider of the INR and bleeding findings.
Ask the wife whether she wants the warfarin held because she manages the pill organizer at home.
Give half of the warfarin dose and recheck the INR after the medication is absorbed.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold and clarify a high-risk medication when current objective data show likely harm. INR 5.2 with active mucocutaneous bleeding makes another warfarin dose unsafe; notifying the provider preserves the medication-order chain while preventing iatrogenic worsening.

options_or_rows_en.text: Teach the client and wife to avoid NSAIDs unless specifically approved by the anticoagulation prescriber.
Tell the wife that over-the-counter medications should be checked with the anticoagulation clinic or prescriber before use.
Instruct the client to stop eating dark green vegetables permanently to prevent future warfarin interactions.
Explain that NSAIDs can increase bleeding risk through platelet inhibition and gastrointestinal mucosal injury, even when the INR does not fully explain the bleeding.
Reassure the family that two doses of naproxen are too few to be clinically relevant.
Document the naproxen dose and timing and notify the provider because this exposure changes the bleeding-risk assessment.

rationale_en: Naproxen exposure is clinically relevant in a client taking warfarin with active bleeding. The nurse should document and escalate the finding, teach avoidance of unapproved NSAIDs, explain the bleeding mechanisms in practical terms, and involve the caregiver who manages medications at home.

stem_en: The provider orders IV vitamin K and 4-factor PCC for major bleeding. Which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 1030 admission record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 63-year-old man, mechanical mitral valve replacement 12 days ago; chronic atrial fibrillation; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction; type 2 diabetes; mild chronic kidney disease.
Home medications: warfarin 7.5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, furosemide 20 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed.
Target INR for mechanical mitral valve: 2.5-3.5. INR was 3.0 at discharge and 3.2 at cardiology follow-up one day later.
Client report: poor appetite, nausea, low fluid intake. Wife manages pill organizer and gave naproxen 220 mg twice over the past 2 days for shoulder pain. No herbal supplements.

options_or_rows_en.text: Dilute vitamin K as ordered and infuse it by pump over the ordered time rather than giving a rapid IV push.
Reassess vital signs, bleeding sites, urine color and output, neurologic status, hemoglobin, and repeat INR as ordered.
Administer 4-factor PCC promptly according to institutional protocol with required verification and monitoring.
Delay PCC until the vitamin K infusion is complete and a repeat INR confirms that the vitamin K has not corrected the coagulopathy.
Verify the orders, allergies, baseline vital signs, IV patency, product availability, and required second-nurse checks before administration.
Monitor during vitamin K infusion for hypotension, flushing, dyspnea, chest tightness, or other hypersensitivity findings.

rationale_en: Appropriate nursing care includes verification before high-risk products, slow diluted IV vitamin K administration with hypersensitivity monitoring, prompt PCC administration per protocol for rapid factor replacement, and close reassessment of bleeding and laboratory response. These actions may overlap in real practice and should not be forced into a single linear sequence.

options_or_rows_en.text: Warfarin should be restarted immediately by the nurse to prevent mechanical valve thrombosis.
Because the head CT is negative, neurologic checks and fall precautions are no longer necessary.

The client remains in a transition period: bleeding appears controlled but the bleeding source has not been fully evaluated, and ongoing vitamin K effect may push INR below target, increasing mechanical valve thrombosis risk. Restarting anticoagulation is anticipated but is a provider decision after bleeding control.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline communication note
options_or_rows_en.content: Age 58. Primary language: Mandarin. Speaks limited English and reads simplified Chinese. Scheduled for outpatient endoscopy with moderate sedation. Consent form is unsigned. Adult daughter is present and says, 'I can translate everything.'

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask the daughter to translate because she is present and willing.
Obtain a certified medical interpreter and notify the provider that the consent discussion must occur in the client's language.
Document refusal of consent because the client cannot answer in English.
Have the client sign because nodding indicates agreement.

rationale_en: Informed consent requires meaningful communication with the provider using language access services. A family member should not replace a certified interpreter for consent when accurate, unbiased interpretation is needed.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline clinic note
options_or_rows_en.content: Age 18 months. Weight 11 kg. Vomiting and watery diarrhea for 18 hours after other children at daycare were ill. No blood in stool. Child is alert, cries with tears, has dry lips, capillary refill 2 seconds, and had one wet diaper in 6 hours. Oral rehydration protocol for this clinic: give 75 mL/kg of oral rehydration solution over 4 hours for mild to moderate dehydration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Alert and interested in a toy
One wet diaper in 6 hours
No tears when crying
Bilious vomiting
Mildly dry mucous membranes
Consistent with oral rehydration teaching
Requires urgent escalation

rationale_en: Alert behavior, some urine output, and mild dryness fit oral rehydration teaching. No tears and bilious vomiting are more concerning cues that require escalation.

options.text: only plain water for the rest of the day

stem_en: Stage 1: The daughter offers to interpret the ostomy discharge teaching. The chart shows no prior qualified interpreter use, no WOC nurse note, and no documented teach-back. What should the nurse do first?

options_or_rows_en.title: 0700 initial record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 3 after open sigmoid colectomy with end colostomy.
History: type 2 diabetes; metformin 1,000 mg twice daily restarted this morning after adequate oral intake and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; BMI 34.
Language: preferred language Spanish; speaks and reads only Spanish. No qualified interpreter use documented during admission.
Current orders: regular diet as tolerated; ambulate four times daily; saline lock; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone PRN; wound and stoma assessments every shift; begin discharge planning today. WOC nurse consult placed postoperative day 1 but not completed.
Night note: 'Patient educated on ostomy care via daughter interpreting. Patient nodded understanding.' No teach-back documented.
Assessment: T 37.2 C, HR 78, BP 134/82, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air. Abdomen soft, mildly tender, no distension. Midline incision clean, dry, approximated with staples. Stoma round, beefy red, moist, about 3 cm diameter, protrudes about 1 cm. Two-piece cut-to-fit wafer with about 1/8-inch clearance. Pouch contains moderate thick greenish-brown stool. Peristomal skin intact. Pain 3/10 by fingers. Ambulates steadily to bathroom.
Labs: WBC 9,800/uL; Hgb 11.4 g/dL; platelets 268,000/uL; BUN 14 mg/dL; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; K 4.0 mEq/L; Na 139 mEq/L; glucose 148 mg/dL; albumin 3.1 g/dL. No labs pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Proceed with teaching through the daughter because she is bilingual and will live with the patient.
Ask the daughter to sign the discharge instructions because she has been doing the pouch care.
Give the Spanish written instructions and document that written teaching was provided.
Arrange a qualified Spanish medical interpreter before formal discharge teaching and keep the daughter present for support if the patient wants.

rationale_en: Formal discharge teaching for a patient with limited English proficiency requires direct communication with the patient through qualified language assistance. The daughter may support the patient, but the case cues show she has been answering for the patient and guiding the skill rather than confirming the patient's own understanding.

stem_en: Stage 1: The daughter offers to interpret the ostomy discharge teaching. The chart shows no prior qualified interpreter use, no WOC nurse note, and no documented teach-back. What should the nurse do first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Final discharge review
options_or_rows_en.content: The surgeon reviews the WOC documentation and confirms discharge criteria are met. Using the qualified interpreter, the nurse reviews medications, follow-up, diet, activity limits, when to call, and when to seek emergency care. Medications: metformin restarted; lisinopril continued; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone prescription for five days with safe storage and disposal teaching. Follow-up: surgeon in 1 week; outpatient WOC nurse in 2 weeks. Diet: advance slowly, chew thoroughly, and introduce gas-producing foods one at a time. Activity: no lifting greater than 10 lb for 6 weeks per surgeon's order. The patient asks whether to check blood glucose at home. The nurse confirms the home glucometer is available and reviews the primary care provider's target fasting glucose range of 70-130 mg/dL. The daughter participates, but teach-back questions are directed to the patient through the interpreter. The patient correctly verbalizes warning signs, pouch emptying and changing, when to call, and the medication schedule. The nurse provides a 24-hour ostomy-support hotline and confirms two weeks of supplies. Discharge vitals: T 37.0 C, HR 76, BP 128/76, RR 14, SpO2 98%. Pain 1/10. Stoma remains red, moist, and functioning. Discharged home with daughter at 1445.

options_or_rows_en.text: Proceed with teaching through the daughter because she is bilingual and will live with the patient.
Ask the daughter to sign the discharge instructions because she has been doing the pouch care.
Give the Spanish written instructions and document that written teaching was provided.
Arrange a qualified Spanish medical interpreter before formal discharge teaching and keep the daughter present for support if the patient wants.

rationale_en: Formal discharge teaching for a patient with limited English proficiency requires direct communication with the patient through qualified language assistance. The daughter may support the patient, but the case cues show she has been answering for the patient and guiding the skill rather than confirming the patient's own understanding.

options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 3 after open sigmoid colectomy with end colostomy.
History: type 2 diabetes; metformin 1,000 mg twice daily restarted this morning after adequate oral intake and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; BMI 34.
Language: preferred language Spanish; speaks and reads only Spanish. No qualified interpreter use documented during admission.
Current orders: regular diet as tolerated; ambulate four times daily; saline lock; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone PRN; wound and stoma assessments every shift; begin discharge planning today. WOC nurse consult placed postoperative day 1 but not completed.
Night note: 'Patient educated on ostomy care via daughter interpreting. Patient nodded understanding.' No teach-back documented.
Assessment: T 37.2 C, HR 78, BP 134/82, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air. Abdomen soft, mildly tender, no distension. Midline incision clean, dry, approximated with staples. Stoma round, beefy red, moist, about 3 cm diameter, protrudes about 1 cm. Two-piece cut-to-fit wafer with about 1/8-inch clearance. Pouch contains moderate thick greenish-brown stool. Peristomal skin intact. Pain 3/10 by fingers. Ambulates steadily to bathroom.
Labs: WBC 9,800/uL; Hgb 11.4 g/dL; platelets 268,000/uL; BUN 14 mg/dL; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; K 4.0 mEq/L; Na 139 mEq/L; glucose 148 mg/dL; albumin 3.1 g/dL. No labs pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: The daughter answers the normal-stoma question for the patient.
The only documented education was done through the daughter, with no qualified interpreter documented.
The patient nods and says brief English words that do not clearly match the questions.
The daughter begins guiding the patient's hands during the pouch-emptying demonstration.
The stoma is beefy red, moist, and protrudes about 1 cm above the skin.
The patient is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and ambulatory to the bathroom.

rationale_en: The discharge order requires the patient's own demonstration of pouch emptying and wafer changing, identification of concerning stoma findings, and explanation of when to call. Lack of qualified interpreter use, unclear acquiescent responses, family-member answering, and hands-on guidance all prevent the nurse from confirming that the patient herself meets those criteria.

options_or_rows_en.title: Final discharge review
options_or_rows_en.content: The surgeon reviews the WOC documentation and confirms discharge criteria are met. Using the qualified interpreter, the nurse reviews medications, follow-up, diet, activity limits, when to call, and when to seek emergency care. Medications: metformin restarted; lisinopril continued; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone prescription for five days with safe storage and disposal teaching. Follow-up: surgeon in 1 week; outpatient WOC nurse in 2 weeks. Diet: advance slowly, chew thoroughly, and introduce gas-producing foods one at a time. Activity: no lifting greater than 10 lb for 6 weeks per surgeon's order. The patient asks whether to check blood glucose at home. The nurse confirms the home glucometer is available and reviews the primary care provider's target fasting glucose range of 70-130 mg/dL. The daughter participates, but teach-back questions are directed to the patient through the interpreter. The patient correctly verbalizes warning signs, pouch emptying and changing, when to call, and the medication schedule. The nurse provides a 24-hour ostomy-support hotline and confirms two weeks of supplies. Discharge vitals: T 37.0 C, HR 76, BP 128/76, RR 14, SpO2 98%. Pain 1/10. Stoma remains red, moist, and functioning. Discharged home with daughter at 1445.

options_or_rows_en.text: The daughter answers the normal-stoma question for the patient.
The only documented education was done through the daughter, with no qualified interpreter documented.
The patient nods and says brief English words that do not clearly match the questions.
The daughter begins guiding the patient's hands during the pouch-emptying demonstration.
The stoma is beefy red, moist, and protrudes about 1 cm above the skin.
The patient is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and ambulatory to the bathroom.

rationale_en: The discharge order requires the patient's own demonstration of pouch emptying and wafer changing, identification of concerning stoma findings, and explanation of when to call. Lack of qualified interpreter use, unclear acquiescent responses, family-member answering, and hands-on guidance all prevent the nurse from confirming that the patient herself meets those criteria.

stem_en: Stage 2: Using the qualified interpreter, the patient says she has been afraid to look at the stoma and has allowed her daughter to handle all pouch care. What is the priority nursing response before continuing skill teaching?

options_or_rows_en.title: 0700 initial record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 3 after open sigmoid colectomy with end colostomy.
History: type 2 diabetes; metformin 1,000 mg twice daily restarted this morning after adequate oral intake and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; BMI 34.
Language: preferred language Spanish; speaks and reads only Spanish. No qualified interpreter use documented during admission.
Current orders: regular diet as tolerated; ambulate four times daily; saline lock; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone PRN; wound and stoma assessments every shift; begin discharge planning today. WOC nurse consult placed postoperative day 1 but not completed.
Night note: 'Patient educated on ostomy care via daughter interpreting. Patient nodded understanding.' No teach-back documented.
Assessment: T 37.2 C, HR 78, BP 134/82, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air. Abdomen soft, mildly tender, no distension. Midline incision clean, dry, approximated with staples. Stoma round, beefy red, moist, about 3 cm diameter, protrudes about 1 cm. Two-piece cut-to-fit wafer with about 1/8-inch clearance. Pouch contains moderate thick greenish-brown stool. Peristomal skin intact. Pain 3/10 by fingers. Ambulates steadily to bathroom.
Labs: WBC 9,800/uL; Hgb 11.4 g/dL; platelets 268,000/uL; BUN 14 mg/dL; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; K 4.0 mEq/L; Na 139 mEq/L; glucose 148 mg/dL; albumin 3.1 g/dL. No labs pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask the daughter to complete the wafer change while the patient watches from bed.
Tell the patient that the stoma looks healthy and move directly to the return demonstration.
Acknowledge the fear, invite the patient to describe her concerns, and guide a gradual first look at the stoma with interpreter support.
Postpone all teaching until the outpatient WOC visit in two weeks.

rationale_en: The patient's avoidance is a psychosocial barrier to self-care. Brief supportive counseling and a gradual, interpreter-facilitated first look address readiness so the later pouch-emptying and wafer-change teaching can be effective.

Peristomal skin remains intact without redness or breakdown.

stem_en: Stage 3: During medication reconciliation, the patient asks whether she should check blood glucose at home now that metformin has been restarted. Complete the nurse's response.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0700 initial record
options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 3 after open sigmoid colectomy with end colostomy.
History: type 2 diabetes; metformin 1,000 mg twice daily restarted this morning after adequate oral intake and creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily; BMI 34.
Language: preferred language Spanish; speaks and reads only Spanish. No qualified interpreter use documented during admission.
Current orders: regular diet as tolerated; ambulate four times daily; saline lock; acetaminophen PRN; oxycodone PRN; wound and stoma assessments every shift; begin discharge planning today. WOC nurse consult placed postoperative day 1 but not completed.
Night note: 'Patient educated on ostomy care via daughter interpreting. Patient nodded understanding.' No teach-back documented.
Assessment: T 37.2 C, HR 78, BP 134/82, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air. Abdomen soft, mildly tender, no distension. Midline incision clean, dry, approximated with staples. Stoma round, beefy red, moist, about 3 cm diameter, protrudes about 1 cm. Two-piece cut-to-fit wafer with about 1/8-inch clearance. Pouch contains moderate thick greenish-brown stool. Peristomal skin intact. Pain 3/10 by fingers. Ambulates steadily to bathroom.
Labs: WBC 9,800/uL; Hgb 11.4 g/dL; platelets 268,000/uL; BUN 14 mg/dL; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL; K 4.0 mEq/L; Na 139 mEq/L; glucose 148 mg/dL; albumin 3.1 g/dL. No labs pending.

options.text: tell the daughter to monitor and report the glucose values instead of asking the patient
confirm the patient's existing home glucose-monitoring plan and supplies
instruct the patient to stop home monitoring because creatinine is normal
low risk of hypoglycemia compared with insulin or insulin secretagogues
high risk of sudden hypoglycemia that requires juice at the bedside
requirement to hold lisinopril whenever fasting glucose is above target

rationale_en: The nurse can reinforce the patient's existing diabetes plan by confirming that the glucometer and supplies are available and reviewing the provider's target range. Metformin alone is not primarily associated with hypoglycemia; the teaching should not overstate low-glucose risk or shift responsibility away from the patient.

options_or_rows_en.text: The nurse documents interpreter use, return demonstration, teach-back responses, supplies, and follow-up arrangements.
The patient completed pouch emptying and wafer application with verbal coaching only during the WOC session.
The patient verbalizes the medication schedule and follow-up plan through the interpreter.
The daughter says she understands the instructions and will take care of the pouch at home.
Through the interpreter, the patient states the stoma warning signs in her own words.
The patient receives written ostomy instructions in Spanish and a supply/vendor plan.

rationale_en: Effective discharge evaluation combines patient-centered teach-back through a qualified interpreter, return demonstration of psychomotor skills, written language-concordant reinforcement, supplies, follow-up, and complete documentation. The daughter's understanding is helpful support but does not by itself prove the patient's readiness under the stated discharge criteria.

stem_en: Before the client becomes hypotensive and if no contraindications are present, which medications may be anticipated for initial STEMI care? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 12-Lead ECG
options_or_rows_en.content: ST-segment elevation in leads V3-V4.

options_or_rows_en.text: Morphine
Clopidogrel
Warfarin
Nitroglycerin
Aspirin

rationale_en: Initial STEMI care commonly includes immediate antiplatelet therapy with aspirin plus a P2Y12 inhibitor. Nitroglycerin may be used for ischemic chest pain if not contraindicated, and morphine may be used for severe or persistent ischemic pain when appropriate. Warfarin is not used for acute STEMI stabilization.

DKA

rationale_en: Kussmaul respirations and fruity breath are classic compensatory mechanisms for metabolic acidosis in DKA. High urine specific gravity occurs due to osmotic diuresis and dehydration.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800 Vital Signs
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temp: 99.1°F (37.3°C)
HR: 118 bpm
RR: 28/min (deep and rapid)
BP: 92/58 mmHg
SpO2: 96% on room air

options_or_rows_en.text: 3% Sodium Chloride
0.45% Sodium Chloride
Dextrose 5% in Water
0.9% Sodium Chloride

rationale_en: Initial DKA resuscitation uses an isotonic crystalloid to restore intravascular volume. Among these options, 0.9% sodium chloride is the appropriate initial fluid; hypotonic, dextrose-containing, and hypertonic saline solutions are not initial resuscitation fluids.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue seizure precautions and frequent maternal-fetal monitoring.
Prepare to administer calcium gluconate as prescribed or per protocol.

Stop the magnesium sulfate infusion.

stem_en: For each immunization, indicate whether it is currently recommended (indicated now) for this 66-year-old client or not indicated at this visit.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Seasonal influenza vaccine
Pneumococcal vaccine
Recombinant zoster (shingles) vaccine
Td/Tdap booster
Indicated now
Not indicated at this visit

rationale_en: All four are indicated. Influenza vaccine is recommended annually for nearly all adults and he has not had it this season. Pneumococcal vaccination is recommended for adults age 50 years or older who have not received a pneumococcal conjugate vaccine or whose history is unknown. Recombinant zoster vaccine is recommended for adults 50 and older. A tetanus-containing booster is due because his last dose was 12 years ago (routine interval is every 10 years).

No colorectal screening is needed until age 75 regardless of prior screening
Colonoscopy (or another approved colorectal screening test)

rationale_en: Average-risk adults should be screened for colorectal cancer beginning at age 45 and continuing through 75; an unscreened 66-year-old is overdue. Colonoscopy is a recommended option (stool-based tests are alternatives). Ultrasound and CEA are not colorectal cancer screening tests.

stem_en: While at the clinic, the client mentions his adult daughter is pregnant and was just diagnosed with leukemia and is receiving chemotherapy. He asks which vaccine she should NOT receive right now. Which response is correct?

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record
options_or_rows_en.content: Type 2 diabetes, on metformin. No known immunodeficiency. Immunizations: last Td/Tdap 12 years ago; no influenza vaccine this season; no pneumococcal vaccine; no zoster (shingles) vaccine. Never screened for colorectal cancer. Non-smoker.

options_or_rows_en.text: Live attenuated vaccines (such as MMR or varicella) should be avoided
The inactivated influenza vaccine should be avoided
The tetanus (Tdap) vaccine should be avoided
All vaccines are safe and none should be avoided

rationale_en: Live attenuated vaccines (e.g., MMR, varicella, live nasal influenza) are contraindicated in pregnancy and in significantly immunocompromised clients such as those receiving chemotherapy, because the weakened organism can cause disease. Inactivated vaccines like the injectable influenza and Tdap are not only safe but recommended in pregnancy.

options_or_rows_en.title: History and immunization record

options_or_rows_en.text: "Keeping my blood sugar controlled lowers my risk of complications."
"Since I feel healthy, I can skip the colorectal cancer screening."
"I should get a flu shot every year."
"The shingles vaccine can help prevent a painful rash and nerve pain."
"Because my last tetanus shot was over 10 years ago, I'm due for a booster."

rationale_en: Annual influenza vaccination, a tetanus booster every 10 years, glycemic control to reduce diabetic complications, and zoster vaccination to prevent shingles and postherpetic neuralgia all reflect correct understanding. Believing that feeling well removes the need for colorectal screening is a misconception, since colorectal cancer is often asymptomatic early.

stem_en: After providing care for this client, how should the nurse perform hand hygiene?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: 8-10 loose, foul-smelling stools per day. Temperature 38.1 C, mild abdominal cramping. WBC 14,000/mm3. Recently completed clindamycin. Shares a semiprivate room with another client.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use an alcohol-based hand rub only
Rinse hands with water alone
Apply alcohol-based hand rub, then don clean gloves
Wash hands with soap and water

rationale_en: For C. difficile teaching and testing, soap-and-water hand hygiene is preferred after care because spores are not reliably inactivated by alcohol-based hand rubs and mechanical washing helps remove them. Correct glove and gown use remains essential to reduce hand contamination.

stem_en: The nurse observes a student caring for the client. Indicate whether each action is correct or requires correction.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: 8-10 loose, foul-smelling stools per day. Temperature 38.1 C, mild abdominal cramping. WBC 14,000/mm3. Recently completed clindamycin. Shares a semiprivate room with another client.

options_or_rows_en.text: Dons a gown and gloves before entering the room
Uses dedicated (single-client) equipment such as a stethoscope and BP cuff
Cleans the room surfaces with a quaternary-ammonium wipe only
Removes gloves and gown and performs hand hygiene before leaving the room
Requires correction
Correct

rationale_en: Donning gown and gloves before entry, using dedicated equipment, and removing PPE with hand hygiene before leaving are all correct contact-precaution practices. Routine quaternary-ammonium cleaners are not sporicidal; surfaces must be disinfected with a sporicidal agent such as a chlorine-based (bleach) product.

Correct

rationale_en: Donning gown and gloves before entry, using dedicated equipment, and removing PPE with hand hygiene before leaving are all correct contact-precaution practices. Routine quaternary-ammonium cleaners are not sporicidal; surfaces must be disinfected with a sporicidal agent such as a chlorine-based (bleach) product.

stem_en: Place the steps for removing personal protective equipment (PPE) in the correct order before leaving the client-care area.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: 8-10 loose, foul-smelling stools per day. Temperature 38.1 C, mild abdominal cramping. WBC 14,000/mm3. Recently completed clindamycin. Shares a semiprivate room with another client.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform hand hygiene with soap and water after removing PPE
Remove gloves
Remove gown
Remove eye protection / goggles

rationale_en: A safe PPE-removal sequence removes the most contaminated items first: gloves, then eye protection by the clean strap or earpieces, then gown, followed by hand hygiene. For C. difficile teaching, final hand hygiene is performed with soap and water because spores are not reliably removed or inactivated by alcohol rub alone.

stem_en: For each chest-drainage finding, indicate whether it is expected for this client or requires immediate follow-up.

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tidaling (water level rises/falls with respiration) in the water-seal chamber
Gentle continuous bubbling in the suction-control chamber
Intermittent bubbling in the water-seal chamber
200 mL of bright red drainage in 1 hour
Requires immediate follow-up
Expected

rationale_en: Tidaling reflects pressure changes and can indicate a patent tube. Gentle continuous bubbling in the suction-control chamber is expected with wet suction. Intermittent bubbling in the water-seal chamber can be expected early with a pneumothorax as trapped pleural air evacuates. Bright red drainage of 200 mL/hr is excessive and signals possible hemorrhage requiring immediate provider notification.

stem_en: Two hours later the nurse notes new continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber that was not present before. What does this finding most likely indicate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: The suction pressure is set too low
An air leak in the system or at the insertion site
Re-expansion of the lung is complete
The tube is kinked or obstructed

rationale_en: New or continuous bubbling in the water-seal chamber indicates air is entering the closed system — an air leak, either within the system (loose connection) or at the insertion site. The nurse should assess connections and the dressing and locate the leak.

stem_en: At 1330 the chest tube has disconnected from the drainage tubing and the client is short of breath with an SpO2 of 89%. What is the nurse's immediate priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Clamp the chest tube near the insertion site with a padded clamp
Submerge the end of the chest tube in a bottle of sterile water to re-establish a water seal
Lay the client flat and call the rapid response team
Reconnect the tubing after wiping both ends with an alcohol swab

rationale_en: If a chest tube disconnects from the drainage system, the priority is to re-establish a water seal by submerging the open end in sterile water (about 2 cm). This lets pleural air escape while preventing atmospheric air from being drawn back into the pleural space, which would worsen the pneumothorax.

stem_en: The seal is restored and the system reconnected. Which later finding best indicates that the client's pneumothorax is resolving as expected?

options_or_rows_en.title: Chest tube insertion note
options_or_rows_en.content: Left chest tube placed in the 5th intercostal space, mid-axillary line, for spontaneous pneumothorax. Connected to closed drainage with wet suction at -20 cm H2O. Occlusive dressing intact at insertion site. Post-insertion chest x-ray ordered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continuous bubbling returns in the water-seal chamber
Subcutaneous crepitus develops around the insertion site
Tidaling stops and water-seal bubbling ceases, with improved breath sounds and a re-expanded lung on x-ray
The collection chamber fills rapidly with bright red fluid

rationale_en: As a pneumothorax resolves, the pleural air is fully evacuated: bubbling stops, tidaling ceases (lung is re-expanded against the chest wall), breath sounds improve, and the chest x-ray confirms re-expansion. These together indicate the expected outcome.

stem_en: Which findings should the nurse recognize as cues that raise concern for intimate partner violence? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client requesting a written list of her discharge medications
Injuries in various stages of healing
A partner who answers for the client and is reluctant to leave
An explanation of injury that is inconsistent with the findings
A delay of two days before seeking care

rationale_en: Injuries at different healing stages, delayed care, an injury story that does not match the physical findings, and a controlling partner who answers for the client and will not leave are all classic red flags for intimate partner violence. Asking for a medication list is normal, appropriate engagement in care.

stem_en: What is the most appropriate nursing action to screen the client for intimate partner violence?

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: Avoid raising the topic unless the client brings it up first
Find a private moment to interview the client alone, away from the partner
Ask the client about abuse directly in front of the partner
Confront the partner about the suspicious injuries

rationale_en: Screening for IPV must occur privately, with the client alone, so she can answer honestly without fear of the partner. The nurse can use a routine reason (such as a test or specimen) to ask the partner to step out. Asking in front of the partner endangers the client, confronting the partner can escalate violence, and waiting silently misses the chance to offer help.

stem_en: Once alone, the client tearfully discloses that her partner hit her but says, 'I'm not ready to leave him.' What is the nurse's priority action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: Insist that she leave the partner immediately before discharge
Tell her she must press charges against her partner today
Assess her immediate safety and help develop a safety plan, including resources she can use
Reassure her that the situation will likely improve on its own

rationale_en: The nurse respects the client's autonomy while prioritizing safety: assess immediate danger and collaboratively develop a safety plan and provide resources (hotline, shelter, escape plan). Pressuring her to leave or press charges removes her control, can escalate danger, and may drive her away from help. Falsely reassuring her is unsafe.
