stem_en: For each nurse statement or action, indicate whether it is therapeutic/appropriate or non-therapeutic/inappropriate when supporting this client.

options_or_rows_en.title: Presentation and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Chief complaint: 'I fell.' Injury: forearm bruising in various stages of healing plus a new wrist injury. The client avoids eye contact, is anxious, and defers to her partner. The partner is reluctant to leave the room. Delay of two days before seeking care.

options_or_rows_en.text: "This is not your fault, and you did not deserve to be hurt."
"I'm concerned for your safety. Here is a hotline number you can keep."
"Why do you stay with someone who hurts you?"
Document the client's own words and objective findings accurately in the record
Non-therapeutic / inappropriate
Therapeutic / appropriate

rationale_en: Affirming that the abuse is not the client's fault, expressing concern while providing resources, and accurately documenting the client's own words and objective findings are all therapeutic and support safety. Asking 'why do you stay' is judgmental, implies blame, and is non-therapeutic.

stem_en: Match each skin finding to its correct pressure injury stage.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Sacrum: full-thickness loss, subcutaneous fat visible, slough present, no bone/tendon/muscle exposed
Right heel: intact skin with non-blanchable redness
Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss, shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough
Stage 2
Stage 3
Stage 1

rationale_en: Stage 3 involves full-thickness skin loss with visible subcutaneous fat and possible slough, but no exposed bone, tendon, or muscle (the sacrum). Stage 1 is intact skin with non-blanchable erythema (the heel). Stage 2 is partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink/red moist wound bed and no slough (the malleolus).

stem_en: Which interventions should the nurse include in the plan of care to prevent further pressure injury? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Float the heels off the bed using a pillow under the calves
Keep the head of the bed elevated above 45 degrees at all times
Vigorously massage the reddened heel to improve circulation
Use a pressure-redistribution support surface
Keep skin clean and dry and manage incontinence promptly
Reposition the client at least every 2 hours

rationale_en: Repositioning at least every 2 hours, floating the heels, using a pressure-redistribution surface, and keeping skin clean and dry while managing moisture all reduce sustained pressure and moisture-associated damage. Massaging reddened bony prominences is contraindicated, and keeping the head of bed high increases shear over the sacrum.

stem_en: Given the client's albumin of 2.8 g/dL, poor intake, and weight loss, which nutrition measures best support wound healing? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Skin assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Sacrum: full-thickness loss with visible subcutaneous fat; no bone, tendon, or muscle exposed; small amount of yellow slough. Right heel: intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Left lateral malleolus: partial-thickness loss with a shallow pink moist wound bed, no slough. Braden score: 12.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ensure adequate vitamin C and zinc
Provide adequate calories and hydration
Increase dietary protein intake
Offer oral nutritional supplements between meals
Restrict all dietary fat and protein to reduce metabolic demand

rationale_en: Wound healing requires increased protein for tissue synthesis, vitamin C and zinc as cofactors for collagen formation and immune function, adequate calories and fluids, and oral supplements to bridge poor intake. Restricting protein and fat would deprive the client of substrates essential for healing.

stem_en: Which findings from the initial assessment are most indicative of acute decompensated heart failure with pulmonary edema? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: Tripod positioning
SpO2 of 85% on room air
S3 gallop
Bilateral crackles
Blood pressure of 188/102 mmHg
History of atrial fibrillation

rationale_en: The classic signs of acute pulmonary edema, a life-threatening manifestation of ADHF, include severe dyspnea (leading to tripod positioning), hypoxemia (low SpO2), and diffuse crackles on auscultation due to fluid in the alveoli. An S3 gallop is a hallmark sign of fluid volume overload and failing left ventricular function. While hypertension is a common precipitating factor and atrial fibrillation is a common comorbidity, they are not as specific to the acute fluid shift into the lungs as the other findings.

stem_en: The nurse needs to take immediate action to stabilize the client. Place the following nursing interventions in the correct order of priority.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place the client in a high-Fowler's position.
Apply supplemental oxygen via a non-rebreather mask.
Administer intravenous furosemide.
Establish intravenous access.

rationale_en: The immediate priorities are to improve oxygenation and reduce the work of breathing. Placing the client upright (high-Fowler's) immediately reduces venous return to the heart and allows for better lung expansion. Applying oxygen addresses the severe hypoxemia. Establishing IV access is necessary before administering IV medications. Finally, administering a potent loop diuretic like furosemide is critical to remove excess fluid, but this requires IV access and follows the immediate stabilization of breathing.

Placing the client upright (high-Fowler's) immediately reduces venous return to the heart and allows for better lung expansion. Applying oxygen addresses the severe hypoxemia. Establishing IV access is necessary before administering IV medications. Finally, administering a potent loop diuretic like furosemide is critical to remove excess fluid, but this requires IV access and follows the immediate stabilization of breathing.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

rationale_en: The client's history of severe heart failure, combined with classic signs of fluid overload (S3, edema, crackles, orthopnea) and the reported non-adherence with his diuretic medication, strongly supports the diagnosis of cardiogenic pulmonary edema. The lack of fever or productive cough makes infection less likely, and the bilateral nature of the findings is not typical for a pulmonary embolism.

stem_en: After administering 80 mg of intravenous furosemide, which assessment finding would be the most reliable indicator that the medication has been effective?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Assessment - 0900
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Temperature: 36.8°C (98.2°F)
- Heart Rate: 128 beats/minute, irregularly irregular
- Respiratory Rate: 34 breaths/minute, labored
- Blood Pressure: 188/102 mmHg
- SpO2: 85% on room air

Assessment:
- General: Anxious, sitting upright in tripod position, using accessory muscles.
- Respiratory: Diffuse bilateral crackles halfway up the lung fields.
- Cardiovascular: S3 gallop present. 2+ pitting edema in bilateral lower extremities.
- Skin: Pale and diaphoretic.

options_or_rows_en.text: The client's heart rate decreases to 110 beats/minute.
The client has a urine output of 400 mL in the first hour.
The client's blood pressure decreases to 150/88 mmHg.
The client reports a decrease in anxiety.

rationale_en: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that works by inhibiting water reabsorption in the nephron, leading to significant diuresis. The primary therapeutic effect in pulmonary edema is the rapid removal of excess fluid from the body. Therefore, a brisk and significant increase in urine output is the most direct and reliable indicator of the drug's effectiveness. While other parameters may improve as a consequence of diuresis, they are secondary effects.

stem_en: Specify the correct medication for each clinical situation.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical History
options_or_rows_en.content: Child uses fluticasone MDI twice daily and albuterol as needed. Parents report child has been waking up twice a week coughing. Child plays soccer and often needs albuterol before the game.

options_or_rows_en.text: 15 minutes before soccer practice
Daily morning and evening dose
During an acute episode of wheezing
Fluticasone (Controller)
Albuterol (Rescue)

rationale_en: Albuterol is a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) and is the fast-acting reliever for both acute symptoms and pre-exercise prophylaxis. Fluticasone is an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) used for daily long-term control; because of its slow onset it has no role as a rescue inhaler. (Note: modern GINA SMART regimens use ICS-formoterol as both controller and reliever, but formoterol is a different drug from plain fluticasone and is not interchangeable with it here.)

options_or_rows_en.text: Rinses their mouth with water before using the inhaler.
Holds their breath for 10 seconds after inhalation.
Takes a quick, shallow breath during inhalation.
Exhales immediately after pressing the canister.

rationale_en: Holding the breath for 10 seconds allows the medication to settle in the lower airways. Rinsing should occur *after* using inhaled steroids (like fluticasone) to prevent oral candidiasis.

options_or_rows_en.text: Avoid blood pressure readings on the left arm.
Increase intake of dark green leafy vegetables.
Check for a thrill and bruit daily.
Sleep with the left arm in a dependent position.

rationale_en: AV fistula care includes avoiding pressure (heavy bags, BP cuffs) and monitoring patency (thrill/bruit). Phosphate binders must be taken with meals to be effective. High-potassium foods (leafy greens) should be limited.

rationale_en: A decrease in PaCO2 indicates improved alveolar ventilation. In a COPD exacerbation with hypercapnia, an SpO2 of 98% on 4 L/min is above the usual controlled-oxygen target and may worsen CO2 retention. Absent breath sounds and increased HR indicate worsening.

options_or_rows_en.title: Post-op Status (1400)
options_or_rows_en.content: Incisional dressing is dry and intact. Client reports pain level 6/10. Pedal pulses 2+ bilaterally. Right leg is abducted with an abduction pillow in place.

Inability to move the right toes
Internal rotation of the right leg
Sudden onset of severe pain

rationale_en: Signs of prosthetic hip dislocation include sudden severe pain, limb shortening, and internal rotation (for posterior approach). Numbness/inability to move toes indicates nerve damage, not necessarily dislocation.

Crossing legs at the ankles
Using an elevated toilet seat
Bending over to tie shoelaces
Permitted
Contraindicated

rationale_en: Hip precautions (posterior approach) include: no hip flexion >90 degrees (low sofas/bending), no adduction (crossing legs), and no internal rotation. Elevated seats help maintain flexion <90 degrees.

options_or_rows_en.content: Incisional dressing is dry and intact. Client reports pain level 6/10. Pedal pulses 2+ bilaterally. Right leg is abducted with an abduction pillow in place.

options_or_rows_en.text: Place a pillow under the knees.
Restrict fluid intake to prevent edema.
Massage the calves twice daily.
Encourage early ambulation and ankle pumps.

rationale_en: Mobility and active muscle contraction (ankle pumps) promote venous return and are the most effective non-pharmacological methods to prevent DVT. Pillows under knees can cause stasis.

stem_en: The client is prescribed Enoxaparin 30 mg SC every 12 hours for DVT prophylaxis. The pharmacy supplies Enoxaparin 40 mg/0.4 mL. How many mL should the nurse administer per dose?

options_or_rows_en.title: Post-op Status (1400)
options_or_rows_en.content: Incisional dressing is dry and intact. Client reports pain level 6/10. Pedal pulses 2+ bilaterally. Right leg is abducted with an abduction pillow in place.

rationale_en: Calculation: (Desired/Have) * Volume = (30 mg / 40 mg) * 0.4 mL = 0.75 * 0.4 = 0.3 mL.

stem_en: The client states, 'Do you see them? The spies are in the vent watching us!' What is the most therapeutic response?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: Client is pacing the hallway, looking over his shoulder frequently. States, 'The government has planted a chip in my brain to monitor my thoughts.' Refuses to eat because he believes the food is poisoned. Appears to be talking to someone when no one is present.

options_or_rows_en.text: There are no spies in the vent; you are imagining things.
I don't see any spies, but I understand that it is frightening for you to feel watched.
Why do you think the government wants to watch you specifically?
Let's go look in the vent together so I can prove you are safe.

rationale_en: This response acknowledges the client's feelings (validation) without agreeing with the delusion (presenting reality). Arguing or participating in the delusion is non-therapeutic.

rationale_en: The client's fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea meet the criteria for SIRS. Hypotension and altered mental status are critical indicators of sepsis-induced organ dysfunction, signifying progression towards septic shock. The history of diabetes is a risk factor for infection but is not a direct finding of the current inflammatory response or organ failure. Note: The Sepsis-3 consensus (2016) defines sepsis by organ dysfunction rather than requiring SIRS criteria, using SOFA/qSOFA scoring. SIRS remains foundational for NCLEX-RN education and continues to appear in entry-level nursing curricula.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics intravenously.
Initiate a rapid intravenous fluid bolus of 30 mL/kg.

rationale_en: The highest priority in septic shock is to restore tissue perfusion and intravascular volume, which is achieved by aggressive fluid resuscitation. Cultures must be obtained before starting antibiotics to identify the pathogen, but this should not delay treatment. Antibiotics should be administered within the first hour. Vasopressors are used after initial fluid resuscitation if hypotension persists, to maintain a target MAP of ≥ 65 mmHg.

This is evidenced by a MAP ≥ 65 mmHg, adequate urine output (≥ 0.5 mL/kg/hr or >30 mL/hr), improved mental status, and clearance of lactate. Cool, clammy skin suggests worsening shock (compensatory vasoconstriction). A stable but still elevated creatinine does not yet show improvement in renal function.

stem_en: The client's MAP remains 60 mmHg despite receiving norepinephrine at 10 mcg/min. The provider orders the addition of a vasopressin infusion. What is the primary rationale for adding vasopressin in this clinical situation?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage Note - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: To replace the body's depleted endogenous stores in a shock state.
To allow for a lower dose of norepinephrine, reducing the risk of peripheral ischemia.
To directly stimulate beta-1 receptors and increase the heart rate.
To provide a potent inotropic effect and increase cardiac contractility.

rationale_en: In septic shock, there is profound vasodilation. Vasopressin is a non-catecholamine vasopressor that acts on V1 receptors, causing vasoconstriction through a different mechanism than norepinephrine (an alpha-agonist). Adding vasopressin can have a synergistic effect, raising blood pressure and allowing the dose of norepinephrine to be reduced. High doses of norepinephrine are associated with significant adverse effects, including severe peripheral vasoconstriction, tachyarrhythmias, and myocardial ischemia.

stem_en: Based on the initial triage assessment, which of the following clinical findings strongly suggest an acute myocardial infarction accompanied by sympathetic nervous system activation? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: A resting heart rate of 110 beats/minute.
ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
Visible, profuse diaphoresis.
An oxygen saturation of 92% on room air.
Crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the jaw.

rationale_en: Crushing chest pain radiating to the jaw is the classic ischemic symptom of a myocardial infarction. ST-segment elevation in V1-V4 provides definitive electrocardiographic evidence of an acute anterior wall STEMI. Diaphoresis and tachycardia (HR 110) are direct results of the body's massive sympathetic nervous system (fight-or-flight) response to severe pain and decreased cardiac output. While the SpO2 of 92% is slightly low and warrants oxygen therapy, it is a secondary finding of mild hypoxia, not a direct indicator of sympathetic activation or the infarction itself.

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Prepare to administer intravenous epinephrine 1 mg.
Call for the resuscitation team and a defibrillator.
Deliver an unsynchronized electrical shock (defibrillation).
Initiate high-quality cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) starting with chest compressions.

rationale_en: According to Basic Life Support (BLS) and Advanced Cardiovascular Life Support (ACLS) guidelines, the first action upon discovering an unresponsive pulseless client is to call for help and a defibrillator. Immediately after calling for help, the nurse must start high-quality CPR (chest compressions) to maintain minimal perfusion to the brain and heart. As soon as the defibrillator arrives and ventricular fibrillation is confirmed, an unsynchronized shock must be delivered, as early defibrillation is the only definitive treatment for V-Fib. Following the shock, CPR is immediately resumed, and pharmacological interventions like epinephrine are prepared and administered.

options_or_rows_en.text: Amiodarone
Epinephrine
Aspirin
Nitroglycerin
Vasodilator
Antiarrhythmic
Antiplatelet Agent
Vasopressor

rationale_en: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic used in ACLS to treat refractory ventricular fibrillation and ventricular tachycardia. Epinephrine is a potent vasopressor (alpha- and beta-agonist) given during cardiac arrest to increase aortic diastolic pressure and coronary perfusion. Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent given early in acute coronary syndrome to prevent further clot aggregation in the coronary arteries. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator used to reduce preload and myocardial oxygen demand, relieving ischemic chest pain.

stem_en: During the resuscitation effort, the nurse continuously monitors the client's physiological responses. Which specific clinical finding provides the most definitive evidence of the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC)?

options_or_rows_en.title: Emergency Department Triage - 1400
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
- Blood Pressure: 155/90 mmHg
- Heart Rate: 110 beats/minute
- Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/minute
- SpO2: 92% on room air

Diagnostic Testing:
- 12-Lead ECG: Reveals 4 mm ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V4.
- Troponin I: Pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: The appearance of an organized sinus rhythm on the cardiac monitor without a palpable pulse.
Bilateral pupillary dilation and unresponsiveness to light.
The presence of occasional, irregular gasping respirations (agonal breathing).
An abrupt and sustained increase in PETCO2 (end-tidal carbon dioxide) to 40 mmHg.

rationale_en: An abrupt and sustained increase in end-tidal carbon dioxide (PETCO2), typically to a normal value of 35-40 mmHg, is the earliest and most reliable non-invasive indicator of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). During cardiac arrest, PETCO2 is low because blood is not circulating to the lungs to release CO2. When the heart restarts and cardiac output is restored, accumulated CO2 is suddenly washed out of the tissues and delivered to the lungs, causing a sharp spike on the capnography monitor. This often occurs even before a pulse can be reliably palpated.

stem_en: The nurse analyzes the client's initial 0800 triage presentation. For each finding, indicate if the cue is expected in uncomplicated hyperthyroidism, or if it specifically indicates a transition to life-threatening thyroid storm.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature of 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Diffuse anterior neck enlargement
Severe agitation and confusion
Heart rate of 152 bpm
Expected in Uncomplicated Hyperthyroidism
Indicates Thyroid Storm

rationale_en: Thyroid storm is an extreme, life-threatening exacerbation of hyperthyroidism. While hyperthyroidism causes heat intolerance, extreme hyperpyrexia (e.g., 104.2 °F) is a hallmark of thyroid storm. Mild anxiety is common in hyperthyroidism, but severe agitation, delirium, and confusion indicate CNS dysfunction specific to thyroid storm. Resting tachycardia (90-110 bpm) is expected in hyperthyroidism, but extreme tachycardia (>130 bpm) or arrhythmias like atrial fibrillation signify thyroid storm. A goiter is an anatomical feature of Graves' disease and hyperthyroidism, not a specific acute marker of a storm.

stem_en: Based on the 0830 orders, the nurse prepares to administer the prescribed medications. Drag and drop the medications into the correct order of administration to safely manage the thyroid storm.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer IV propranolol
Administer IV hydrocortisone
Administer Lugol's iodine solution
Administer propylthiouracil (PTU)

rationale_en: The pharmacological sequence in thyroid storm is critical to prevent exacerbating the condition. First, a beta-blocker (propranolol) is administered immediately to block peripheral sympathetic effects, lowering the dangerously high heart rate and preventing cardiovascular collapse. Second, an antithyroid medication like PTU is given to block the synthesis of new thyroid hormones. Third, an iodine solution (Lugol's) is given to block the release of pre-formed thyroid hormones. Crucially, iodine must be delayed for 1-2 hours AFTER PTU; if given first, iodine will act as substrate and fuel massive new thyroid hormone synthesis. Fourth, corticosteroids (hydrocortisone) are administered to treat relative adrenal insufficiency caused by hypermetabolism and to block the peripheral conversion of T4 to active T3.

stem_en: To manage the client's hypermetabolic state and maintain safety between 0800 and 1200, which nursing interventions are indicated? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0800: Triage Assessment
options_or_rows_en.content:
Vital Signs:
Temperature: 104.2 °F (40.1 °C)
Heart Rate: 152 bpm (irregular)
Blood Pressure: 168/94 mm Hg
Respiratory Rate: 28 breaths/min
SpO2: 95% on room air

Nursing Assessment:
Neurological: Extremely agitated, tremors present in bilateral upper extremities, oriented to person only.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, palpable bounding pulses.
Skin: Flushed, hot, and profusely diaphoretic.
Neck: Diffuse anterior neck enlargement (goiter) noted.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer prescribed aspirin for antipyretic relief.
Apply a cooling blanket and ice packs to axillae and groin.
Maintain a cool, dark, and quiet room environment.
Administer prescribed acetaminophen for hyperthermia.
Restrict intravenous fluids to prevent pulmonary edema.
Initiate seizure precautions.

rationale_en: Nursing care for thyroid storm focuses on reducing hyperthermia, preventing injury from extreme agitation, and providing supportive care. Acetaminophen and physical cooling (cooling blankets, ice packs) are used aggressively to lower core temperature. A cool, dark, quiet environment minimizes sympathetic nervous system stimulation, reducing agitation and cardiac demand. Seizure precautions are necessary because severe hyperpyrexia and CNS hyper-excitability place the client at high risk for seizures. Aspirin is contraindicated because salicylates displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins, increasing free active thyroid hormone levels and worsening the storm. Fluid restriction is incorrect; these clients have profound insensible fluid losses from diaphoresis and hyperventilation and require aggressive IV fluid replacement.

Serum potassium of 3.8 mEq/L (3.8 mmol/L)
White blood cell count of 2,800/mm³ (2.8 × 10^9/L)
Free T4 of 4.2 ng/dL

rationale_en: A severe and potentially fatal adverse effect of propylthiouracil (PTU) and methimazole is agranulocytosis (a profound drop in white blood cells, specifically neutrophils). A total WBC count of 2,800/mm³ is abnormally low (leukopenia) and places the client at extreme risk for life-threatening sepsis, warranting immediate provider notification to stop the drug and implement reverse isolation. The potassium and BUN levels are within normal limits. The Free T4 remains elevated (normal 0.8-2.8 ng/dL); while abnormal, this is an expected finding at this stage, as it takes time for circulating hormone levels to fall even after synthesis and release are blocked.

stem_en: For each admission finding, classify the most clinically relevant meaning in this client.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options_or_rows_en.text: Urine specific gravity 1.032
Urine sodium 8 mEq/L
FENa 0.4%
Creatinine 4.2 mg/dL, baseline 1.4 mg/dL
BP 88/54 with HR 112
Dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, flat neck veins
Potassium 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves
Clear lung sounds with no crackles
Bladder scan 40 mL
BUN-to-creatinine ratio 16.2:1
Indicates dangerous hyperkalemia
Not the strongest prerenal cue here
Supports prerenal AKI from volume depletion
Rules out or argues against another cause

rationale_en: The strongest prerenal pattern is the cluster of severe volume depletion plus kidneys that are still conserving sodium and water: concentrated urine, urine sodium 8 mEq/L, FENa 0.4%, acute creatinine rise, hypotension, tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, poor turgor, flat neck veins, and cool extremities. Potassium 6.1 mEq/L with peaked T waves is a separate immediate danger: hyperkalemia with myocardial irritability. Clear lungs support the absence of fluid overload, and a 40 mL bladder scan argues against urinary retention. The BUN-to-creatinine ratio is elevated but not the strongest cue in this scaffold, so the nurse should not over-weight it over the urine sodium, FENa, concentrated urine, and clinical dehydration.

stem_en: For each admission finding, classify the most clinically relevant meaning in this client.

options_or_rows_en.title: Response to treatment
options_or_rows_en.content: Calcium gluconate was administered at hour 4.5; insulin and dextrose were administered at hour 5. By hour 8: K 5.4 mEq/L, creatinine 4.3 mg/dL, BUN 70 mg/dL, HCO₃ 19 mEq/L, lactate 2.0 mmol/L, glucose 134 mg/dL. ECG: PR 0.18 seconds, QRS 0.10 seconds, peaked T waves diminished. Urine output from hours 6-10 improves to 35 mL/hr, about 0.43 mL/kg/hr. The client is oriented to person and place, BP 104/66, HR 92. Nephrology recommends continued isotonic fluids, urine output target >0.5 mL/kg/hr, BMP every 6 hours, holding all nephrotoxic medications, and reassessing for renal replacement therapy if urine output fails to improve or potassium rises again. By hour 14: urine output 48 mL/hr (0.59 mL/kg/hr), K 4.9 mEq/L, creatinine 3.8 mg/dL, BP 112/70, HR 84, alert and oriented x3, tolerating small sips of clear liquids.

stem_en: At Stage 1, which medication-related nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options_or_rows_en.text: Continue holding lisinopril during this hypovolemic AKI episode.
Teach the patient and family not to use over-the-counter NSAIDs during recovery unless the provider approves.
Monitor the lactate and acidosis while recognizing hypoperfusion can also contribute.
Continue holding metformin while renal function is acutely impaired.
Give metformin because the admission glucose is 188 mg/dL.
Clarify that ibuprofen is explicitly held and documented as contraindicated during the AKI episode.

rationale_en: Metformin, lisinopril, and ibuprofen all matter in this AKI episode. Metformin should be held because reduced renal clearance can increase accumulation and lactic acidosis risk; the elevated lactate needs monitoring, though hypoperfusion is also a plausible contributor. Lisinopril should be held because ACE inhibition can reduce the efferent arteriolar tone that helps preserve glomerular filtration pressure during hypovolemia. Ibuprofen should be explicitly held and documented because NSAIDs can impair prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriolar dilation and may be inadvertently restarted if not addressed. Teaching should include avoiding OTC NSAIDs during recovery unless approved.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which problem is the nurse's highest immediate priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options_or_rows_en.text: Creatinine rising from 4.2 to 4.5 mg/dL
Nausea that makes oral medications difficult to retain
Oliguria with urine output 0.14 mL/kg/hr
Worsening hyperkalemia with PR prolongation and QRS widening

rationale_en: The potassium has risen to 6.4 mEq/L and the ECG has progressed from peaked T waves to PR prolongation and QRS widening. That pattern signals advancing hyperkalemia-related conduction toxicity and can deteriorate to a lethal rhythm. Oliguria and rising creatinine are serious, but they do not create the same minute-to-minute cardiac arrest risk. The nurse should prepare for emergent membrane stabilization with calcium gluconate while the renal problem continues to be managed.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's clinical reasoning about the Stage 2 hyperkalemia protocol.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options.text: calcium gluconate at the ordered/protocol rate
continuing lisinopril to improve renal perfusion
waiting for creatinine to improve before treating potassium
regular insulin with dextrose per protocol
used as the only urgent intervention
binders stabilize the myocardium faster than calcium
the client is actively nauseated and vomiting, making oral administration unsafe and unlikely to be retained
recheck blood glucose at protocol intervals and keep dextrose available
stop ECG monitoring once the insulin is given

rationale_en: With hyperkalemia and ECG conduction changes, calcium gluconate is first because it stabilizes the cardiac membrane quickly but does not lower serum potassium. Insulin with dextrose follows to shift potassium intracellularly. Sodium bicarbonate may also be used because it is ordered in this acidotic client, but oral SPS should be questioned because active nausea and vomiting make oral administration unsafe and unreliable. Insulin can cause delayed hypoglycemia even when dextrose is given, so glucose monitoring must continue after administration.

stem_en: At Stage 2, which nursing actions should be included in the immediate bedside response? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use fall/safety precautions because the client is lethargic and confused.
Have regular insulin, dextrose, and a glucometer ready at the bedside.
Set reminders for post-insulin blood glucose checks according to protocol.
Assess the peripheral IV for patency and infiltration before and during calcium gluconate administration.
Administer calcium gluconate at the ordered/protocol rate while observing continuous ECG monitoring.
Continue strict intake and output with hourly urine measurements from the Foley catheter.

rationale_en: The immediate response is coordinated rather than purely linear. The nurse gives calcium gluconate at the ordered/protocol rate through a patent IV while monitoring ECG response and the IV site, prepares insulin/dextrose and glucose monitoring, continues hourly urine output measurement, and protects the confused client from injury. Rapid uncontrolled calcium administration is unsafe, and oral SPS should not be given unchanged to a client who is actively nauseated and vomiting.

stem_en: At Stage 3, classify each finding as evidence of improvement, a reason for continued close monitoring, or both.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: Baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL three months ago with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73 m². Home medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and ibuprofen as needed; facility MAR confirms ibuprofen 400 mg two days ago. On arrival he is dry, fatigued, oriented to person only, repeatedly asking where he is, with cracked lips, tacky mucous membranes, diffuse abdominal cramping, nausea, and no urination for several hours. Orders include peripheral IV access, 500 mL normal saline bolus, continuous cardiac monitoring, 12-lead ECG, BMP, CBC, urinalysis with microscopy, point-of-care glucose, bladder scan, and holding metformin and lisinopril pending renal review.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium decreased from 6.4 to 5.4 and then 4.9 mEq/L
PR interval normalized to 0.18 seconds and QRS narrowed to 0.10 seconds
Urine output improved to 48 mL/hr, about 0.59 mL/kg/hr
Creatinine decreased to 3.8 mg/dL but baseline is 1.4 mg/dL
Blood pressure improved to 112/70 and heart rate to 84
Mental status improved to alert and oriented x3
Potassium may rebound as insulin shifting effect wears off
Vomiting or diarrhea could recur
Requires continued close monitoring
Evidence of improvement

rationale_en: The patient is improving because potassium, ECG conduction, urine output, creatinine, hemodynamics, lactate, and mental status are moving in the right direction. The nurse must still monitor closely because potassium can rebound after insulin shifts wear off, the creatinine remains far above baseline, urine output must stay above the perfusion target, and recurrent GI losses could restart the prerenal cycle. Outcome evaluation depends on trajectory and durability, not one isolated better number.

stem_en: Using the admission data only, classify each finding by what it most directly tells the nurse.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission history and presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: History: cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago; declined elective cholecystectomy. BMI 34, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, no alcohol use, no prescription medications, no known allergies. Presentation: severe constant epigastric pain 9/10 beginning 45 minutes after a large fatty meal, radiating to the mid-back; four episodes of vomiting; no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea; no urination since pain began.

options_or_rows_en.text: Known history of gallstones with pain after a fatty meal
ALT 280 U/L, AST 210 U/L, direct bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 310 U/L, and mild scleral icterus
Lipase 1,840 U/L
Hematocrit 48% with tacky mucous membranes and concentrated urine
Total calcium 7.6 mg/dL and ionized calcium 3.6 mg/dL
WBC 14,500/mcL and glucose 180 mg/dL
Supports biliary etiology
Confirms acute pancreatitis
Volume depletion marker
Severity or prognostic marker

rationale_en: The gallstone history, post-fatty-meal onset, cholestatic/direct bilirubin pattern, ALT-predominant transaminase elevation, and scleral icterus point to a biliary cause. Lipase greater than three times the upper limit confirms acute pancreatitis but does not identify the cause. Hemoconcentration and concentrated urine reflect intravascular depletion from third spacing. Hypocalcemia, leukocytosis, and hyperglycemia are severity markers rather than etiologic clues.

stem_en: Using the admission data only, classify each finding by what it most directly tells the nurse.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: 36-48 hours after admission
options_or_rows_en.content: ERCP at hour 28 removed a 9-mm distal CBD stone by sphincterotomy and balloon sweep. At hour 40: T 37.6 C, HR 92, BP 124/76, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air. Pain is 4/10 and dull; she is alert and conversational. Abdomen less distended; bowel sounds returned but hypoactive. Hour 42 labs: WBC 11,800/mcL, lipase 980, total bilirubin 1.8, AST 88, ALT 140, calcium 8.4, ionized calcium 4.4, glucose 148, CRP 190, lactate 1.2, Hct 36%. Urine output is 300 mL over 6 hours (50 mL/hr). She is hungry. Provider orders low-fat solid food as tolerated; she tolerates clear liquids and a small amount of toast and applesauce without increased pain or vomiting. Surgery is consulted for cholecystectomy during this admission after inflammation improves. CT is deferred because she is improving; team notes contrast CT would be indicated if she fails to improve or deteriorates, ideally after 72-96 hours from symptom onset for necrosis assessment.

stem_en: Complete the interpretation of the patient's Stage 1 hypocalcemia.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission history and presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: History: cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago; declined elective cholecystectomy. BMI 34, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, no alcohol use, no prescription medications, no known allergies. Presentation: severe constant epigastric pain 9/10 beginning 45 minutes after a large fatty meal, radiating to the mid-back; four episodes of vomiting; no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea; no urination since pain began.

options.text: renal calcium wasting from diuretic therapy
symptomatic neuromuscular irritability requiring provider notification and IV calcium as ordered
an expected benign finding that only requires routine morning labs
isolated anxiety-related hyperventilation without electrolyte significance

rationale_en: In acute pancreatitis, released lipase digests peripancreatic fat. Free fatty acids bind ionized calcium and form insoluble calcium soaps, lowering physiologically active calcium. The low ionized calcium confirms true hypocalcemia despite low albumin. Provoked facial twitching, carpopedal spasm, and perioral tingling are symptomatic neuromuscular irritability and justify prompt notification and IV calcium replacement as ordered.

stem_en: Complete the interpretation of the patient's Stage 1 hypocalcemia.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: 36-48 hours after admission
options_or_rows_en.content: ERCP at hour 28 removed a 9-mm distal CBD stone by sphincterotomy and balloon sweep. At hour 40: T 37.6 C, HR 92, BP 124/76, RR 18, SpO2 96% room air. Pain is 4/10 and dull; she is alert and conversational. Abdomen less distended; bowel sounds returned but hypoactive. Hour 42 labs: WBC 11,800/mcL, lipase 980, total bilirubin 1.8, AST 88, ALT 140, calcium 8.4, ionized calcium 4.4, glucose 148, CRP 190, lactate 1.2, Hct 36%. Urine output is 300 mL over 6 hours (50 mL/hr). She is hungry. Provider orders low-fat solid food as tolerated; she tolerates clear liquids and a small amount of toast and applesauce without increased pain or vomiting. Surgery is consulted for cholecystectomy during this admission after inflammation improves. CT is deferred because she is improving; team notes contrast CT would be indicated if she fails to improve or deteriorates, ideally after 72-96 hours from symptom onset for necrosis assessment.

options.text: renal calcium wasting from diuretic therapy
symptomatic neuromuscular irritability requiring provider notification and IV calcium as ordered
an expected benign finding that only requires routine morning labs
isolated anxiety-related hyperventilation without electrolyte significance

rationale_en: In acute pancreatitis, released lipase digests peripancreatic fat. Free fatty acids bind ionized calcium and form insoluble calcium soaps, lowering physiologically active calcium. The low ionized calcium confirms true hypocalcemia despite low albumin. Provoked facial twitching, carpopedal spasm, and perioral tingling are symptomatic neuromuscular irritability and justify prompt notification and IV calcium replacement as ordered.

stem_en: Which nursing actions are appropriate at Stage 2? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission history and presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: History: cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago; declined elective cholecystectomy. BMI 34, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, no alcohol use, no prescription medications, no known allergies. Presentation: severe constant epigastric pain 9/10 beginning 45 minutes after a large fatty meal, radiating to the mid-back; four episodes of vomiting; no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea; no urination since pain began.

options_or_rows_en.text: Confirm blood cultures are obtained and anticipate empiric IV antibiotics as ordered for suspected cholangitis.
Facilitate urgent ERCP consultation and preparation for biliary decompression.
Notify the provider about declining urine output and rising lactate; reassess volume status and anticipate a fluid bolus or rate adjustment.
Titrate supplemental oxygen and monitor for worsening respiratory status related to the pleural effusion.
Start oral clear liquids now because she has not vomited for eight hours.
Continue ordered IV opioid analgesia and manage hyperglycemia per the insulin sliding scale.

rationale_en: The patient has suspected cholangitis with an obstructing CBD stone plus signs of hypoperfusion, respiratory compromise, persistent pain, and hyperglycemia. Appropriate nursing actions include escalation and reassessment for additional goal-directed fluids, cultures and antibiotics as ordered, ERCP preparation, oxygen/respiratory monitoring, effective analgesia, and insulin per order. Feeding is not appropriate right now because she declines and feels too nauseated during active deterioration. Immediate CT for necrosis would distract from the time-sensitive biliary source control, and the falling hematocrit alone does not override declining urine output and rising lactate.

stem_en: At Stage 3, the patient asks to eat after ERCP. Which response by the nurse is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission history and presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: History: cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago; declined elective cholecystectomy. BMI 34, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, no alcohol use, no prescription medications, no known allergies. Presentation: severe constant epigastric pain 9/10 beginning 45 minutes after a large fatty meal, radiating to the mid-back; four episodes of vomiting; no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea; no urination since pain began.

options_or_rows_en.text: Keep the patient NPO until lipase returns to the normal range.
Offer the ordered low-fat solid food as tolerated because pain is improving, bowel sounds have returned, vomiting has stopped, and she is hungry.
Require a clear-liquid to full-liquid to soft-diet progression before any low-fat solid food.
Allow only ice chips for 24 more hours because any oral intake can trigger recurrent pancreatitis.

rationale_en: Readiness for oral intake in improving acute pancreatitis is based on clinical tolerance, not lipase normalization. This patient has decreasing pain, returned bowel sounds, no vomiting with liquids, hunger, and an order for low-fat solid food as tolerated. Lipase may remain elevated after clinical improvement. A mandatory liquid-to-solid progression is not required if low-fat solids are tolerated.

options_or_rows_en.title: Admission history and presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: History: cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago; declined elective cholecystectomy. BMI 34, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, no alcohol use, no prescription medications, no known allergies. Presentation: severe constant epigastric pain 9/10 beginning 45 minutes after a large fatty meal, radiating to the mid-back; four episodes of vomiting; no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea; no urination since pain began.

options_or_rows_en.text: Pain 8/10 to 4/10, HR 118 to 92, BP 108/62 to 124/76, temperature 38.4 C to 37.6 C
SpO2 94% on 2 L nasal cannula to 96% on room air
Urine output improves to 50 mL/hr
WBC, bilirubin, calcium, and lactate improve after ERCP
Lipase remains elevated at 980 U/L but is trending down
Recurrent fever or worsening oxygenation after initial improvement
Unable to tolerate oral nutrition after 72 hours
Supports improvement
Escalation/CT trigger if persistent or worsening
Not a management target by itself

rationale_en: The patient is improving because pain, hemodynamics, oxygenation, urine output, bilirubin, WBC, calcium, and lactate are all moving in the right direction after ERCP. Lipase can remain elevated and should not be used alone to decide feeding or recovery. Recurrent fever, worsening oxygenation, organ dysfunction, persistent pain, or inability to tolerate nutrition after 72 hours would require escalation and possible contrast-enhanced CT at an appropriate time to evaluate complications such as necrosis.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that should make the nurse urgently communicate concern for Guillain-Barre syndrome rather than treating the client as stable because he is still ambulatory.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Three weeks before arrival, the client had four days of bloody diarrhea, fever, and cramping. Stool culture at urgent care grew Campylobacter jejuni. He now reports five days of tingling and burning pain that began in both feet and ascended to the mid-thighs. Two days ago he had difficulty climbing stairs; today he had to grip the handrail to climb one flight. He denies shortness of breath, dysphagia, visual changes, bowel or bladder incontinence, or urinary retention at presentation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Three weeks ago, stool culture grew Campylobacter jejuni after bloody diarrhea with fever.
He reports five days of tingling and burning pain that began in both feet and ascended to the mid-thighs.
He had difficulty climbing stairs and now must grip the handrail to climb one flight.
SpO2 is 98% on room air.
Upper extremity strength is 5/5 at presentation.
Lower extremity strength is reduced with bilateral ankle dorsiflexion 3/5 and great toe extension 3/5.
Achilles and patellar reflexes are absent bilaterally.
There is no sensory level on the trunk and rectal tone is normal.
The gait is wide-based with bilateral foot drop.
CBC, electrolytes, CK, ESR, and CRP are unremarkable.

rationale_en: The urgent pattern is a recent Campylobacter infection followed by rapidly progressive, symmetric, ascending sensory symptoms and weakness with areflexia and foot drop. The absence of a sensory level and preserved rectal tone make a spinal cord syndrome less likely, supporting urgent neurologic evaluation for GBS with respiratory monitoring.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that should make the nurse urgently communicate concern for Guillain-Barre syndrome rather than treating the client as stable because he is still ambulatory.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: ICU days 2 through 5
options_or_rows_en.content: The client remains intubated and mechanically ventilated. IVIG is completed through day 5. Autonomic instability persists initially with HR 56-128, BP 88/54-172/104, and two brief self-terminating SVT runs. Short-acting agents are titrated for BP swings. On ICU day 3, ileus develops with absent bowel sounds and abdominal distension; NG tube is placed to low intermittent suction and enteral feeding is held. By ICU day 4, weakness plateaus: upper extremities 2/5, lower extremities 1/5, stable facial weakness, absent gag bilaterally. FVC on a brief spontaneous breathing trial is 0.9 L (11 mL/kg) and MIP -18 cmH2O. He communicates by blinking and mouthing words, is tearful, and fears he will never walk again. Passive ROM, DVT prophylaxis, pain control, and SLP communication support continue. By ICU day 5, autonomic swings narrow to HR 68-102 and BP 104/66-146/88. Bowel sounds return and enteral nutrition restarts. FVC is 1.1 L (13 mL/kg) and MIP -22 cmH2O, improved from nadir but still below safe extubation parameters. The ICU team discusses possible tracheostomy if FVC does not improve toward at least 20 mL/kg over the next several days.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Three weeks before arrival, the client had four days of bloody diarrhea, fever, and cramping. Stool culture at urgent care grew Campylobacter jejuni. He denies shortness of breath, dysphagia, visual changes, bowel or bladder incontinence, or urinary retention at presentation.

options_or_rows_en.text: CSF protein 98 mg/dL with CSF WBC 3 cells/uL
Nerve conduction studies show slowed conduction, conduction block, and prolonged F-wave latencies
Serial bedside FVC and MIP measurements
SpO2 remains 98% on room air at presentation
Baseline FVC 42 mL/kg and MIP -62 cmH2O
Primary respiratory trend to monitor
Indicates bacterial meningitis
Supports GBS/AIDP diagnosis
Potentially misleading if used alone

rationale_en: Albuminocytologic dissociation and demyelinating nerve conduction findings support GBS/AIDP. FVC and MIP directly track respiratory muscle capacity and must be followed serially. A normal SpO2 can be misleading because oxygenation may remain normal until ventilatory failure is advanced.

stem_en: The provider has diagnosed GBS and ordered IVIG, serial FVC/MIP, and ICU notification if respiratory parameters decline. Which statements show correct nursing understanding of the treatment plan and provider-stated respiratory thresholds? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Three weeks before arrival, the client had four days of bloody diarrhea, fever, and cramping. Stool culture at urgent care grew Campylobacter jejuni. He now reports five days of tingling and burning pain that began in both feet and ascended to the mid-thighs. Two days ago he had difficulty climbing stairs; today he had to grip the handrail to climb one flight. He denies shortness of breath, dysphagia, visual changes, bowel or bladder incontinence, or urinary retention at presentation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Per the case parameters, FVC less than 20 mL/kg or MIP weaker than -30 cmH2O should trigger preparation for intubation/escalation.
If a corticosteroid order appears for GBS treatment alone, the nurse should clarify it before administration.
A falling FVC and a MIP that becomes less negative indicate weakening respiratory muscles.
IVIG and plasma exchange are disease-modifying therapies used for GBS; IVIG should be started as ordered.
Normal SpO2 means the client is not at risk for respiratory failure during the next several hours.
High-dose corticosteroids are expected first-line therapy because GBS is immune-mediated.

rationale_en: The ordered IVIG is appropriate disease-modifying therapy. Respiratory decisions in this case are driven by serial FVC and MIP: falling FVC and less negative MIP mean worsening muscle strength. The case-stated escalation thresholds are FVC less than 20 mL/kg or MIP weaker than -30 cmH2O. Corticosteroids are not recommended for GBS treatment and should be clarified if ordered for that purpose.

stem_en: At hour 20, the client has FVC 19 mL/kg, MIP -26 cmH2O, ineffective cough, diaphoresis, labored RR 28, SpO2 93% on 2 L nasal cannula, autonomic instability, and unilateral absent gag reflex. Which nursing actions are priorities? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Three weeks before arrival, the client had four days of bloody diarrhea, fever, and cramping. Stool culture at urgent care grew Campylobacter jejuni. He now reports five days of tingling and burning pain that began in both feet and ascended to the mid-thighs. Two days ago he had difficulty climbing stairs; today he had to grip the handrail to climb one flight. He denies shortness of breath, dysphagia, visual changes, bowel or bladder incontinence, or urinary retention at presentation.

options_or_rows_en.text: Maintain continuous cardiac telemetry and anticipate treatment of labile blood pressure with short-acting titrated agents as ordered.
Prepare for and assist with emergent endotracheal intubation.
Ensure suction, oxygen delivery equipment, IV access, rapid sequence medication readiness as directed, and continuous monitoring are in place.
Continue the IVIG infusion on schedule through the airway transition unless a separate infusion reaction or safety issue occurs.
Wait for SpO2 to fall below 90% before escalating airway management.
Initiate BiPAP as the preferred substitute for intubation because he is still awake.

rationale_en: Both provider-stated respiratory thresholds have been crossed, and the clinical signs confirm impending ventilatory failure with poor airway protection. The nurse should prepare for intubation, support safe airway management, continue disease-modifying IVIG, and maintain telemetry because dysautonomia can cause dangerous BP and rhythm swings.

stem_en: At hour 20, the client has FVC 19 mL/kg, MIP -26 cmH2O, ineffective cough, diaphoresis, labored RR 28, SpO2 93% on 2 L nasal cannula, autonomic instability, and unilateral absent gag reflex. Which nursing actions are priorities? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: ICU days 2 through 5
options_or_rows_en.content: The client remains intubated and mechanically ventilated. IVIG is completed through day 5. Autonomic instability persists initially with HR 56-128, BP 88/54-172/104, and two brief self-terminating SVT runs. Short-acting agents are titrated for BP swings. On ICU day 3, ileus develops with absent bowel sounds and abdominal distension; NG tube is placed to low intermittent suction and enteral feeding is held. By ICU day 4, weakness plateaus: upper extremities 2/5, lower extremities 1/5, stable facial weakness, absent gag bilaterally. FVC on a brief spontaneous breathing trial is 0.9 L (11 mL/kg) and MIP -18 cmH2O. He communicates by blinking and mouthing words, is tearful, and fears he will never walk again. Passive ROM, DVT prophylaxis, pain control, and SLP communication support continue. By ICU day 5, autonomic swings narrow to HR 68-102 and BP 104/66-146/88. Bowel sounds return and enteral nutrition restarts. FVC is 1.1 L (13 mL/kg) and MIP -22 cmH2O, improved from nadir but still below safe extubation parameters. The ICU team discusses possible tracheostomy if FVC does not improve toward at least 20 mL/kg over the next several days.

options_or_rows_en.text: Maintain continuous cardiac telemetry and anticipate treatment of labile blood pressure with short-acting titrated agents as ordered.
Prepare for and assist with emergent endotracheal intubation.
Ensure suction, oxygen delivery equipment, IV access, rapid sequence medication readiness as directed, and continuous monitoring are in place.
Continue the IVIG infusion on schedule through the airway transition unless a separate infusion reaction or safety issue occurs.
Wait for SpO2 to fall below 90% before escalating airway management.
Initiate BiPAP as the preferred substitute for intubation because he is still awake.

rationale_en: Both provider-stated respiratory thresholds have been crossed, and the clinical signs confirm impending ventilatory failure with poor airway protection. The nurse should prepare for intubation, support safe airway management, continue disease-modifying IVIG, and maintain telemetry because dysautonomia can cause dangerous BP and rhythm swings.

stem_en: Complete the nurse's evaluation of the day-5 ICU status.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Three weeks before arrival, the client had four days of bloody diarrhea, fever, and cramping. Stool culture at urgent care grew Campylobacter jejuni. He now reports five days of tingling and burning pain that began in both feet and ascended to the mid-thighs. Two days ago he had difficulty climbing stairs; today he had to grip the handrail to climb one flight. He denies shortness of breath, dysphagia, visual changes, bowel or bladder incontinence, or urinary retention at presentation.

stem_en: Using the Rule of Nines, calculate the percent total body surface area burned. Include only the entire face/anterior scalp, entire anterior trunk, and both anterior arms from shoulders to wrists.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history, no known allergies, and no home medications. Found conscious but confused in a smoke-filled bedroom approximately 40 minutes before ED arrival. Estimated weight is 80 kg. Visible burns involve the face, anterior chest, abdomen, and both anterior arms. Eyebrows and nasal hairs are singed. Carbonaceous sputum is noted when he coughs. He reports throat tightness and difficulty swallowing and speaks in a hoarse, raspy voice.

rationale_en: The burned areas are the anterior head/face 4.5%, anterior trunk 18%, right anterior arm 4.5%, and left anterior arm 4.5%, for a total of 31.5% TBSA. The posterior trunk, posterior arms, legs, and perineum are unburned and do not contribute to the calculation.

stem_en: Using the Rule of Nines, calculate the percent total body surface area burned. Include only the entire face/anterior scalp, entire anterior trunk, and both anterior arms from shoulders to wrists.

options_or_rows_en.title: Over-resuscitation cues
options_or_rows_en.content: At 1600, urine output is 55 mL/hr, HR 100/min, BP 112/72 mmHg, and the patient is hemodynamically improving. At 1800, urine output has been 120 mL/hr for two consecutive hours. New bilateral basilar crackles are present; the face and eyelids are markedly more edematous; extremities are also becoming edematous. Labs: sodium 134 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, hematocrit 38%, albumin 2.1 g/dL. At 2000, urine output remains 110 mL/hr, peak airway pressures rise from 22 to 30 cmH2O, abdominal girth has increased by 4 cm from 1400, and the previously unburned hands are tense with capillary refill of 5 seconds and weaker radial pulses. The burn team notes that delivered volume has exceeded the time-phased expected Parkland trajectory by about 20%. At 2200, after the infusion rate is reduced, urine output is 70 mL/hr, peak airway pressure is 28 cmH2O, abdominal girth is stable, capillary refill in the hands is 4 seconds, and bladder pressure is 18 mmHg.

rationale_en: The burned areas are the anterior head/face 4.5%, anterior trunk 18%, right anterior arm 4.5%, and left anterior arm 4.5%, for a total of 31.5% TBSA. The posterior trunk, posterior arms, legs, and perineum are unburned and do not contribute to the calculation.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that support impending upper-airway obstruction requiring emergent intubation rather than continued observation on a non-rebreather mask.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history, no known allergies, and no home medications. Found conscious but confused in a smoke-filled bedroom approximately 40 minutes before ED arrival. Estimated weight is 80 kg. options_or_rows_en.text: Found in a smoke-filled bedroom.
Awake but anxious.
Hoarse, raspy voice that worsens over 30 minutes.
Reports throat tightness and difficulty swallowing.
Carbonaceous sputum when coughing.
Posterior pharynx has soot deposits with erythema and edema.
Progressive facial and lip edema.
Inspiratory stridor audible without a stethoscope.
SpO2 94% on a non-rebreather mask.
Blood pressure 88/58 mmHg and heart rate 130/min.

rationale_en: Enclosed-space smoke exposure plus worsening hoarseness, throat tightness/dysphagia, carbonaceous sputum, pharyngeal soot/edema, progressive facial/lip edema, and audible stridor indicate inhalation injury with impending upper-airway obstruction. SpO2 can remain deceptively acceptable, especially with carbon monoxide exposure and supplemental oxygen, while edema progresses.

stem_en: The provider orders lactated Ringer's using the commonly taught Parkland estimate: 4 mL x kg x %TBSA, with half in the first 8 hours from the burn time of 0600. The patient weighs 80 kg, has 31.5% TBSA burned, and arrives at 0640. Calculate the total 24-hour volume and the initial calculated rate from 0640 to 1400 before urine-output titration.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history, no known allergies, and no home medications. Found conscious but confused in a smoke-filled bedroom approximately 40 minutes before ED arrival. Estimated weight is 80 kg. Visible burns involve the face, anterior chest, abdomen, and both anterior arms. Eyebrows and nasal hairs are singed. Carbonaceous sputum is noted when he coughs. He reports throat tightness and difficulty swallowing and speaks in a hoarse, raspy voice.

rationale_en: Total volume is 4 mL x 80 kg x 31.5 = 10,080 mL. Half is 5,040 mL due by 1400, which is 8 hours after the 0600 burn. Because the patient arrives 40 minutes after the burn, the remaining first-half window is 7 hours 20 minutes, or 7.33 hours. 5,040 mL divided by 7.33 hours is approximately 687 mL/hr, then the rate is titrated to urine output.

stem_en: At 1000, the urine output has improved from 25 mL/hr to 35 mL/hr, but the ordered target is 40 to 80 mL/hr. The urine has lightened from dark amber to dark yellow. Which action should the nurse take?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation

options_or_rows_en.text: Increase the lactated Ringer's infusion rate per the titration protocol and reassess hourly.
Maintain the current rate because the urine color is improving.
Decrease the infusion rate to prevent fluid overload because the blood pressure is improving.
Stop titrating fluids and request a genitourinary workup for dark urine.

rationale_en: Urine output remains below the minimum target of 40 mL/hr, so the infusion should be increased per protocol and reassessed hourly. The urine color trend is reassuring but does not replace the urine-output target.

Which nursing actions are appropriate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and initial presentation
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history, no known allergies, and no home medications. Found conscious but confused in a smoke-filled bedroom approximately 40 minutes before ED arrival. Estimated weight is 80 kg. Visible burns involve the face, anterior chest, abdomen, and both anterior arms. Eyebrows and nasal hairs are singed. Carbonaceous sputum is noted when he coughs. He reports throat tightness and difficulty swallowing and speaks in a hoarse, raspy voice.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform serial hand perfusion assessments including pulses, capillary refill, sensation, and swelling.
Initiate or continue serial bladder pressure monitoring.
Notify the burn team about crackles, rising airway pressures, increasing abdominal girth, and extremity edema.
Maintain or increase the infusion because the Parkland estimate covers 24 hours.
Treat the crackles by increasing ventilator support without changing the fluid rate.
Reduce the lactated Ringer's infusion rate according to the titration order.

rationale_en: The findings indicate fluid creep: urine output is above target and there are signs of pulmonary edema, increased intra-abdominal pressure risk, and extremity edema compromising perfusion. Appropriate nursing actions are to reduce the infusion per protocol, notify the burn team, monitor bladder pressure, and perform serial extremity perfusion assessments.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are most concerning for acute pulmonary embolism with possible right ventricular compromise.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Postoperative day 2 after right total knee arthroplasty. History: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, BMI 33, 30-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago. No prior VTE or known coagulopathy. Postoperative VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, SCDs to both legs, incentive spirometry every hour while awake, and early ambulation with physical therapy twice daily. Overnight documentation: refused SCDs at 2200 because they were uncomfortable; declined 0600 ambulation because knee pain was 7/10. Surgical drain removed on postoperative day 1 with minimal output. Wound dressing is clean, dry, and intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: Postoperative day 2 after right total knee arthroplasty.
Refused SCDs overnight and declined morning ambulation because of knee pain.
Sudden onset of dyspnea and pleuritic right-sided chest pain.
Temperature 37.2 C.
Heart rate 122/min.
Blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, below preoperative baseline 138/82 mmHg.
SpO2 87% on room air.
Lung sounds clear bilaterally.
Visible jugular venous distension at 45 degrees.
Surgical dressing clean, dry, and intact.
Right calf warm, erythematous, edematous, and tender with a 5-cm larger circumference than the left.
Client is pale, diaphoretic, anxious, and speaking in short phrases.

rationale_en: The key cue cluster is sudden pleuritic chest pain, severe dyspnea and hypoxemia, tachycardia, diaphoresis and pallor, blood pressure below baseline, JVD, and unilateral calf findings in a postoperative client with immobility and refusal of SCDs. These findings collectively point to acute PE with DVT source and possible RV strain.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that are most concerning for acute pulmonary embolism with possible right ventricular compromise.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: ICU monitoring and response to therapy
options_or_rows_en.content: The pulmonary team classifies the PE as intermediate-risk/submassive because of RV strain and positive biomarkers without sustained SBP below 90 mmHg. Systemic thrombolysis is not ordered because the client is hemodynamically stable and is postoperative day 2 from major orthopedic surgery. The plan is ICU transfer, therapeutic UFH, close monitoring, and escalation if sustained hypotension or obstructive shock develops. At 1000: chest pain 6/10, HR 112/min, BP 102/70 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 94% on non-rebreather, urine output 25 mL over 30 minutes. At 1200: HR 106/min, BP 108/72 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 95% on non-rebreather, chest pain 4/10, speaking in full sentences, urine output 35 mL/hr over the past 2 hours. The surgical dressing remains clean, dry, and intact. At 1330: troponin I 0.42 ng/mL and lactate 2.4 mmol/L. The first aPTT is scheduled for 1520, and repeat echocardiography is planned in 24 hours.

Systemic thrombolysis is not ordered because the client is hemodynamically stable and is postoperative day 2 from major orthopedic surgery. The plan is ICU transfer, therapeutic UFH, close monitoring, and escalation if sustained hypotension or obstructive shock develops. At 1000: chest pain 6/10, HR 112/min, BP 102/70 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 94% on non-rebreather, urine output 25 mL over 30 minutes. At 1200: HR 106/min, BP 108/72 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 95% on non-rebreather, chest pain 4/10, speaking in full sentences, urine output 35 mL/hr over the past 2 hours. The surgical dressing remains clean, dry, and intact. At 1330: troponin I 0.42 ng/mL and lactate 2.4 mmol/L. The first aPTT is scheduled for 1520, and repeat echocardiography is planned in 24 hours.

options_or_rows_en.text: ST-elevation myocardial infarction requiring immediate coronary catheterization
Aortic dissection requiring impulse control and surgical evaluation
Pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain
Postoperative atelectasis requiring incentive spirometry and reassurance

rationale_en: The unilateral calf findings, recent orthopedic surgery with reduced prophylaxis adherence, sudden pleuritic dyspnea, hypoxemia, CT-confirmed saddle embolus, elevated RV strain biomarkers, and right-heart-strain ECG findings make pulmonary embolism with RV strain the primary condition.

stem_en: Which statements best explain the appropriateness of the current treatment plan? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Postoperative day 2 after right total knee arthroplasty. History: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, BMI 33, 30-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago. No prior VTE or known coagulopathy. Postoperative VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, SCDs to both legs, incentive spirometry every hour while awake, and early ambulation with physical therapy twice daily. Overnight documentation: refused SCDs at 2200 because they were uncomfortable; declined 0600 ambulation because knee pain was 7/10. Surgical drain removed on postoperative day 1 with minimal output. Wound dressing is clean, dry, and intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: Recent major orthopedic surgery increases the bleeding risk of systemic thrombolysis while the client remains hemodynamically stable.
Protamine sulfate can reverse UFH more completely than it reverses LMWH.
Systemic thrombolysis is indicated now because a saddle embolus is large, even without sustained hypotension.
Therapeutic anticoagulation should be withheld until the chest pain completely resolves.
UFH can be titrated quickly and has a short half-life, which is helpful if bleeding or urgent intervention occurs.
If sustained hypotension or obstructive shock develops, escalation to catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy may be considered.

rationale_en: UFH is a reasonable facility-protocol choice in this high bleeding-risk postoperative context because it can be titrated, stopped quickly, and reversed. Systemic thrombolysis is not the current priority because the client is intermediate-risk/submassive rather than massive/high-risk and is only 2 days from major orthopedic surgery. If he develops sustained hypotension or obstructive shock, escalation to advanced reperfusion options may be considered.

stem_en: The UFH bolus and infusion have been ordered. Which nursing actions are appropriate now? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background and risk factors
options_or_rows_en.content: Postoperative day 2 after right total knee arthroplasty. History: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, BMI 33, 30-pack-year smoking history, quit 5 years ago. No prior VTE or known coagulopathy. Postoperative VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, SCDs to both legs, incentive spirometry every hour while awake, and early ambulation with physical therapy twice daily. Wound dressing is clean, dry, and intact.

options_or_rows_en.text: Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring and prepare for ICU transfer.
Administer the ordered UFH bolus and begin the infusion without delaying for ICU transfer.
Place the client supine or in Trendelenburg to increase venous return and improve blood pressure.
Maintain the non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min with continuous SpO2 monitoring.
Keep the head of the bed elevated in high Fowler's or a semi-recumbent position.
Assess and document the surgical site for new bleeding or expanding hematoma after anticoagulation begins.

rationale_en: The nurse should start ordered therapeutic anticoagulation promptly, continue high-flow oxygen with SpO2 monitoring, keep the client upright or semi-recumbent to support ventilation and avoid worsening RV overload, monitor continuously, prepare for ICU transfer, and assess for postoperative bleeding after anticoagulation begins. These interventions are concurrent; ICU transfer preparation should not delay anticoagulation or oxygen support.

options.text: heart rate, blood pressure, oxygenation, urine output, lactate, and pain are trending in the right direction
D-dimer is elevated and confirms that anticoagulation has dissolved the clot
the client no longer requires a non-rebreather mask
an expected biomarker trend that does not outweigh the improving clinical status
proof that systemic thrombolysis is now required
evidence that the primary problem is STEMI instead of PE
continue the current plan and monitor for hypotension, worsening hypoxemia, decreased level of consciousness, low urine output, and bleeding
request immediate systemic thrombolysis based only on the troponin trend
stop the UFH infusion because the chest pain has improved

rationale_en: The overall trajectory is improving: heart rate is lower, blood pressure is higher, SpO2 and respiratory rate are improved, pain has decreased, urine output has recovered, and lactate has fallen. A small troponin rise can occur after the initial RV strain and does not by itself indicate deterioration when bedside perfusion and oxygenation data are improving. The client still requires ICU monitoring and therapeutic anticoagulation.
