stem_en: Highlight the findings that support the pattern of acute variceal hemorrhage in a decompensated cirrhotic patient.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Known Child-Pugh B alcohol-related cirrhosis. Prior screening EGD showed grade II esophageal varices 18 months ago. He has ascites managed with furosemide and spironolactone, mild intermittent hepatic encephalopathy treated with lactulose, no anticoagulants, no NSAIDs, and no beta-blocker because baseline systolic pressures were in the low 90s.

options_or_rows_en.text: Large-volume bright red hematemesis about 45 minutes before arrival.
Second smaller episode of hematemesis occurred during transport.
HR 118 beats/min.
BP 88/54 mmHg.
Pale, diaphoretic appearance with capillary refill of 3 seconds.
SpO2 96% on 2 L nasal cannula.
Clear breath sounds bilaterally.
Moderate-to-large ascites with fluid wave and shifting dullness.
Caput medusae on the anterior abdominal wall.
Spider angiomata on the upper chest.
Scleral icterus.
No asterixis and coherent speech.
INR 1.9.
Platelets 72,000/µL.
Albumin 2.4 g/dL.
Glucose 102 mg/dL.

rationale_en: The emergency pattern is massive hematemesis with shock physiology, plus clear evidence of portal hypertension and impaired hepatic synthetic function. Normal oxygenation, clear lungs, normal glucose, and absence of asterixis are useful baselines but do not explain the acute hemorrhage.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that support the pattern of acute variceal hemorrhage in a decompensated cirrhotic patient.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3: 12-24 hours after arrival
options_or_rows_en.content: Overnight, the patient becomes confused and somnolent, opens eyes to voice, follows simple commands, believes he is at home, and develops asterixis. Vitals: HR 82, BP 108/66, RR 14, SpO2 97% room air, T 37.9 C. Hgb 7.6 g/dL is stable with no further hematemesis. Labs: creatinine 1.8 mg/dL, urine output 15 mL/hr, ammonia 124 µmol/L, lactate 1.8 mmol/L. Diagnostic paracentesis shows ascitic WBC 620 cells/µL with 78% neutrophils, absolute neutrophil count 484 cells/µL, and SAAG 1.3 g/dL. Orders include lactulose every 2 hours titrated to 3-4 bowel movements/day, rifaximin every 12 hours, holding diuretics, and urgent SBP evaluation/treatment reassessment.

options.text: ongoing hemorrhagic shock with inadequate tissue perfusion
stable recovery after the fluid bolus
a lactate rise from 3.4 to 4.1 mmol/L
only 25 mL urine output in the first hour
hemoglobin now above the target range
oxygen saturation of 97% on nasal cannula

rationale_en: The marginal BP response, persistent tachycardia, repeat hematemesis, rising lactate, and oliguria show that perfusion remains inadequate. A hemoglobin of 7.2 g/dL in this context supports single-unit PRBC transfusion to the stated restrictive target, not liberal transfusion.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Known Child-Pugh B alcohol-related cirrhosis. Prior screening EGD showed grade II esophageal varices 18 months ago. options_or_rows_en.text: Mallory-Weiss tear from forceful retching
Lower gastrointestinal bleeding from hemorrhoids
Esophageal variceal hemorrhage related to portal hypertension
Peptic ulcer bleeding from NSAID use

rationale_en: Known cirrhosis, portal hypertension, documented esophageal varices, large-volume hematemesis, ascites, caput medusae, and coagulopathy all point to variceal hemorrhage. The nurse should anticipate variceal-specific therapy while endoscopy is being arranged.

stem_en: The provider orders 1 unit PRBC with a target hemoglobin of 7-8 g/dL. Which concurrent interventions should the nurse implement or anticipate? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Known Child-Pugh B alcohol-related cirrhosis. Prior screening EGD showed grade II esophageal varices 18 months ago. He has ascites managed with furosemide and spironolactone, mild intermittent hepatic encephalopathy treated with lactulose, no anticoagulants, no NSAIDs, and no beta-blocker because baseline systolic pressures were in the low 90s.

options_or_rows_en.text: Maintain NPO status and prepare for emergent EGD with band ligation.
Transfuse additional PRBCs until hemoglobin reaches 10 g/dL.
Administer ceftriaxone prophylaxis as ordered.
Position with head of bed elevated and maintain airway readiness.
Give fresh frozen plasma solely to normalize the INR before endoscopy.
Continue the octreotide infusion as ordered.

rationale_en: Octreotide, ceftriaxone, endoscopy readiness, NPO status, and airway protection are concurrent priorities in suspected variceal hemorrhage. The case protocol sets a restrictive PRBC target; routine liberal transfusion or large-volume plasma just to normalize cirrhosis-related INR can worsen volume/portal-pressure problems and delay definitive hemostasis.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Known Child-Pugh B alcohol-related cirrhosis. Prior screening EGD showed grade II esophageal varices 18 months ago. options_or_rows_en.text: Continue the octreotide infusion per protocol.
Monitor for true rebleeding: new hematemesis, hemodynamic decline, or falling serial hemoglobin.
Treat the small amount of melena alone as proof of active rebleeding.
Stop ceftriaxone because endoscopic hemostasis was achieved.
Monitor mental status, ammonia trend, urine output, and creatinine.
Transfuse more PRBCs now because hemoglobin is 7.8 g/dL.
Not appropriate now
Appropriate now

rationale_en: After banding, the hemorrhage markers are improving, but early rebleeding, infection, hepatic encephalopathy, and renal injury remain major risks. Melena alone can reflect digested earlier blood when hemoglobin and hemodynamics are stable.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history
options_or_rows_en.content: Known Child-Pugh B alcohol-related cirrhosis. Prior screening EGD showed grade II esophageal varices 18 months ago. options_or_rows_en.text: Notify the provider that ascitic PMN count meets the SBP diagnostic threshold and anticipate antibiotic reassessment.
Recognize hepatic encephalopathy and administer lactulose/rifaximin as ordered.
Continue serial neurological assessments, including orientation, level of consciousness, and asterixis.
Attribute the confusion to residual procedural sedation and defer lactulose until the next routine assessment.
Closely monitor urine output and creatinine because SBP with AKI raises concern for hepatorenal syndrome.
Give additional PRBCs to raise hemoglobin above 8 g/dL because the patient looks worse.

rationale_en: The hemorrhage appears controlled, but the patient has new hepatic encephalopathy, SBP by ascitic PMN count, and worsening renal function. The nursing priority shifts to treating encephalopathy, communicating SBP results, and tracking neurologic and renal trajectories.

stem_en: Highlight the assessment findings that require immediate follow-up for postpartum hemorrhage from uterine atony.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background
options_or_rows_en.content: G5P5, one hour postpartum after vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant. Second stage lasted 3 hours; oxytocin augmentation was used during first stage. Placenta delivered intact and appeared complete. Pregnancy-induced hypertension was diagnosed at 34 weeks and treated with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. History includes mild intermittent asthma. No bleeding disorder, prior PPH, or uterine surgery. Admission BP 148/94 mmHg. Admission Hgb 11.8 g/dL, Hct 35.2%. Blood type O-positive; type and screen sent on admission. Oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR has been infusing for about 50 minutes after placental delivery.

options_or_rows_en.text: Temperature 36.8 °C.
Heart rate 118 beats/min.
Blood pressure 90/62 mmHg, down from her hypertensive admission baseline of 148/94 mmHg.
SpO2 97% on room air.
The uterus is two fingerbreadths above the umbilicus, deviated to the right, and boggy.
It does not stay firm after gentle massage.
Lochia is heavy with a steady dark-red flow and several small clots.
The repaired second-degree perineal laceration has no active bleeding and no visible hematoma.
The bladder is palpable above the symphysis pubis.
Skin is cool and clammy with capillary refill of 3 seconds.
Estimated blood loss is 600 mL and visibly increasing.
She denies chest pain or shortness of breath.

options.text: the boggy fundus briefly firms with massage but immediately relaxes

stem_en: The provider orders a second uterotonic. Which medication should the nurse administer from the available unit options?

options_or_rows_en.title: Background
options_or_rows_en.content: G5P5, one hour postpartum after vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant. Second stage lasted 3 hours; oxytocin augmentation was used during first stage. Placenta delivered intact and appeared complete. Pregnancy-induced hypertension was diagnosed at 34 weeks and treated with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. History includes mild intermittent asthma. No bleeding disorder, prior PPH, or uterine surgery. Admission BP 148/94 mmHg. Admission Hgb 11.8 g/dL, Hct 35.2%. Blood type O-positive; type and screen sent on admission. Oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR has been infusing for about 50 minutes after placental delivery.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer carboprost tromethamine 250 mcg IM.
Administer methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM.
Administer misoprostol 800 mcg rectally.
Hold all additional uterotonics until the repeat CBC and coagulation panel result.

rationale_en: Misoprostol is the safe second-line uterotonic in this client because methylergonovine is contraindicated by pregnancy-induced hypertension and carboprost is unsafe with asthma. Current hemorrhagic hypotension does not remove the methylergonovine contraindication.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background
options_or_rows_en.content: G5P5, one hour postpartum after vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant. Second stage lasted 3 hours; oxytocin augmentation was used during first stage. Placenta delivered intact and appeared complete. Pregnancy-induced hypertension was diagnosed at 34 weeks and treated with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. History includes mild intermittent asthma. No bleeding disorder, prior PPH, or uterine surgery. Admission BP 148/94 mmHg. Admission Hgb 11.8 g/dL, Hct 35.2%. Blood type O-positive; type and screen sent on admission. Oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR has been infusing for about 50 minutes after placental delivery.

options_or_rows_en.text: Have another team member send the type and crossmatch specimen immediately.
Maintain continuous external fundal massage or assist with bimanual compression.
Stop fundal massage so the primary nurse can draw the laboratory specimens first.
Start tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes per the PPH protocol after uterotonic administration is underway.
Continue rapid warmed IV fluid resuscitation through large-bore access.
Administer the selected second uterotonic without delay.

rationale_en: This is not a rigid ordered sequence. The team must maintain source control with massage/compression and a safe uterotonic while restoring circulating volume, starting ordered TXA within the protocol window, and preparing blood products through another team member. Lab and blood-bank tasks should not interrupt uterine compression.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background
options_or_rows_en.content: G5P5, one hour postpartum after vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant. Second stage lasted 3 hours; oxytocin augmentation was used during first stage. No bleeding disorder, prior PPH, or uterine surgery. Admission BP 148/94 mmHg. Admission Hgb 11.8 g/dL, Hct 35.2%. Blood type O-positive; type and screen sent on admission. options_or_rows_en.text: Uterus is firm and contracted at the umbilicus.
Lochia has decreased to moderate dark-red flow without clots.
Quantitative blood loss has plateaued at 1,450 mL with no new accumulation for 15 minutes.
Client is alert, oriented, and no longer confused.
Blood pressure has improved from 80/52 to 94/60 mmHg.
Hemoglobin is 7.4 g/dL after crystalloid resuscitation and transfusion has begun.
Fibrinogen has trended down from 410 to 280 to 240 mg/dL.
Foley output is 25 mL/hr of concentrated amber urine.
Heart rate is improved but remains 112 beats/min.
Evidence of early stabilization
Requires continued concern/monitoring

rationale_en: The case is improving because uterine tone is restored, lochia is reduced, QBL has plateaued, mentation is clear, and BP is trending up. She is not fully stable: hemoglobin remains severely low, fibrinogen is falling, urine output is below 30 mL/hr, and tachycardia persists, so transfusion and close monitoring must continue.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background
options_or_rows_en.content: G5P5, one hour postpartum after vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg infant. Second stage lasted 3 hours; oxytocin augmentation was used during first stage. Placenta delivered intact and appeared complete. Pregnancy-induced hypertension was diagnosed at 34 weeks and treated with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. History includes mild intermittent asthma. No bleeding disorder, prior PPH, or uterine surgery. Admission BP 148/94 mmHg. Admission Hgb 11.8 g/dL, Hct 35.2%. Blood type O-positive; type and screen sent on admission. Oxytocin 20 units in 1 L LR has been infusing for about 50 minutes after placental delivery.

options_or_rows_en.text: Quantitative blood loss resumes climbing despite uterotonics and compression.
Urine output remains 25 mL/hr but bleeding remains controlled.
The uterus becomes boggy again with renewed heavy lochia.
Fibrinogen falls below 200 mg/dL with ongoing bleeding risk.
Hemoglobin is 7.4 g/dL while the uterus is firm and QBL is plateaued.
Heart rate improves from 132 to 112 beats/min.

rationale_en: Escalation is driven by loss of source control or evolving coagulopathy: recurrent bogginess with heavy bleeding, QBL that rises despite uterotonics and compression, or fibrinogen falling below the critical threshold. Low hemoglobin, oliguria, or persistent tachycardia require treatment and monitoring, but when bleeding is controlled they do not by themselves mandate surgical escalation.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that form the celiac malabsorption and dermatitis herpetiformis cue cluster.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history. Six months of foul-smelling, pale, bulky stools 3-5 times daily, bloating/cramping after meals, 10-lb unintentional weight loss, fatigue, occasional mouth sores, and a 3-month pruritic blistering rash on elbows and knees. Denies bloody stools, fever, joint swelling, or recent travel. Thin-appearing, BMI 19.0. Abdomen mildly distended and diffusely mildly tender without guarding or rebound. Symmetric erythematous papulovesicular lesions on extensor elbows and knees; pale conjunctivae; mild koilonychia.

options_or_rows_en.text: Six months of foul-smelling, pale, bulky stools that are difficult to flush.
Unintentional 10-lb weight loss despite unchanged appetite.
Abdominal bloating and cramping are worse after meals.
Two small aphthous ulcers are present on the buccal mucosa.
Hemoglobin is 10.4 g/dL with MCV 74 fL, pale conjunctivae, and koilonychia.
Calcium is 7.6 mg/dL and albumin is 3.3 g/dL.
Clusters of intensely pruritic papulovesicular lesions are symmetric on the extensor elbows and knees.
ESR and CRP are within normal limits.
Stool occult blood is negative.
No fever, bloody stool, recent travel, or joint swelling is reported.

rationale_en: Steatorrhea, weight loss, postprandial bloating, aphthous ulcers, iron-deficiency-pattern anemia, hypocalcemia/hypoalbuminemia, and a symmetric pruritic papulovesicular extensor-surface rash cluster around proximal small-bowel malabsorption with dermatitis herpetiformis. Normal inflammatory markers and negative occult blood help move inflammatory bowel disease or hemorrhagic causes lower on the differential.

stem_en: Highlight the findings that form the celiac malabsorption and dermatitis herpetiformis cue cluster.

options_or_rows_en.title: Treatment response evaluation
options_or_rows_en.content: The patient eliminated hidden gluten sources and has been strictly gluten-free for three months. She uses wheat-free tamari and gluten-free communion wafers. She takes iron, folic acid, and vitamin D. She is on dapsone 50 mg daily with dermatology planning taper after rash control. Rash is nearly resolved; stools are formed, brown, once daily; bloating resolved; energy improved; mouth sores have not recurred; weight is 123 lb. Labs: Hgb 12.1 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 22 ng/mL, folate 8.6 ng/mL, vitamin D 32 ng/mL, calcium 8.6 mg/dL, PTH 42 pg/mL, albumin 3.8 g/dL, tTG-IgA 38 U/mL. The provider explains the falling tTG-IgA supports response and orders DEXA because prior vitamin D deficiency, hypocalcemia, and secondary hyperparathyroidism increase bone-health risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Six months of foul-smelling, pale, bulky stools that are difficult to flush.
Unintentional 10-lb weight loss despite unchanged appetite.
Abdominal bloating and cramping are worse after meals.
Two small aphthous ulcers are present on the buccal mucosa.
Hemoglobin is 10.4 g/dL with MCV 74 fL, pale conjunctivae, and koilonychia.
Calcium is 7.6 mg/dL and albumin is 3.3 g/dL.
Clusters of intensely pruritic papulovesicular lesions are symmetric on the extensor elbows and knees.
ESR and CRP are within normal limits.
Stool occult blood is negative.
No fever, bloody stool, recent travel, or joint swelling is reported.

rationale_en: Steatorrhea, weight loss, postprandial bloating, aphthous ulcers, iron-deficiency-pattern anemia, hypocalcemia/hypoalbuminemia, and a symmetric pruritic papulovesicular extensor-surface rash cluster around proximal small-bowel malabsorption with dermatitis herpetiformis. Normal inflammatory markers and negative occult blood help move inflammatory bowel disease or hemorrhagic causes lower on the differential.

options_or_rows_en.text: tTG-IgA 128 U/mL with total IgA 245 mg/dL
Duodenal biopsy: villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, increased intraepithelial lymphocytes
Iron 28 µg/dL, ferritin 8 ng/mL, MCV 74 fL
Vitamin D 14 ng/mL, low calcium, PTH 78 pg/mL
Vitamin B12 280 pg/mL
Normal ESR/CRP and negative stool occult blood
Confirms or supports celiac disease
Shows malabsorption-related deficiency
Expected/sparing pattern or counter-cue

rationale_en: Stage 1 confirms celiac disease by pairing markedly positive tTG-IgA with normal total IgA and characteristic duodenal histology. The iron/folate/vitamin D/calcium pattern reflects proximal small-bowel malabsorption. B12 is normal in this case and is not the main proximal-malabsorption signal, while normal ESR/CRP and negative occult blood are counter-cues against active IBD or bleeding.

options_or_rows_en.text: Crohn disease with extraintestinal skin manifestations
Irritable bowel syndrome with eczema

Celiac disease with dermatitis herpetiformis

rationale_en: The markedly elevated tTG-IgA with normal total IgA, Marsh 3a duodenal biopsy, steatorrhea, proximal malabsorption deficiencies, and classic extensor papulovesicular rash point to celiac disease with dermatitis herpetiformis. Perilesional skin biopsy is used to confirm the skin manifestation; the duodenal biopsy confirms intestinal celiac disease.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history. Six months of foul-smelling, pale, bulky stools 3-5 times daily, bloating/cramping after meals, 10-lb unintentional weight loss, fatigue, occasional mouth sores, and a 3-month pruritic blistering rash on elbows and knees. Denies bloody stools, fever, joint swelling, or recent travel. Thin-appearing, BMI 19.0. Abdomen mildly distended and diffusely mildly tender without guarding or rebound. options_or_rows_en.text: Advise stopping dapsone now because the gluten-free diet should immediately clear the rash.
Teach the patient that soy sauce containing wheat and standard communion wafers are ongoing gluten exposures.
Review cross-contamination prevention and ask the pharmacist to verify medications and supplements for gluten-containing inactive ingredients.
Reinforce folate supplementation because the folate level is low and red blood cell production must recover.
Explain that dapsone can relieve dermatitis herpetiformis symptoms but does not treat intestinal villous injury.
Tell the patient that partial GI improvement means small weekly gluten exposures are acceptable.

rationale_en: The incomplete response is explained by ongoing hidden gluten exposure and incomplete supplement adherence. The nurse should target the identified gluten sources, reinforce strict lifelong avoidance and cross-contamination prevention, explain dapsone's symptomatic role, and support correction of documented deficiencies.

Labs: Hgb 12.1 g/dL, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 22 ng/mL, folate 8.6 ng/mL, vitamin D 32 ng/mL, calcium 8.6 mg/dL, PTH 42 pg/mL, albumin 3.8 g/dL, tTG-IgA 38 U/mL. The provider explains the falling tTG-IgA supports response and orders DEXA because prior vitamin D deficiency, hypocalcemia, and secondary hyperparathyroidism increase bone-health risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: Advise stopping dapsone now because the gluten-free diet should immediately clear the rash.
Teach the patient that soy sauce containing wheat and standard communion wafers are ongoing gluten exposures.
Review cross-contamination prevention and ask the pharmacist to verify medications and supplements for gluten-containing inactive ingredients.
Reinforce folate supplementation because the folate level is low and red blood cell production must recover.
Explain that dapsone can relieve dermatitis herpetiformis symptoms but does not treat intestinal villous injury.
Tell the patient that partial GI improvement means small weekly gluten exposures are acceptable.

rationale_en: The incomplete response is explained by ongoing hidden gluten exposure and incomplete supplement adherence. The nurse should target the identified gluten sources, reinforce strict lifelong avoidance and cross-contamination prevention, explain dapsone's symptomatic role, and support correction of documented deficiencies.

stem_en: Classify each teaching statement as appropriate or inappropriate for this patient.

options_or_rows_en.title: Initial history and assessment
options_or_rows_en.content: No significant past medical history. Six months of foul-smelling, pale, bulky stools 3-5 times daily, bloating/cramping after meals, 10-lb unintentional weight loss, fatigue, occasional mouth sores, and a 3-month pruritic blistering rash on elbows and knees. Denies bloody stools, fever, joint swelling, or recent travel. Thin-appearing, BMI 19.0. Abdomen mildly distended and diffusely mildly tender without guarding or rebound. Symmetric erythematous papulovesicular lesions on extensor elbows and knees; pale conjunctivae; mild koilonychia.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use only oats labeled gluten-free; introduce them cautiously once the diet is stable.
All oats must be permanently avoided by every person with celiac disease.
The rash may take much longer than GI symptoms to resolve, so dapsone may be used while the diet takes effect.
Because stools are improving, folate is no longer necessary.
Report cyanosis, shortness of breath, jaundice, fever, sore throat, severe fatigue, or widespread rash while taking dapsone.
The nurse should stress scheduled blood monitoring early in dapsone therapy.
Inappropriate teaching
Appropriate teaching

rationale_en: Teaching should be practical and individualized: certified gluten-free oats may be acceptable for many patients, dermatitis herpetiformis often lags behind GI improvement, documented folate deficiency still requires treatment, and dapsone requires toxicity teaching plus monitoring.

options.text: treatment failure requiring immediate repeat biopsy
successful response to the gluten-free diet
proof that she continues to ingest gluten intentionally

rationale_en: At four months, formed stools, resolved bloating, weight gain, cleared rash, improved energy, normalized hemoglobin/MCV, improved ferritin, folate, vitamin D, calcium, PTH, albumin, and a falling tTG-IgA together indicate response. stem_en: At Stage 1, classify each assessment finding as consistent with the suspected left MCA stroke syndrome or normal/unrelated to that syndrome.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED arrival record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 68-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Home medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and warfarin for stroke prevention. Anticoagulation clinic INR five days ago was 2.3. Wife witnessed sudden onset; EMS documented last known well 45 minutes before ED arrival. No prior stroke, recent surgery, known bleeding disorder, or prior intracranial hemorrhage.

Presentation: awake and attentive, looking to the left. Produces only single words or short effortful phrases with paraphasic errors; cannot reliably repeat sentences. Follows simple commands with the left side but does not move the right arm or right leg to command. Wife reports he dropped a coffee cup with the right hand and could not form words.

options_or_rows_en.text: Right arm and right leg weakness, with the arm more affected
Expressive aphasia with impaired repetition
Right lower facial droop
Forced leftward gaze preference
Irregularly irregular heart rhythm with atrial fibrillation history
Full strength in the left arm and left leg
Equal, reactive 3-mm pupils on arrival
Clear lung sounds and normal troponin
Normal or unrelated to this stroke syndrome
Consistent with left MCA stroke/cardioembolic mechanism

rationale_en: Right hemiplegia, expressive aphasia, right lower facial droop, and left gaze preference localize to a dominant left MCA cortical stroke. Atrial fibrillation and sudden onset support a cardioembolic mechanism. Intact left-sided strength, initially equal reactive pupils, clear lungs, and a normal troponin do not explain the focal left MCA syndrome.

stem_en: At Stage 1, classify each assessment finding as consistent with the suspected left MCA stroke syndrome or normal/unrelated to that syndrome.

options_or_rows_en.title: Confirmed hemorrhage and stabilization orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
The nurse immediately notifies the neurointerventionalist and neurology team. Emergent non-contrast CT head shows a 3 cm left frontoparietal intraparenchymal hemorrhage with surrounding edema and 4 mm left-to-right midline shift. Provider gives a stat order to titrate nicardipine to maintain systolic BP below 140 mmHg. Neurosurgery is consulted. Client is intubated for airway protection; GCS is 7. Wife is updated by the attending neurologist and goals-of-care discussion is initiated.

Repeat labs: INR 1.6, hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, platelets 174,000/µL. Provider orders IV vitamin K 10 mg and four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate for residual warfarin effect. Head of bed is elevated to 30 degrees. Seizure precautions are in place. Continuous ICP monitoring is being arranged.

options.text: delayed until the clinic INR from five days ago is repeated
indicated because the CT is negative
the blood pressure is above 180 systolic
the current INR of 1.8 exceeds the institutional threshold
the client took warfarin this morning regardless of the INR
communicate the stat INR and support the pivot to CTA/thrombectomy evaluation
give aspirin immediately for secondary prevention
prepare alteplase while waiting for the historical clinic INR

rationale_en: The case states a closed-world institutional protocol: IV alteplase is contraindicated when the INR exceeds 1.7 at intended administration. This client's current stat INR is 1.8, so the nurse should communicate that finding and support rapid CTA and thrombectomy evaluation rather than focusing on the clinic INR from five days ago or assuming a negative CT alone makes alteplase appropriate.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED arrival record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 68-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Home medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and warfarin for stroke prevention. Anticoagulation clinic INR five days ago was 2.3. Wife witnessed sudden onset; EMS documented last known well 45 minutes before ED arrival. No prior stroke, recent surgery, known bleeding disorder, or prior intracranial hemorrhage.

Presentation: awake and attentive, looking to the left. Produces only single words or short effortful phrases with paraphasic errors; cannot reliably repeat sentences. Follows simple commands with the left side but does not move the right arm or right leg to command. Wife reports he dropped a coffee cup with the right hand and could not form words.

options_or_rows_en.text: Primary hypertensive encephalopathy; delay vascular imaging until BP is normal
Hemorrhagic stroke; prepare anticoagulation reversal before further imaging
Acute ischemic stroke from a likely cardioembolic left MCA occlusion; proceed with CTA for thrombectomy evaluation
Lacunar stroke from chronic hypertension; no large-vessel imaging is needed

rationale_en: The sudden onset, atrial fibrillation, aphasia, gaze preference, and dense right-sided deficits fit a large-vessel left MCA ischemic stroke, most likely cardioembolic. The initial CT excludes hemorrhage, and alteplase is excluded by the institutional INR criterion, so CTA for large-vessel occlusion and thrombectomy evaluation is the priority.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the client is a thrombectomy candidate with BP 186/102 mmHg. Which nursing actions are appropriate before transfer to the neurointerventional suite? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED arrival record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 68-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Home medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and warfarin for stroke prevention. Anticoagulation clinic INR five days ago was 2.3. Wife witnessed sudden onset; EMS documented last known well 45 minutes before ED arrival. No prior stroke, recent surgery, known bleeding disorder, or prior intracranial hemorrhage.

Presentation: awake and attentive, looking to the left. Produces only single words or short effortful phrases with paraphasic errors; cannot reliably repeat sentences. Follows simple commands with the left side but does not move the right arm or right leg to command. Wife reports he dropped a coffee cup with the right hand and could not form words.

options_or_rows_en.text: Maintain strict NPO status until a trained dysphagia screen is completed
Start the prescribed IV nicardipine infusion and titrate per protocol toward the pre-thrombectomy BP threshold
Request IV nitroprusside as the preferred first-line agent for rapid BP reduction
Ensure two patent IV lines and continuous cardiac monitoring
Verify that proxy consent has been obtained because the client cannot provide informed consent due to aphasia
Administer oral antihypertensive medication with sips of water to lower the BP gradually

rationale_en: For a thrombectomy candidate, the nurse supports ordered titratable BP control, maintains NPO status because dysphagia risk has not been cleared, ensures IV access and monitoring, and confirms proxy consent when the neurologic deficit prevents informed consent. Oral intake before swallowing screening is unsafe. Nitroprusside is generally avoided as an initial first-line stroke BP agent because it can raise intracranial pressure and cause abrupt hypotension; the case order is nicardipine.

stem_en: At Stage 3, 45 minutes after neuro-ICU arrival, the client becomes drowsy, stops following commands, has no verbal output, and develops a new sluggish left pupil. Which actions should the nurse take immediately? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED arrival record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 68-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Home medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and warfarin for stroke prevention. Anticoagulation clinic INR five days ago was 2.3. Wife witnessed sudden onset; EMS documented last known well 45 minutes before ED arrival. No prior stroke, recent surgery, known bleeding disorder, or prior intracranial hemorrhage.

Presentation: awake and attentive, looking to the left. Produces only single words or short effortful phrases with paraphasic errors; cannot reliably repeat sentences. Follows simple commands with the left side but does not move the right arm or right leg to command. Wife reports he dropped a coffee cup with the right hand and could not form words.

options_or_rows_en.text: Perform and document a rapid focused neurologic assessment compared with the post-procedure baseline
Let the client rest until the next scheduled neurologic check because fatigue is expected after the procedure
Continue close BP monitoring while emergency treatment is initiated
Prepare for emergent non-contrast CT head
Maintain airway patency and prepare for possible intubation
Notify the neurointerventionalist and neurology team stat

rationale_en: Abrupt decline from an improving post-thrombectomy baseline, especially with new pupil asymmetry, is a neurologic emergency consistent with intracranial hemorrhage or mass effect until proven otherwise. The nurse should notify the team stat, prepare for emergent CT, protect the airway, rapidly document the neurologic change from baseline, and continue BP monitoring. Waiting for the next scheduled check or focusing on a clean groin site would delay care.

options_or_rows_en.title: ED arrival record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: 68-year-old man with hypertension and atrial fibrillation. Home medications include amlodipine 10 mg daily, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, and warfarin for stroke prevention. Anticoagulation clinic INR five days ago was 2.3. Wife witnessed sudden onset; EMS documented last known well 45 minutes before ED arrival. No prior stroke, recent surgery, known bleeding disorder, or prior intracranial hemorrhage.

Presentation: awake and attentive, looking to the left. Produces only single words or short effortful phrases with paraphasic errors; cannot reliably repeat sentences. Follows simple commands with the left side but does not move the right arm or right leg to command. Wife reports he dropped a coffee cup with the right hand and could not form words.

options_or_rows_en.text: Systolic BP trend and response to the nicardipine infusion
Appearance of the right groin dressing as the main marker of intracranial hemorrhage stability
Serial troponin levels because the initial rhythm was atrial fibrillation
NIHSS score as the primary tool while the client is intubated and nonverbal
Serial neurologic examination: GCS, pupil size/reactivity, and motor response

rationale_en: In acute intracerebral hemorrhage with mass effect, the most actionable bedside indicators are serial neurologic status and BP response. Worsening GCS, fixed or enlarging pupil, or worsening motor response suggests expanding hematoma or rising ICP. Uncontrolled systolic BP can drive hematoma expansion, so response to the nicardipine target must be tracked. NIHSS is less useful in an intubated nonverbal client; troponin and the clean groin site do not evaluate the intracranial hemorrhage.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the clinical cues from Stage 1. For each cue, indicate whether it supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), neutropenic fever/sepsis risk, or is a nonspecific finding. Each row may have more than one correct category.

options_or_rows_en.title: Background / Admission orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
A 58-year-old man with relapsed diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is admitted to the bone marrow transplant and cellular therapy unit for axicabtagene ciloleucel (axi-cel) CAR-T cell infusion. He previously received two lines of chemoimmunotherapy with initial response followed by progressive disease. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily and type 2 diabetes managed with metformin 1000 mg twice daily; metformin was held 48 hours before admission per protocol. He has no history of cardiac disease, pulmonary disease, or neurological conditions. Lymphodepleting chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide was completed three days ago, and the CAR-T cell product was infused this morning at 0800 without immediate infusion reaction.

Standing orders include continuous telemetry, strict intake and output (patient is using a urinal, and urine output is measured every 4 hours), neurological assessments every 4 hours using the ICE (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Encephalopathy) scoring tool, vital signs every 4 hours for the first 24 hours then escalating to every 2 hours or more frequently if any signs of cytokine release syndrome (CRS) emerge. Tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV is available at bedside for CRS management per institutional protocol, and dexamethasone is readily accessible. IV normal saline is infusing at 75 mL/hr for maintenance hydration. The patient has a double-lumen peripherally inserted central catheter (PICC).

options_or_rows_en.text: Fever, rigors, and myalgias
Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) 0.1 × 10³/µL
CRP 86 mg/L and ferritin 1,450 ng/mL
Mild fatigue reported at 1600
Supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS)
Supports neutropenic fever/sepsis risk
Nonspecific or expected finding

rationale_en: Recognizing the clinical signs of both CRS and neutropenic fever is critical in the post-CAR-T setting. Fever, rigors, and myalgias are hallmark initial signs of CRS but are also classic symptoms of sepsis in a neutropenic patient. An ANC of 0.1 × 10³/µL places the patient at profound risk for neutropenic fever and sepsis, requiring immediate intervention. The elevation of CRP and ferritin strongly supports the onset of CRS. Mild fatigue is a nonspecific finding often expected following lymphodepleting chemotherapy.

stem_en: The nurse reviews the clinical cues from Stage 1. For each cue, indicate whether it supports early Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), neutropenic fever/sepsis risk, or is a nonspecific finding. Each row may have more than one correct category.

options_or_rows_en.content:

The patient is alert and oriented to year, month, city, and hospital. ICE score has improved from 6/10 to 9/10 (one point off for naming; he was unable to name one of the three objects but could write a full sentence, sustain attention, and follow commands). His wife says, "He seems much more like himself." Lungs are clear; crackles have resolved. Urine output over the past 4 hours is 220 mL (improving).

Repeat labs: CRP 110 mg/L (still elevated but trending down from 245), ferritin 5,400 ng/mL (down from 8,200), fibrinogen 240 mg/dL (improving from 180), LDH 420 U/L, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (improving from 1.6), AST 52 U/L, ALT 48 U/L, D-dimer 1.6 µg/mL FEU, potassium 4.4 mEq/L.

The provider continues dexamethasone on a taper schedule, maintains tocilizumab availability for a second dose if the patient re-spikes fever or becomes hypotensive again, and asks the nurse to continue frequent vital signs and neurological assessments every 4 hours. options_or_rows_en.content:

options_or_rows_en.text: Assume the fever is solely expected CRS and defer infection workup.
Anticipate initiating empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics per neutropenic fever protocol.
Notify the provider and activate the institutional CRS pathway.
Administer acetaminophen as ordered for symptom management.
Obtain blood cultures per protocol, including central-line lumens.
Increase the frequency of vital signs monitoring and continue ICE scoring.

rationale_en: In a profoundly neutropenic patient post-CAR-T infusion, a new fever must be treated concurrently as potential Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS) and a neutropenic fever/sepsis medical emergency. The nurse should notify the provider to activate the CRS pathway, obtain blood cultures per protocol, anticipate initiating broad-spectrum antibiotics, administer acetaminophen for symptom management, and increase the frequency of monitoring and ICE scoring. Waiting for culture results before giving antibiotics or assuming the fever is only CRS can cause fatal delays in treating sepsis.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer dexamethasone IV as ordered for concurrent probable ICANS.
Begin the ordered normal saline IV fluid bolus.
Prepare for possible ICU transfer while continuing frequent monitoring.
Apply low-flow oxygen via nasal cannula and raise the head of the bed.
Administer tocilizumab IV as ordered for CRS Grade 2.

rationale_en: Using the ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) framework, the nurse must first stabilize oxygenation by applying low-flow oxygen and raising the head of the bed. Second, initiate the IV fluid bolus to address hypotension and support perfusion. Once basic physiological stability is addressed, pharmacological interventions follow: administer tocilizumab (the definitive treatment for CRS) and then dexamethasone (for concurrent ICANS). Finally, prepare for potential ICU transfer while continuing frequent monitoring. Transfer preparation and team communication may occur in parallel, but they must not delay life-saving oxygen, fluids, or ordered medications.

options_or_rows_en.text: Hypotension (BP 88/52) and low-flow oxygen need
ICE score 6/10, disorientation, and attention difficulty
Creatinine rising from 1.0 to 1.6 mg/dL with oliguria
Falling fibrinogen with rising D-dimer
Persistent fever to 39.8 °C and rigors
Indicates CRS progression or severity
Indicates Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS)
Indicates early end-organ or coagulation dysfunction

rationale_en: CRS severity grading is primarily driven by fever, hypotension, and hypoxia. Therefore, hypotension requiring fluids and hypoxia requiring low-flow oxygen, along with persistent fever, indicate CRS severity. ICANS is distinct and indicated by neurological changes such as a dropping ICE score, disorientation, and attention difficulty. Rising creatinine with oliguria and falling fibrinogen with rising D-dimer suggest early end-organ and coagulation dysfunction, which are consequences of severe systemic CRS inflammation.

options_or_rows_en.content:

He previously received two lines of chemoimmunotherapy with initial response followed by progressive disease. options.text: an increased need for oxygen support
a resolving fever and improved blood pressure without vasopressors
improving urine output and an ICE score of 9/10
confirms complete resolution of the inflammatory process
indicates that neurotoxicity is fully reversed
is an unreliable marker alone because tocilizumab suppresses CRP production
monitoring can be safely reduced to baseline frequency
the patient can be immediately discharged
frequent monitoring must continue because CRS or ICANS can recur

rationale_en: Clinical improvement after tocilizumab and dexamethasone must be evaluated using multiple parameters. A resolving fever, improved blood pressure without vasopressors, improved urine output, and an ICE score of 9/10 indicate genuine recovery from CRS and ICANS. However, the nurse must recognize that tocilizumab suppresses CRP production by blocking the IL-6 pathway, making a declining CRP an unreliable marker alone. Frequent monitoring must continue because CRS or ICANS can recur or fluctuate after initial improvement.

stem_en: The nurse receives the patient on ED arrival. Which of the following is the most appropriate interpretation of the patient's current status based on the prehospital report?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient requires a third dose of intravenous lorazepam in the ED before progressing to second-line agents.
The patient is experiencing an expected postictal phase after prehospital treatment.
The patient's continuous seizure activity is likely due to prehospital medication administration errors.
The patient is in established status epilepticus because two adequate doses of first-line therapy have failed.

rationale_en: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting more than five minutes or two or more seizures without return to baseline consciousness. Once adequate benzodiazepine doses have failed, the Neurocritical Care Society and AES guidelines direct immediate administration of a second-line agent. Delay increases morbidity, mortality, and the likelihood of pharmacoresistance. Recognizing the failure of first-line therapy is the critical nursing cue that drives the urgency of escalation.

stem_en: The nurse receives the patient on ED arrival. Which of the following is the most appropriate interpretation of the patient's current status based on the prehospital report?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course
options_or_rows_en.content: 35 to 70 minutes after ED arrival (approximately 50–85 minutes of total seizure duration and into the first ICU hour). After 10 minutes of the midazolam infusion, visible motor seizure activity ceases. However, continuous EEG reveals ongoing electrographic seizure activity (rhythmic spike-and-wave discharges) for an additional 8 minutes before the midazolam infusion rate is titrated upward to 0.3 mg/kg/hr and electrographic seizures finally terminate. The patient is now in a pharmacologically induced state of burst-suppression on EEG, which is the ordered ICU protocol target for this case. Post-intubation vitals begin to stabilize: T 38.4 °C (trending down with cooling measures), HR 112, BP 128/78 (now on vasopressor-free arterial-line monitoring), RR set at 16 on the ventilator, SpO₂ 99% on FiO₂ 60%. Urine output over the first ICU hour is 120 mL, which is below the target of 1–3 mL/kg/hr; the urine is tea-colored and urine myoglobin returns markedly positive. The nurse reports this, and IV isotonic fluids are titrated up toward 300 mL/hr per protocol/order for rhabdomyolysis management. Repeat labs at the one-hour ICU mark: lactate 5.2 mmol/L (falling), CK 2,800 U/L (still rising but expected to peak and then fall), potassium 5.3 mEq/L (stable, being monitored closely), creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (slight rise), ABG pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 38 mmHg (improved with mechanical ventilation), PaO₂ 142 mmHg (on FiO₂ 60%), bicarbonate 18 mEq/L (improving). The nurse maintains continuous EEG monitoring and reports any return of electrographic seizure patterns to the provider, keeps the head of bed elevated at 30 degrees, monitors the midazolam infusion, performs neurological assessments (pupil reactivity, response to stimuli) at least hourly, monitors urine output and color for rhabdomyolysis resolution, and maintains seizure precautions including padded side rails and suction at bedside.

options_or_rows_en.text: The patient requires a third dose of intravenous lorazepam in the ED before progressing to second-line agents.
The patient is experiencing an expected postictal phase after prehospital treatment.
The patient's continuous seizure activity is likely due to prehospital medication administration errors.
The patient is in established status epilepticus because two adequate doses of first-line therapy have failed.

rationale_en: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting more than five minutes or two or more seizures without return to baseline consciousness. Once adequate benzodiazepine doses have failed, the Neurocritical Care Society and AES guidelines direct immediate administration of a second-line agent. Delay increases morbidity, mortality, and the likelihood of pharmacoresistance. Recognizing the failure of first-line therapy is the critical nursing cue that drives the urgency of escalation.

stem_en: Review the baseline assessment and laboratory data. For each finding, determine if it indicates a primary cause of the status epilepticus or a secondary consequence of the prolonged seizure.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level
Lactate 6.8 mmol/L
Bicarbonate 16 mEq/L
Mildly elevated CK (480 U/L)
Secondary Consequence
Likely Precipitating Factor / Major Contributor

rationale_en: Medication nonadherence is a common major contributor to status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy, and the subtherapeutic levetiracetam level strongly supports this. Identifying this helps the team target therapy while continuing evaluation for other precipitants after stabilization. Lactate elevation and metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) are expected consequences of sustained seizure-related muscle activity and do not independently require sodium bicarbonate infusion — they resolve when seizures stop. Early CK elevation is a secondary consequence that flags the need for aggressive IV hydration to prevent myoglobin-induced acute kidney injury.

stem_en: Review the baseline assessment and laboratory data. For each finding, determine if it indicates a primary cause of the status epilepticus or a secondary consequence of the prolonged seizure.

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course
options_or_rows_en.content: 35 to 70 minutes after ED arrival (approximately 50–85 minutes of total seizure duration and into the first ICU hour). After 10 minutes of the midazolam infusion, visible motor seizure activity ceases. However, continuous EEG reveals ongoing electrographic seizure activity (rhythmic spike-and-wave discharges) for an additional 8 minutes before the midazolam infusion rate is titrated upward to 0.3 mg/kg/hr and electrographic seizures finally terminate. The patient is now in a pharmacologically induced state of burst-suppression on EEG, which is the ordered ICU protocol target for this case. Post-intubation vitals begin to stabilize: T 38.4 °C (trending down with cooling measures), HR 112, BP 128/78 (now on vasopressor-free arterial-line monitoring), RR set at 16 on the ventilator, SpO₂ 99% on FiO₂ 60%. Urine output over the first ICU hour is 120 mL, which is below the target of 1–3 mL/kg/hr; the urine is tea-colored and urine myoglobin returns markedly positive. The nurse reports this, and IV isotonic fluids are titrated up toward 300 mL/hr per protocol/order for rhabdomyolysis management. Repeat labs at the one-hour ICU mark: lactate 5.2 mmol/L (falling), CK 2,800 U/L (still rising but expected to peak and then fall), potassium 5.3 mEq/L (stable, being monitored closely), creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (slight rise), ABG pH 7.30, PaCO₂ 38 mmHg (improved with mechanical ventilation), PaO₂ 142 mmHg (on FiO₂ 60%), bicarbonate 18 mEq/L (improving). The nurse maintains continuous EEG monitoring and reports any return of electrographic seizure patterns to the provider, keeps the head of bed elevated at 30 degrees, monitors the midazolam infusion, performs neurological assessments (pupil reactivity, response to stimuli) at least hourly, monitors urine output and color for rhabdomyolysis resolution, and maintains seizure precautions including padded side rails and suction at bedside.

options_or_rows_en.text: Subtherapeutic levetiracetam level
Lactate 6.8 mmol/L
Bicarbonate 16 mEq/L
Mildly elevated CK (480 U/L)
Secondary Consequence
Likely Precipitating Factor / Major Contributor

rationale_en: Medication nonadherence is a common major contributor to status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy, and the subtherapeutic levetiracetam level strongly supports this. Identifying this helps the team target therapy while continuing evaluation for other precipitants after stabilization. Lactate elevation and metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) are expected consequences of sustained seizure-related muscle activity and do not independently require sodium bicarbonate infusion — they resolve when seizures stop. Early CK elevation is a secondary consequence that flags the need for aggressive IV hydration to prevent myoglobin-induced acute kidney injury.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 1. The nurse is administering the prescribed fosphenytoin IV. Which of the following are priority nursing interventions during this infusion? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the infusion as an IV push over 1-2 minutes.
Monitor blood pressure every 5 minutes.
Observe for cessation of seizure activity.
Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring.
Ensure the infusion rate does not exceed 150 mg PE/min.

rationale_en: The nurse must monitor for cardiac arrhythmias (continuous telemetry), hypotension (blood pressure every 5 minutes during infusion), and observe for seizure cessation as the therapeutic endpoint. Fosphenytoin can cause QT prolongation, bradycardia, and hypotension through its effect on cardiac sodium channels, particularly at rapid infusion rates. Cardiovascular collapse is a known fatal complication of too-rapid administration. The infusion rate must not exceed 150 mg PE/min.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 2. The nurse observes that motor seizure activity persists, SpO₂ is 91%, end-tidal CO₂ is 58 mmHg, and the patient has copious secretions with increasing cyanosis. Which of the following problems should the nurse prioritize at this time?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Refractory seizure activity requiring additional antiepileptics
Developing rhabdomyolysis
Compromised airway and impending respiratory failure
Worsening metabolic acidosis

rationale_en: Even though terminating the seizure is the ultimate goal, the patient cannot survive the seizure if she cannot oxygenate and ventilate. The falling SpO₂, rising CO₂, and copious secretions signal impending respiratory failure. Applying the ABCs framework, airway protection through intubation takes precedence. Furthermore, the continuous infusion agents used for refractory status epilepticus (midazolam, propofol) cause respiratory depression and require a secured airway before they can be safely administered. Therefore, intubation both protects the patient's breathing and enables definitive seizure treatment.

options.text: cessation of visible motor activity does not equal seizure termination
the midazolam dose is too low to produce any effect
the patient is paralyzed from the succinylcholine alone

rationale_en: In status epilepticus, especially after prolonged seizure activity and the administration of neuromuscular blocking agents during RSI (succinylcholine), motor manifestations may cease while cortical seizure activity continues — this is called nonconvulsive status epilepticus. Ongoing electrographic seizure activity causes continued neuronal injury. Continuous EEG is the gold standard for confirming seizure termination. In this case, the ordered therapeutic target for the continuous midazolam infusion is burst-suppression on EEG.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course for Stage 3. The patient's urine output over the first ICU hour is 120 mL, and the urine myoglobin is markedly positive. Which of the following nursing actions is most appropriate?

options_or_rows_en.title: Baseline Assessment & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Temperature 38.3 °C (tympanic), heart rate 138 bpm and regular on the monitor, blood pressure 178/102 mmHg, respiratory rate 28 (shallow and irregular, partially obscured by motor activity), SpO₂ 88% on room air. Pupils are equal at 4 mm but reactivity is difficult to assess during convulsive activity. Skin is diaphoretic and flushed. Bite injury to the lateral tongue with blood-tinged saliva. No signs of head trauma. The single peripheral IV is intact and running normal saline at a keep-vein-open rate. Continuous cardiac monitoring shows sinus tachycardia without ectopy. End-tidal CO₂ (if capnography is available) is 52 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation. Fingerstick blood glucose is 142 mg/dL. A point-of-care urine pregnancy test is obtained and returns negative.

Laboratory results (drawn on arrival, returning over next 20-30 min):
Sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 16 mEq/L (low — lactic acidosis from prolonged muscle activity), BUN 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 148 mg/dL, calcium (ionized) 1.14 mmol/L (normal), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (normal), phosphorus 4.2 mg/dL, lactate 6.8 mmol/L (elevated), WBC 14,200/µL (stress leukocytosis), hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, platelets 224,000/µL, CK 480 U/L (mildly elevated, expected to rise), levetiracetam serum level 4.2 µg/mL (subtherapeutic; reference range 12–46 µg/mL). Arterial blood gas: pH 7.24, PaCO₂ 48 mmHg, PaO₂ 68 mmHg, HCO₃ 16 mEq/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Decrease the IV fluid rate to 50 mL/hr to prevent fluid volume overload during mechanical ventilation.
Titrate IV isotonic fluids up toward 300 mL/hr as ordered to manage rhabdomyolysis.
Remove the indwelling urinary catheter to reduce the risk of catheter-associated infection.
Administer an IV fluid bolus of dextrose 5% in water to correct metabolic acidosis.

rationale_en: Sustained tonic-clonic muscle activity causes massive skeletal muscle breakdown, releasing myoglobin into the bloodstream. Myoglobin is freely filtered by the glomerulus and precipitates in renal tubules, causing acute kidney injury. The combination of rising CK, dark urine, and positive urine myoglobin confirms rhabdomyolysis. Early aggressive crystalloid hydration (e.g., isotonic fluids titrated to maintain high urine output per protocol, often 1-3 mL/kg/hr or up to 300 mL/hr) is the cornerstone of prevention of renal failure.

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to administer the scheduled immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy before Stage 1 begins. Based on the initial assessment and baseline laboratory data, which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the PRN ondansetron for the patient's nausea before starting the infusion.
Hold the infusion and immediately notify the oncologist of the laboratory results and patient symptoms.
Proceed with the infusion and monitor the patient's vital signs every 15 minutes.
Hold the infusion and plan to communicate the findings to the oncologist during routine rounds.

rationale_en: The nurse should hold the infusion and immediately notify the oncologist of the elevated troponin I (0.18 ng/mL), elevated BNP, transaminase elevations, new exertional chest symptoms, and resting tachycardia. Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause fulminant myocarditis, which carries a mortality rate of up to 25–50% when diagnosed late. An elevated troponin in a patient on combination ICI therapy with new cardiac symptoms is a red-flag cue that must be reported before administering another dose of the causative agent, because additional dosing could accelerate the immune-mediated injury.

stem_en: The nurse is preparing to administer the scheduled immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy before Stage 1 begins. Based on the initial assessment and baseline laboratory data, which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 Clinical Course
options_or_rows_en.content: 18 to 24 hours after initiation of pulse-dose corticosteroids. The patient has received two doses of methylprednisolone 1 g IV. Repeat vital signs: T 37.0 °C, HR 94 and regular, BP 114/72, RR 18, SpO₂ 96% on 2 L nasal cannula. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm at 94 bpm with resolution of the frequent PVCs; PR interval has shortened to 0.20 seconds, though ST depressions persist. The patient reports chest heaviness at 2 out of 10 and can sit up and speak in full sentences without dyspnea. Repeat labs: troponin I 0.64 ng/mL (declining from 1.42), BNP 390 pg/mL (declining), AST 132 U/L (declining), ALT 148 U/L (declining), total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL, CRP 4.1 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, CK 420 U/L (mildly elevated), blood glucose 218 mg/dL (new hyperglycemia attributed to high-dose corticosteroids). The patient's urine output has averaged 55 mL/hr over the last 8 hours. The care team discusses the treatment plan: a corticosteroid taper over several weeks, ongoing troponin and liver-enzyme monitoring every 6 to 12 hours, cardiology follow-up with a repeat echocardiogram in 48 to 72 hours, and the permanent discontinuation of combination ICI therapy. The oncologist notes that for this patient’s grade 3–4 ICI myocarditis, the treatment plan is permanent discontinuation and the patient should not restart checkpoint inhibitor therapy unless a future specialist oncology/cardiology team explicitly determines otherwise. A new concern at this stage is the steroid-induced hyperglycemia, which requires blood glucose monitoring every 4 to 6 hours and possible sliding-scale insulin coverage.

options_or_rows_en.text: Administer the PRN ondansetron for the patient's nausea before starting the infusion.
Hold the infusion and immediately notify the oncologist of the laboratory results and patient symptoms.
Proceed with the infusion and monitor the patient's vital signs every 15 minutes.
Hold the infusion and plan to communicate the findings to the oncologist during routine rounds.

Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Metastatic disease progression into the cardiopulmonary system.
Immune checkpoint inhibitor–induced myocarditis with concurrent immune-mediated hepatitis.
Viral myocarditis presenting as an isolated infectious process.
Acute coronary syndrome resulting from atherosclerotic plaque rupture.

rationale_en: The combination of diffuse (not territory-specific) ST-segment changes, a new conduction abnormality (prolonging PR interval), troponin elevation, BNP elevation, and concurrent hepatic transaminase elevation in a patient actively receiving combination anti–PD-1/anti–CTLA-4 therapy is the hallmark presentation of ICI myocarditis with multi-organ immune-related adverse events. The concurrent hepatotoxicity suggests a systemic immune-mediated process rather than a single-organ etiology.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the patient is hemodynamically deteriorating. The nurse receives several urgent orders. In what order should the nurse perform the following actions? All options must be used.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ensure continuous telemetry and reliable IV access.
Administer the high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone.
Apply supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO₂ ≥ 94%.
Prepare the patient for transfer to a higher-acuity monitored cardiac unit.

rationale_en: After oxygen is applied, the nurse rapidly verifies continuous telemetry and patent IV access so high-dose IV methylprednisolone can be administered without delay. These steps should occur immediately and should not postpone the first steroid dose. Transfer is important but follows oxygenation, monitoring/access verification, and initiation of time-critical immunosuppression.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. **LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Serial troponin and liver-enzyme monitoring every 6 to 12 hours.
Blood glucose monitoring every 4 to 6 hours.
Discontinuation of cardiac monitoring once the patient's PR interval normalizes.
Assessment for overlapping myositis or myasthenia-like symptoms.
Continuous telemetry with alarms set for arrhythmias and conduction changes.

rationale_en: The nurse should generate a solution set that includes continuous telemetry with alarms set for arrhythmias and conduction changes (especially worsening AV block, which can progress to complete heart block), serial troponin levels to track trajectory, serial liver-enzyme monitoring, and blood glucose monitoring anticipating steroid-induced hyperglycemia. The nurse should also assess for signs of other immune-related adverse events, because ICI myocarditis can overlap with neuromuscular immune toxicity. Discontinuing cardiac monitoring is unsafe because ICI myocarditis can deteriorate rapidly to complete heart block or fatal arrhythmia.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the oncologist writes an order to permanently discontinue all ICI therapy. What is the most appropriate action by the nurse regarding this medication order?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs
options_or_rows_en.content:
**ASSESSMENT FINDINGS**
Baseline assessment on arrival: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 102 and regular, blood pressure 118/74, respiratory rate 20, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Heart sounds are present without murmur, rub, or gallop; lung fields are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation, with no hepatomegaly appreciable at the costal margin. Skin shows faint residual erythema on the trunk from the prior rash; no new rash. Peripheral pulses are 2+ and symmetric; no peripheral edema. Telemetry at baseline shows sinus tachycardia at 102 beats per minute with normal P-wave morphology, PR interval 0.16 seconds, and QRS duration 0.09 seconds. The patient rates her chest heaviness as 3 out of 10 and states it is not pleuritic.

**LABORATORY DATA**
Baseline labs drawn this morning: WBC 7.2 × 10³/µL (differential: neutrophils 68%, lymphocytes 18%, monocytes 10%, eosinophils 4%), hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 × 10³/µL, sodium 139 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, glucose 104 mg/dL, AST 88 U/L (normal up to 35), ALT 112 U/L (normal up to 40), alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, troponin I 0.18 ng/mL (normal < 0.04), BNP 245 pg/mL (normal < 100), CRP 3.8 mg/dL (normal < 0.5), TSH 2.1 mIU/L, LDH 310 U/L.

options_or_rows_en.text: Document the ICI discontinuation as a temporary hold and inform the patient that therapy will resume after cardiac recovery.
Verify the ICI is held/discontinued in the MAR per the provider order, ensure the adverse drug reaction is documented, and educate the patient about the permanent discontinuation.
Independently delete the ICI order from the patient's active medication list to prevent inadvertent administration by another nurse.
Request an order to transition the patient from combination therapy to single-agent immunotherapy.

rationale_en: The nurse should verify the ICI is held/discontinued in the MAR per the provider order; document the medication as not given with the clinical reason; notify pharmacy/oncology if the order remains active; ensure the adverse drug reaction is documented per facility policy; and educate the patient that the immunotherapy was stopped because of a serious heart-and-liver immune reaction. Permanent discontinuation is the standard of care for grade 3–4 ICI myocarditis, given the high fatality risk with re-exposure.
