stem_en: Review the patient's baseline assessment findings. The nurse should recognize that the combination of escalating mid-back pain, new bilateral leg weakness, and sensory/bladder changes is most highly suggestive of which oncologic emergency?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation. Lower extremity motor strength is 4 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps and 4 minus out of 5 in ankle dorsiflexors, with the left side slightly weaker than the right. She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: Opioid-induced neurotoxicity
Malignant spinal cord compression
Paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration
Hypercalcemia of malignancy

rationale_en: The combination of escalating back pain at the level of known metastases, new motor weakness with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, positive Babinski), a definable sensory level, and autonomic (bladder) dysfunction is the classic presentation of malignant spinal cord compression. This is an oncologic emergency requiring urgent intervention.

stem_en: Review the patient's baseline assessment findings. The nurse should recognize that the combination of escalating mid-back pain, new bilateral leg weakness, and sensory/bladder changes is most highly suggestive of which oncologic emergency?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course — Postoperative Day 1
options_or_rows_en.content: Approximately 30 hours after admission (roughly 18 hours after posterior laminectomy): The patient is transferred back to the oncology unit from the PACU. She is awake, alert, and oriented. Surgical site dressing at the posterior thoracic spine is clean, dry, and intact with a Jackson-Pratt drain in place with 40 mL of serosanguineous output over the past 8 hours. Vital signs: temperature 37.8 °C, heart rate 78, blood pressure 124/74, respiratory rate 16, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L nasal cannula. Pain is 5 out of 10 at the surgical site, managed on IV hydromorphone. On neurological reassessment: lower extremity motor strength is now 3 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps — improved from the 2 out of 5 seen preoperatively. Ankle dorsiflexion is 2 out of 5 bilaterally (improved on the left from 1 out of 5). She can wiggle all toes weakly on command. Deep tendon reflexes remain hyperreflexic (3+) but clonus is no longer sustained — only 1 beat at the left ankle, absent on the right. Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally. Sensory level has improved: she now perceives light touch beginning at T8 and pinprick at T9. Perineal sensation remains absent. The indwelling urinary catheter remains in place, draining clear yellow urine at 60 mL per hour. Blood glucose is 210 mg/dL; a sliding-scale insulin order is in place. Repeat labs: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (dropped from 10.8, expected surgical loss), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (stable), corrected calcium 10.4 mg/dL (slightly improved from 10.8, bisphosphonate and steroids helping), alkaline phosphatase 298 U/L (stable). The dexamethasone is continued at 4 mg IV every 6 hours with a planned taper over the coming weeks. Radiation oncology plans to initiate adjuvant radiation to the T6–T8 field in 10 to 14 days once the surgical wound has begun to heal. The patient's mood is cautiously hopeful. She asks the nurse, "Will I need to learn to use a wheelchair?"

Hypercalcemia of malignancy

rationale_en: The combination of escalating back pain at the level of known metastases, new motor weakness with upper motor neuron signs (hyperreflexia, positive Babinski), a definable sensory level, and autonomic (bladder) dysfunction is the classic presentation of malignant spinal cord compression. This is an oncologic emergency requiring urgent intervention.

options_or_rows_en.text: Provide emotional support and reassurance regarding the risk of paralysis
Administer the IV dexamethasone bolus as prescribed
Perform a complete head-to-toe nursing assessment
Prepare the patient for the insertion of an indwelling urinary catheter

rationale_en: Administering the urgent MSCC dexamethasone dose is the highest-priority pharmacological intervention for malignant spinal cord compression and should not be delayed. It reduces vasogenic edema surrounding the compressed cord, which can halt or slow neurological deterioration while awaiting definitive treatment.

stem_en: Review the patient's Clinical Course at Stage 1. Which action is the nurse's immediate priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Course — Postoperative Day 1
options_or_rows_en.content: Approximately 30 hours after admission (roughly 18 hours after posterior laminectomy): The patient is transferred back to the oncology unit from the PACU. She is awake, alert, and oriented. Surgical site dressing at the posterior thoracic spine is clean, dry, and intact with a Jackson-Pratt drain in place with 40 mL of serosanguineous output over the past 8 hours. Vital signs: temperature 37.8 °C, heart rate 78, blood pressure 124/74, respiratory rate 16, SpO₂ 97% on 2 L nasal cannula. Pain is 5 out of 10 at the surgical site, managed on IV hydromorphone. On neurological reassessment: lower extremity motor strength is now 3 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps — improved from the 2 out of 5 seen preoperatively. Ankle dorsiflexion is 2 out of 5 bilaterally (improved on the left from 1 out of 5). She can wiggle all toes weakly on command. Deep tendon reflexes remain hyperreflexic (3+) but clonus is no longer sustained — only 1 beat at the left ankle, absent on the right. Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally. Sensory level has improved: she now perceives light touch beginning at T8 and pinprick at T9. Perineal sensation remains absent. The indwelling urinary catheter remains in place, draining clear yellow urine at 60 mL per hour. Blood glucose is 210 mg/dL; a sliding-scale insulin order is in place. Repeat labs: hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (dropped from 10.8, expected surgical loss), creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (stable), corrected calcium 10.4 mg/dL (slightly improved from 10.8, bisphosphonate and steroids helping), alkaline phosphatase 298 U/L (stable). The dexamethasone is continued at 4 mg IV every 6 hours with a planned taper over the coming weeks. Radiation oncology plans to initiate adjuvant radiation to the T6–T8 field in 10 to 14 days once the surgical wound has begun to heal. The patient's mood is cautiously hopeful. She asks the nurse, "Will I need to learn to use a wheelchair?"

stem_en: At Stage 1, the patient's bladder scan shows 420 mL of urine and she has new perineal numbness. The nurse should recognize that these findings indicate which of the following?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation. Lower extremity motor strength is 4 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps and 4 minus out of 5 in ankle dorsiflexors, with the left side slightly weaker than the right. She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: A localized urinary tract infection secondary to impaired mobility
An expected adverse effect of her recent oxycodone administration
Dehydration requiring an increase in her IV fluid rate
Interruption of descending autonomic pathways indicating progressive spinal cord compression

rationale_en: Urinary retention in the context of thoracic spinal cord compression reflects the interruption of descending autonomic pathways. It is an ominous neurological finding when accompanied by worsening lower-extremity weakness, a rising sensory level, and new perineal sensory change.

stem_en: Review the Clinical Course at Stage 2. The neurosurgeon recommends emergent surgical decompression. Which of the following actions should the nurse take to safely prepare the patient for surgery? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: Explain the surgical risks and prognostic outcome of the laminectomy to the family.
Verify that the surgeon has discussed the procedure and obtained informed consent.
Ensure current laboratories, a type and screen, and imaging are available.
Hold the scheduled enoxaparin dose and communicate this to the surgical team.
Continue serial neurological assessments to document the trajectory of decline.
Administer a stat dose of regular insulin to correct the steroid-induced hyperglycemia to a target of 100 mg/dL.

rationale_en: Preparing a patient for emergent spinal decompression requires verifying informed consent, ensuring safety (holding anticoagulation), preparing necessary preoperative data (labs, imaging, type and screen), and continuing to monitor the rapid neurological decline. Holding the enoxaparin is critical to prevent epidural hematoma. The nurse's role in consent is to verify it was obtained by the provider, not to explain surgical risks or prognosticate.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the patient's daughter asks the nurse, "Will surgery make my mother walk again?" Which is the most appropriate response by the nurse?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment Findings & Laboratory Data
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline vital signs: temperature 37.1 °C, heart rate 92 beats per minute, blood pressure 138/82 mmHg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, SpO₂ 96% on room air. She is alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Cranial nerves are intact. Upper extremity strength is 5 out of 5 bilaterally with normal sensation. Lower extremity motor strength is 4 out of 5 bilaterally in hip flexors and quadriceps and 4 minus out of 5 in ankle dorsiflexors, with the left side slightly weaker than the right. She has difficulty lifting her legs against gravity with sustained resistance. Deep tendon reflexes at the patellar tendons are hyperreflexic (3+) bilaterally; Achilles reflexes are 3+ bilaterally. Babinski sign is equivocal on the right and extensor (positive, upgoing toe) on the left. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished below approximately the T8 dermatome, with a vague band of hyperesthesia at the T6–T7 level. Proprioception in the great toes is mildly reduced bilaterally. She has point tenderness on palpation over the T6 and T8 spinous processes. Rectal tone is present but diminished on exam performed by the admitting provider. A bladder scan shows a post-void residual of 280 mL. Skin is intact, warm, and dry. She has mild bilateral lower extremity edema (1+), which she says is chronic.

Baseline labs: WBC 6,800/µL, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, platelets 188,000/µL, sodium 141 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, BUN 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, calcium (corrected) 10.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone metastases), ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, glucose 118 mg/dL, PT/INR 1.0, aPTT 29 seconds. Urinalysis is unremarkable.

options_or_rows_en.text: "I understand how frightening this is. The goal of surgery is to take pressure off the spinal cord and prevent further neurologic loss. Some patients regain function, and the fact that your mother still has some leg movement is encouraging, but the neurosurgeon is the best person to discuss expected recovery."
"The damage from the tumor is usually permanent. Surgery is just a palliative measure to reduce pain, so she will likely need a wheelchair."
"Don't worry, the neurosurgeon is highly skilled. The surgery will fix the compression and she will be able to walk again after rehabilitation."
"I am not allowed to discuss her prognosis with you. You must wait until the surgeon comes out of the operating room."

rationale_en: This response balances honesty, empathy, and safe nursing boundaries. It correctly identifies the primary goal of surgery (preventing further loss), provides grounded hope based on clinical data (remaining motor function is a good prognostic sign), and appropriately refers definitive prognostication to the surgeon.

options_or_rows_en.text: Bilateral lower extremity motor strength is 3 out of 5
Babinski sign remains positive bilaterally
Sensory level is at T8–T9
Absence of perineal sensation
Persistent Dysfunction
Improvement from Stage 2 Preoperative Status

rationale_en: Evaluation requires serial comparison. Preoperatively, motor strength had dropped to 2/5; 3/5 is an improvement. The sensory level had risen to T6; dropping back to T8–T9 is an improvement. However, positive Babinski signs reflect persistent upper motor neuron damage, and absent perineal sensation reflects persistent autonomic/sacral dysfunction, which typically recovers last.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after the fluid bolus and electrolyte replacements, the patient's condition continues to worsen. Based on the new clinical findings, what is the most likely emerging complication, and which cue is the highest priority to communicate to the provider?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Localized PICC insertion site infection; new erythema extending 2 cm without purulent drainage.
Hypovolemic shock resulting from severe mucositis-associated dehydration; oliguria of 30 mL over 2 hours.
Sepsis likely originating from a catheter-related bloodstream infection; worsening hypotension despite the fluid bolus.
Medication-induced anaphylaxis; the onset of shivering with rigors after antibiotic and electrolyte administration.

rationale_en: The nurse should recognize that the constellation of findings—fever in a neutropenic patient, hemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids, rising lactate, and new PICC insertion site erythema—is most consistent with sepsis, likely from a catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI). The single most critical cue to emphasize is the persistent hemodynamic deterioration despite the fluid bolus, as it signals progression toward septic shock.

stem_en: At Stage 1, after the fluid bolus and electrolyte replacements, the patient's condition continues to worsen. Based on the new clinical findings, what is the most likely emerging complication, and which cue is the highest priority to communicate to the provider?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Update: Stage 3
options_or_rows_en.content: The PICC-drawn blood culture is confirmed as coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (later speciated as Staphylococcus epidermidis), with the PICC culture turning positive more than 2 hours before the peripheral culture, confirming CRBSI. The provider orders PICC removal. Because urgent central access is required to continue TPN and maintain glycemic stability, a new triple-lumen PICC is placed at a new site in the left arm under ID guidance with repeat cultures planned, and TPN is resumed at 60 mL/hr through the new line. Vancomycin is continued; cefepime is continued for neutropenic fever coverage. After 8 hours on the insulin drip, blood glucose has stabilized at 180–200 mg/dL. Repeat labs: potassium 3.8 mEq/L (improving after aggressive replacement), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (improving), creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (plateau, slight improvement), lactate 1.9 mmol/L (trending down), triglycerides 280 mg/dL (still elevated but decreasing with the reduced TPN rate). Temperature is 38.1 °C (trending down from peak), heart rate 98, blood pressure 110/68, respiratory rate 18, SpO₂ 97% on room air (supplemental oxygen weaned). Urine output has improved to 35–40 mL/hr. The patient reports mouth pain is now 6 out of 10 after the PCA dose was optimized and a scheduled IV acetaminophen was added; she is able to tolerate gentle sodium bicarbonate rinses again with coaching. The new PICC site is clean, dry, and without erythema. The nurse evaluates these trends and determines whether the patient is improving, identifies ongoing monitoring priorities, and plans for nutritional reassessment as the mucositis trajectory changes.

options_or_rows_en.text: Localized PICC insertion site infection; new erythema extending 2 cm without purulent drainage.
Hypovolemic shock resulting from severe mucositis-associated dehydration; oliguria of 30 mL over 2 hours.
Sepsis likely originating from a catheter-related bloodstream infection; worsening hypotension despite the fluid bolus.
Medication-induced anaphylaxis; the onset of shivering with rigors after antibiotic and electrolyte administration.

rationale_en: The nurse should recognize that the constellation of findings—fever in a neutropenic patient, hemodynamic instability unresponsive to fluids, rising lactate, and new PICC insertion site erythema—is most consistent with sepsis, likely from a catheter-related bloodstream infection (CRBSI). The single most critical cue to emphasize is the persistent hemodynamic deterioration despite the fluid bolus, as it signals progression toward septic shock.

stem_en: At Stage 1, the patient's blood glucose is 324 mg/dL and rising despite sliding-scale insulin coverage, and the TPN infusion continues at 85 mL/hr. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Assessment & Baseline Labs (Start of Shift)
options_or_rows_en.content:
Baseline assessment at start of night shift: Temperature 38.9 °C (102.0 °F) orally (elevated from 37.2 °C twelve hours earlier), heart rate 112 and regular, blood pressure 96/58 (down from 118/72 earlier in the day), respiratory rate 22, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Weight is 64.2 kg, up 1.8 kg from admission weight of 62.4 kg. The patient is alert but appears fatigued and in significant distress from pain. Oral examination reveals confluent, deep ulcerations across the buccal mucosa, tongue, and soft palate with white-yellow pseudomembranes and areas of bleeding — consistent with World Health Organization grade IV mucositis. Lips are cracked and bleeding. Thick, ropy saliva pools in the oropharynx. The patient gags and winces when attempting to open her mouth fully. Skin is warm, flushed, and dry. The right upper arm PICC dressing is intact but the insertion site shows new erythema extending approximately 2 cm from the insertion point, with mild tenderness on palpation; no purulent drainage is visible, but the area was not erythematous at the previous dressing assessment 48 hours ago. Abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with hypoactive bowel sounds. No stool in 3 days. Peripheral edema is trace bilateral in the lower extremities. Urine output has been 120 mL over the last 8 hours (approximately 15 mL/hr), decreased from 40–50 mL/hr the day prior.

Baseline labs drawn 6 hours prior to the nurse's shift: WBC 0.3 × 10³/µL (ANC less than 100/µL), hemoglobin 7.8 g/dL, platelets 18,000/µL, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL (up from baseline 1.3), sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L (low), chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 22 mEq/L, magnesium 1.4 mg/dL (low), phosphorus 2.8 mg/dL, calcium 8.0 mg/dL, albumin 2.1 g/dL (low), prealbumin 8 mg/dL (low, marking nutrition risk and inflammation alongside elevated CRP), blood glucose 268 mg/dL (elevated; was 312 mg/dL four hours before that), AST 42 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL, triglycerides 310 mg/dL (elevated), lactate 2.8 mmol/L (mildly elevated), C-reactive protein 14.2 mg/dL (elevated). Blood cultures were drawn from the PICC and peripherally at the time of the temperature spike but results are pending.

options_or_rows_en.text: Pause the TPN for one hour, administer the sliding-scale insulin, and restart the TPN once the blood glucose drops below 200 mg/dL.
Independently stop the TPN infusion immediately to prevent severe hyperglycemia from the continuous dextrose load.
Notify the provider of the worsening trend and anticipate an order for a continuous insulin infusion and a possible reduction in the TPN rate.
Continue monitoring the blood glucose every 6 hours and administer the sliding-scale insulin as ordered.

rationale_en: The nurse should notify the provider that the TPN dextrose load is exceeding the patient's insulin capacity, exacerbated by sepsis-related insulin resistance. The correct approach is rate reduction plus an insulin drip, which requires provider orders. The nurse must not independently stop or adjust the TPN rate.

stem_en: At Stage 1, the patient's blood glucose is 324 mg/dL and rising despite sliding-scale insulin coverage, and the TPN infusion continues at 85 mL/hr. What is the most appropriate nursing action?

options_or_rows_en.title: Clinical Update: Stage 3
options_or_rows_en.content: The PICC-drawn blood culture is confirmed as coagulase-negative Staphylococcus (later speciated as Staphylococcus epidermidis), with the PICC culture turning positive more than 2 hours before the peripheral culture, confirming CRBSI. The provider orders PICC removal. Because urgent central access is required to continue TPN and maintain glycemic stability, a new triple-lumen PICC is placed at a new site in the left arm under ID guidance with repeat cultures planned, and TPN is resumed at 60 mL/hr through the new line. Vancomycin is continued; cefepime is continued for neutropenic fever coverage. After 8 hours on the insulin drip, blood glucose has stabilized at 180–200 mg/dL. Repeat labs: potassium 3.8 mEq/L (improving after aggressive replacement), magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (improving), creatinine 1.7 mg/dL (plateau, slight improvement), lactate 1.9 mmol/L (trending down), triglycerides 280 mg/dL (still elevated but decreasing with the reduced TPN rate). Temperature is 38.1 °C (trending down from peak), heart rate 98, blood pressure 110/68, respiratory rate 18, SpO₂ 97% on room air (supplemental oxygen weaned). Urine output has improved to 35–40 mL/hr. The patient reports mouth pain is now 6 out of 10 after the PCA dose was optimized and a scheduled IV acetaminophen was added; she is able to tolerate gentle sodium bicarbonate rinses again with coaching. The new PICC site is clean, dry, and without erythema. The nurse evaluates these trends and determines whether the patient is improving, identifies ongoing monitoring priorities, and plans for nutritional reassessment as the mucositis trajectory changes.

options_or_rows_en.text: Verify that empiric antibiotic coverage (vancomycin) is actively infusing.
Communicate the early flagging of the PICC culture to the provider.
Remove the infected PICC line under provider orders.
Coordinate with the provider for an alternative central access and glycemic management plan.

rationale_en: The correct sequence ensures safe escalation and source control without compromising essential therapies. First, communicate the differential time to positivity (early flagging of the PICC culture), which strongly suggests CRBSI. Second, ensure that systemic treatment (empiric antibiotics) is actively treating the bacteremia. Third, coordinate alternative central access and a glucose plan, because removing the patient's only central line will abruptly stop the TPN, risking hypoglycemia. Finally, once the replacement plan is secure, remove the infected PICC line.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the patient has not performed mouth rinses in over 6 hours due to severe pain from confluent mucositis with bleeding, and she refuses further oral care attempts. What is the priority nursing intervention?

options_or_rows_en.text: Use a standard soft-bristle toothbrush to thoroughly clean the oral cavity, as skipping oral care increases the risk of secondary infection.
Coordinate analgesia (PCA bolus or topical analgesic) 15 to 20 minutes before assisting the patient with atraumatic sodium bicarbonate rinses.
Forcefully swab the oral cavity with a lemon-glycerin swab to ensure mucosal integrity is maintained despite the patient's refusal.
Defer all oral care until the patient's pain is completely resolved to avoid causing mucosal trauma against her will.

rationale_en: Oral care is a critical intervention to reduce secondary infection risk, but it must be facilitated through adequate pain management, not forced. The nurse should coordinate analgesia before care and use atraumatic technique (gentle rinses or ultra-soft/sponge swabs) given the severe thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk.

options_or_rows_en.text: No, because a newly placed PICC line must be used for TPN for at least 72 hours to ensure catheter patency before any enteral trials can begin.
No, because the patient has grade IV mucositis with severe systemic inflammation, and tolerating mouth rinses is not equivalent to tolerating nutritional intake.
Yes, because the patient's ability to tolerate sodium bicarbonate rinses indicates sufficient mucosal recovery to safely swallow clear liquids.
Yes, because prolonged TPN dependence significantly increases the risk of another central line infection, making enteral nutrition the safer immediate option.

rationale_en: TPN must be continued. Transitioning to enteral nutrition requires evidence that the mucosa can tolerate intake without aspiration or severe pain. Tolerating small volumes of rinses that are spit out is not equivalent to swallowing nutritional volumes through an ulcerated esophagus.

options_or_rows_en.title: Discharge-day client record
options_or_rows_en.content:
History: permanent atrial fibrillation, hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic kidney disease stage 3a, osteoarthritis. Left total knee arthroplasty was performed 4 days ago.
Home medications documented at admission: warfarin 5 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 500 mg twice daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen 650 mg as needed.
Inpatient course: warfarin held perioperatively; enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneously every 12 hours used while warfarin was held/restarted. Warfarin restarted on postoperative day 2.
Assessment: alert and oriented; daughter present as caregiver. T 36.8 C, HR 78 irregularly irregular, BP 134/76, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air. Left knee incision clean, dry, intact; mild ecchymosis and expected postoperative edema. No gum bleeding, petechiae, melena, or hematuria reported.
Laboratory results: INR 1.8, PT 21.4 seconds, aPTT 34 seconds, hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL and stable from postoperative day 1, platelets 198,000/mcL, creatinine 1.7 mg/dL, eGFR 44 mL/min, BUN 28 mg/dL, potassium 4.6 mEq/L, fasting glucose 142 mg/dL.

options_or_rows_en.text: Discharge list includes warfarin 5 mg daily and enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily for 14 days.
Discharge list changes metformin to 1000 mg twice daily, while the verified home list and MAR show 500 mg twice daily.
Hemoglobin is 10.8 g/dL and stable compared with postoperative day 1.
Left knee incision is clean, dry, and intact with mild surrounding ecchymosis.
Lisinopril and amlodipine doses match the verified home medication list.
Does not require medication-order clarification
Clarify before discharge

rationale_en: The simultaneous warfarin/enoxaparin discharge entries and unexplained metformin dose increase are medication-reconciliation discrepancies that must be clarified before prescriptions are transmitted. The stable hemoglobin, expected incision findings, and unchanged antihypertensive doses do not by themselves indicate a discharge medication-order discrepancy.

stem_en: For each finding in Stage 1, classify whether it requires clarification before discharge prescriptions are transmitted.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient and caregiver teaching
options_or_rows_en.content: With the corrected discharge prescriptions verified, the nurse reviews each medication with the client and daughter. The daughter asks why the abdominal injections used in the hospital are not continuing at home. The nurse explains that the final discharge plan removed enoxaparin after prescriber-pharmacist clarification and that the family should not add injections unless a prescriber specifically orders and verifies them. The nurse reviews warfarin safety, signs of bleeding, dietary consistency with vitamin K-containing foods, the INR appointment in 3 days, avoidance of new over-the-counter NSAIDs unless approved, and the corrected metformin dose using the home pill bottle label. The client and daughter use teach-back and confirm follow-up appointments.

options_or_rows_en.text: Delete enoxaparin from the list because the nurse recognizes it as a duplicate anticoagulant.
Proceed with teaching because the client has no current signs of bleeding.
Transmit the prescriptions so the pharmacy can begin filling them, then call about the metformin dose.

options_or_rows_en.text: Recheck the final discharge list against the verified home list, MAR, and pharmacist documentation before teaching.
Request that discharge prescriptions remain on hold until corrected orders are entered.
Remove enoxaparin from the discharge list after the resident verbally agrees it should come off.

The nurse should not independently remove medications or give family teaching that contradicts the verified discharge plan.

options_or_rows_en.text: Client discharged after routine medication teaching. Daughter present.
Enoxaparin removed from discharge list because it duplicated warfarin; metformin dose corrected by nurse after discussion.
Medication discrepancy found, but corrected orders are visible in the electronic health record; no additional documentation needed.

Resident and pharmacist notified of duplicate anticoagulant and unverified metformin dose; prescriptions held pending clarification; corrected orders verified; warfarin, metformin, bleeding precautions, INR follow-up, and teach-back documented.

stem_en: Stage 1: Which findings require the nurse to perform a direct suicide risk assessment? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background and current orders
options_or_rows_en.content: History: type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy, hypertension, major depressive disorder, and remote prior suicide attempt. Current orders include vancomycin IV, piperacillin-tazobactam IV, sliding-scale insulin, metformin, lisinopril, wound care consult, surgical consult, and standard fall-risk protocol. No psychiatric consult has been placed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Death of his wife 14 months ago and current social isolation
Poor sleep, tearfulness overnight, and refusal to eat breakfast
Remote history of a suicide attempt requiring ICU admission
Statement, 'What's the point,' with flat affect and poor eye contact
Positive PHQ-2 on admission without follow-up assessment
Clean, dry, intact left foot dressing

rationale_en: The positive PHQ-2, hopeless statement, depressive affect, prior attempt, bereavement, isolation, poor sleep, tearfulness, and poor intake are converging suicide-risk cues. The wound dressing status is important medical information but does not itself indicate suicide risk.

stem_en: Stage 1: Which findings require the nurse to perform a direct suicide risk assessment? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 3 — Psychiatric consultation and reassessment
options_or_rows_en.content: The psychiatric consultation is completed. The psychiatrist documents high acute suicide risk, recommends restarting sertraline 50 mg PO daily with titration plan, continuing 1:1 observation, daily reassessment, and social work involvement for discharge safety planning and means restriction. The client is calmer, tearful, and cooperative. He says, 'I'm sorry I yelled. I'm just tired of everything.' He asks whether his daughter has been called. The chart shows no emergency contact has been notified and the client has not previously given permission. He now says, 'Maybe you should call her. I don't know what to say to her.' He ate half of lunch, and the sitter reports no further agitation or attempts to leave. Wound care recommends continued IV antibiotics and vascular surgery evaluation before any amputation decision. Pharmacy notes that vancomycin exposure assessment and renal monitoring remain pending per facility protocol. The social worker begins discharge safety planning. The client denies firearms in the home and identifies his daughter as the only trusted person who could remove the stockpiled pills before discharge.

Remote history of a suicide attempt requiring ICU admission
Statement, 'What's the point,' with flat affect and poor eye contact
Positive PHQ-2 on admission without follow-up assessment
Clean, dry, intact left foot dressing

rationale_en: The positive PHQ-2, hopeless statement, depressive affect, prior attempt, bereavement, isolation, poor sleep, tearfulness, and poor intake are converging suicide-risk cues. The wound dressing status is important medical information but does not itself indicate suicide risk.

stem_en: Stage 1: After the client discloses active suicidal thoughts, an identified method at home, intent, and a prior attempt, which action is the nurse's priority?

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background and current orders
options_or_rows_en.content: History: type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy, hypertension, major depressive disorder, and remote prior suicide attempt. Current orders include vancomycin IV, piperacillin-tazobactam IV, sliding-scale insulin, metformin, lisinopril, wound care consult, surgical consult, and standard fall-risk protocol. No psychiatric consult has been placed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Remain with the client or ensure continuous qualified observation while activating immediate help and notifying the charge nurse and provider.
Leave the room to find the charge nurse because the client denies having means in the hospital.
Finish administering the scheduled morning medications before calling the provider.
Tell the client that these thoughts are common after a loss and reassess at the end of the shift.

rationale_en: High acute suicide risk requires immediate safety action. The nurse should not create an unobserved interval; the nurse should stay with the client or ensure another qualified staff member does so while summoning help and notifying the provider for ordered suicide precautions and psychiatric evaluation.

stem_en: Stage 2: The client becomes agitated during the room sweep, pulls at his IV, and moves toward the door. Which response by the nurse is best?

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background and current orders
options_or_rows_en.content: History: type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy, hypertension, major depressive disorder, and remote prior suicide attempt. Current orders include vancomycin IV, piperacillin-tazobactam IV, sliding-scale insulin, metformin, lisinopril, wound care consult, surgical consult, and standard fall-risk protocol. No psychiatric consult has been placed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stand directly in the doorway and tell him he cannot leave because he caused this by talking about suicide.
Threaten restraints unless he sits down immediately.

Use a calm tone, acknowledge that the precautions feel intrusive, explain they are to keep him safe while support is arranged, keep observation in place, and notify the team per policy if he attempts to leave.
Tell the sitter to step out so the client feels less watched.

rationale_en: Therapeutic de-escalation validates the client's distress, preserves rapport, explains the safety purpose of precautions, and maintains observation. Coercive threats, blame, or removing observation increase risk and may escalate agitation.

options_or_rows_en.content: The psychiatric consultation is completed. The psychiatrist documents high acute suicide risk, recommends restarting sertraline 50 mg PO daily with titration plan, continuing 1:1 observation, daily reassessment, and social work involvement for discharge safety planning and means restriction. The client is calmer, tearful, and cooperative. He says, 'I'm sorry I yelled. I'm just tired of everything.' He asks whether his daughter has been called. The chart shows no emergency contact has been notified and the client has not previously given permission. He now says, 'Maybe you should call her. I don't know what to say to her.' He ate half of lunch, and the sitter reports no further agitation or attempts to leave. Wound care recommends continued IV antibiotics and vascular surgery evaluation before any amputation decision. Pharmacy notes that vancomycin exposure assessment and renal monitoring remain pending per facility protocol. The social worker begins discharge safety planning. The client denies firearms in the home and identifies his daughter as the only trusted person who could remove the stockpiled pills before discharge.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stand directly in the doorway and tell him he cannot leave because he caused this by talking about suicide.
Threaten restraints unless he sits down immediately.

Use a calm tone, acknowledge that the precautions feel intrusive, explain they are to keep him safe while support is arranged, keep observation in place, and notify the team per policy if he attempts to leave.
Tell the sitter to step out so the client feels less watched.

rationale_en: Therapeutic de-escalation validates the client's distress, preserves rapport, explains the safety purpose of precautions, and maintains observation. Coercive threats, blame, or removing observation increase risk and may escalate agitation.

stem_en: Stage 3: Complete the statement about contacting the client's daughter.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background and current orders
options_or_rows_en.content: History: type 2 diabetes mellitus with peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy, hypertension, major depressive disorder, and remote prior suicide attempt. Current orders include vancomycin IV, piperacillin-tazobactam IV, sliding-scale insulin, metformin, lisinopril, wound care consult, surgical consult, and standard fall-risk protocol. No psychiatric consult has been placed.

options.text: obtain and document the client's consent and clarify what information may be shared
call the daughter immediately because she is listed as the emergency contact
tell the social worker to disclose the full psychiatric assessment
ask the daughter to remove pills from the home without telling the client what was shared
refuse all contact because psychiatric information can never be shared with family
facilitate a call with the client present or help him call her, sharing only authorized information

rationale_en: When a client has capacity and is willing to involve family, the consent pathway protects privacy and supports therapeutic trust. The nurse should clarify what may be shared, document consent, and coordinate with social work so the daughter can support means restriction only within the information the client authorizes, unless the team documents an imminent-danger exception.

stem_en: At 0830, how should the nurse interpret each assessment cue?

options_or_rows_en.title: Surgical history and active orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
58-year-old man, 14 hours after open right hemicolectomy for a cecal mass. History: obstructive sleep apnea (uses CPAP at home and brought machine to hospital), obesity (BMI 38), hypertension controlled with hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, GERD treated with pantoprazole 40 mg daily. No known drug allergies.

Postoperative orders: morphine sulfate 4 mg IV every 3 hours PRN moderate-to-severe pain; ondansetron 4 mg IV every 6 hours PRN nausea; lactated Ringer's at 125 mL/hr; sequential compression devices; incentive spirometry every 2 hours while awake; continuous pulse oximetry. CPAP machine is at bedside but has not been applied since surgery because the client has mostly been upright and awake.

Morphine doses received: 0200, 0500, and 0745. Current time: 0830.

options_or_rows_en.text: POSS score 3; opens eyes only briefly with loud voice and firm tactile stimulation
RR 8/min and shallow with SpO2 89% on room air
Pupils 2 mm bilaterally, constricted but reactive
Abdominal dressing dry and Jackson-Pratt output not appreciably increased
Would more strongly support bleeding or hypovolemia
Decreases concern for bleeding as the primary cause
Supports opioid-induced respiratory depression

rationale_en: The most concerning pattern is new oversedation with hypoventilation, oxygen desaturation, and miosis within the expected post-IV morphine effect window. The dry dressing and unchanged drain output make active surgical bleeding less likely as the primary explanation, although perfusion and urine output still require monitoring.

stem_en: At 0830, how should the nurse interpret each assessment cue?

options_or_rows_en.title: Response and ongoing plan
options_or_rows_en.content:
Rapid response team arrived at 0842. Naloxone 0.04 mg IV was administered at 0843 per protocol/order, with a second 0.04 mg IV dose at 0846 for partial response. By 0850, the client opens eyes spontaneously, follows commands, and takes deeper breaths when prompted.

0855 vital signs: HR 78/min, BP 126/78 mm Hg, RR 14/min, SpO2 94% on 2 L/min nasal cannula. Pupils 3 mm bilaterally. POSS: 2, slightly drowsy and easy to arouse. The client reports incisional pain 7/10 and asks, "Can I get something for this pain?" The morphine order has been discontinued. The surgical team is considering multimodal analgesia alternatives. The team notes that naloxone may wear off before the previously administered morphine effect is fully resolved.

stem_en: At 0830, which immediate nursing actions are appropriate while the nurse remains with the client? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.text: Use loud verbal and tactile stimulation to encourage breathing and assess arousability.
Hold any further opioid dose and reassess sedation and respiratory status before additional analgesia is given.
Administer another IV morphine dose if the client wakes and reports severe pain.
Apply supplemental oxygen and continue close respiratory monitoring.
Reposition the client to support the airway, such as elevating the head of bed as tolerated.
Document that the client is sleeping and return in 30 minutes to allow rest.

rationale_en: The client has opioid oversedation with hypoventilation. Immediate nursing actions focus on preventing additional opioid exposure, stimulating respirations, supporting airway positioning, providing oxygen, and reassessing continuously while escalating if the response is inadequate.

options_or_rows_en.text: Activate or confirm rapid response activation, notify the provider, support ventilation, and prepare naloxone for administration per protocol or order.
Continue stimulation and increase oxygen flow; reassess in 15 minutes because SpO2 improved from 89% to 91%.
Administer the next PRN morphine dose early because uncontrolled postoperative pain may be causing shallow breathing.
Apply the home CPAP machine and delay further escalation unless the oxygen saturation falls below 88%.

rationale_en: Persistent hypoventilation, apneic pauses, and inability to follow breathing commands after initial measures indicate that conservative bedside measures are not enough. The nurse should bring emergency support to the bedside, notify the prescriber, continue airway and ventilation support, and prepare naloxone according to facility protocol or order.

options.text: one full vial as a rapid bolus

The exact dose and dilution should follow the facility protocol or prescriber order; the case uses 0.04 mg IV increments as an example of titration.

options_or_rows_en.text: Severe postoperative hemorrhage because the hemoglobin is lower than the preoperative value.
Loss of anticoagulation because sequential compression devices have been in place.
Recurrent sedation and respiratory depression as naloxone effect decreases before the opioid effect has fully resolved.
Immediate bowel obstruction because absent bowel sounds were present at handoff.

rationale_en: After naloxone response, the client can become sedated again if the antagonist effect wanes while the previously administered opioid remains active. The nurse should maintain continuous respiratory and sedation monitoring, keep escalation resources available, hold further opioids until the prescriber revises the plan, and use ordered non-opioid or nonpharmacologic pain measures as appropriate.

stem_en: Which findings or handoff points should the nurse communicate to help reduce recurrence of opioid-induced respiratory depression? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Surgical history and active orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
58-year-old man, 14 hours after open right hemicolectomy for a cecal mass. History: obstructive sleep apnea (uses CPAP at home and brought machine to hospital), obesity (BMI 38), hypertension controlled with hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, GERD treated with pantoprazole 40 mg daily. No known drug allergies.

Postoperative orders: morphine sulfate 4 mg IV every 3 hours PRN moderate-to-severe pain; ondansetron 4 mg IV every 6 hours PRN nausea; lactated Ringer's at 125 mL/hr; sequential compression devices; incentive spirometry every 2 hours while awake; continuous pulse oximetry. CPAP machine is at bedside but has not been applied since surgery because the client has mostly been upright and awake.

Morphine doses received: 0200, 0500, and 0745. Current time: 0830.

options_or_rows_en.text: Hydrochlorothiazide and pantoprazole are the most likely direct causes of the sedation episode.
BMI 38, which increases risk for airway obstruction and opioid-related respiratory compromise.
The client has obstructive sleep apnea and his CPAP had not been used while he was sleeping or sedated postoperatively.
The client received three IV morphine doses within the same morning-to-shift interval, including the 0745 dose before deterioration.
The surgical team should reassess the analgesic plan, consider multimodal non-opioid strategies when appropriate, and continue close sedation and respiratory monitoring.
Because the client still reports pain, restarting a smaller morphine dose is the safest immediate plan.

rationale_en: The recurrence-prevention handoff should identify patient-specific and treatment-related risk factors: obstructive sleep apnea without CPAP during sleep/sedation, obesity, and repeated IV morphine exposure. It should also prompt a revised analgesic and monitoring plan rather than simply restarting opioids after naloxone response.

stem_en: For each finding in Stage 1, indicate whether it is expected in the situation or requires follow-up by the nurse.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1 — Nursing note
options_or_rows_en.content: 0600: A 62-year-old client is postoperative day 2 after lysis of adhesions for small-bowel obstruction. The client is NPO with a nasogastric tube to low intermittent suction. The client reports nausea and generalized weakness. NG output over the past 12 hours is 1,150 mL of green drainage. A 20-gauge peripheral IV is infusing 0.9% sodium chloride at 100 mL/hr in the left forearm. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions. The nurse notes flattened T waves and visible U waves on the bedside monitor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Potassium 2.8 mEq/L
Magnesium 1.4 mg/dL
NG output 1,150 mL over 12 hours
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Flattened T waves, U waves, and occasional PVCs
Expected/no immediate follow-up
Requires follow-up

rationale_en: The priority cues are severe hypokalemia, coexisting hypomagnesemia, high NG losses, and ECG changes/PVCs. The creatinine is not elevated in this exhibit, so it does not independently require urgent follow-up, although renal status must still be monitored during potassium therapy.

While the third potassium infusion is running through the peripheral IV, the client reports burning at the left forearm IV site. The nurse sees mild redness along the vein but no respiratory distress or chest pain.

stem_en: After reviewing the Stage 1 order, which action should the nurse take first?

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1 — Nursing note
options_or_rows_en.content: 0600: A 62-year-old client is postoperative day 2 after lysis of adhesions for small-bowel obstruction. The client is NPO with a nasogastric tube to low intermittent suction. The client reports nausea and generalized weakness. NG output over the past 12 hours is 1,150 mL of green drainage. A 20-gauge peripheral IV is infusing 0.9% sodium chloride at 100 mL/hr in the left forearm. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions. The nurse notes flattened T waves and visible U waves on the bedside monitor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Hold the potassium infusion and clarify the concentration, route, and rate with the provider and pharmacist before administration.
Stop telemetry until after potassium replacement is complete to reduce alarm fatigue.
Draw potassium chloride into a syringe and administer it slowly through the peripheral IV.
Start the potassium infusion as ordered because the potassium is critically low.

rationale_en: The original order would deliver 40 mEq over 2 hours through a peripheral IV. Even though the client needs urgent replacement, the nurse should not administer an unsafe or nonstandard potassium order unchanged. The priority is to hold the infusion, maintain monitoring, and clarify or revise the order before administration.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1 — Nursing note
options_or_rows_en.content: 0600: A 62-year-old client is postoperative day 2 after lysis of adhesions for small-bowel obstruction. Review repeat potassium, magnesium, and renal function results as ordered.
Assess the peripheral IV site for pain, redness, swelling, or leaking during therapy.
Continue telemetry while replacement is in progress.

options_or_rows_en.title: Stage 1 — Nursing note
options_or_rows_en.content: 0600: A 62-year-old client is postoperative day 2 after lysis of adhesions for small-bowel obstruction. The client is NPO with a nasogastric tube to low intermittent suction. The client reports nausea and generalized weakness. NG output over the past 12 hours is 1,150 mL of green drainage. A 20-gauge peripheral IV is infusing 0.9% sodium chloride at 100 mL/hr in the left forearm. Telemetry shows sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions. The nurse notes flattened T waves and visible U waves on the bedside monitor.

options_or_rows_en.text: Stop the potassium infusion, assess the IV site, maintain IV access with a new site as needed, and notify the provider/pharmacist.
Increase the potassium rate because the potassium is still below the usual reference range.
Clamp the NG tube for 8 hours so electrolyte levels rise faster.
Tell the client burning is expected and continue the infusion until the bag is empty.

rationale_en: Burning and redness at a peripheral potassium infusion site suggest irritation, phlebitis, infiltration, or early extravasation. The nurse should stop the infusion and assess the site rather than continue or speed the infusion. Ongoing electrolyte replacement can continue only through a safe, verified route.

stem_en: Stage 1: For each finding, classify whether it is a cue supporting escalation for clinical deterioration or a finding that can be managed/monitored when not accompanied by other concerning changes.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0700 nurse assessment and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 1 after open cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis with gangrenous changes noted intraoperatively. History: type 2 diabetes mellitus; benign prostatic hyperplasia; mild chronic kidney disease with baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL; well-controlled atrial fibrillation. Metformin and apixaban are held perioperatively.
Orders include: normal saline IV at 100 mL/hr; hydromorphone 0.5 mg IV every 4 hours as needed for pain; cefazolin IV; subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis; blood glucose checks with sliding-scale insulin; incentive spirometry; ambulation; Jackson-Pratt drain to bulb suction from the gallbladder fossa.
0700 assessment: pleasant, conversational, oriented x4; incisional pain 4/10; mild nausea without vomiting; reports feeling a little foggy after sleeping poorly.
Vital signs: T 37.6 °C oral, HR 82 irregular, BP 134/78, RR 16, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Lungs diminished at right base. Abdomen soft and mildly distended with expected incisional tenderness; dressing dry and intact. Jackson-Pratt drain has 40 mL serosanguineous output from the past 8 hours. Foley output for the past 8 hours is 320 mL amber clear urine. Skin warm and dry; capillary refill 2 seconds; no calf tenderness or edema.

options_or_rows_en.text: Heart rate increases from 82 to 102 while blood pressure falls from 134/78 to 112/68
New intermittent confusion in a client who was oriented and conversational at 0700
Jackson-Pratt output becomes increasingly sanguineous and the dressing stain expands
Urine output declines to 20 mL in the 1000–1100 hour
Hypoactive bowel sounds on postoperative day 1 after open abdominal surgery
Incisional pain rated 5/10 during the first ambulation attempt
Supports escalation for deterioration
Manage/monitor if isolated

rationale_en: The priority cues are not isolated postoperative discomforts; they show worsening perfusion and possible bleeding. Tachycardia with falling blood pressure, acute mental-status change, increasing sanguineous drainage with expanding staining, and declining urine output require prompt escalation. Hypoactive bowel sounds and moderate incisional pain can occur after open abdominal surgery and should be managed and monitored, but they do not by themselves explain the broader deterioration.

stem_en: Stage 1: For each finding, classify whether it is a cue supporting escalation for clinical deterioration or a finding that can be managed/monitored when not accompanied by other concerning changes.

options_or_rows_en.title: Orders, imaging, and pre-transport status
options_or_rows_en.content:
After the nurse invokes the chain of command, the attending surgeon arrives at 1315 and examines the client. Orders include CT abdomen with IV contrast; type and crossmatch for 2 units packed red blood cells; 500 mL normal saline bolus; broaden antibiotics to piperacillin-tazobactam; and transfer to the surgical intermediate care unit while preparing for operative management. CT shows moderate hemoperitoneum with active extravasation near the cystic artery stump, and the client is taken to the operating room for re-exploration.
Pre-transport status: T 38.4 °C, HR 116 irregular, BP 98/58, RR 26, SpO₂ 93% on 4 L nasal cannula after oxygen was applied for SpO₂ below 92%. Foley output for the past hour is 5 mL. The client responds only to voice and cannot state his name. The nurse verifies that the surgeon has initiated the appropriate consent, surrogate, or emergency-consent process per policy; verifies blood-product availability; and gives the OR nurse an SBAR handoff with the documented trends.

options_or_rows_en.text: Heart rate increases from 82 to 102 while blood pressure falls from 134/78 to 112/68
New intermittent confusion in a client who was oriented and conversational at 0700
Jackson-Pratt output becomes increasingly sanguineous and the dressing stain expands
Urine output declines to 20 mL in the 1000–1100 hour
Hypoactive bowel sounds on postoperative day 1 after open abdominal surgery
Incisional pain rated 5/10 during the first ambulation attempt
Supports escalation for deterioration
Manage/monitor if isolated

rationale_en: The priority cues are not isolated postoperative discomforts; they show worsening perfusion and possible bleeding. 0700 assessment: pleasant, conversational, oriented x4; incisional pain 4/10; mild nausea without vomiting; reports feeling a little foggy after sleeping poorly.

Abdomen soft and mildly distended with expected incisional tenderness; dressing dry and intact. Jackson-Pratt drain has 40 mL serosanguineous output from the past 8 hours. Foley output for the past 8 hours is 320 mL amber clear urine. Skin warm and dry; capillary refill 2 seconds; no calf tenderness or edema.

options_or_rows_en.text: Opioid-related sedation as the primary cause of the client’s change
Expected inflammatory response after open abdominal surgery
Postoperative hemorrhage with evolving hypovolemic shock
Pulmonary embolism as the most likely immediate explanation

rationale_en: The cluster of falling blood pressure, rising heart rate, declining urine output, new confusion, expanding dressing staining, and increasing sanguineous drain output is most consistent with postoperative bleeding and evolving hypovolemic shock. A low-grade fever and leukocytosis may coexist after surgery or with infection risk, but they do not explain the hemorrhagic drainage and hemoperfusion pattern. Opioids can contribute to sedation, but they should not be accepted as the primary explanation when perfusion indicators are worsening.

options_or_rows_en.text: Assessment: present the trend in vital signs from 0700 to 1100 rather than only the most recent values
Recommendation: request bedside assessment now and discuss stat labs, imaging, IV access, or other orders appropriate to the provider’s scope
Background: emphasize that mild confusion and low-grade fever can be common after surgery
Assessment: report the mental-status change, falling urine output, increasing sanguineous drain output, and expanding dressing stain

rationale_en: Effective SBAR communicates the nurse’s concern, relevant background, objective assessment trends, and a specific request for timely provider action. In this case the urgent message is not merely fever or confusion; it is a trajectory of possible bleeding and hypoperfusion. The nurse should request immediate bedside assessment and discuss provider-scope diagnostics or interventions, while avoiding language that minimizes the concern or delays evaluation.

Document the resident’s response fully before making any further calls
Hold all opioid medication and reassess in 1 hour to see whether the confusion improves

options_or_rows_en.text: Document objective findings, communication times, provider responses, and escalation after urgent safety actions are underway
Invoke the chain of command and request immediate attending-level evaluation because the client is worsening
Obtain or delegate ordered/protocol labs and prepare for anticipated interventions without delaying the escalation call
Keep the client safe in bed, repeat focused ABC/perfusion assessment, start close monitoring, and confirm a patent IV line

rationale_en: The nurse first protects the client and reassesses immediate stability while ensuring monitoring and IV access. Next, escalation is required because definitive evaluation and treatment are provider-level and the resident response was inadequate. Labs and preparation for likely interventions are important and may be delegated, but they should not delay escalation. Thorough documentation follows or occurs concurrently once urgent actions are underway.

Next, escalation is required because definitive evaluation and treatment are provider-level and the resident response was inadequate. Labs and preparation for likely interventions are important and may be delegated, but they should not delay escalation. Thorough documentation follows or occurs concurrently once urgent actions are underway.

stem_en: Stage 3: For each nursing action or interpretation before OR transfer, select whether it is appropriate or unsafe/incomplete.

options_or_rows_en.title: 0700 nurse assessment and orders
options_or_rows_en.content:
Postoperative day 1 after open cholecystectomy for acute cholecystitis with gangrenous changes noted intraoperatively. History: type 2 diabetes mellitus; benign prostatic hyperplasia; mild chronic kidney disease with baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL; well-controlled atrial fibrillation. Metformin and apixaban are held perioperatively.
Orders include: normal saline IV at 100 mL/hr; hydromorphone 0.5 mg IV every 4 hours as needed for pain; cefazolin IV; subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis; blood glucose checks with sliding-scale insulin; incentive spirometry; ambulation; Jackson-Pratt drain to bulb suction from the gallbladder fossa.
0700 assessment: pleasant, conversational, oriented x4; incisional pain 4/10; mild nausea without vomiting; reports feeling a little foggy after sleeping poorly.
Vital signs: T 37.6 °C oral, HR 82 irregular, BP 134/78, RR 16, SpO₂ 95% on room air. Lungs diminished at right base. Abdomen soft and mildly distended with expected incisional tenderness; dressing dry and intact. Jackson-Pratt drain has 40 mL serosanguineous output from the past 8 hours. Foley output for the past 8 hours is 320 mL amber clear urine. Skin warm and dry; capillary refill 2 seconds; no calf tenderness or edema.

options_or_rows_en.text: Reassess vital signs, urine output, and mental status after the 500 mL fluid bolus
Interpret HR 116, BP 98/58, urine output 5 mL/hr, and worsening responsiveness as lack of stabilization
Verify that the surgeon has initiated the consent, surrogate, or emergency-consent process per policy
Provide the OR nurse only the current vital signs and CT result because the prior trend is already documented
Delay transport until the confused client can personally sign the surgical consent
Communicate blood availability, IV access, allergies if known, last vital signs, interventions performed, and the full deterioration timeline
Unsafe or incomplete
Appropriate

rationale_en: The nurse evaluates whether initial resuscitation is effective by reassessing perfusion indicators. Persistent tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, and worsening mental status show that the client has not stabilized with crystalloid alone and needs definitive source control and blood availability. The nurse should verify that the responsible provider has addressed the appropriate consent pathway per policy, not attempt to obtain consent from a confused client or delay emergency transport for personal signature. A safe OR handoff includes the full deterioration timeline and interventions, not just the latest snapshot.

options_or_rows_en.title: Orders, imaging, and pre-transport status
options_or_rows_en.content:
After the nurse invokes the chain of command, the attending surgeon arrives at 1315 and examines the client. Orders include CT abdomen with IV contrast; type and crossmatch for 2 units packed red blood cells; 500 mL normal saline bolus; broaden antibiotics to piperacillin-tazobactam; and transfer to the surgical intermediate care unit while preparing for operative management. CT shows moderate hemoperitoneum with active extravasation near the cystic artery stump, and the client is taken to the operating room for re-exploration.
Pre-transport status: T 38.4 °C, HR 116 irregular, BP 98/58, RR 26, SpO₂ 93% on 4 L nasal cannula after oxygen was applied for SpO₂ below 92%. Foley output for the past hour is 5 mL. The client responds only to voice and cannot state his name. The nurse verifies that the surgeon has initiated the appropriate consent, surrogate, or emergency-consent process per policy; verifies blood-product availability; and gives the OR nurse an SBAR handoff with the documented trends.

stem_en: At Stage 1, how should the nurse interpret the signed consent form?

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: 58-year-old woman admitted to same-day surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Primary language is Spanish; she speaks limited conversational English. History: type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, symptomatic cholelithiasis with two prior episodes of biliary colic. No known drug allergies. Preoperative orders: NPO since midnight; 0.9% sodium chloride at 75 mL/hr; capillary blood glucose every 4 hours; hold the morning metformin dose. A surgical resident presented the consent form about 30 minutes before the nurse's first contact. The consent form is signed.

options_or_rows_en.text: The consent is not valid for proceeding because the client states she did not understand the risks and felt pressure to sign.
The consent can be accepted if the nurse documents that the client speaks some English.
The consent is valid if the daughter confirms that the client knows the gallbladder will be removed.
The consent is valid because the form is signed and the client appears calm.

rationale_en: A signature is evidence of a consent process, not proof that informed consent is valid. The client's statement that she did not understand the risks and signed because the physician seemed to want her to is a direct cue that comprehension and voluntariness are not established. The nurse should not treat this form as sufficient for transport to the operating room.

stem_en: At Stage 1, how should the nurse interpret the signed consent form?

options_or_rows_en.title: Outcome update
options_or_rows_en.content: The surgical resident returns and, using the certified VRI interpreter, re-explains the procedure, risks of bleeding, infection, bile duct injury and possible conversion to open surgery, benefits, alternatives including continued conservative management, and the right to refuse. The interpreter relays each segment. The client asks whether she will have a large scar and how long she will stay in the hospital. The resident answers both questions. The client states through the interpreter, "Now I understand. I want to have the surgery today." A new consent form is completed with the interpreter's name and identification number documented. The nurse witnesses the client's signature after confirming that the client verbalized understanding and that a qualified interpreter was used. BP 136/84 mm Hg, HR 78/min, SpO₂ 99% on room air. The client appears calmer and is cleared for transport to the operating room.

stem_en: Which nursing actions are appropriate at Stage 1? Select all that apply.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: 58-year-old woman admitted to same-day surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Primary language is Spanish; she speaks limited conversational English. History: type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, symptomatic cholelithiasis with two prior episodes of biliary colic. No known drug allergies. Preoperative orders: NPO since midnight; 0.9% sodium chloride at 75 mL/hr; capillary blood glucose every 4 hours; hold the morning metformin dose. A surgical resident presented the consent form about 30 minutes before the nurse's first contact. The consent form is signed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Ask the daughter to interpret the risks because the client trusts her and the daughter is pre-med.
Notify the surgical resident that the client did not understand the risk explanation and needs the provider to repeat the consent discussion through an interpreter.
Independently explain the surgical risks, benefits, and alternatives and obtain a new signature.
Allow the daughter to remain for emotional support if the client wants, while using the qualified interpreter for clinical interpretation.
Arrange a qualified medical interpreter for the consent discussion.
Hold transport to the operating room until valid informed consent is established.

rationale_en: The nurse should pause the process, obtain a qualified interpreter, and notify the provider that the consent discussion must be repeated because the client has not demonstrated understanding. The daughter may remain as support if the client wants, but she should not serve as the interpreter for this surgical consent discussion.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: 58-year-old woman admitted to same-day surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Primary language is Spanish; she speaks limited conversational English. History: type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, symptomatic cholelithiasis with two prior episodes of biliary colic. No known drug allergies. A surgical resident presented the consent form about 30 minutes before the nurse's first contact. The consent form is signed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Arrange a qualified medical interpreter rather than using the daughter for the consent discussion.
Use the interpreter to explain the reason for the delay, reassure the client that the goal is understanding, and assess for questions or ambivalence.
Notify the surgical resident that the consent discussion must be repeated through the interpreter.
Hold transport to the operating room because the signed form is not sufficient after the client's statement.
Witness the new consent only after the provider re-explains the procedure and the client verbalizes understanding and voluntary agreement.

rationale_en: The nurse first prevents an unsafe/legal process error by holding transport, then obtains qualified language access, communicates the barrier to the provider, supports the client during the delay, and witnesses a new consent only after the provider repeats the consent discussion and the client demonstrates understanding and voluntary agreement.

stem_en: At Stage 2, the resident says, "She already signed — we're going to lose our OR time." What is the nurse's best response?

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: 58-year-old woman admitted to same-day surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Primary language is Spanish; she speaks limited conversational English. History: type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, symptomatic cholelithiasis with two prior episodes of biliary colic. No known drug allergies. Preoperative orders: NPO since midnight; 0.9% sodium chloride at 75 mL/hr; capillary blood glucose every 4 hours; hold the morning metformin dose. A surgical resident presented the consent form about 30 minutes before the nurse's first contact. The consent form is signed.

options_or_rows_en.text: Give an anxiolytic medication so the client's blood pressure returns to baseline before transport.
Ask the daughter to quickly explain the risks so the OR schedule is not disrupted.
Send the client to the operating room and document that the resident accepted responsibility for consent.
State that the procedure cannot proceed on the current consent, request provider re-explanation through the interpreter, and involve the charge nurse or chain of command if pressure continues.

rationale_en: The nurse has an independent advocacy obligation not to facilitate a procedure when the patient has indicated lack of understanding. Scheduling pressure does not override informed-consent requirements. The correct action is to maintain the hold, request provider re-explanation through the qualified interpreter, and use the charge nurse or chain of command if needed.

stem_en: At Stage 3, the provider has repeated the consent discussion through the certified VRI interpreter, and the client states she understands and wants surgery. Complete the documentation statement.

options_or_rows_en.title: Patient background
options_or_rows_en.content: 58-year-old woman admitted to same-day surgery for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Primary language is Spanish; she speaks limited conversational English. History: type 2 diabetes managed with metformin, hypertension treated with lisinopril, symptomatic cholelithiasis with two prior episodes of biliary colic. No known drug allergies. A surgical resident presented the consent form about 30 minutes before the nurse's first contact. The consent form is signed.

options.text: the daughter confirmed that the client trusts her
the original signed form was already in the chart
the client verbalized understanding and voluntary agreement after the provider's interpreted explanation
that the nurse personally explained all surgical risks and alternatives
only the time the operating room was delayed and the resident's objection
the qualified interpreter's name or identification number, the VRI mode, and that the prior form was superseded according to facility policy

rationale_en: After the provider repeats the consent discussion through a qualified interpreter and the client verbalizes understanding and voluntary agreement, the nurse may witness the new consent. Documentation should identify the interpreter and mode used and accurately show that the earlier defective consent was superseded or handled according to facility policy, without backdating or implying that the nurse provided the surgical consent explanation.
